topics.title: Overview of Computational Complexity: Decision Problem vs Optimization Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Computational Complexity: Decision Problem vs Optimization Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Computational Problem?

In the language of theoretical computer science, a **computational problem** is a mathematical object that defines an infinite set of inputs (called *instances*) together with a specification of the desired output for each instance. The two most fundamental categories we encounter in algorithmic design are **Decision Problems** and **Optimization Problems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Every student must internalize that *most optimization problems studied in this course (Vertex Cover, TSP, Knapsack, Set Cover, etc.) are NP-Hard*. Because no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known (or believed to exist) for these, we must study *approximation algorithms*. Understanding the decision-vs-optimization distinction is the gateway to the theory of NP-completeness that motivates this entire course.

---

### 1.2 Decision Problem — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Decision Problem):**
> A **decision problem** $\Pi$ is a function
> $$\Pi : \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$$
> that maps every binary-encoded instance $x \in \{0,1\}^{*}$ to exactly one of two answers: **YES (1)** or **NO (0)**. Equivalently, $\Pi$ can be defined as a language $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^{*}$ such that $x \in L \iff \Pi(x) = \text{YES}$.

The output is *binary* — the algorithm simply answers "yes" or "no"; it does not produce a witness object. The complexity is measured purely in terms of the running time to verify the answer for the worst-case input of size $n$.

**Canonical Example — VERTEX-COVER (decision version):**
Given a graph $G=(V,E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ have a vertex cover of size *at most* $k$?

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you walk into a job interview and the recruiter asks you: *"Can you lift a 50 kg box?"* — You answer **YES** or **NO**. You don't have to lift it, you just have to answer the binary question. A *decision problem* is exactly this kind of yes/no interrogation.

---

### 1.3 Optimization Problem — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Optimization Problem):**
> An **optimization problem** $\Pi$ is a triple $(\mathcal{I}, \mathcal{S}, f)$ where
> - $\mathcal{I}$ is the set of instances,
> - $\mathcal{S}(I) \subseteq \{0,1\}^{*}$ is the set of *feasible solutions* for each instance $I \in \mathcal{I}$,
> - $f : \mathcal{I} \times \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a *measure / cost function*.
>
> The goal is to find an $S^{*} \in \mathcal{S}(I)$ that **minimizes** $f(I, S)$ (a minimization problem) or **maximizes** $f(I, S)$ (a maximization problem).

In contrast to the decision version, the optimization version *actually produces a witness* — a concrete candidate solution — together with its measured cost.

**Canonical Example — VERTEX-COVER (optimization version):**
Given a graph $G=(V,E)$, find a vertex cover $C \subseteq V$ of *minimum* cardinality. The answer is a *set*, not just YES/NO.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Now imagine the same job interview, but the recruiter asks: *"What is the maximum weight you can lift?"* — You must perform an experiment and return a *number* (and ideally a demonstration of how). An *optimization problem* returns the *best* value and the *best* configuration achieving it.

---

### 1.4 Side-by-Side Comparison Table

| Aspect | Decision Problem | Optimization Problem |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | **YES / NO** | A feasible solution + its cost |
| Mathematical Form | A language $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^{*}$ | A function $f : \mathcal{I} \times \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| Goal | Decide membership | Find extremum (min / max) |
| Complexity Metric | Worst-case running time $T(n)$ | Worst-case running time + quality of solution |
| Example | "Is there a path of length $\le k$?" | "Find the shortest path." |
| In NP-completeness | Defines class **NP** | Often **NP-Hard** |
| Algorithms Used | Polynomial verification | Exact (often exponential) or approximation |

---

### 1.5 The Polynomial-Time Bridge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** An optimization problem and its decision counterpart *live in the same complexity neighbourhood*. A polynomial-time algorithm for the optimization version immediately yields a polynomial-time algorithm for the decision version, but **not vice versa** (in general). This asymmetry is precisely why NP-complete decision problems can have approximation-friendly optimization versions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decision Problems and the Class NP

A decision problem $\Pi$ belongs to the class **P** if there exists a deterministic Turing machine $M$ and a polynomial $p(\cdot)$ such that for every instance $x$, $M$ halts on $x$ within $p(\vert x \vert)$ steps and outputs $\Pi(x)$ correctly.

A decision problem $\Pi$ belongs to the class **NP** if there exists a deterministic Turing machine $V$ (the *verifier*) and a polynomial $p(\cdot)$ such that:
- For every YES-instance $x$, there exists a *certificate / witness* $w$ with $\vert w \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$ such that $V(x, w) = 1$ in polynomial time.
- For every NO-instance $x$, for every $w$ with $\vert w \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$, we have $V(x, w) = 0$.

The famous open conjecture $\mathbf{P \neq NP}$ is the cornerstone of modern complexity theory. Every **NP-complete** problem (Cook-Levin theorem) lies in NP and every NP problem reduces to it in polynomial time. SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) is the canonical NP-complete problem.

---

### 2.2 Optimization Problems and NP-Hardness

An optimization problem is **NPO** (NP-optimization) if its instances, solutions, and cost function are *recognizable in polynomial time*. Its decision version, obtained by thresholding the cost, must lie in NP.

An NPO problem is **NPO-complete** (informally **NP-hard**) if its decision version is NP-complete. This means: *if you can solve the optimization version exactly in polynomial time, then P = NP*. Since most theorists believe P $\neq$ NP, we cannot hope for exact polynomial-time algorithms.

This is the **theoretical justification for approximation algorithms**: we relax the demand of *optimality* and instead demand a provable approximation ratio.

---

### 2.3 Reduction: From Optimization to Decision (and Back)

The standard technique used to establish NP-hardness is **polynomial-time many-one reduction**:

$$A \le_{p} B$$
means there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$.

Given an optimization problem $\Pi_{OPT}$ with cost function $f$, its **threshold (decision) version** $\Pi_{DEC}(k)$ is:

$$\Pi_{DEC}(k) : \text{``Does } I \text { admit a feasible solution } S \text{ with } f(I,S) \le k \text{?''}$$

If we could solve $\Pi_{OPT}$ exactly in time $T(n)$, then a binary-search call on $k$ (over $\log(\text{cost-range})$ thresholds) solves $\Pi_{DEC}$ in time $O(T(n) \cdot \log \text{Range})$.

Conversely, solving $\Pi_{DEC}$ via binary search gives us the **optimal cost** $OPT(I)$; reconstructing the actual optimal solution requires a **self-reducibility** argument (typical for NP problems where the witness is small).

---

### 2.4 Approximation Ratio — The Currency of This Course

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Approximation Ratio):**
> For a minimization problem, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ achieves an approximation ratio $\rho(n) \ge 1$ if for every instance $I$ of size $n$,
> $$\frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{OPT(I)} \le \rho(n)$$
> For a maximization problem, the ratio is inverted:
> $$\frac{OPT(I)}{\mathcal{A}(I)} \le \rho(n)$$

A **PTAS** (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme) achieves $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation in time $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$. An **FPTAS** achieves it in time polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\Pi$ | A computational problem | Definition |
| $\mathcal{I}$ | Set of all instances | Optimization |
| $\mathcal{S}(I)$ | Feasible solutions for instance $I$ | Optimization |
| $f(I, S)$ | Cost / measure of solution $S$ on $I$ | Optimization |
| $OPT(I)$ | Optimal cost on instance $I$ | Analysis |
| $\mathcal{A}(I)$ | Cost produced by algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ | Analysis |
| $\rho(n)$ | Approximation ratio at size $n$ | Analysis |
| $\le_{p}$ | Polynomial-time many-one reduction | Hardness proof |
| $L$ | Formal language (set of YES-instances) | Decision problem |
| $\vert x \vert$ | Length of binary string $x$ | Complexity notation |
| **P** | Polynomial-time solvable | Decision class |
| **NP** | Polynomial-time verifiable | Decision class |
| **NPO** | NP optimization problems | Optimization class |
| **APX** | Constant-ratio approximable | Optimization class |
| **PTAS** | $(1+\epsilon)$ in $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | Stronger scheme |
| **FPTAS** | $(1+\epsilon)$ in poly$(n, 1/\epsilon)$ | Strongest scheme |

---

### 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

Every real-world engineering system that involves *planning under combinatorial explosion* (VLSI chip routing, network design, scheduling, bioinformatics alignment, ML hyperparameter search, ride-sharing dispatch) is an instance of an optimization problem. The decision-vs-optimization lens lets us classify them by *how badly* complexity bites, and choose between exact exponential algorithms (e.g., branch-and-bound), heuristics (no guarantees), or approximation algorithms (provable guarantees). Understanding this taxonomy is essential for any computer scientist or engineer building scalable systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing the Decision Version of Knapsack

Consider the **Knapsack** optimization problem:

**Instance:** $n$ items, each with weight $w_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ and value $v_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$; a knapsack of capacity $W$.

**Optimization Goal:** Maximize $\sum_{i \in S} v_i$ subject to $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \le W$.

Its **decision version** introduces a threshold $K$:

**Decision Goal:** Does there exist $S \subseteq \{1, \dots, n\}$ such that $\sum_{i \in S} v_i \ge K$ **and** $\sum_{i \in S} w_i \le W$?

---

### 3.2 Reduction Sketch: Optimization $\Rightarrow$ Decision

Suppose we have a polynomial-time algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ that solves the optimization version of Knapsack, returning both the optimal value $V^{*}$ and the optimal set $S^{*}$. We show how to solve the decision version:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input to Decision:} & \quad \langle (w_1, v_1), \dots, (w_n, v_n), W, K \rangle \\
\text{Step 1 — Run } \mathcal{A}: & \quad S^{*}, V^{*} \leftarrow \mathcal{A}\big((w_1,v_1),\dots,(w_n,v_n), W\big) \\
\text{Step 2 — Compare:} & \quad \text{if } V^{*} \ge K \text{ then return YES else return NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

This algorithm runs in time $O(T(n) + n)$, where $T(n)$ is the time of $\mathcal{A}$. **Hence** a polynomial-time optimization algorithm implies a polynomial-time decision algorithm. The *converse* (using binary search on $K$) is also true provided the values are polynomially bounded:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input to Optimization:} & \quad \langle (w_1, v_1), \dots, (w_n, v_n), W \rangle \\
\text{Step 1 — Bound the range:} & \quad V_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \le n \cdot V_{\max\text{-item}} \\
\text{Step 2 — Binary search on } K: & \quad K \in [0, V_{\max}], \text{ at most } \lceil \log_2 V_{\max} \rceil \text{ queries} \\
\text{Step 3 — Decision oracle call:} & \quad \text{For each } K, \text{ run the decision algorithm} \\
\text{Step 4 — Self-reducibility:} & \quad \text{Use the witness } S \text{ from the smallest feasible } K
\end{aligned}
$$

The number of binary-search iterations is bounded by $O(\log V_{\max}) = O(n \log V_{\max\text{-item}})$, which is *polynomial* in the input length.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: TSP Decision Version

**Given:** $n$ cities with pairwise distances $d(i,j) \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$, a bound $B$.

**Decision Goal:** Does there exist a Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities with total length $\le B$?

This decision problem is **NP-complete** (proved by reduction from Hamiltonian Cycle). Therefore, the optimization version (find the *minimum* tour) is **NP-hard**. No polynomial-time algorithm is believed to exist.

**Approximation Consequence:** Since TSP with general metric distances admits Christofides' $1.5$-approximation but general TSP has *no* $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation unless $\mathbf{P = NP}$, the gap between decision hardness and approximability is **structural**, not accidental.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hardness and Approximability Across Canonical Problems

| Problem | Decision Class | Optimization Class | Best Approximation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shortest Path | **P** | **P** | Exact | Dijkstra / Bellman-Ford |
| Vertex Cover | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $2$-approx | Tight under UGC |
| Set Cover | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $\ln n$-approx | Greedy is essentially optimal |
| TSP (metric) | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $1.5$-approx | Christofides' algorithm |
| TSP (general) | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | **No $(1+\epsilon)$** | Unless P = NP |
| Knapsack | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | FPTAS | Only weakly NP-hard |
| Max-Cut | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $0.878$-approx | Goemans–Williamson |
| Max-SAT | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $0.796$-approx | Random / biased coin |
| Independent Set | **NP-complete** | **NP-hard** | $n^{1-\epsilon}$ lower bound | Worst among classic problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that Knapsack is the *only* classic NP-hard problem in this list admitting an **FPTAS**. This is because its complexity is *weakly* NP-hard (the bad inputs come from large integer values, not from combinatorial structure). Hard problems coming from combinatorial structure (Vertex Cover, Max-Cut) typically only admit constant-factor or logarithmic-factor approximations.

---

### 3.5 Worked Computation: Verifying an NP Certificate

Consider the certificate verification for **VERTEX-COVER**:

**Instance:** Graph $G=(V,E)$ with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges; integer $k$.

**Certificate:** A proposed vertex cover $C \subseteq V$.

**Verifier Algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{If } \vert C \vert > k, \text{ reject immediately.} \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{For each edge } (u,v) \in E, \text{ check if } u \in C \text{ or } v \in C. \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{If all } m \text{ edges are covered, accept; else reject.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The verifier runs in time $O(n + m)$, which is *polynomial* in the input size. Hence VERTEX-COVER lies in NP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Computational Complexity Classes

The following diagram maps the relationship between the major complexity classes relevant to this course:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pclass fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#2d6a2d,color:#000
    classDef npclass fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef npcclass fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef nphclass fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c87,color:#000
    classDef apxclass fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000

    P[P - Polynomial Time]:::pclass
    NP[NP - Nondeterministic Polynomial]:::npclass
    NPC[NP Complete]:::npcclass
    NPH[NP Hard]:::nphclass
    APX[APX - Approximable]:::apxclass
    PTAS[PTAS]:::apxclass
    FPTAS[FPTAS]:::apxclass

    DEC[Decision Problems]:::npclass
    OPT[Optimization Problems]:::nphclass
    NPO[NPO Class]:::nphclass

    P --> NP
    NP --> NPC
    NPC --> NPH
    OPT --> NPO
    NPO --> APX
    APX --> PTAS
    PTAS --> FPTAS
    DEC -.->|threshold| OPT
    NPC -.->|reduction| NPO

    SAT[SAT Problem]:::npcclass
    SAT --> NPC
    TSP[TSP Decision]:::npcclass
    TSP --> NPC
    VC[Vertex Cover Decision]:::npcclass
    VC --> NPC
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** The green node **P** is fully contained in **NP** (yellow). **NP-complete** (red) is the intersection of **NP** and **NP-hard** (purple). Optimization problems (NPO) are linked to decision problems via the *threshold* reduction. Approximation classes **APX, PTAS, FPTAS** form a nested hierarchy of increasingly efficient approximation guarantees.

---

### 4.2 Decision-to-Optimization Reduction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef inputBox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef processBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#2d6a2d,color:#000

    I1[Optimization Instance I]:::inputBox
    I2[Decision Instance I with threshold K]:::inputBox
    A1[Algorithm A solves optimization]:::processBox
    A2[Binary search over cost values]:::processBox
    O1[Optimal solution S star and cost]:::outputBox
    O2[YES NO answer]:::outputBox

    I1 --> A1 --> O1
    O1 -.->|triggers| I2
    I2 --> A2 --> O2
    O2 -.->|informs next| A2
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** Forward direction (top): an optimization solver yields an optimal solution and cost. Reverse direction (bottom): binary search on the threshold K converts decision oracle calls into the optimal cost value.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Approximation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef blockInput fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef blockProc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef blockVerify fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c87,color:#000
    classDef blockOutput fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#2d6a2d,color:#000

    inp[Input Instance I of size n]:::blockInput
    parse[Parser and Feasibility Checker]:::blockProc
    algo[Core Approximation Algorithm A]:::blockProc
    verify[Ratio Verifier - checks rho bound]:::blockVerify
    out[Output - Approximate solution S and cost A of I]:::blockOutput

    inp --> parse
    parse --> algo
    algo --> verify
    verify --> out
    out -.->|iterative refinement| algo
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** The **Parser** ensures the instance is valid. The **Core Algorithm** runs in polynomial time and produces a feasible solution. The **Ratio Verifier** mathematically proves that the output cost lies within $\rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$. This three-stage pipeline is the canonical architecture for every approximation algorithm studied in this course.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between a decision problem and an optimization problem with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **decision problem** is one whose answer is a binary YES/NO. Formally, it is a function from instance strings to $\{0,1\}$. Example: *"Given a graph $G$ and integer $k$, does $G$ contain a vertex cover of size at most $k$?"*

An **optimization problem** asks for the *best* feasible solution, minimizing or maximizing a cost function. Example: *"Given a graph $G$, find a vertex cover of minimum cardinality."*

The key contrast: a decision problem returns a Boolean verdict; an optimization problem returns a *witness solution* together with its optimal cost.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *State the formal definition of the class NP. Why is every problem in P also in NP?*

**Model Answer:**

The class **NP** consists of all decision problems $\Pi$ for which there exists a polynomial-time deterministic verifier $V$ and a polynomial $p$ such that:
- For every YES-instance $x$, $\exists$ certificate $w$ with $\vert w \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$ and $V(x, w)$ accepts.
- For every NO-instance $x$, $\forall w$ with $\vert w \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$, $V(x, w)$ rejects.

Every problem in **P** is also in **NP** because if a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm solves the decision problem outright, we can use the *empty string* $\varepsilon$ as the certificate — the verifier just runs the polynomial-time solver on $x$ and accepts if and only if the answer is YES.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Question A]**

**Q3. (14 Marks)**
**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define **decision problem** and **optimization problem** rigorously. Construct the decision version of the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** and show that it belongs to NP by exhibiting a polynomial-time verifier.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the concept of **polynomial-time reduction**. Using it, prove that the **decision version of TSP is NP-hard** by sketching a reduction from the **Hamiltonian Cycle** problem.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The **decision problem** is defined as a function $\Pi : \{0,1\}^{*} \to \{0,1\}$ mapping each input string to YES/NO. The **optimization problem** is a triple $(\mathcal{I}, \mathcal{S}, f)$ where $f : \mathcal{I} \times \mathcal{S} \to \mathbb{R}$ assigns a cost to each feasible solution.

The **decision version of TSP** is: *Given $n$ cities with pairwise distances $d(i,j) \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ and a bound $B \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$, does there exist a Hamiltonian cycle of total length $\le B$?*

**Verifier for TSP-decision:**
- Input: $(G, B)$, certificate: a sequence of $n$ distinct vertices $\langle v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n \rangle$.
- Verify the sequence visits each city exactly once: $O(n)$.
- Sum the $n$ edge lengths and check if total $\le B$: $O(n)$.
- Total verifier time: $O(n)$ — polynomial. Hence TSP-decision $\in$ NP.

**(b)** A **polynomial-time many-one reduction** from problem $A$ to problem $B$ is a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$. We write $A \le_{p} B$. If $B \in P$, then $A \in P$.

**Reduction Hamiltonian-Cycle $\le_{p}$ TSP-decision:**
Given an undirected graph $G'=(V', E')$ with $n = \vert V' \vert$ vertices, construct a complete graph $G$ on the same $n$ vertices with edge weights:
$$d(u,v) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (u,v) \in E' \\ 2 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
Set the threshold $B = n$. A Hamiltonian cycle in $G'$ corresponds to a tour of length exactly $n$ in $G$. Any Hamiltonian cycle in $G$ using non-$E'$ edges has length $> n$. Therefore $G'$ has a Hamiltonian cycle iff $G$ has a tour of length $\le B$. Since Hamiltonian-Cycle is NP-complete, TSP-decision is NP-hard. Combined with part (a), TSP-decision is **NP-complete**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Question B]**

**Q4. (14 Marks)**
**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define the **approximation ratio** for both minimization and maximization problems. Give an example of an NP-hard optimization problem that admits a polynomial-time constant-factor approximation, and another that admits a fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS).

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Convert the **Vertex Cover optimization problem** into its decision version. Show that if the decision version were solvable in polynomial time, then so would be the optimization version (via binary search plus self-reducibility).

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** For a minimization problem, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ has approximation ratio $\rho \ge 1$ if for every instance $I$,
$$\frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{OPT(I)} \le \rho$$
For a maximization problem,
$$\frac{OPT(I)}{\mathcal{A}(I)} \le \rho$$

- **Constant-factor approximation example:** *Vertex Cover* admits a simple $2$-approximation: pick both endpoints of any uncovered edge greedily. The cover produced has size $\le 2 \cdot OPT$.
- **FPTAS example:** *Knapsack* admits an FPTAS using dynamic programming with scaled values. For any $\epsilon > 0$, the running time is polynomial in $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

**(b)** **Decision version of Vertex Cover:** Given $G=(V,E)$ and integer $k$, does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\le k$?

**Reduction (Decision $\Rightarrow$ Optimization):**
- Lower bound: $k_{\min} = 0$.
- Upper bound: $k_{\max} = \vert V \vert$ (trivial cover).
- Binary search over $k$ in $[0, \vert V \vert]$: requires $\lceil \log_2 \vert V \vert \rceil$ decision-oracle calls.
- Each call returns YES/NO. The smallest $k$ for which the answer is YES is $OPT(G)$.
- **Self-reducibility:** Once $OPT(G)$ is known, remove any vertex $v$. Query the decision oracle for $G - v$ with threshold $OPT(G) - 1$. If YES, then $v$ is *not* needed in the optimal cover. If NO, then $v$ must be in every optimal cover. Repeat.

Thus, a polynomial-time decision algorithm yields a polynomial-time optimization algorithm. Since the decision version is NP-complete, the optimization version is **NP-hard**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing NP with NP-Hard.** NP is a class of *decision* problems verifiable in polynomial time. NP-hard is a class of *at least as hard as NP-complete* — they need not be in NP (e.g., the TSP *optimization* version is NP-hard but not in NP). Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the certificate in NP proofs.** A common error is to argue NP-membership without exhibiting a polynomial-length certificate or polynomial-time verifier. Always provide the *certificate and the verifier algorithm*.
> 3. **Dropping the polynomial bound in reductions.** Reductions must be computable in polynomial time; otherwise the entire chain of NP-hardness collapses.
> 4. **Mixing up approximation ratio conventions.** A $2$-approximation for a minimization problem means $A(I) \le 2 \cdot OPT(I)$, but for maximization it means $A(I) \ge OPT(I)/2$. Verify the convention before writing.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decision problems** answer YES/NO; **optimization problems** return a witness solution with optimal cost.
- A decision problem $\Pi$ is formalized as a language $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^{*}$; an optimization problem as $(\mathcal{I}, \mathcal{S}, f)$.
- **Class P** = polynomial-time solvable decision problems. **Class NP** = polynomial-time verifiable decision problems. Clearly $\mathbf{P \subseteq NP}$.
- A problem is **NP-hard** if every NP problem reduces to it via polynomial-time many-one reductions. **NP-complete** = NP $\cap$ NP-hard.
- The decision version of an optimization problem $\Pi$ is the *threshold language*: $\{I : \exists S \in \mathcal{S}(I) \text{ with } f(I,S) \le k\}$.
- If the optimization version is polynomial-time solvable, so is the decision version (direct call). Conversely, decision solvability + self-reducibility + bounded cost-range yields optimization solvability (via binary search).
- **Approximation ratio $\rho \ge 1$** is the standard quality measure for NP-hard optimization problems: $\mathcal{A}(I) \le \rho \cdot OPT(I)$ for minimization; $OPT(I) \le \rho \cdot \mathcal{A}(I)$ for maximization.
- **APX** = constant-factor approximable. **PTAS** = $(1+\epsilon)$-approximable in $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$. **FPTAS** = $(1+\epsilon)$-approximable in poly$(n, 1/\epsilon)$.
- Every **NP-complete** decision problem is reducible from SAT (Cook-Levin theorem), the canonical starting point of all hardness proofs.
- **Knapsack is the unique classic weakly NP-hard problem** admitting an FPTAS; most combinatorially-structured NP-hard problems (Vertex Cover, Max-Cut, Set Cover) only admit constant or logarithmic approximations.
- Keep the **P, NP, NP-complete, NP-hard** hierarchy crystal-clear in your head before stepping into any Module 2+ approximation-algorithm design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P, co-NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete Classes - Interrelationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** P, co-NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete Classes - Interrelationships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Four Foundational Complexity Classes

In computational complexity theory, problems are classified into hierarchical classes based on the resources (time, space) required by the best-known algorithms or theoretical machines to solve or verify them. For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on **Approximation Algorithms**, understanding these classes is **mandatory**, because the entire justification for *approximating* a problem relies on knowing that the original problem is unlikely to be solvable exactly in polynomial time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition Block**
> Let $\Sigma$ be a finite alphabet, and let $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ be a language. The complexity classes are defined using **deterministic Turing machines (DTM)** and **nondeterministic Turing machines (NTM)**:
>
> - **P (Polynomial Time):** The class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in $O(n^k)$ time for some constant $k \geq 1$, where $n$ is the input length.
> - **NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time):** The class of decision problems whose "yes" instances can be *verified* in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine, given an appropriate certificate/witness.
> - **co-NP:** The class of decision problems whose "no" instances can be *verified* in polynomial time. Equivalently, $\text{co-NP} = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in \text{NP} \}$.
> - **NP-Hard:** A problem $H$ is NP-Hard if every problem $L \in \text{NP}$ can be reduced to $H$ in polynomial time (i.e., $L \leq_p H$).
> - **NP-Complete (NPC):** A problem that satisfies both $L \in \text{NP}$ *and* $L$ is NP-Hard. Formally, $\text{NPC} = \text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$.

### 1.2 The Grading Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are a university professor grading answer scripts:

| Class | Real-World Analogy | Resource Modeled |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | You can grade a paper yourself in a reasonable time ($n^2$). | Deterministic, polynomial time |
| **NP** | You cannot grade it quickly, but if a student hands you an answer key, you *verify* it in polynomial time. | Verifiable with certificate |
| **co-NP** | Same as NP, but you can efficiently verify *wrong* answers. | Complement verifiable |
| **NP-Hard** | A problem so general that if you could solve it, you could solve *every* grading problem in NP. | Universal reduction target |
| **NP-Complete** | The hardest "grading" problems — both verifiable AND as hard as everything in NP. | Intersection of NP and NP-Hard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The Cook-Levin Theorem (1971) established that **SAT (Boolean Satisfiability)** is NP-Complete. Every subsequent NP-Complete proof is established by reducing SAT (or another known NPC) to the target problem via a polynomial-time many-one reduction.

### 1.3 Why This Matters for Approximation Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $P = NP$, then NP-Hard optimization problems (like Traveling Salesman, Vertex Cover, Knapsack) admit *exact* polynomial-time algorithms — making approximation unnecessary. The widespread belief that $P \neq NP$ is the **theoretical foundation** that justifies the entire field of Approximation Algorithms covered in this course.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Structural Logic

**Step 1: Decision Problems vs. Optimization Problems**
- A **decision problem** answers YES/NO (e.g., "Does there exist a vertex cover of size $\leq k$?").
- An **optimization problem** asks for the best solution (e.g., "Find the minimum vertex cover").
- Every optimization problem has a decision version; hardness of the decision version implies hardness of the optimization version.

**Step 2: Polynomial-Time Reductions ($\leq_p$)**
A problem $A$ is polynomial-time reducible to problem $B$, written $A \leq_p B$, if there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that:

$$x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$$

This means: *if we can solve $B$ in polynomial time, we can also solve $A$ in polynomial time*.

**Step 3: The Logic of NP Membership**
To prove $L \in \text{NP}$, one must exhibit a *certificate* (polynomial-length string $y$) and a polynomial-time verifier $V(x,y)$ such that $V$ outputs YES iff $x \in L$. This is the "guessing + checking" paradigm.

**Step 4: The Logic of NP-Hardness**
To prove $L$ is NP-Hard, one must show $A \leq_p L$ for **every** $A \in \text{NP}$. In practice, we pick a *known* NP-Complete problem (typically SAT, 3-SAT, or Hamiltonian Cycle) and reduce it to $L$ in polynomial time.

**Step 5: Establishing NP-Completeness**
A problem $L$ is NP-Complete if and only if:
1. $L \in \text{NP}$ (verifiable), **AND**
2. $L$ is NP-Hard (every NP problem reduces to $L$).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all key definitions, formulas, and inclusion properties tested in KTU university examinations.

| Concept | Formal Definition / Formula | KTU Board Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Class P | $\text{P} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$ | Deterministic poly-time |
| Class NP | $\text{NP} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{NTIME}(n^k)$ | Nondeterministic poly-time |
| Class co-NP | $\text{co-NP} = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in \text{NP} \}$ | Complement in NP |
| Polynomial Reduction | $A \leq_p B$ via $f$ where $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$ | Many-one reduction |
| NP-Hardness | $\forall A \in \text{NP}: A \leq_p H$ | Universal lower bound |
| NP-Completeness | $\text{NPC} = \text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$ | Self-referential hardest |
| Cook-Levin Statement | $\text{SAT} \in \text{NPC}$ | First NPC problem |
| Inclusion Chain (Known) | $\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP}$ | Always true |
| Open Problem 1 | $\text{P} \overset{?}{=} \text{NP}$ | Millennium Prize |
| Open Problem 2 | $\text{NP} \overset{?}{=} \text{co-NP}$ | Implies P = NP if false |
| Approximation Ratio | $\rho = \max\left(\frac{C(S)}{C^*(S)}, \frac{C^*(S)}{C(S)}\right)$ | Used in Module 2 onward |

### 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility

Understanding these classes drives critical engineering decisions:
- **Cryptography (RSA, AES):** Built on the assumption that Factoring and Discrete Logarithm are *not* in P (though they are in NP $\cap$ co-NP).
- **Compiler Design:** Register allocation, instruction scheduling — many subproblems are NP-Hard, so heuristics/approximations are deployed.
- **VLSI Chip Routing:** Steiner Tree and Minimum Cut problems are NP-Hard; practical tools rely on approximation algorithms with provable guarantees.
- **Operations Research:** Vehicle routing, warehouse scheduling, and bin packing are NP-Hard optimization problems; approximation is the only feasible industrial approach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-NP}$

**Statement to Prove:** Every language in P is also in both NP and co-NP.

**Logical Derivation:**

Let $L \in \text{P}$. By definition, there exists a deterministic Turing machine $M$ that decides $L$ in time $O(n^k)$.

**To show $L \in \text{NP}$:**
Construct a verifier $V(x, y)$ that ignores the certificate $y$ and simply runs $M(x)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } x \in L &: M(x) \text{ accepts in } O(n^k) \\
\text{If } x \notin L &: M(x) \text{ rejects in } O(n^k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $|y|$ can be empty (size 0, polynomial), and $V$ runs in polynomial time, $L$ is verified in polynomial time. Hence $L \in \text{NP}$.

**To show $L \in \text{co-NP}$:**
By definition, $\text{co-NP} = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in \text{NP} \}$. Since $M$ decides $L$, it also decides $\overline{L}$ by swapping accept/reject states. Therefore $\overline{L} \in \text{P} \subseteq \text{NP}$, which directly implies $L \in \text{co-NP}$.

$$
\boxed{\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP}}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is a *proven* theorem, not a conjecture. The remaining questions ($P \overset{?}{=} NP$, $NP \overset{?}{=} \text{co-NP}$) are the famous open problems.

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: The Cook-Levin Reduction Skeleton

**Theorem (Cook, 1971; Levin, 1973):** SAT is NP-Complete.

**Proof Outline (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Membership:** SAT $\in$ NP.
- Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ and an assignment $\sigma$ (the certificate), evaluate $\phi(\sigma)$ in time $O(n)$. Polynomial. $\checkmark$

**Step 2 — Hardness:** For every $L \in \text{NP}$ and every input $x \in \{0,1\}^n$, construct a Boolean formula $\phi_x$ of size polynomial in $n$ such that:
$$
x \in L \iff \phi_x \text{ is satisfiable}
$$

The construction simulates the NP-verifier's computation table using Boolean variables:
- $T[i][j][b]$: "Cell at row $i$, column $j$ contains symbol $b$."
- Clauses enforce: (a) valid initial configuration, (b) valid transitions per the transition function $\delta$, (c) accepting final state.

The resulting formula has size $O(n^k)$ (polynomial in input length), and the reduction runs in polynomial time.

$$
\boxed{\forall L \in \text{NP}: L \leq_p \text{SAT}}
$$

Combined with Step 1, this proves SAT $\in$ NPC. Every subsequent NP-Completeness proof leverages this as the foundation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision vs. Optimization Hardness

| Optimization Problem | Decision Version | Reduction Source | Complexity Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vertex Cover | "Is there VC of size $\leq k$?" | 3-SAT | NP-Complete |
| Traveling Salesman | "Is there tour of cost $\leq k$?" | Hamiltonian Cycle | NP-Complete |
| Knapsack | "Is there subset with value $\geq k$?" | Subset Sum | NP-Complete |
| Independent Set | "Is there IS of size $\geq k$?" | Clique | NP-Complete |
| Graph Coloring | "Is $k$-coloring possible?" | 3-SAT | NP-Complete |
| Set Cover | "Is there cover of size $\leq k$?" | Vertex Cover | NP-Complete |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Approximation:** The optimization versions of all problems above are NP-Hard. This is *exactly* why approximation algorithms (Modules 2–5 of PEITT751) are studied — exact polynomial-time solutions are presumed impossible unless $P = NP$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Complexity Class Inclusion Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical relationship among P, NP, co-NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete classes. All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph baseLayer ["FOUNDATION LAYER"]
        nodeP["P\nPolynomial Time"]
        nodeCoNP["co-NP\nComplement of NP"]
    end
    subgraph middleLayer ["MIDDLE LAYER"]
        nodeNP["NP\nNondeterministic Poly Time"]
        nodeNPC["NP COMPLETE\nNP intersect NP Hard"]
    end
    subgraph topLayer ["UPPER BOUND LAYER"]
        nodeNPHard["NP HARD\nAt least as hard as NP"]
    end

    nodeP -->|"subset"| nodeNP
    nodeP -->|"subset"| nodeCoNP
    nodeNP -->|"intersect with NP HARD gives"| nodeNPC
    nodeNP -->|"contained in"| nodeNPHard
    nodeCoNP -->|"contained in"| nodeNPHard
    nodeNPC -->|"subset of both NP and NP HARD"| nodeNPHard

    style nodeP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeNP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style nodeCoNP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style nodeNPC fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000
    style nodeNPHard fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016
```

### 4.2 The Polynomial-Time Reduction Architecture (Mermaid)

This block diagram explains the structural flow of how NP-Completeness proofs are constructed in practice — a critical pattern for KTU 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["Known NPC Problem A\ne.g. SAT or 3SAT"]
    transform["Polynomial Time\nReduction Function f"]
    targetB["Target Problem B\nCandidate for NPC"]
    verify1["Step 1: Show B in NP\nProvide polynomial verifier"]
    verify2["Step 2: Show A less than equal p B\nConstruct f in poly time"]
    conclude["CONCLUSION\nB is NP COMPLETE"]

    startA --> transform
    transform -->|"f maps instances of A\nto equivalent instances of B"| targetB
    targetB --> verify1
    targetB --> verify2
    verify1 --> conclude
    verify2 --> conclude

    style startA fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style transform fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style targetB fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style conclude fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

### 4.3 The "P versus NP" Decision Tree (Mermaid)

This schematic captures the logical implications of resolving (or not resolving) the central open problem in complexity theory.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootQ["IS P EQUAL TO NP?"]
    branchY["YES: P EQUALS NP"]
    branchN["NO: P NOT EQUAL NP"]

    implY1["All NP problems become\npolynomial time solvable"]
    implY2["NP Hard optimization problems\nadmit exact poly time algorithms"]
    implY3["Cryptography RSA ECC\nbreaks completely"]
    implY4["Approximation Algorithms\nbecome unnecessary"]

    implN1["Exact poly time algorithms\ndo NOT exist for NPC"]
    implN2["Approximation Algorithms\nare NECESSARY for NP Hard"]
    implN3["PEITT751 course content\nremains practically relevant"]
    implN4["P proper subset of NP\nis the working assumption"]

    rootQ --> branchY
    rootQ --> branchN
    branchY --> implY1 --> implY2 --> implY3 --> implY4
    branchN --> implN1 --> implN2 --> implN3 --> implN4

    style rootQ fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style branchY fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style branchN fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style implY4 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style implN2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NP-Completeness Proof Pipeline

```
+-----------------------------------------------------------------+
|              NP-COMPLETENESS PROOF PIPELINE                     |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                                 |
|  [INPUT]    Candidate Problem B                                 |
|     |                                                           |
|     v                                                           |
|  [STAGE 1]  B is in NP?                                         |
|             -- Provide polynomial certificate + verifier        |
|             -- Verify verifier runs in O(n^k)                   |
|     |                                                           |
|     +---> NO -----> [REJECT: B is not in NP, cannot be NPC]     |
|     |                                                           |
|     v (YES)                                                     |
|  [STAGE 2]  Known NPC source A selected (SAT, 3SAT, HAM-CYCLE)  |
|     |                                                           |
|     v                                                           |
|  [STAGE 3]  Construct polynomial reduction f: A -> B            |
|             -- f(x) in A  <=>  x in B                           |
|             -- Time complexity of f: O(n^c) for some constant c |
|     |                                                           |
|     v                                                           |
|  [STAGE 4]  Correctness verification:                           |
|             (a) Soundness:  x in A  =>  f(x) in B               |
|             (b) Completeness: f(x) in B  =>  x in A             |
|     |                                                           |
|     v                                                           |
|  [OUTPUT]   B is NP-COMPLETE                                    |
|             B in NP AND every NP problem reduces to B           |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------+
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define the class NP. How does it differ from class P? Give one example of a problem in NP that is not known to be in P.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** NP is the class of decision problems whose "yes" instances can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine, given a certificate (witness). Formally, $L \in \text{NP}$ if there exists a polynomial-time verifier $V(x, y)$ and polynomial-length certificate $y$ such that $x \in L \iff \exists y: V(x,y) = \text{YES}$.

> **Difference from P (1 Mark):** P requires *solving* in polynomial time deterministically. NP requires only *verifying* a proposed solution in polynomial time. Hence $\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP}$, but whether $\text{P} = \text{NP}$ is open.

> **Example (1 Mark):** **Hamiltonian Cycle** — given a graph and a proposed cycle, verification is polynomial ($O(V+E)$), but no polynomial-time algorithm is known. Alternatively, **SAT** or **3-SAT**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is a polynomial-time reduction? Why is it central to the theory of NP-Completeness?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A polynomial-time (many-one) reduction from problem $A$ to problem $B$, written $A \leq_p B$, is a polynomial-time computable function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$.

> **Importance to NP-Completeness (1.5 Marks):** Polynomial reductions establish relative hardness. If $A \leq_p B$ and $B$ is solvable in polynomial time, then so is $A$. This lets us propagate hardness: once SAT is proven NP-Complete (Cook-Levin), we reduce SAT to other problems to *propagate* NP-Completeness without re-proving hardness from scratch. Every NP-Completeness proof in the KTU syllabus relies on this transitive property.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Define the classes P, NP, co-NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete. State the inclusion relationships among them clearly. Explain why $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-NP}$ is always true. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** State and explain the Cook-Levin Theorem. Outline the proof that SAT is NP-Complete, distinguishing between membership in NP and NP-Hardness. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition Block [7 Marks]:**

| Class | Definition | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| P | Problems solvable by a deterministic TM in $O(n^k)$ time. | 1 |
| NP | Problems whose YES instances are verifiable in poly-time by a DTM given a certificate. | 1 |
| co-NP | $\text{co-NP} = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in \text{NP} \}$. Problems whose NO instances are poly-time verifiable. | 1 |
| NP-Hard | Problem $H$ such that $\forall L \in \text{NP}: L \leq_p H$. At least as hard as every NP problem. | 1 |
| NP-Complete | $L \in \text{NP}$ AND $L$ is NP-Hard. Hence $\text{NPC} = \text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$. | 1 |

**Inclusion Relationships [1 Mark]:**
$$
\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP} \cup \text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP-Hard}
$$
And $\text{NPC} = \text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$.

**Why $P \subseteq NP \cap \text{co-NP}$ [1 Mark]:**
If $L \in \text{P}$, a deterministic TM $M$ decides $L$ in time $O(n^k)$. Construct a verifier that ignores the certificate and runs $M(x)$, which is polynomial. Hence $L \in \text{NP}$. Also, $\overline{L}$ is decided by the same $M$ with accept/reject swapped, so $\overline{L} \in \text{P} \subseteq \text{NP}$, meaning $L \in \text{co-NP}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) Cook-Levin Theorem [7 Marks]:**

> **Statement (1 Mark):** The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete.

**Step 1: SAT $\in$ NP [2 Marks]:**
Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ with $n$ variables and certificate $y$ (an assignment of truth values), evaluate $\phi(y)$ in $O(n)$ time. If $\phi(y) = \text{TRUE}$, accept; else reject. The certificate has polynomial length, and verification is polynomial.

**Step 2: SAT is NP-Hard [4 Marks]:**
Let $L \in \text{NP}$. There exists a nondeterministic TM $N$ deciding $L$ in $O(n^k)$ time. For any input $x$, construct a Boolean formula $\phi_x$ that simulates $N$ on $x$:

- **Variables:** $T[i][j][b]$ for each tape cell $(i,j)$ and symbol $b \in \Gamma$, plus state variables.
- **Clauses enforce:**
  1. Initial configuration: $T[0,0,x_1] \land \dots \land T[0,n-1,x_n] \land T[0,n,\sqcup]$ and $N$ in start state.
  2. Valid transitions: Each cell at time $t+1$ is consistent with the transition function $\delta$ applied at time $t$.
  3. Accepting condition: There exists a timestep $T \leq O(n^k)$ where $N$ is in accepting state.

The formula size is $O(n^k)$ — polynomial — and the reduction runs in polynomial time. Crucially:
$$
x \in L \iff \phi_x \text{ is satisfiable}
$$

This establishes $\forall L \in \text{NP}: L \leq_p \text{SAT}$. Hence SAT is NP-Hard. $\blacksquare$

Combining Steps 1 and 2: **SAT $\in$ NPC**. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Define polynomial-time many-one reduction. Prove that if $A \leq_p B$ and $B \in \text{P}$, then $A \in \text{P}$. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the relationship between NP and co-NP. If $NP \neq \text{co-NP}$, what can be concluded? Provide two examples — one problem known to be in NP $\cap$ co-NP, and one problem conjectured (but not proven) to be NP-Complete but not in co-NP. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Polynomial-Time Reduction + Closure Proof [7 Marks]:**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** A polynomial-time many-one reduction from language $A$ to language $B$ is a function $f: \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that:
> 1. $f$ is computable by a deterministic TM in polynomial time $O(n^c)$.
> 2. For every $x \in \Sigma^*$: $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$.

> **Theorem Statement (1 Mark):** If $A \leq_p B$ and $B \in \text{P}$, then $A \in \text{P}$.

> **Proof (4 Marks):**
> Let $M_B$ be the polynomial-time DTM deciding $B$, running in time $O(n^b)$. Let $T_f$ be the polynomial-time transducer computing $f$, running in time $O(n^c)$. Construct a DTM $M_A$ that decides $A$:
>
> ```
> M_A on input x of length n:
>   1. Compute y = f(x)              [time: O(n^c)]
>   2. Run M_B on y                   [time: O(|y|^b)]
>   3. Output M_B's answer
> ```
>
> **Time analysis:** Since $f$ runs in $O(n^c)$, the output $y = f(x)$ satisfies $|y| \leq a \cdot n^c$ for some constant $a$. Therefore $M_B(y)$ runs in $O(|y|^b) = O(n^{bc})$ time.
>
> $$
> T_{M_A}(n) = O(n^c) + O(n^{bc}) = O(n^{bc})
> $$
>
> which is polynomial in $n$. Therefore $M_A$ is a polynomial-time decider for $A$, so $A \in \text{P}$. $\blacksquare$

**(b) NP vs co-NP Relationship [7 Marks]:**

> **Definitions (2 Marks):**
> - $\text{NP} = \{ L \mid \text{YES instances of } L \text{ are poly-time verifiable} \}$
> - $\text{co-NP} = \{ L \mid \text{NO instances of } L \text{ are poly-time verifiable} \} = \{ L \mid \overline{L} \in \text{NP} \}$
>
> A language is in $\text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP}$ if both its YES and NO instances are efficiently verifiable.

> **Implication if NP $\neq$ co-NP (2 Marks):**
> If $\text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP}$, then there exists at least one language in NP that is not in co-NP. A deep consequence: by the **Theorem of Fortune (1979)**, if any NP-Complete problem lies in co-NP, then $\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$. Therefore:
>
> $$\text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP} \implies \text{NPC} \cap \text{co-NP} = \emptyset$$
>
> Equivalently, *no* NP-Complete problem can have its complement also in NP.

> **Examples (3 Marks):**
>
> | Example | Class | Justification |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Integer Factorization** | Believed to be in NP $\cap$ co-NP (not proven) | YES: exhibit factors. NO: harder to certify non-factorability, but assumed via randomized algorithms (Miller-Rabin). |
> | **Primality Testing** | In NP $\cap$ co-NP, and (AKS 2002) now in P | YES: factor certificate. NO: Pratt certificate. Now polynomial via AKS. |
> | **SAT (Boolean Satisfiability)** | NP-Complete, conjectured $\notin$ co-NP | YES verifiable via assignment. NO instances cannot be efficiently certified (otherwise NP = co-NP). |

> **Open Status:** While $\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP}$, it is unknown whether $\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$. Most complexity theorists believe $\text{NP} \neq \text{co-NP}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Complexity Class Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "verifiable" with "solvable":** Students often write "$L \in \text{NP}$ means $L$ can be solved in polynomial time." This is WRONG. NP means *verifiable*, not solvable. Losing **1–2 marks** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the two-condition NP-Completeness check:** When asked "Is $X$ NP-Complete?", students often show only $X \in \text{NP}$ OR only NP-Hardness. **Both must be proved.** Losing up to **3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Polynomial reduction requires BOTH directions of equivalence:** A reduction $A \leq_p B$ demands $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$ — both forward ($x \in A \Rightarrow f(x) \in B$) and backward ($f(x) \in B \Rightarrow x \in A$). Forgetting one direction costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Misplacing NP-Complete vs NP-Hard in Venn diagrams:** NP-Complete is the **intersection** of NP and NP-Hard, not a subset of NP-Hard alone. Examiner deduction: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Stating $P = NP$ as proven:** $P = NP$ is *unproven*. Students writing "Since $P = NP$..." lose **2 marks** for asserting an open conjecture as fact.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1**
>
> - **P** = Polynomial-time *decidable* problems (deterministic).
> - **NP** = Polynomial-time *verifiable* problems (certificate-based).
> - **co-NP** = Polynomial-time *verifiable NO-instances*; equivalently, complements of NP languages.
> - **NP-Hard** = Problems to which *every* NP problem polynomial-time reduces.
> - **NP-Complete** = $\text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$. Two-step proof: (1) membership in NP, (2) NP-Hardness via reduction.
> - **Cook-Levin Theorem:** SAT is the canonical NP-Complete problem (1971).
> - **Inclusion Chain (PROVEN):** $\text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{co-NP} \subseteq \text{NP-Hard}$.
> - **Inclusion Chain (CONJECTURED but UNPROVEN):** $\text{P} \subsetneq \text{NP} \subsetneq \text{NP-Hard}$.
> - **Open Problems:** $P \overset{?}{=} NP$, $NP \overset{?}{=} \text{co-NP}$. Both are Millennium Prize problems.
> - **Reductions:** $A \leq_p B$ means "$A$ is no harder than $B$." If $B \in \text{P}$, then $A \in \text{P}$.
> - **Fortune's Theorem:** If any NP-Complete problem is in co-NP, then $\text{NP} = \text{co-NP}$.
> - **Course Connection:** Approximation algorithms exist because NP-Hard optimization problems are believed to lack exact polynomial-time solutions.
> - **Key Examples:** SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle, Traveling Salesman, Knapsack — all NP-Complete (decision versions) / NP-Hard (optimization versions).
> - **Verifier Format:** When proving $L \in \text{NP}$, always specify: (i) the certificate, (ii) the verifier algorithm, (iii) the polynomial time bound.
> - **Reduction Soundness + Completeness:** Always verify both directions $x \in A \iff f(x) \in B$ when constructing a polynomial reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intractability of NP-Complete Problems - Challenges and Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intractability of NP-Complete Problems - Challenges and Importance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of NP-Completeness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEITT751 — Module 1):**
> A decision problem $\Pi$ is said to be **NP-Complete** if and only if it satisfies **both** of the following conditions:
> 1. $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ (i.e., any proposed "yes"-instance can be *verified* in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine).
> 2. Every problem in $\mathbf{NP}$ is *polynomial-time reducible* to $\Pi$ (i.e., $\Pi$ is **NP-Hard**).

Formally, a problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Complete** if:

$$\Pi \in \mathbf{NP} \quad \land \quad \forall\, \Pi' \in \mathbf{NP}, \;\; \Pi' \;\leq_{p}\; \Pi$$

where $\leq_{p}$ denotes **Karp (many-one) polynomial-time reduction**.

A problem that satisfies condition (2) but **not necessarily** condition (1) is called **NP-Hard**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Locked Treasure Room"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing in front of a giant vault containing thousands of treasures. The vault's lock has **no key** — the only way to open it is to try every possible combination, one by one. For 10 dials, you try $10^{10}$ combinations. For 20 dials, it becomes $10^{20}$. For 50 dials, even the world's fastest computer would take longer than the age of the universe.

| Vault Concept | Computational Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Trying every combination | Brute-force search over $2^n$ candidates |
| Verifying a proposed combination | Polynomial-time certificate verification |
| Vaults with **no shortcut** | **NP-Complete** problems |
| Vaults with a known shortcut | Problems in **P** |
| Vaults harder than all known ones | **NP-Hard** problems |

**Intuition:** *Finding* a solution to an NP-Complete problem is believed to require *exponential* effort in the worst case, but *checking* whether a given candidate is a solution is "easy" (polynomial).

---

## 1.3 The Central Question — P vs NP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Millennium Prize Problem (Clay Mathematics Institute, 2000):**
> Does $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$?
> Equivalently: *If a solution to a problem can be verified quickly (in polynomial time), can it also be found quickly?*

Current consensus: **$\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$** (unproven, but overwhelmingly believed). This single assumption is the **foundation** upon which the entire field of **Approximation Algorithms** is built.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for PEITT751:**
> If $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$, every NP-Complete problem could be solved *exactly* in polynomial time — making approximation algorithms **unnecessary**. Since we (strongly) believe $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$, approximation algorithms become our **best practical strategy** for tackling otherwise intractable problems.

---

## 1.4 Canonical Examples of NP-Complete Problems

| Problem | Domain | Decision Version |
|---|---|---|
| **SAT** (Boolean Satisfiability) | Logic | Is there a truth assignment satisfying a Boolean formula? |
| **3-SAT** | Logic (3-CNF) | Same, restricted to 3 literals per clause |
| **Vertex Cover** | Graph Theory | Is there a set of $\leq k$ vertices covering all edges? |
| **Clique** | Graph Theory | Is there a clique of size $\geq k$? |
| **Hamiltonian Cycle** | Graph Theory | Does the graph contain a Hamiltonian cycle? |
| **Travelling Salesman (TSP)** | Combinatorics | Is there a tour of length $\leq k$? |
| **Subset Sum** | Number Theory | Is there a subset summing to $k$? |
| **Graph Coloring** | Graph Theory | Can the graph be colored with $\leq k$ colors? |
| **Knapsack** | Optimization | Is there a subset of items with value $\geq V$ and weight $\leq W$? |
| **Independent Set** | Graph Theory | Is there an independent set of size $\geq k$? |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complexity Class Hierarchy

The four key complexity classes relevant to PEITT751 are organized as follows:

$$\mathbf{P} \;\subseteq\; \mathbf{NP} \;\supseteq\; \mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete} \;\subseteq\; \mathbf{NP\text{-}Hard}$$

where the **overlap region** $\mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete} = \mathbf{NP} \cap \mathbf{NP\text{-}Hard}$ contains the "hardest problems inside NP."

### 2.1.1 Class P (Polynomial Time)

A problem belongs to **P** if there exists a deterministic algorithm $A$ and a polynomial $p(n)$ such that for every instance $x$ of size $n = \vert x \vert$, $A(x)$ terminates within $p(n)$ steps.

$$\mathbf{P} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \mathbf{DTIME}\!\left(n^k\right)$$

These are the **tractable** problems: sorting, shortest paths, MST, matrix multiplication, linear programming.

### 2.1.2 Class NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time)

A decision problem belongs to **NP** if, for every "yes"-instance $x$, there exists a *certificate* (or *witness*) $y$ of length polynomial in $\vert x \vert$ such that a deterministic verifier $V(x,y)$ answers "yes" in polynomial time.

$$\mathbf{NP} = \left\{\, L \;:\; \exists \text{ poly-time verifier } V,\; x \in L \iff \exists y,\, \vert y \vert \leq p(\vert x \vert),\; V(x,y) = 1 \,\right\}$$

### 2.1.3 Class NP-Hard

A problem $H$ is **NP-Hard** if every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to $H$. NP-Hard problems need **not** themselves lie in NP — they may be strictly harder (e.g., the Halting Problem is NP-Hard but not in NP).

### 2.1.4 Class NP-Complete

$$\mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete} \;=\; \mathbf{NP} \;\cap\; \mathbf{NP\text{-}Hard}$$

---

## 2.2 Polynomial-Time Reduction — The Reduction Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polynomial-Time Karp Reduction ($\leq_p$):**
> Given two decision problems $\Pi_1$ and $\Pi_2$, we say $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_2$ if there exists a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that for every instance $x$ of $\Pi_1$:
> $$x \in \Pi_1 \iff f(x) \in \Pi_2$$
> The reduction $f$ must be computable in time $p(\vert x \vert)$ for some polynomial $p$.

**Significance of reductions:**
- If $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_2$ and $\Pi_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $\Pi_1 \in \mathbf{P}$.
- If $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_2$ and $\Pi_1 \notin \mathbf{P}$, then $\Pi_2 \notin \mathbf{P}$ (assuming $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$).
- If $\Pi_2 \in \mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete}$ and $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_2$ with $\Pi_1 \in \mathbf{NP}$, then $\Pi_1 \in \mathbf{NP\text{-}Complete}$.

---

## 2.3 Cook-Levin Theorem — The Foundation Stone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):**
> **SAT** (Boolean Satisfiability) is **NP-Complete**.

This is the *first* problem ever proven NP-Complete. Every subsequent NP-Completeness proof reduces SAT (or another already-known NP-Complete problem) to the candidate problem.

The proof works by encoding the *computation* of a nondeterministic Turing machine as a Boolean formula in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) such that the formula is satisfiable **iff** the Turing machine accepts.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $p(n)$ | A polynomial in input size $n$ | Poly-time bound |
| $\mathbf{DTIME}(f(n))$ | Decision problems solvable in $O(f(n))$ deterministic time | Used in class definitions |
| $\mathbf{NTIME}(f(n))$ | Nondeterministic time | $\mathbf{NP} = \bigcup_k \mathbf{NTIME}(n^k)$ |
| $\leq_p$ | Karp polynomial-time reduction | Many-one reduction |
| $\leq_T^p$ | Turing polynomial-time reduction | More general |
| $n^k$ | Polynomial growth rate | Tractable |
| $2^n$, $n!$, $n^n$ | Super-polynomial / exponential | **Intractable** |
| $\rho(n) = \frac{C^*(I)}{C(I)}$ | Approximation ratio | $C^*$ = optimal cost, $C$ = algorithm's cost |
| $\text{Vertex Cover}(G,k)$ | Is there a VC of size $\leq k$ in $G$? | NP-Complete decision version |
| $\text{SAT}(\varphi)$ | Is $\varphi$ satisfiable? | **First** NP-Complete problem |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | NP-Complete Problem Encountered | Why Approximation Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **VLSI Design** | Minimum-area circuit layout | Direct placement affects chip cost |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Vehicle Routing, TSP | Saves fuel, time, manpower |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein folding, Sequence alignment | Drug discovery pipelines |
| **Network Design** | Minimum latency routing, Steiner Trees | Telecom backbone planning |
| **Compiler Optimization** | Register allocation, Instruction scheduling | Direct runtime speedup |
| **AI / ML** | Bayesian network structure learning, Neural architecture search | Model performance |
| **Cryptography** | Subset-sum-based schemes | Breaking crypto is NP-Hard — intentional |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Whenever a real-world optimization problem reduces to an NP-Complete decision problem, *exact* polynomial-time algorithms are (believed to be) impossible. Therefore, **approximation algorithms with provable performance guarantees** are the only rigorous way forward — which is precisely the scope of PEITT751.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Verification vs. Search Cost

| Property | Problem in P (e.g., MST) | Problem in NP-Complete (e.g., TSP-Decision) |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision form** | "Is MST weight $\leq W$?" | "Is there a tour of length $\leq K$?" |
| **Search complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ via Kruskal/Prim | Best known exact: $O(2^n \cdot n^2)$ (DP) |
| **Verification complexity** | Trivial (output is the MST) | $O(n)$ — just add edge lengths of proposed tour |
| **Certificate length** | $O(n \log W)$ | $O(n)$ — the sequence of cities |
| **Status** | **Tractable** | **Intractable** (under $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$) |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Proving a New Problem is NP-Complete

To prove that a decision problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Complete**, we must prove **two lemmas**:

### Lemma 1: $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$

**Step 1 — Identify the certificate.**
For a "yes"-instance $x$ of $\Pi$, define the certificate $y$. For example, for Vertex Cover, the certificate is a *set of vertices* $S$.

**Step 2 — Design a deterministic verifier.**
Write a verifier algorithm $V(x,y)$ that runs in time polynomial in $\vert x \vert$.

**Step 3 — Correctness of verification.**
Show that $V(x,y)$ accepts $\iff$ $y$ is a valid witness for $x$.

### Worked Sketch — Vertex Cover is in NP

> **Goal:** Show Vertex Cover is in NP.

A certificate for a "yes" instance $(G, k)$ is a subset $S \subseteq V(G)$ with $\vert S \vert \leq k$. The verifier $V$:

1. Checks $\vert S \vert \leq k$ — this takes $O(n)$ time, polynomial.
2. Iterates over every edge $(u,v) \in E(G)$ and verifies $\{u,v\} \cap S \neq \emptyset$ — this takes $O(\vert E \vert) = O(n^2)$ time, polynomial.

Total verification time:

$$T(n) = O(n) + O(n^2) = O(n^2) = \text{poly}(n)$$

Hence Vertex Cover $\in \mathbf{NP}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Lemma 2: $\Pi$ is NP-Hard (i.e., a known NP-Complete problem $\Pi'$ reduces to $\Pi$)

**Step 1 — Pick a known NP-Complete problem $\Pi'$.**
Common choices: 3-SAT, Independent Set, Clique, Vertex Cover, Subset Sum.

**Step 2 — Construct a polynomial-time transformation $f$.**
Given any instance $x$ of $\Pi'$, produce an instance $f(x)$ of $\Pi$ in time $p(\vert x \vert)$.

**Step 3 — Prove the equivalence.**
Show that $x$ is a "yes" instance of $\Pi'$ $\iff$ $f(x)$ is a "yes" instance of $\Pi$.

### Worked Sketch — Reduction: Independent Set $\leq_p$ Vertex Cover

> **Claim:** Independent Set $\leq_p$ Vertex Cover.
> **Proof Idea:** Complement the edges of the graph.

**Construction:** Given $(G, k)$, an instance of Independent Set, construct $f(G, k) = (G, n-k)$ where $n = \vert V(G) \vert$. (The graph itself is unchanged.)

**Equivalence proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(G,k) \in \text{Independent Set} &\iff \exists\, S \subseteq V(G),\; \vert S \vert \geq k,\; \text{no two vertices in } S \text{ adjacent} \\
&\iff \exists\, T = V(G) \setminus S,\; \vert T \vert \leq n-k,\; T \text{ touches every edge of } G \\
&\iff (G, n-k) \in \text{Vertex Cover}
\end{aligned}$$

**Time complexity of $f$:** $O(1)$ — trivial construction, well within polynomial time.

Hence Independent Set $\leq_p$ Vertex Cover. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Transitivity of Reductions:**
> If $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_2$ and $\Pi_2 \leq_p \Pi_3$, then $\Pi_1 \leq_p \Pi_3$. This is the engine that propagates NP-Completeness through thousands of problems after Cook-Levin's foundational proof.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Hardness Implication for Approximation

Assume $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$. We show that finding an exact polynomial-time algorithm for any NP-Complete optimization problem is *impossible*.

**Step 1:** Suppose, for contradiction, that some NP-Complete optimization problem $\Pi^*$ admits a polynomial-time *exact* solver $A$.

**Step 2:** Use $A$ to solve the decision version of $\Pi^*$ in polynomial time: binary-search over the objective value (which is at most exponential in $n$, taking $O(n)$ bits to represent).

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision}(x, K) &= \text{is } \mathrm{OPT}(x) \leq K? \\
\text{Solve via binary search:} &\quad \text{low} = 0,\; \text{high} = 2^n,\; \text{iterations} = O(n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** This yields a polynomial-time algorithm for an NP-Complete decision problem.

**Step 4:** By polynomial-time reductions, *every* problem in NP becomes polynomial-time solvable.

**Step 5:** Hence $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$, contradicting our assumption.

$$\therefore \text{No polynomial-time exact solver exists for any NP-Complete optimization problem (unless } \mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}\text{).} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **central motivation** for designing approximation algorithms — they trade a *guaranteed multiplicative factor* on the optimum for a polynomial running time.

---

## 3.4 Approximation Ratio — The Quality Guarantee

For a minimization problem $\Pi$:

$$\rho(n) = \max_{I : \vert I \vert = n} \frac{A(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \;\geq\; 1$$

For a maximization problem $\Pi$:

$$\rho(n) = \min_{I : \vert I \vert = n} \frac{A(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \;\leq\; 1$$

A problem admits a **polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS)** if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation running in time $n^{O(1/\varepsilon)}$.

An **FPTAS** runs in time $(1/\varepsilon)^{O(1)} \cdot \text{poly}(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> There is a deep result: if $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$, several problems (e.g., general TSP) **do not even admit any constant-factor polynomial-time approximation** (unless stronger assumptions hold). This makes the *classification* of approximability a central theme of PEITT751.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

        |
        threshold beyond which exact solver becomes infeasible

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Define NP-Completeness. State the two necessary conditions for a decision problem to be classified as NP-Complete.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A decision problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Complete** if and only if:

1. **$\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$:** Every "yes" instance of $\Pi$ admits a *certificate* that can be verified by a deterministic algorithm in time polynomial in the input size. *(1 mark)*

2. **$\Pi$ is NP-Hard:** Every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to $\Pi$ (via Karp reduction $\leq_p$). Equivalently, a known NP-Complete problem $\Pi'$ reduces to $\Pi$ in polynomial time. *(1 mark)*

3. Both conditions together imply $\Pi$ is at least as hard as *every* problem in NP, yet no harder than NP itself. *(1 mark — synthesis)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between NP-Hard and NP-Complete problems with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NP-Hard | NP-Complete |
|---|---|---|
| Membership in NP | Not required | Must be in NP |
| Reducibility | Every NP problem $\leq_p$ it | Same, **and** it is in NP |
| Example | **Halting Problem**, **QBF** (Quantified Boolean Formula) | **SAT**, **Vertex Cover**, **TSP-Decision** *(1 mark each)* |

The Halting Problem is NP-Hard but undecidable (hence **not** in NP), so it is *not* NP-Complete. SAT, by contrast, is in NP and is NP-Hard (Cook-Levin), making it NP-Complete. *(1 mark — distinguishing statement)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]`

**(a)** State and explain the Cook-Levin theorem. Describe, with a high-level outline, how the proof establishes that SAT is NP-Complete. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using a reduction from 3-SAT, prove that the **Vertex Cover** decision problem is NP-Complete. State clearly the reduction function $f$, its polynomial-time constructibility, and the equivalence proof. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Solution (a):**

**Cook-Levin Theorem Statement (1 mark):**
> Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) is NP-Complete.

**Proof Outline (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — SAT $\in$ NP (2 marks):**
Given a Boolean formula $\varphi$ in CNF and a candidate truth assignment $\sigma$, the verifier evaluates each clause under $\sigma$. If all clauses evaluate to TRUE, accept; otherwise reject. Each clause has size $O(n)$, evaluation is $O(n)$ per clause, total verification time $O(n \cdot m)$ where $m$ is the number of clauses — polynomial.

**Step 2 — SAT is NP-Hard (4 marks):**
Let $M$ be any nondeterministic Turing machine and let $x$ be an input of length $n$. We construct a Boolean formula $\varphi_{M,x}$ such that:

$$\begin{aligned}
M \text{ accepts } x &\iff \varphi_{M,x} \text{ is satisfiable} \\
\text{size}(\varphi_{M,x}) &\leq p(n) \text{ for some polynomial } p
\end{aligned}$$

The construction uses the following variables:
- $Q_{i,t}$ — the machine is in state $q_i$ at time $t$.
- $H_{j,t}$ — head is at cell $j$ at time $t$.
- $S_{j,k,t}$ — cell $j$ contains symbol $s_k$ at time $t$.

Clauses enforce:
1. **Initial configuration** matches $(q_0, x)$. *(1 mark)*
2. **Transition relation** — at each step, the local configuration obeys $M$'s transition function (encoded as a Boolean formula per cell). *(1 mark)*
3. **Final acceptance** — there exists a time $T \leq p(n)$ where $M$ enters state $q_{\text{accept}}$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Consistency** — each cell holds exactly one symbol at each time. *(1 mark)*

Therefore, an accepting computation of $M$ on $x$ exists **iff** $\varphi_{M,x}$ is satisfiable. Since $M$ and $x$ were arbitrary, every NP problem reduces to SAT in polynomial time. Hence SAT is NP-Hard.

Combined with Step 1, SAT is NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution (b):**

**Vertex Cover Decision Problem:**
$$\text{VC} = \{\, (G, k) : G \text{ has a vertex cover of size } \leq k \,\}$$

**Step 1 — VC $\in$ NP (2 marks):**
Certificate: a vertex subset $S$ with $\vert S \vert \leq k$. Verifier checks: (i) $\vert S \vert \leq k$ in $O(n)$ time, (ii) for each edge $(u,v) \in E$, $\{u,v\} \cap S \neq \emptyset$ in $O(m)$ time. Total: $O(n + m) = O(n^2)$, polynomial.

**Step 2 — Reduction from 3-SAT (5 marks):**

Given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi = C_1 \wedge C_2 \wedge \dots \wedge C_m$ with variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, we construct $(G_\varphi, k)$ where:

- For each variable $x_i$, create two vertices $x_i$ and $\overline{x_i}$ joined by an edge (a *variable gadget*).
- For each clause $C_j = (\ell_{j,1} \vee \ell_{j,2} \vee \ell_{j,3})$, create a triangle connecting the three literal vertices corresponding to $\ell_{j,1}, \ell_{j,2}, \ell_{j,3}$ (a *clause gadget*).
- Set $k = n + 2m$.

**Construction time:** $O(n + m)$, polynomial. *(1 mark)*

**Equivalence Proof (3 marks):**

**Forward ($\Rightarrow$):** Suppose $\varphi$ is satisfiable. Pick a satisfying truth assignment. From each variable gadget, select the true literal vertex — this contributes $n$ vertices covering all variable edges. From each clause gadget (triangle), since at least one literal is true, pick the two *false* literal vertices to cover the triangle's edges — this contributes $2m$ vertices. Total cover size: $n + 2m = k$. $(1.5 \text{ marks})$

**Backward ($\Leftarrow$):** Suppose $(G_\varphi, k)$ has a vertex cover of size $\leq n + 2m$. Since variable gadgets are disjoint edges, any VC must include at least one endpoint from each, contributing $\geq n$ vertices. Each triangle (clause gadget) has 3 edges; covering them requires $\geq 2$ vertices. Hence the VC uses exactly one endpoint per variable gadget and exactly two per triangle. The uncovered endpoint in each variable gadget can be set TRUE; in each triangle, the uncovered vertex corresponds to a TRUE literal — making every clause satisfiable. $(1.5 \text{ marks})$

Therefore Vertex Cover is NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the P vs NP problem. Discuss its implications on the design of approximation algorithms for NP-Complete optimization problems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define **polynomial-time approximation scheme (PTAS)** and **fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS)**. With an example, illustrate how a $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation for the **Knapsack problem** can be obtained. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Solution (a):**

**The P vs NP Question (3 marks):**
The question asks whether every problem whose solution can be *verified* in polynomial time can also be *solved* in polynomial time:
$$\mathbf{P} \stackrel{?}{=} \mathbf{NP}$$
It remains open; Clay Mathematics Institute has designated it a **Millennium Prize Problem** with a **\$1,000,000** reward.

**Implications if $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$ (2 marks):**
Every NP-Complete optimization problem admits a polynomial-time *exact* algorithm. Approximation algorithms become unnecessary — they are dominated by exact solvers.

**Implications if $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$ (2 marks — the consensus belief):**
No exact polynomial-time algorithm exists for NP-Complete problems. The **only rigorous recourse** is:
1. Heuristics with no guarantees (practical but unsound).
2. **Approximation algorithms** that run in polynomial time and provably return solutions within a factor $\rho$ of the optimum.

Approximation algorithms thus become **indispensable engineering tools** in VLSI, networking, bioinformatics, and operations research.

---

**Solution (b):**

**PTAS Definition (2 marks):**
A polynomial-time approximation scheme is an algorithm family $\{A_\varepsilon\}$ such that for every $\varepsilon > 0$:
- $A_\varepsilon$ returns a solution with ratio $\leq (1+\varepsilon)$ (minimization).
- $A_\varepsilon$ runs in time $n^{O(1/\varepsilon)}$ — polynomial in $n$ for fixed $\varepsilon$, but exponential in $1/\varepsilon$.

**FPTAS Definition (1 mark):**
An FPTAS runs in time $(1/\varepsilon)^{O(1)} \cdot \text{poly}(n)$ — polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$.

**Knapsack FPTAS Sketch (4 marks):**

The classic exact DP for 0/1 Knapsack runs in $O(nV)$ where $V$ is the total value. Let $V_{\max} = \sum_i v_i$.

**Scaling step:** For each item value $v_i$, define:

$$v_i' = \left\lfloor \frac{v_i}{\mu} \right\rfloor, \quad \text{where } \mu = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot V_{\max}}{n}$$

Then $v_i' \leq n/\varepsilon$ for all $i$, so the scaled DP runs in $O(n \cdot n/\varepsilon) = O(n^2/\varepsilon)$, polynomial in both $n$ and $1/\varepsilon$.

**Approximation guarantee:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{OPT}_{\text{scaled}} &\geq \text{OPT}_{\text{original}} - n\mu \\
&= \text{OPT}_{\text{original}} - \varepsilon \cdot V_{\max} \\
