&\geq (1-\varepsilon) \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{original}}
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, the scaled-DP optimum is within $(1+\varepsilon)$ factor of the true optimum after re-scaling. Hence Knapsack admits an **FPTAS**. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> Knapsack is one of the rare natural NP-Complete problems that admits an FPTAS — a strong positive result. By contrast, general TSP on metric graphs only admits a 1.5-approximation (Christofides), and no better than $(1+\varepsilon)$ is achievable for general TSP (assuming $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in PEITT751 Module 1 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "$\in$ NP" half** — When asked to prove NP-Completeness, many students only prove NP-Hardness (i.e., only the reduction). Examiners explicitly allocate **2 marks** for proving membership in NP. Always include both steps.
>
> 2. **Not stating the polynomial-time constructibility of the reduction** — Saying "$f$ is computable" is not enough. You must explicitly compute the time complexity: $O(n^k)$ for some explicit polynomial $k$.
>
> 3. **Conflating NP-Hard with NP-Complete** — Halting Problem is NP-Hard but **not** NP-Complete. Many students wrongly call it NP-Complete. *(Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)*
>
> 4. **Skipping the equivalence proof** — In a Karp reduction, you must prove **both directions**: $x \in \Pi_1 \Rightarrow f(x) \in \Pi_2$ **and** $x \notin \Pi_1 \Rightarrow f(x) \notin \Pi_2$. Examiners allocate ~3 marks for this bidirectional proof.
>
> 5. **Confusing decision vs. optimization form** — TSP-Decision is NP-Complete, but TSP-Optimization is NP-Hard (it is *not* a decision problem, so it does not formally belong to NP). Be precise.
>
> 6. **Writing "polynomial reduction" without specifying Karp or Turing** — Always state "Karp polynomial-time many-one reduction" for NP-Completeness proofs.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PEITT751 Module 1 — Intractability of NP-Complete Problems:**

- [ ] **P** = decision problems solvable in *deterministic* polynomial time.
- [ ] **NP** = decision problems whose "yes" instances have *polynomial-length certificates* verifiable in polynomial time.
- [ ] **NP-Hard** = problems to which every NP problem polynomial-time reduces; need not be in NP.
- [ ] **NP-Complete** = NP-Hard $\cap$ NP.
- [ ] **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** SAT is NP-Complete — the *seed* of all subsequent NP-Completeness proofs.
- [ ] **Reduction chain pattern:** SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ Independent Set / Vertex Cover / Clique $\to$ Hamiltonian Cycle $\to$ TSP-Decision / Subset Sum.
- [ ] **Karp reduction** ($\leq_p$) requires polynomial-time computability + bidirectional equivalence.
- [ ] **P vs NP** is **open**; widely believed $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$; Clay Millennium Problem.
- [ ] Under $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$, no exact polynomial-time algorithm exists for any NP-Complete optimization problem.
- [ ] **Approximation ratio $\rho$:** multiplicative guarantee on OPT — the central object of study in PEITT751.
- [ ] **PTAS:** $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation in $n^{O(1/\varepsilon)}$ time.
- [ ] **FPTAS:** $(1+\varepsilon)$-approximation in $(1/\varepsilon)^{O(1)} \cdot \text{poly}(n)$ time — strictly stronger.
- [ ] **Knapsack admits FPTAS**; **general TSP** admits no constant-factor approximation (under standard assumptions); **Metric TSP** admits 1.5-approximation (Christofides).
- [ ] **Engineering domains** where NP-Completeness bites: VLSI, bioinformatics, network design, AI/ML, cryptography.
- [ ] **Examiner pet-peeve:** Never call a problem NP-Complete without *both* proving membership in NP *and* the reduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polynomial Time Reductions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polynomial Time Reductions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Polynomial Time Reductions — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **polynomial-time reduction** is a formal transformation technique used in computational complexity theory that converts an instance of one decision/optimization problem into an equivalent instance of another problem, such that the transformation itself can be computed in time bounded by a polynomial in the size of the input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Standard Wording):**
> A language (or problem) **L₁** is said to be *polynomial-time reducible* to a language **L₂**, denoted **L₁ ≤ₚ L₂**, if there exists a **polynomial-time computable function** *f : Σ\* → Σ\** such that for every input string *x*:
> $$x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$$

The function **f** is called the **reduction function**, and the algorithm that computes **f** is the **reduction algorithm**. The polynomial bound is expressed as **O(nᵏ)** for some constant **k ≥ 1**, where **n** is the input size.

### 1.1.1 Two Principal Variants of Reductions

| Reduction Type | Formal Name | Key Property | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Karp Reduction** (Many-One) | ≤ₘᵖ | Maps instance x of A directly to instance f(x) of B via a single polynomial-time function | NP-completeness proofs, gap reductions |
| **Turing Reduction** | ≤ᵀₚ | Solves A using a polynomial number of calls to an oracle for B | Cook-style reductions, approximability preservation |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus for **Approximation Algorithms**, the Karp-style reduction is the primary focus because it cleanly translates *approximation ratios* from one problem to another, which is essential for proving hardness-of-approximation results.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 1.2.1 The "Translator at a Foreign Embassy" Analogy

Imagine you are an Indian citizen who needs a **German visa**, but you do not speak German. You walk into the **German Embassy** and meet a **certified translator**. The translator:

1. **Takes your application (instance of your problem)**.
2. **Converts it** into the official German format **in a reasonable, finite amount of time** (polynomial time).
3. The **German officer** (the algorithm for Problem B) can now process the translated application.

If the German officer **approves the application**, then you, back in India, know that **your original application would have been approved** had the system spoken your language. Conversely, if rejected, your application is also rejected.

In this analogy:
- **Your application** = Instance of problem **A** (e.g., Vertex Cover)
- **The translator** = Polynomial-time reduction **f**
- **The translated document** = Instance of problem **B** (e.g., Set Cover)
- **German officer** = Decision/optimization algorithm for **B**

> [!TIP]
> **The Crux**: If you can solve problem B efficiently, then by first applying the reduction *f* (cheaply, in polynomial time) and then using the efficient algorithm for B, you can also solve problem A efficiently. Therefore, B is **at least as hard** as A.

## 1.3 Why Polynomial Time? The Significance of the Polynomial Bound

A reduction is only *useful* if the transformation itself does not cost more than the original problem. The polynomial bound **O(nᵏ)** ensures:

- The reduction is **tractable** for any reasonable input size.
- The **composition** of two polynomial-time reductions remains polynomial (by transitivity).
- Hardness results (e.g., NP-hardness) **propagate** through polynomial-time reductions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Confusing polynomial-time reductions with exponential-time or log-space reductions. Always verify that the construction of the reduced instance is computable in time **O(nᵏ)** for some fixed constant k, *not* in exponential or even pseudo-polynomial time.

## 1.4 Role of Reductions in Approximation Algorithms

In approximation algorithms, polynomial-time reductions serve two distinct but related purposes:

1. **Algorithmic Reductions (Upper Bounds)**: If problem A ≤ₚ problem B and B has a *c-approximation*, then A inherits a similar approximation guarantee. This is formalized by **L-reductions** (see Section 2).

2. **Hardness Reductions (Lower Bounds)**: If problem A is NP-hard and A ≤ₚ problem B (with a *gap-preserving* reduction), then B cannot be approximated within a certain factor unless **P = NP**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The course **PEITT751 — Approximation Algorithms** is fundamentally built on the concept that polynomial-time reductions allow us to **port algorithmic techniques** (e.g., greedy, LP-rounding) from one problem to another, and conversely, to **transfer inapproximability results** across problems.

---

### Topic at a Glance

| Aspect | Value |
|---|---|
| **Symbolic Notation** | A ≤ₚ B |
| **Reduction Function** | f : {0,1}\* → {0,1}\* |
| **Time Bound** | O(nᵏ) for some constant k ≥ 1 |
| **Transitivity** | If A ≤ₚ B and B ≤ₚ C, then A ≤ₚ C |
| **Primary Use in PEITT751** | Preserving approximation ratios, gap-creating reductions |
| **Inventors** | Karp (1972), Cook (1971) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formal Properties of Polynomial-Time Reductions

Polynomial-time reductions satisfy three core algebraic properties that are essential for KTU board-level questions.

### Property 1: Transitivity (Composability)

If **A ≤ₚ B** via reduction *f₁* with time bound **O(nᵏ¹)** and **B ≤ₚ C** via reduction *f₂* with time bound **O(nᵏ²)**, then **A ≤ₚ C** via the composed reduction *f₂ ∘ f₁*.

> [!NOTE]
> The composition *f₂ ∘ f₁* runs in time **O(nᵏ¹·ᵏ²)**, which is still a polynomial. Hence, the class of polynomial-time reductions is **closed under composition**.

### Property 2: Reflexivity (Trivial Reduction)

Every problem **A** is polynomial-time reducible to itself: **A ≤ₚ A** via the identity reduction *f(x) = x*.

### Property 3: Use in NP-Completeness Framework

A problem **L** is **NP-complete** if and only if:
1. **L ∈ NP** (verifiable in polynomial time), and
2. **L' ≤ₚ L** for every **L' ∈ NP** (NP-hardness).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note**: In approximation algorithms, we relax condition (1) to consider **NPO (NP-optimization) problems** and use reductions that preserve the objective function values (e.g., L-reductions).

## 2.2 Types of Reductions Critical to Approximation Algorithms

### 2.2.1 L-Reduction (Linear Reduction)

Proposed by **Papadimitriou and Yannakakis (1988)**, an **L-reduction** is a special polynomial-time reduction specifically designed to preserve approximation ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition of L-Reduction:**
> Problem A is L-reducible to problem B if there exist constants **α, β > 0** and two polynomial-time computable functions *f* and *g* such that:
> 1. *f* maps an instance **x** of A to an instance **f(x)** of B.
> 2. For any feasible solution **y** of **f(x)**, *g* maps it to a feasible solution **g(x, y)** of A.
> 3. **opt_B(f(x)) ≤ α · opt_A(x)** (instance-size bound).
> 4. For any solution **y** of **f(x)** with cost **c(y)**:
> $$\vert c(g(x,y)) - \text{opt}_A(x) \vert \;\leq\; \beta \cdot \vert c(y) - \text{opt}_B(f(x)) \vert$$
> (solution-quality preservation).

**Key Consequence**: If A is L-reducible to B and B has a polynomial-time **ρ(n)-approximation**, then A has a polynomial-time **(1 + αβ·(ρ(n) − 1))-approximation**.

### 2.2.2 Gap Reduction (Gap-Creating Reduction)

A **gap reduction** is used to establish *hardness-of-approximation* results. Given a decision problem (e.g., 3-SAT) and a gap parameter **ε**, the reduction transforms instances into optimization instances where the objective function value either:
- Falls in one range (indicating a **YES** instance), or
- Falls in a strictly separated range (indicating a **NO** instance).

> [!TIP]
> If the gap is sufficiently large, any algorithm that approximates the optimization problem within a factor smaller than the gap could be used to solve the original decision problem in polynomial time, which is impossible unless P = NP.

### 2.2.3 AP-Reduction (Asymptotic Polynomial-Time Reduction)

A relaxation of L-reduction where approximation ratios are preserved only *asymptotically*. Used in problems where the exact optimum cannot be tightly bounded, only estimated within a **logarithmic factor**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reduction Definition | x ∈ A ⇔ f(x) ∈ B | Foundation of all reductions |
| 2 | Polynomial Bound | Time(f) = O(nᵏ), k ≥ 1 | Tractability requirement |
| 3 | Transitivity | A ≤ₚ B and B ≤ₚ C ⟹ A ≤ₚ C | Composing reductions |
| 4 | L-Reduction Instance Bound | opt_B(f(x)) ≤ α · opt_A(x) | Cost scaling |
| 5 | L-Reduction Solution Bound | \vert c(g(x,y)) − opt_A(x) \vert ≤ β · \vert c(y) − opt_B(f(x)) \vert | Quality preservation |
| 6 | L-Reduction Implied Approximation | ρ_A ≤ 1 + αβ(ρ_B − 1) | Approximation transfer |
| 7 | Gap Reduction (3-SAT) | YES: opt ≤ m; NO: opt > m(1 − ε) | Hardness of approximation |
| 8 | Self-Reducibility | A ≤ₚ A via f(x) = x | Reflexive property |
| 9 | Oracle Time Bound | Turing reduction: T(n) = poly(n) | Multiple oracle queries |
| 10 | Approximation Ratio Preservation | If A ≤ₚ B and B is ρ-approx, A is (1+αβ(ρ−1))-approx | L-reduction corollary |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Engineering Domain | Reduction Application |
|---|---|
| **VLSI Design** | Reducing circuit partitioning to graph bisection / minimum cut |
| **Telecommunications** | Reducing network design to set cover / facility location |
| **Bioinformatics** | Reducing sequence alignment to graph matching problems |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Reducing vehicle routing to TSP / k-median |
| **Cybersecurity** | Reducing intrusion detection to vertex cover / clique |
| **Machine Learning** | Reducing feature selection to set cover |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Care**: A reduction discovered in 1985 may suddenly solve a 2024 industrial problem because the reduction *ports* a new algorithmic technique across decades of research without re-inventing the wheel.

## 2.5 Structural Anatomy of a Polynomial-Time Reduction

Every valid polynomial-time reduction must satisfy the following checklist (this is exactly what a KTU examiner looks for in a 14-mark answer):

1. **Instance Transformation** — Define *f(x)* clearly.
2. **Feasibility Preservation** — Show that valid solutions of *f(x)* map back to valid solutions of *x*.
3. **Cost/Value Preservation** — Show that the objective function values are bounded by a constant factor.
4. **Polynomial Time Proof** — Prove that the construction runs in **O(nᵏ)**.
5. **Correctness Proof** — Prove both directions of the equivalence (or the inequality in the case of L-reductions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Classical Worked Example: Vertex Cover ≤ₚ Independent Set

This is a foundational KTU 2024 example. We show that the **Vertex Cover (VC)** problem is polynomial-time reducible to the **Independent Set (IS)** problem.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statements

> **Vertex Cover (VC)**: Given an undirected graph **G = (V, E)** and an integer **k**, determine whether there exists a subset **S ⊆ V** with **\vert S \vert ≤ k** such that every edge has at least one endpoint in **S**.

> **Independent Set (IS)**: Given an undirected graph **G = (V, E)** and an integer **k**, determine whether there exists a subset **S ⊆ V** with **\vert S \vert ≥ k** such that no two vertices in **S** are adjacent.

### 3.1.2 The Reduction Function *f*

**Input**: An instance **(G, k)** of Vertex Cover, where **G = (V, E)** with **\vert V \vert = n** and **\vert E \vert = m**.

**Output**: An instance **(G, n − k)** of Independent Set, using the **same graph G** unchanged.

**Step-by-Step Construction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Read the input graph } G = (V, E). \\
&\text{Step 2: Compute } n = \vert V \vert. \\
&\text{Step 3: Output the instance } (G, n - k).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity of *f***: We only read the input and compute a single subtraction, so the time is **O(n + m) ⊆ O(n²)**, which is polynomial.

### 3.1.3 Correctness Proof (Equivalence)

We must prove the biconditional:

$$
(G, k) \in \text{VC} \iff (G, n - k) \in \text{IS}
$$

**Forward Direction (⇒)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume } (G, k) \in \text{VC. There exists } S \subseteq V, \vert S \vert \leq k, \text{ covering all edges.} \\
&\text{Define } T = V \setminus S. \text{ Then } \vert T \vert = n - \vert S \vert \geq n - k. \\
&\text{Claim: } T \text{ is an independent set.} \\
&\text{Suppose for contradiction that } \exists \{u, v\} \in E \text{ with } u, v \in T. \\
&\text{Then } u, v \notin S, \text{ so edge } \{u, v\} \text{ is uncovered by } S. \\
&\text{Contradiction with } S \text{ being a vertex cover.} \\
&\text{Therefore, } T \text{ is an independent set of size } \geq n - k. \\
&\text{So } (G, n - k) \in \text{IS.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reverse Direction (⇐)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume } (G, n - k) \in \text{IS. There exists } T \subseteq V, \vert T \vert \geq n - k, \text{ with no two adjacent vertices.} \\
&\text{Define } S = V \setminus T. \text{ Then } \vert S \vert = n - \vert T \vert \leq k. \\
&\text{Claim: } S \text{ is a vertex cover.} \\
&\text{Suppose for contradiction that } \exists \{u, v\} \in E \text{ with } u, v \notin S. \\
&\text{Then } u, v \in T, \text{ so } T \text{ contains an edge, contradicting independence.} \\
&\text{Therefore, } S \text{ is a vertex cover of size } \leq k. \\
&\text{So } (G, k) \in \text{VC.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Conclusion

The reduction is valid:

$$
\text{Vertex Cover} \leq_p \text{Independent Set}
$$

and a polynomial-time algorithm for Independent Set immediately yields a polynomial-time algorithm for Vertex Cover.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reduction Map for Common Problems

This matrix summarizes the standard polynomial-time reductions used throughout PEITT751.

| From Problem | To Problem | Reduction Function *f* | α | β | Approximation Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vertex Cover | Independent Set | (G, k) → (G, n−k) | 1 | 1 | 2-approx preserved exactly |
| Vertex Cover | Set Cover | Each edge → element; each vertex → set | 2 | 1 | 2-approx of SC gives 2-approx of VC |
| Independent Set | Clique | Complement the graph G → Ḡ | 1 | 1 | Equivalence of max IS and max Clique |
| 3-SAT | Independent Set | Per-clause gadget + variable vertices | — | — | Used in NP-completeness proof |
| Max k-Cut | — | — | 1/2 | 1/2 | 0.878-approx via Goemans-Williamson |
| Metric TSP | Hamiltonian Cycle | Replace edges with weight ≥ 2 if missing | — | — | Approximation-preserving |
| Dominating Set | Set Cover | (G, k) → (V, E, k) with V = V(G), E = N[v] | log n | 1 | log n-approx transfer |
| Steiner Tree | — | — | 2 | 1 | 2-approx via MST |
| Facility Location | Set Cover | Open facilities ↔ choose set cover | — | — | O(log n)-approx transfer |

## 3.3 Worked Example: L-Reduction from Vertex Cover to Set Cover

This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 problem** that demonstrates the mechanics of L-reductions.

### 3.3.1 Setup

**Vertex Cover Instance**: Graph **G = (V, E)**, integer **k**.

**Set Cover Instance**: Universe **U = E** (treating each edge as an element), and for each vertex **v ∈ V**, a set **S_v = {e ∈ E : e is incident to v}** (all edges touching v). An integer **k'**.

### 3.3.2 The Reduction Functions

**Instance Map *f***:
$$
f((G, k)) = (U, \mathcal{S}, k)
$$
where **U = E**, **𝒮 = {S_v : v ∈ V}**, and the budget is the same **k**.

**Solution Map *g***: Given any set cover **𝒯 ⊆ 𝒮** (a collection of vertices), map it to the vertex set **g(𝒯) = ⋃_{S ∈ 𝒯} S** (the union of all vertex labels in the cover).

### 3.3.3 Bounding the Constants α and β

**Instance Bound (α)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Every edge in } G \text{ must be covered by some vertex.} \\
&\text{If } C \text{ is a vertex cover of size } \text{opt}_{VC}(G), \text{ then } \mathcal{T} = \{S_v : v \in C\} \text{ covers all edges.} \\
&\text{So } \text{opt}_{SC}(f(G, k)) \leq \text{opt}_{VC}(G, k). \\
&\text{Conversely, any set cover } \mathcal{T} \text{ of size } \ell \text{ corresponds to a vertex cover of size } \leq \ell. \\
&\text{Therefore } \text{opt}_{SC}(f(G, k)) = \text{opt}_{VC}(G, k). \\
&\text{Hence, setting } \alpha = 1 \text{ works.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution Bound (β)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } \mathcal{T} \text{ be any set cover of } f(G, k) \text{ with } \vert \mathcal{T} \vert = c(\mathcal{T}). \\
&\text{The vertex cover } g(\mathcal{T}) \text{ has size } \leq \vert \mathcal{T} \vert \text{ (since each set } S_v \text{ is labeled by one vertex).} \\
&\text{So } \vert c(g(\mathcal{T})) - \text{opt}_{VC} \vert \leq \vert c(\mathcal{T}) - \text{opt}_{SC} \vert. \\
&\text{Setting } \beta = 1 \text{ satisfies the L-reduction condition.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.4 Approximation Transfer

If Set Cover has a polynomial-time **(ln n + 1)-approximation** (the classical greedy result), then by the L-reduction corollary with **α = β = 1**:

$$
\rho_{VC} \leq 1 + 1 \cdot 1 \cdot ((\ln n + 1) - 1) = \ln n + 1
$$

Thus Vertex Cover inherits an **O(ln n)-approximation** via this reduction. (A tighter 2-approximation exists directly, but the L-reduction demonstrates the *transfer mechanism*.)

## 3.4 Exhaustive Analytical Checklist for Any Polynomial-Time Reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this 5-point checklist for any KTU 2024 reduction question worth 7–14 marks:**

1. **Specify the input and output formats** of both problems.
2. **Define the reduction function f explicitly** (not just "we transform the instance").
3. **Compute the time complexity** of computing *f* and confirm it is polynomial.
4. **Prove the equivalence** (or approximation-preservation inequality) rigorously in **both directions**.
5. **State the constants** (α, β for L-reductions) and the **approximation transfer bound**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of a Polynomial-Time Reduction

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the flow of a Karp-style polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    instA["INSTANCE OF PROBLEM A"] --> redF["POLYNOMIAL TIME REDUCTION FUNCTION f"]
    redF --> instB["INSTANCE OF PROBLEM B"]
    instB --> algB["ALGORITHM FOR PROBLEM B"]
    algB --> solB["SOLUTION FOR PROBLEM B"]
    solB --> redG["POLYNOMIAL TIME POST PROCESSING g"]
    redG --> solA["SOLUTION FOR PROBLEM A"]
    redF -.->|"Time: O n to the k"| redF
    algB -.->|"OPTIMAL or APPROXIMATE"| algB
```

## 4.2 Detailed Architecture of an L-Reduction

This schematic shows the complete architecture of an **L-reduction**, including the constants α and β, and the feedback loop representing the approximation guarantee.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["INSTANCE x OF PROBLEM A"] -->|"f in O n to the k1"| B0["INSTANCE f of x OF PROBLEM B"]
    B0 -->|"Run rho approximation algo for B"| B1["SOLUTION y FOR f of x WITH COST c y"]
    B1 -->|"g in O n to the k2"| A1["SOLUTION g of x y FOR PROBLEM A"]
    A1 --> A2["FINAL SOLUTION FOR A"]

    B0 -.->|"opt B f of x LESS THAN alpha times opt A x"| CB1["INSTANCE BOUND alpha"]
    B1 -.->|"Cost diff in A bounded by beta times cost diff in B"| CB2["SOLUTION BOUND beta"]

    A2 -.->|"rho A equals 1 + alpha times beta times rho B minus 1"| RA["APPROXIMATION TRANSFER"]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchy of Reductions in Complexity Theory

This diagram visualizes the relationship between different reduction types and the complexity classes they generate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASECLASS["BASE CLASS OF REDUCTIONS"]
        R1["MANY ONE REDUCTION leq m"]
    end
    subgraph MIDCLASS["INTERMEDIATE CLASSES"]
        R2["POLYNOMIAL TIME REDUCTION leq p"]
        R3["LOG SPACE REDUCTION leq logspace"]
    end
    subgraph ADVCLASS["ADVANCED CLASSES FOR APPROXIMATION"]
        R4["L REDUCTION L"]
        R5["AP REDUCTION AP"]
        R6["GAP REDUCTION GAP epsilon"]
    end
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R2 --> R4
    R2 --> R5
    R2 --> R6
    R4 -.->|"Preserves approximation ratios exactly"| NOTE1["USED FOR UPPER BOUNDS"]
    R6 -.->|"Creates hard gaps"| NOTE2["USED FOR LOWER BOUNDS"]
```

## 4.4 Worked Example: Reduction of Vertex Cover to Set Cover (Visualized)

The following Mermaid diagram traces the reduction pipeline for the example from Section 3.3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["GRAPH G with vertices V and edges E"] -->|"Each edge e becomes a universe element"| U1["UNIVERSE U = E"]
    G1 -->|"Each vertex v becomes a set of incident edges"| S1["SET FAMILY S contains S v for each v in V"]
    U1 --> SC["SET COVER INSTANCE U S k"]
    S1 --> SC
    SC -->|"Apply greedy ln n plus 1 approximation"| GS["GREEDY SET COVER SOLUTION T"]
    GS -->|"Map back each S v to its vertex v"| VC["VERTEX COVER SOLUTION g of T"]
    VC --> OUT["OUTPUT VERTEX COVER OF SIZE at most ln n plus 1 times OPT"]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Reduction Type

This decision tree helps KTU students select the appropriate reduction for a given problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Is the goal an UPPER BOUND on approximation?"} -->|"YES"| Q2{"Do you need tight preservation of ratio?"}
    Q1 -->|"NO"| Q3{"Is the goal a LOWER BOUND on approximation?"}
    Q2 -->|"YES"| R1["USE L REDUCTION"]
    Q2 -->|"NO"| R2["USE AP REDUCTION"]
    Q3 -->|"YES"| Q4{"Is the source problem NP HARD like 3 SAT?"}
    Q3 -->|"NO"| R3["RECONSIDER PROBLEM"]
    Q4 -->|"YES"| R4["USE GAP REDUCTION WITH PARAMETER epsilon"]
    Q4 -->|"NO"| R5["USE STANDARD KARP REDUCTION"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> Define polynomial-time reduction. State and explain the transitivity property with an example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B is a function **f** computable in **O(nᵏ)** time such that **x ∈ A ⇔ f(x) ∈ B**.

**Transitivity**: If **A ≤ₚ B** via *f₁* in time **O(nᵏ¹)** and **B ≤ₚ C** via *f₂* in time **O(nᵏ²)**, then **A ≤ₚ C** via *f₂ ∘ f₁* in time **O(nᵏ¹·ᵏ²)**, which is still polynomial.

**Example**: 3-SAT ≤ₚ Independent Set ≤ₚ Clique. Therefore 3-SAT ≤ₚ Clique directly, with the composed reduction running in polynomial time.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip**: Award 1 mark each for the definition, the transitivity statement, and a valid example.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

> Distinguish between Karp reduction and Turing reduction. Which one is more commonly used in approximation algorithm analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Karp Reduction | Turing Reduction |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Many-one (single call) | Oracle-based (multiple calls allowed) |
| **Function** | Single mapping *f* | Polynomial-many oracle queries |
| **Strength** | Weaker but cleaner | More powerful |
| **Used for** | NP-completeness, L-reductions | Cook's theorem, general algorithm design |

In approximation algorithms, **Karp reductions** (especially **L-reductions**) are preferred because they directly preserve approximation ratios without the complexity of multiple oracle calls.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

> **(a)** Define an **L-reduction** between two NPO problems. State the four conditions formally. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Show that **Vertex Cover** is L-reducible to **Set Cover** with constants **α = β = 1**. Hence, derive the approximation ratio transferred from the greedy Set Cover algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **L-Reduction Formal Definition (Papadimitriou–Yannakakis, 1988):**
> An NPO problem **A** is L-reducible to an NPO problem **B** if there exist constants **α, β > 0** and two polynomial-time computable functions *f* and *g* such that for every instance *x* of A:

**Condition 1 (Instance Mapping)**: *f(x)* is an instance of B.

**Condition 2 (Solution Mapping)**: For every feasible solution *y* of *f(x)*, *g(x, y)* is a feasible solution of *x*.

**Condition 3 (Instance Bound)**:
$$\text{opt}_B(f(x)) \leq \alpha \cdot \text{opt}_A(x)$$

**Condition 4 (Solution Bound)**:
$$\vert c(g(x,y)) - \text{opt}_A(x) \vert \leq \beta \cdot \vert c(y) - \text{opt}_B(f(x)) \vert$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation**: 1 mark for the general setup, 1.5 marks for each of the four conditions, 0.5 mark for citing Papadimitriou–Yannakakis.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the reduction (2 marks)**:

Given a Vertex Cover instance **(G, k)** with **G = (V, E)**, construct the Set Cover instance *f(G, k) = (U, 𝒮, k)* where:
- **Universe**: **U = E** (every edge is an element to cover).
- **Sets**: **𝒮 = {S_v : v ∈ V}** where **S_v = {e ∈ E : e is incident to v}**.
- **Budget**: **k** (unchanged).

**Step 2 — Instance bound, α = 1 (1.5 marks)**:

Any vertex cover of size **p** in **G** corresponds to a set cover of size **p** in *f(G, k)* by selecting the sets **S_v** for each vertex **v** in the cover. Conversely, any set cover of size **q** corresponds to a vertex cover of size **≤ q**. Hence:
$$\text{opt}_{SC}(f(G, k)) = \text{opt}_{VC}(G, k)$$
and **α = 1** satisfies the instance bound.

**Step 3 — Solution bound, β = 1 (1.5 marks)**:

Given any set cover **𝒯 ⊆ 𝒮** with cost **\vert 𝒯 \vert**, define **g(x, 𝒯) = {v ∈ V : S_v ∈ 𝒯}** (the labels of the chosen sets). Then:
$$\vert \vert g(x, \mathcal{T}) \vert - \text{opt}_{VC} \vert \leq \vert \vert \mathcal{T} \vert - \text{opt}_{SC} \vert$$
Hence **β = 1**.

**Step 4 — Approximation transfer (2 marks)**:

The greedy algorithm for Set Cover achieves a **(\ln n + 1)**-approximation where **n = \vert U \vert**. By the L-reduction corollary:
$$\rho_{VC} \leq 1 + \alpha \cdot \beta \cdot (\rho_{SC} - 1) = 1 + 1 \cdot 1 \cdot ((\ln n + 1) - 1) = \ln n + 1$$
So Vertex Cover inherits an **O(ln n)-approximation** via this reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often forget to verify that the L-reduction time bounds are polynomial. Award zero credit for the approximation transfer if the polynomial-time computability of *f* and *g* is not established.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

> **(a)** Define **gap reduction** with reference to the **3-SAT** problem. How is it used to establish inapproximability results? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Given the polynomial-time reduction **Vertex Cover ≤ₚ Independent Set** with function *f((G, k)) = (G, n − k)*, prove both directions of the equivalence and determine the time complexity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Definition of Gap Reduction (3 marks)**:

> A **gap reduction** from a decision problem **L** (typically 3-SAT) to an optimization problem **A** with gap parameter **ε** is a polynomial-time function *f* that transforms an instance *x* of L into an instance of A such that:
> - If *x* is a **YES** instance of L, then the optimum of *f(x)* is at most some value **M**.
> - If *x* is a **NO** instance of L, then the optimum of *f(x)* is strictly greater than **M(1 + ε)** (or **M(1 − ε)** for maximization problems).

**Example from 3-SAT to Max-3SAT (2 marks)**:

For a 3-SAT formula with *m* clauses, a gap reduction yields:
- **YES instance**: Satisfiable, so the optimum number of satisfied clauses is exactly **m**.
- **NO instance**: At most a **1 − ε** fraction of clauses are simultaneously satisfiable, so the optimum is at most **m(1 − ε)**.

**Inapproximability Consequence (2 marks)**:

If there existed a polynomial-time **ρ(n)-approximation** algorithm for Max-3SAT with **ρ(n) < 1/(1 − ε)**, then the algorithm would correctly distinguish YES from NO instances, solving 3-SAT in polynomial time, contradicting **P ≠ NP**. Therefore, Max-3SAT cannot be approximated within a factor of **1/(1 − ε)** for any **ε > 0**, implying that it is NP-hard to approximate Max-3SAT within a factor of **7/8 + δ** for any **δ > 0** (using the Håstad result).

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Allocation**: 3 marks for the formal definition with the YES/NO split, 2 marks for the concrete 3-SAT example, 2 marks for the inapproximability conclusion.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Define the reduction (1 mark)**:

Given instance **(G, k)** of Vertex Cover with **G = (V, E)** and **\vert V \vert = n**, define:
$$f((G, k)) = (G, n - k)$$

**Step 2 — Time complexity (1 mark)**:

The reduction reads the graph and computes **n − k** by a single subtraction. Hence the time is **O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) ⊆ O(n²)**, which is polynomial.

**Step 3 — Forward direction (VC ⇒ IS) (2.5 marks)**:

Assume *(G, k)* is a YES instance of VC, so there exists **S ⊆ V** with **\vert S \vert ≤ k** covering all edges. Define **T = V \ S**. Then **\vert T \vert = n − \vert S \vert ≥ n − k**. For any edge **{u, v} ∈ E**, since S is a vertex cover, at least one of u, v lies in S, so they cannot both be in T. Hence T is an independent set of size at least **n − k**, proving *(G, n − k)* is a YES instance of IS.

**Step 4 — Reverse direction (IS ⇒ VC) (2.5 marks)**:

Assume *(G, n − k)* is a YES instance of IS, so there exists **T ⊆ V** with **\vert T \vert ≥ n − k** and no two adjacent vertices. Define **S = V \ T**. Then **\vert S \vert = n − \vert T \vert ≤ k**. For any edge **{u, v} ∈ E**, if both u, v were outside S, then both would be in T, contradicting the independence of T. Hence at least one endpoint lies in S, proving S is a vertex cover of size at most **k**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute n explicitly** in the reduction (zero credit on time complexity if **O(n²)** is not stated).
> 2. **Omitting the contradiction argument** in either direction of the proof (1–2 marks deducted per direction).
> 3. **Failing to state the biconditional explicitly** before starting the proof.
> 4. **Confusing "≤ k" with "= k"** in the Vertex Cover definition (a classic error worth 0.5–1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEITT751 — Module 1**

- **Definition**: A ≤ₚ B means a polynomial-time function *f* exists with **x ∈ A ⇔ f(x) ∈ B**.
- **Notation**: Use **≤ₚ** (not ≤, not ⇐) for polynomial-time reducibility.
- **Time Bound**: Reduction must run in **O(nᵏ)** for some constant k.
- **Transitivity**: A ≤ₚ B and B ≤ₚ C ⇒ A ≤ₚ C. Composition of two polynomial-time reductions is polynomial.
- **Two Main Types**:
  - **Karp (Many-One)**: Single function call — clean, preserves ratios.
  - **Turing (Oracle)**: Multiple calls — more powerful, used in Cook's theorem.
- **L-Reduction Constants**: **α** for instance-size bound, **β** for solution-quality bound.
- **L-Reduction Approximation Transfer**: **ρ_A ≤ 1 + αβ(ρ_B − 1)**.
- **Gap Reductions** = Hardness of Approximation. Used to derive **7/8** inapproximability for Max-3SAT.
- **AP-Reductions** = Asymptotic preservation. Useful for logarithmic approximation ratios.
- **VC ⇔ IS Complement**: **G** and its complement graph are related by a single polynomial-time reduction.
- **VC ≤ₚ SC** is the canonical L-reduction example with **α = β = 1**.
- **3-SAT ≤ₚ IS** is the canonical Karp reduction for NP-completeness of IS.
- **Polynomial Time is Closed under Composition** — a fact used in every multi-step reduction.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics**: L-reductions, gap reductions, VC-to-SC, IS-to-Clique, 3-SAT gap examples.
- **Common Errors to Avoid**:
  - Using exponential time in the reduction.
  - Forgetting to verify polynomial time of the post-processing function *g*.
  - Confusing the direction of the reduction (A ≤ₚ B means B is "harder" or at least as hard as A).
  - Not stating the biconditional or the inequality explicitly in the correctness proof.
  - Confusing **α-approximation** (within factor α of optimum) with **α-factor gap** (gap of size α in the objective value).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy**: For any reduction question, follow the **5-point analytical checklist** from Section 3.4. Examiners award marks for **instance definition**, **polynomial time verification**, **correctness proof in both directions**, **constants** (if L-reduction), and **approximation transfer** (if asked). Memorize this structure and adapt it to every reduction problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of NP-Complete Problems: SAT, 3-SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of NP-Complete Problems: SAT, 3-SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Given a Boolean formula $\Phi$ in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) over variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, the **SAT Problem** asks: does there exist an assignment of truth values (True/False) to the variables such that $\Phi$ evaluates to **True**? Formally,
$$\Phi(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \;=\; \bigwedge_{i=1}^{m} C_i \;=\; \text{True}$$
where each clause $C_i$ is a disjunction of literals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cook–Levin Theorem (1971):** SAT was the **first** problem proven NP-Complete. This means every problem in the class **NP** can be polynomially reduced to SAT.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a complex recipe with many steps, where each step has alternative ingredients (OR conditions), and ALL steps must succeed simultaneously (AND condition). SAT asks: *Is there ANY combination of ingredient choices that lets the entire recipe work?* For small recipes, brute force works. For large recipes (millions of variables), the problem explodes combinatorially.

---

### 1.2 3-SAT (Restricted SAT)

**Formal Definition:**
3-SAT is a special case of SAT where **every clause contains exactly 3 literals**. Each clause is of the form:
$$C_i \;=\; (\ell_{i,1} \lor \ell_{i,2} \lor \ell_{i,3})$$
where each $\ell_{i,j}$ is either a variable $x_k$ or its negation $\neg x_k$.

> [!NOTE]
> 3-SAT is NP-Complete (proven via reduction from the general SAT problem by introducing auxiliary variables). It is the **canonical starting point** for reductions to graph-theoretic problems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of 3-SAT as a "trio voting" system. Every committee (clause) of exactly 3 people must have at least one person vote "YES." The question is: *Can we find a single set of yes/no votes that satisfies ALL committees at once?*

---

### 1.3 The Clique Problem

**Formal Definition:**
Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and a positive integer $k$, the **Clique Decision Problem** asks: does $G$ contain a clique of size $\geq k$?
A **clique** is a subset $S \subseteq V$ such that every pair of vertices in $S$ is connected by an edge:
$$\forall\, u, v \in S,\; u \neq v \;\Longrightarrow\; \{u, v\} \in E$$

The **optimization version (MAX-CLIQUE)** asks for the largest clique, denoted $\omega(G)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Clique is NP-Complete. It is often used as a **target** for reductions, especially from 3-SAT.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
A clique is a "friend group" where **every** person knows **every** other person in the group. Imagine a social network: the Clique problem asks *What is the largest such fully-connected friend group?* It is computationally hard because checking all subsets is exponential.

---

### 1.4 The Vertex Cover Problem

**Formal Definition:**
Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and a positive integer $k$, the **Vertex Cover Decision Problem** asks: does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\leq k$?
A **vertex cover** is a subset $C \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $C$:
$$\forall\, \{u, v\} \in E,\; u \in C \;\lor\; v \in C$$

The **optimization version (MIN-VC)** asks for the minimum vertex cover, denoted $\tau(G)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The famous **König–Gallai identity** holds for bipartite graphs: $\tau(G) = \nu(G)$ (size of max matching equals size of min vertex cover). This makes MIN-VC polynomial on bipartite graphs.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a city's road network as a graph where intersections are vertices and roads are edges. We want to place security cameras at intersections so that **every road is monitored**. The Vertex Cover problem asks: *What is the minimum number of cameras needed?*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are These Problems NP-Complete?

All four problems share three properties:
1. They belong to the class **NP** (a "yes" certificate is verifiable in polynomial time).
2. Every problem in NP reduces to them (NP-Hardness).
3. They are decision problems with clear "yes/no" answers.

**Reduction Chain (Foundation for Approximation Algorithms):**
$$\text{SAT} \;\leq_p\; \text{3-SAT} \;\leq_p\; \text{Independent Set} \;\equiv_p\; \text{Clique} \;\equiv_p\; \text{Vertex Cover}$$

Where $\leq_p$ denotes polynomial-time Karp reduction and $\equiv_p$ denotes polynomial-time equivalence (via graph complement).

### 2.2 Structural Logic Steps (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 — Start with SAT.** Cook–Levin establishes that any nondeterministic polynomial computation can be encoded as a Boolean formula. Hence SAT is NP-Hard and (trivially) in NP, so it is NP-Complete.

**Step 2 — Restrict to 3-SAT.** Any CNF clause with $k \geq 4$ literals can be rewritten using auxiliary variables into equivalent 3-CNF clauses with polynomial blowup. Hence 3-SAT inherits NP-Completeness.

**Step 3 — Reduce 3-SAT to Independent Set.** Each clause becomes a triangle of 3 vertices; edges connect "conflicting" literals. A satisfying assignment ↔ an independent set of size $m$ (one vertex per clause).

**Step 4 — Convert Independent Set ↔ Clique.** Take the graph complement: $G \to \overline{G}$. Independent sets in $G$ become cliques in $\overline{G}$.

**Step 5 — Convert Independent Set ↔ Vertex Cover.** In any graph, $S$ is a vertex cover **iff** $V \setminus S$ is an independent set. Thus $\tau(G) = n - \alpha(G)$, where $\alpha(G)$ is the maximum independent set size.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| CNF Formula | $\Phi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{m} C_i$ | $n$ vars, $m$ clauses | 3-SAT: each $\vert C_i \vert = 3$ |
| Clause length | $\vert C_i \vert$ | Typically 3 | 3-SAT restriction |
| Max Independent Set | $\alpha(G)$ | Cardinality of largest IS | NP-Hard to compute |
| Min Vertex Cover | $\tau(G)$ | Cardinality of smallest VC | NP-Hard; $\tau(G) = n - \alpha(G)$ |
| Max Clique | $\omega(G)$ | Cardinality of largest clique | NP-Hard; $\omega(G) = \alpha(\overline{G})$ |
| Gallai's Identities | $\alpha(G) + \tau(G) = n$ | Both graphs | $n = \vert V \vert$ |
| König's Theorem (bipartite) | $\tau(G) = \nu(G)$ | $\nu$ = max matching | Only for bipartite |
| Approximation Ratio (VC) | $\rho \leq 2$ | $A(I) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$ | Best known polynomial |
| MAX-SAT ratio | $\rho \geq 1/2$ | Random or greedy | Trivial bound |
| MAX-3SAT ratio | $\rho \geq 7/8$ | Randomized rounding | Tight via PCP |

> [!NOTE]
> All four NP-Complete problems above are decision problems. The corresponding **optimization** versions (MAX-SAT, MAX-3SAT, MAX-CLIQUE, MIN-VC) form the foundation of approximation algorithm design.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SAT/3-SAT Solvers:** Power modern formal verification in chip design (Intel, AMD), software model checking, and AI planning. Annual **SAT Competition** drives industrial-grade solvers.
- **Clique:** Used in computational chemistry (finding tightly bound molecular clusters), social network analysis (community detection), and bioinformatics (protein–protein interaction networks).
- **Vertex Cover:** Central to network security (minimum guard placement), wireless sensor deployment, fault-tolerant VLSI design, and database query optimization.
- **Reductions:** Provide the theoretical scaffolding for proving inapproximability — e.g., if MAX-3SAT is hard to approximate better than $7/8$, the entire PCP framework propagates this hardness to many other problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 3-SAT ≤ Independent Set (The Foundation Reduction)

**Construction.** Given a 3-CNF formula $\Phi$ with $m$ clauses over variables $x_1, \dots, x_n$:

1. For each clause $C_i = (\ell_{i,1} \lor \ell_{i,2} \lor \ell_{i,3})$, create 3 vertices in a triangle.
2. For every pair of literals $\ell_{i,a}$ and $\ell_{j,b}$ (where $i \neq j$), add an edge between them **if and only if** $\ell_{i,a} \equiv \neg \ell_{j,b}$ (they are logical negations of the same variable).

**Claim:** $\Phi$ is satisfiable **iff** the resulting graph $G_\Phi$ has an independent set of size $\geq m$.

**Proof (⇒ Direction).** Suppose $\Phi$ is satisfied by assignment $A$. For each clause $C_i$, at least one literal is satisfied — pick exactly one such literal and select its corresponding vertex $v_i$ in the triangle. The set $S = \{v_1, \dots, v_m\}$ has size $m$. It is independent because:
- Within a triangle, we pick only one vertex (no internal edges).
- Between triangles, picked vertices cannot be negations of each other (both true simultaneously is impossible in classical logic).

**Proof (⇐ Direction).** Suppose $G_\Phi$ has an independent set $S$ of size $\geq m$. Since there are exactly $m$ triangles and at most one vertex per triangle can be in an independent set, $S$ must contain exactly one vertex per triangle. Set the corresponding literal to True, and extend to a full assignment. Each clause is satisfied because its chosen vertex's literal is True. The independence property ensures no variable is set to both True and False.

**Polynomial Bound.** The reduction creates $3m$ vertices and at most $3m \cdot 3(m-1) = O(m^2)$ edges, computed in $O(nm)$ time. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Independent Set ↔ Clique (Graph Complement)

Let $\overline{G} = (V, \overline{E})$ where $\{u, v\} \in \overline{E} \iff \{u, v\} \notin E$ and $u \neq v$.

**Claim:** $S \subseteq V$ is an independent set in $G$ **iff** $S$ is a clique in $\overline{G}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
S \text{ is IS in } G &\iff \forall\, u \neq v \in S,\; \{u, v\} \notin E \\
&\iff \forall\, u \neq v \in S,\; \{u, v\} \in \overline{E} \\
&\iff S \text{ is a clique in } \overline{G}
\end{aligned}$$

The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation. The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation.

Hence $\omega(\overline{G}) = \alpha(G)$, and the reduction is trivially polynomial ($O(\vert V \vert^2)$ time to build $\overline{G}$).

### 3.3 Derivation: Independent Set ↔ Vertex Cover (Complement of $V$)

**Claim:** $C \subseteq V$ is a vertex cover of $G$ **iff** $V \setminus C$ is an independent set of $G$.

$$\begin{aligned}
C \text{ is a VC} &\iff \forall\, \{u, v\} \in E,\; u \in C \lor v \in C \\
&\iff \forall\, \{u, v\} \in E,\; \text{not both } u, v \in V \setminus C \\
&\iff \text{no edge has both endpoints in } V \setminus C \\
&\iff V \setminus C \text{ is an IS in } G
\end{aligned}$$

The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation. The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation.

**Corollary (Gallai's Identity):**
$$\tau(G) + \alpha(G) = n = \vert V \vert$$
This single equation immediately transfers all hardness results between MIN-VC and MAX-IS.

### 3.4 Derivation: 2-Approximation for MIN-VERTEX COVER

**Algorithm (Maximal Matching Heuristic):**

```
VC(G = (V, E)):
    M ← ∅
    while E ≠ ∅:
        pick any edge e = {u, v} from E
        M ← M ∪ {e}
        remove all edges incident to u or v from E
    C ← set of all endpoints of edges in M
    return C
```

**Approximation Analysis:**

Let $M^*$ be a maximum matching in $G$ and let $C^*$ be an optimal vertex cover.

- **Bound 1:** $\vert C \vert = 2 \vert M \vert$ (each matched edge contributes 2 endpoints).
- **Bound 2:** $\vert M \vert \leq \vert M^* \vert$ (greedy is not necessarily max, but...).
- **Bound 3:** $\vert M^* \vert \leq \vert C^* \vert$ (every edge in a matching needs a distinct vertex in any cover).

Combining:
$$\vert C \vert \;=\; 2 \vert M \vert \;\leq\; 2 \vert M^* \vert \;\leq\; 2 \vert C^* \vert$$
The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation. The blank line above and below ensures LaTeX isolation.

Therefore **$A(I) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$** — a 2-approximation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Approximability Landscape

| Problem | Best Approximation | Hardness Threshold | Inapproximable Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| MAX-SAT | $\geq 1/2$ (trivial) | — | $7/8$ (Håstad) |
| MAX-3SAT | $\geq 7/8$ (random) | $7/8$ (Håstad) | $7/8 + \epsilon$ |
| MAX-CLIQUE | $O(n / \log^2 n)$ | — | $n^{1 - \epsilon}$ (Håstad) |
| MIN-VERTEX COVER | $2 - \frac{\log \log n}{2 \log n}$ | — | $10\sqrt{5} - 21 \approx 1.36$ (Dinur) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **PCP Theorem** (1992) and **Håstad's 3-bit PCP** (2001) are the engines behind the inapproximability columns. They explain why no $1.36$-approximation for VC exists (assuming $P \neq NP$), and why MAX-3SAT cannot beat $7/8$.

### 3.6 Worked Example: 3-SAT → Independent Set Reduction

**Instance:** $\Phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3) \land (x_1 \lor x_2 \lor x_3)$

**Step 1 — Create triangles.** Three triangles, one per clause.

**Step 2 — Add conflict edges.** For example, $x_1$ in clause 1 and $\neg x_1$ in clause 2 conflict → edge between them.

**Step 3 — Verify.** A satisfying assignment is $x_1 = T, x_2 = T, x_3 = T$:
- Clause 1: pick $x_1$ (vertex $v_{1,1}$).
- Clause 2: pick $x_2$ (vertex $v_{2,2}$).
- Clause 3: pick $x_3$ (vertex $v_{3,3}$).

Set $S = \{v_{1,1}, v_{2,2}, v_{3,3}\}$ has size $m = 3$ and is independent. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NP-Completeness Reduction Chain (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["SAT\nCook-Levin 1971"]:::core
    node2["3-SAT\nKarp 1972"]:::core
    node3["Independent Set\nGraph IS"]:::core
    node4["Clique\nComplement Graph"]:::core
    node5["Vertex Cover\nV minus IS"]:::core
    node6["MAX-SAT\nOptimization"]:::opt
    node7["MAX-CLIQUE\nOptimization"]:::opt
    node8["MIN-VERTEX COVER\nOptimization"]:::opt

    node1 -->|"restrict clauses to 3"| node2
    node2 -->|"triangle construction"| node3
    node3 -->|"graph complement"| node4
    node3 -->|"V setminus S"| node5
    node1 -->|"count true clauses"| node6
    node4 -->|"max clique size"| node7
    node5 -->|"min cover size"| node8

    classDef core fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Arrows show polynomial-time Karp reductions. Decision problems (red) reduce to optimization problems (blue), which are the actual targets of approximation algorithms.

### 4.2 Approximation Strategy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start with NP-Hard Optimization Problem]):::start
    q1{Does a polynomial\nalgorithm exist?}:::decision
    path1[Exact Algorithm]:::exact
    q2{Is the LP relaxation\nintegral?}:::decision
    path2[LP Rounding]:::approx
    q3{Is there a natural\ngreedy step?}:::decision
    path3[Greedy / Matching]:::approx
    q4{Need probabilistic\nguarantee?}:::decision
    path4[Randomized Rounding]:::approx
    q5[Approximation Ratio\nAnalysis]:::verify
    endA([Return A of I over OPT of I]):::stop

    startA --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes, e.g. bipartite VC| path1
    q1 -->|No, NP-Hard| q2
    q2 -->|Yes, e.g. matching in bipartite| path2
    q2 -->|No, fractional vertices| q3
    q3 -->|Yes, e.g. maximal matching VC| path3
    q3 -->|No clear greedy| q4
    q4 -->|Yes, e.g. MAX-SAT| path4
    q4 -->|No| endA
    path1 --> q5
    path2 --> q5
    path3 --> q5
    path4 --> q5
    q5 --> endA

    classDef start fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stop fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef exact fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef approx fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5a2d82,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef verify fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Structural Anatomy of a 3-SAT to Independent-Set Reduction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph C1["Clause 1: x1 OR not x2 OR x3"]
        v11["x1"]:::lit
        v12["not x2"]:::lit
        v13["x3"]:::lit
        v11 --- v12
        v12 --- v13
        v11 --- v13
    end
    subgraph C2["Clause 2: not x1 OR x2 OR not x3"]
        v21["not x1"]:::lit
        v22["x2"]:::lit
        v23["not x3"]:::lit
        v21 --- v22
        v22 --- v23
        v21 --- v23
    end
    subgraph C3["Clause 3: x1 OR x2 OR x3"]
        v31["x1"]:::lit
        v32["x2"]:::lit
        v33["x3"]:::lit
        v31 --- v32
        v32 --- v33
        v31 --- v33
    end

    v11 -.->|conflict: x1 vs not x1| v21
    v12 -.->|conflict: not x2 vs x2| v22
    v13 -.->|conflict: x3 vs not x3| v23
    v21 -.->|conflict: not x1 vs x1| v31
    v22 -.->|conflict: x2 vs x2| v32
    v23 -.->|conflict: not x3 vs x3| v33
    v11 -.->|conflict: x1 vs x1| v31
    v12 -.->|conflict: not x2 vs x2| v22
    v13 -.->|conflict: x3 vs x3| v33

    classDef lit fill:#fffacd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Legend:** Solid lines = intra-clause triangle edges. Dotted lines = inter-clause conflict edges (only when one literal is the negation of the other). The graph contains $3m$ vertices and is constructed in $O(nm)$ time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** State the SAT problem formally. Why is it called the "mother of NP-Completeness"?

**Model Answer:**
SAT is the decision problem: *Given a Boolean formula $\Phi$ in CNF over variables $x_1, \dots, x_n$, does there exist an assignment that makes $\Phi$ True?*

SAT is called the mother of NP-Completeness because **Cook's Theorem (1971)** established SAT as the **first** problem proven NP-Complete, before any other NP-Complete problem was known. This provided the anchor point from which **Richard Karp (1972)** reduced 21 classical problems, creating the entire NP-Completeness framework. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define a vertex cover and a clique. State the Gallai identity that relates vertex cover and independent set.

**Model Answer:**
- A **vertex cover** of $G = (V, E)$ is a subset $C \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $C$.
- A **clique** in $G$ is a subset $S \subseteq V$ such that every pair of vertices in $S$ is adjacent.

**Gallai's Identity:**
$$\tau(G) + \alpha(G) = n$$
where $\tau(G)$ is the minimum vertex cover size, $\alpha(G)$ is the maximum independent set size, and $n = \vert V \vert$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Explain the 3-SAT problem. Show that 3-SAT is NP-Complete by giving a polynomial-time reduction from SAT.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of 3-SAT (2 marks):** 3-SAT is the satisfiability problem restricted to CNF formulas where every clause contains **exactly 3 literals**. Formally, given $\Phi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{m} C_i$ with $\vert C_i \vert = 3$ for all $i$, decide if there is a truth assignment satisfying all clauses.

**Membership in NP (1 mark):** Given a candidate assignment, we can verify in $O(m)$ time whether all clauses are satisfied. So 3-SAT $\in$ NP.

**Reduction from SAT to 3-SAT (4 marks):**
Given a CNF formula $\Psi$ with clause $C = (\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor \dots \lor \ell_k)$:
- If $k = 1$: $C = (\ell_1)$. Introduce auxiliary variables $y_1, y_2$ and replace with $(\ell_1 \lor y_1 \lor y_2) \land (\ell_1 \lor \neg y_1 \lor y_2) \land (\ell_1 \lor y_1 \lor \neg y_2) \land (\ell_1 \lor \neg y_1 \lor \neg y_2)$.
- If $k = 2$: $C = (\ell_1 \lor \ell_2)$. Introduce $y$ and replace with $(\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor y) \land (\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor \neg y)$.
- If $k = 3$: leave as is.
- If $k > 3$: introduce new variables $y_1, \dots, y_{k-3}$ and split:
$$(\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor y_1) \land (\neg y_1 \lor \ell_3 \lor y_2) \land \dots \land (\neg y_{k-3} \lor \ell_{k-1} \lor \ell_k)$$

This construction introduces at most $O(k)$ new variables and clauses per original clause, so the total size grows by a polynomial factor.

**Correctness Sketch:** Each rewritten clause is **logically equivalent** to the original under the right setting of auxiliary variables. Therefore $\Psi$ is satisfiable **iff** the resulting 3-CNF formula is satisfiable. Hence SAT $\leq_p$ 3-SAT, so 3-SAT is NP-Hard. Combined with NP-membership, 3-SAT is NP-Complete. ✓

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Design and prove a 2-approximation algorithm for the MIN-VERTEX COVER problem. Show the algorithm is tight via a worst-case example.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
```
GREEDY-VC(G = (V, E)):
    M ← ∅
    while E ≠ ∅:
        pick any edge e = {u, v} from E
        M ← M ∪ {e}
        delete all edges incident to u or v from E
    return C = {endpoints of edges in M}
```

**Proof of 2-Approximation (3 marks):**
Let $M$ be the maximal matching produced. Then:
- $\vert C \vert = 2 \vert M \vert$ (each edge in $M$ contributes 2 endpoints).
- Let $C^*$ be any optimal vertex cover. Every edge in $M$ has at least one endpoint in $C^*$, and edges in $M$ are disjoint, so $\vert M \vert \leq \vert C^* \vert$.

Combining: $\vert C \vert = 2 \vert M \vert \leq 2 \vert C^* \vert$. So $A(I) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$.

**Tightness (1 mark):**
Consider a complete bipartite graph $K_{n,n}$. Greedy may match all $n$ left vertices with $n$ right vertices, yielding $\vert C \vert = 2n$. But $\text{OPT} = n$ (one side of the bipartition). So $A / \text{OPT} = 2$, achieving the bound. (Note: this is not a counter-example to approximation ratio being *strictly less* than 2 in the worst case, but shows the bound is achievable in limit.)

---

#### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define the MAX-CLIQUE problem. Reduce 3-SAT to Independent Set, and then show how this implies MAX-CLIQUE is NP-Hard.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of MAX-CLIQUE (1 mark):** Given $G = (V, E)$, find a subset $S \subseteq V$ of maximum cardinality such that $S$ induces a complete subgraph. Formally maximize $\vert S \vert$ subject to $\forall\, u, v \in S, u \neq v \Rightarrow \{u, v\} \in E$.

**Reduction 3-SAT → Independent Set (4 marks):**
Given 3-CNF formula $\Phi$ with $m$ clauses:
- For each clause $C_i = (\ell_{i,1} \lor \ell_{i,2} \lor \ell_{i,3})$, create 3 vertices forming a triangle.
- For each pair of literals $\ell_{i,a}$ and $\ell_{j,b}$ with $i \neq j$ such that $\ell_{i,a} \equiv \neg \ell_{j,b}$ (i.e., they are negations of the same variable), add an edge between their corresponding vertices.

The construction uses $3m$ vertices and runs in $O(nm)$ time, hence polynomial.

**Correctness (1 mark):** $\Phi$ is satisfiable **iff** $G_\Phi$ has an independent set of size $\geq m$. (Full proof as in Section 3.1.)

**Implication for MAX-CLIQUE (1 mark):** Independent Set $\leq_p$ Clique via graph complement (Section 3.2). Hence 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Clique, so MAX-CLIQUE is NP-Hard. Decision version is in NP, so it is NP-Complete.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Consider the MAX-3SAT problem. Describe a randomized 7/8-approximation algorithm and prove its expected ratio.

**Model Solution:**

**Problem (1 mark):** Given a 3-CNF formula $\Phi$ with $m$ clauses over $n$ variables, find a truth assignment that satisfies the **maximum number of clauses**.

**Algorithm — Randomized Rounding (2 marks):**
1. Solve the **LP relaxation** of MAX-3SAT, obtaining fractional values $x_j \in [0, 1]$ for each variable.
2. Set each variable $x_j = 1$ independently with probability $x_j$ (and 0 otherwise).

**Analysis (4 marks):**
For a single clause $C = (\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor \ell_3)$, the probability that the random assignment **fails** to satisfy $C$ is the probability that all three literals are false. Since the three literals involve disjoint sets of variables (at most), and each literal has probability $(1 - x_j)$ of being false:
$$P[\text{C fails}] \;\leq\; (1 - x_1)(1 - x_2)(1 - x_3)$$
Using the LP constraint $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \geq 1$ and the AM-GM inequality (or direct calculation):
$$P[\text{C fails}] \;\leq\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^3 \;=\; \frac{8}{27}$$
Hence:
$$P[\text{C satisfied}] \;\geq\; 1 - \frac{8}{27} \;=\; \frac{19}{27} \;\geq\; \frac{7}{8} \cdot \frac{(x_1 + x_2 + x_3)}{1}$$

By linearity of expectation, the expected number of satisfied clauses is at least $\frac{7}{8} \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \geq \frac{7}{8} \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}$.

**Conclusion (0 marks — for completeness):** The expected ratio is at least $7/8$. This is **tight** by Håstad's PCP theorem (no polynomial-time algorithm can achieve $7/8 + \epsilon$ unless $P = NP$).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting NP-Membership (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students prove NP-Hardness via reduction but forget to argue that the problem is in NP. Both parts are required for NP-Completeness.
> 2. **Wrong direction in reductions (–3 marks):** When reducing problem $A$ to $B$, you must show "A yes-instance → B yes-instance" AND "B yes-instance → A yes-instance." One direction is not enough.
> 3. **Ignoring polynomial-time bound (–1 mark):** Every reduction MUST be polynomial-time. Always state the running time and vertex/edge count.
> 4. **Confusing Independent Set with Clique (–2 marks):** In the complement graph, IS becomes Clique. Many students forget to apply the complement.
> 5. **Approximation ratio proof skipping (–2 marks):** When claiming $\rho = 2$ for VC, you MUST show three inequalities: $\vert C \vert = 2\vert M \vert$, $\vert M \vert \leq \text{OPT}$, hence $\vert C \vert \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$. Skipping any loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing König's Theorem (bipartite VC = matching) with general VC:** König's Theorem is only for bipartite graphs; do not cite it for general MIN-VC.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SAT** is the canonical NP-Complete problem (Cook–Levin, 1971). A Boolean formula in CNF has $m$ clauses, each a disjunction of literals.
- **3-SAT** restricts every clause to exactly 3 literals. NP-Complete by reduction from SAT using auxiliary variables.
- **MAX-CLIQUE** finds the largest complete subgraph. Decision version is NP-Complete.
- **MIN-VERTEX COVER** finds the smallest set of vertices touching all edges. Decision version is NP-Complete.
- **Gallai's Identity** $\tau(G) + \alpha(G) = n$ links Vertex Cover and Independent Set.
- **Complement trick:** $\alpha(G) = \omega(\overline{G})$ — Independent Set and Clique are equivalent.
- **Reduction chain:** $\text{SAT} \leq_p \text{3-SAT} \leq_p \text{IS} \equiv_p \text{Clique} \equiv_p \text{VC}$.
- **2-Approximation for VC:** Maximal matching heuristic gives $A \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$. Tighter ratios are possible with LP rounding and SDP.
- **MAX-3SAT has $7/8$-approximation** via randomized rounding, and is **tight** by Håstad's PCP.
- **MAX-CLIQUE is famously hard to approximate** — no constant factor unless $P = NP$.
- **PCP Theorem** + **Håstad's 3-bit PCP** are the engines of inapproximability results used in KTU paper-setting.
- **Always remember:** Decision ∈ NP + NP-Hard (via reduction) = NP-Complete. All four problems satisfy this trifecta.
- **KTU 2024 Outcome Alignment:** This topic maps to **CO1** (Understanding NP-Hardness and the need for approximation) and uses **Bloom's Level L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)** cognitive verbs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approaches to Solve NP-Complete Problems (Concept Only): Approximation Algorithms, Exact Algorithms, Randomized Algorithms, Fixed-Parameter Tractable Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Approaches to Solve NP-Complete Problems (Concept Only): Approximation Algorithms, Exact Algorithms, Randomized Algorithms, Fixed-Parameter Tractable Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Central Problem: NP-Completeness

NP-Complete problems form a class of decision/optimization problems for which **no known polynomial-time algorithm exists**, but a proposed solution can be *verified* in polynomial time. Since Stephen Cook's foundational 1971 work and Richard Karp's 1972 list of **21 NP-complete problems**, thousands of real-world problems have been classified as NP-Complete (e.g., SAT, Vertex Cover, Traveling Salesman, Independent Set, Hamiltonian Cycle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The P vs NP Question**: If a polynomial-time algorithm exists for *any* NP-Complete problem, then P = NP. Since this remains unresolved, computer scientists design **alternative algorithmic paradigms** that work *around* the intractability barrier rather than solving it head-on.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Mountain Climbing Problem

Imagine you are standing at the base of a fog-covered mountain, trying to find the highest peak:
- You cannot see the global summit (exact optimal solution) because the terrain is too complex.
- You have **four strategies**:
  1. **Approximation**: Hike *most* of the way up — accept a viewpoint that is provably close to the true summit.
  2. **Exact (Exponential)**: Use a high-power telescope and a survey team to find the *true* peak, but it takes a very long time.
  3. **Randomized**: Toss a coin at each fork in the path; sometimes you get lucky and reach near the top quickly.
  4. **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)**: Realize the mountain has only a few "ridges" (a small parameter $k$); now climbing becomes easy even if the base is huge.

This is the philosophical core of dealing with NP-Completeness: **trade one resource (time/quality/certainty) for another**.

### 1.3 The Four Paradigms at a Glance

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Typical Guarantee | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Approximation** | Polynomial-time *near-optimal* solution | Bounded ratio vs. optimum | Large instances, provable quality |
| **Exact (Exponential)** | True optimum, but worst-case exponential | Always optimal | Small-to-medium $n$ |
| **Randomized** | Use randomness; expected or probabilistic bounds | High-probability correctness | Average-case hardness, simpler code |
| **FPT** | Complexity is $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ for small parameter $k$ | Exact, depends on $k$ | Problems with small natural parameter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note**: This module is *concept-only* (no proofs of NP-Completeness, no specific algorithmic code). The expected depth is the ability to *compare and contrast* these four paradigms, recognize when each is applicable, and understand the formal performance metrics.

### 1.4 Formal Class Definitions (Preliminaries)

Let $\Pi$ be an NP-hard optimization problem. For any instance $I$:

- $OPT(I)$ = value of the optimal solution
- $ALG(I)$ = value produced by algorithm
- $n = \vert I \vert$ = size of the input

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Class P**: Decision problems solvable in time $O(n^{c})$ for some constant $c$.
>
> **Key Class NP**: Decision problems whose YES-instances have certificates verifiable in polynomial time.
>
> **Key Class NP-Hard**: Problems at least as hard as *any* NP problem (under polynomial-time reductions).
>
> **Key Class NP-Complete**: Problems in NP that are NP-Hard.

These four paradigms are the four main "escape routes" when a problem is shown to be NP-Complete.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Paradigm 1 — Approximation Algorithms

**Goal**: Produce a polynomial-time solution $ALG(I)$ whose value is provably *close* to $OPT(I)$.

**Approximation Ratio (Minimization)**:
$$
\rho(I) \;=\; \frac{ALG(I)}{OPT(I)} \;\geq\; 1
$$

**Approximation Ratio (Maximization)**:
$$
\rho(I) \;=\; \frac{OPT(I)}{ALG(I)} \;\geq\; 1
$$

An algorithm is a **$\rho$-approximation** if $\rho(I) \leq \rho$ for *every* valid instance $I$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Approximation Classes**:
> - **APX**: Constant-factor approximation exists.
> - **PTAS** (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme): For every $\epsilon > 0$, a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation runs in time $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$.
> - **FPTAS** (Fully PTAS): Same as PTAS but time is $f(1/\epsilon) \cdot poly(n)$ (polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$).
> - **APX-Hard**: Cannot be approximated within some constant unless P = NP.

**Why It Works**: Many NP-hard problems have polynomial-time algorithms that, while not optimal, can be *analyzed* using combinatorial lower/upper bounds (e.g., greedy matroid theory, LP relaxation + rounding, primal-dual schema).

### 2.2 Paradigm 2 — Exact Exponential Algorithms

**Goal**: Solve the problem *exactly*, but with running time $O(c^{n})$ where $c < $ brute force constant.

**Big-O-Star Notation ($O^{*}$)**:
$$
T(n) \;=\; O^{*}(f(n)) \quad \iff \quad T(n) \;=\; f(n) \cdot poly(n)
$$

The $poly(n)$ factor is suppressed; the *exponential base* $f(n)$ is the true measure of hardness.

**Classical Results Table**:

| Problem | Brute Force | Best Known Exact |
|---|---|---|
| 3-SAT | $O(2^{n})$ | $O^{*}(1.307^{n})$ |
| Independent Set | $O(2^{n})$ | $O^{*}(1.1996^{n})$ |
| Vertex Cover | $O(2^{n})$ | $O^{*}(1.2738^{k})$ |
| Hamiltonian Cycle | $O(n!)$ | $O^{*}(2^{n})$ |
| TSP (exact) | $O(n!)$ | $O^{*}(2^{n})$ via DP |

**Techniques**:
- **Branch & Bound** (search-tree pruning)
- **Measure & Conquer** (weighted instance size for tighter analysis)
- **Inclusion-Exclusion Principle** (subset DP)
- **Dynamic Programming over Subsets**

### 2.3 Paradigm 3 — Randomized Algorithms

**Goal**: Use random coin flips to either (a) get expected polynomial time, or (b) get high-probability correct answers.

**Two Canonical Types**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Las Vegas:} \quad & \text{Always correct; expected polynomial time.} \\
\text{Monte Carlo:} \quad & \text{Runs in polynomial time; correct with probability } \geq \tfrac{3}{4}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Probabilistic Tools**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expectation:} \quad & \mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{x} x \cdot \Pr[X=x] \\
\text{Markov's Inequality:} \quad & \Pr[X \geq t] \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[X]}{t} \\
\text{Chernoff Bound:} \quad & \Pr[\vert X - \mu \vert \geq \delta\mu] \leq 2e^{-\mu\delta^{2}/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derandomization**: A randomized algorithm can often be converted to deterministic via the **Method of Conditional Expectations** or by enumerating the "good" random seeds.

### 2.4 Paradigm 4 — Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT) Algorithms

**Goal**: Exploit a small problem-specific parameter $k$ to achieve running time $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$, where $f$ is *any* computable function.

**Formal Definition**:
$$
L \in \text{FPT} \quad \iff \quad L \text{ is decidable in time } f(k) \cdot n^{c}, \; c \text{ constant}
$$

**W-Hierarchy** (for problems that are *not* FPT, under standard assumptions):
$$
\text{FPT} \;\subseteq\; W[1] \;\subseteq\; W[2] \;\subseteq\; \cdots \;\subseteq\; W[P]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Kernelization**: An FPT-equivalent technique where the input is reduced in polynomial time to an *equivalent instance* of size at most $g(k)$ (the "kernel"). For example, Vertex Cover has a kernel with at most $2k$ vertices.

**Key Parameters**:
- $k$ = solution size (e.g., treewidth, vertex cover number, clique number)
- $k$ = treewidth (graph structure parameter)
- $k$ = distance from trivial structure

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Definition | Formula / Bound |
|---|---|---|
| Approximation Ratio (min) | $\rho = ALG / OPT$ | $\rho \geq 1$; smaller is better |
| Approximation Ratio (max) | $\rho = OPT / ALG$ | $\rho \geq 1$; smaller is better |
| Inapproximability | APX-Hard | $\rho$-inapprox unless P = NP |
| Exact algorithm time | $O^{*}(c^{n})$ | $c$ is the exponential base |
| Las Vegas time | Expected poly | $\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = poly(n)$ |
| Monte Carlo error | $\Pr[\text{error}]$ | $\leq 1/4$ (amplifiable to $\leq 2^{-t}$) |
| FPT complexity | $f(k) \cdot n^{c}$ | $f$ arbitrary, $c$ constant |
| Kernel size | $g(k)$ | Polynomial in $k$ |
| PTAS runtime | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | Polynomial in $n$, not in $1/\epsilon$ |
| FPTAS runtime | $f(1/\epsilon) \cdot poly(n)$ | Polynomial in *both* |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Approximation** → Cloud computing resource allocation, VLSI design, route planning in Google Maps.
- **Exact Exponential** → SAT solvers in hardware verification, cryptanalysis of small keys, bioinformatics phylogeny.
- **Randomized** → Primality testing (RSA), hash table design, Monte Carlo simulations in physics/finance, randomized load balancing.
- **FPT** → Database query optimization (treewidth), network analysis, bioinformatics (phylogenetic tree reconstruction with bounded parameters).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Approximation Ratio Tightness for Vertex Cover

We derive the *standard 2-approximation* guarantee for the **Vertex Cover** problem.

**Problem**: Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, find the minimum-cardinality set $S \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $S$.

**Greedy 2-Approximation Algorithm**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{1. } C \leftarrow \emptyset \\
& \text{2. While } E \neq \emptyset \text{ do:} \\
& \text{3. } \quad \text{Pick any edge } e = (u, v) \in E \\
& \text{4. } \quad C \leftarrow C \cup \{u, v\} \\
& \text{5. } \quad \text{Remove all edges incident to } u \text{ or } v \text{ from } E \\
& \text{6. Return } C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analysis**:

Let $F = \{e_{1}, e_{2}, \ldots, e_{t}\}$ be the set of edges selected in Step 3.

By construction, every edge in $F$ shares **no endpoint** with any other edge in $F$ (since we delete all incident edges after each pick). Therefore:
$$
F \text{ is a matching} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{edges in } F \text{ are pairwise disjoint.}
$$

Now, any vertex cover $OPT$ must cover every edge in $F$. Since the edges in $F$ are disjoint, $OPT$ requires *at least one distinct vertex per edge*:
$$
\vert OPT \vert \;\geq\; \vert F \vert
$$

The algorithm picks exactly **2 vertices per edge** picked from $F$:
$$
\vert C \vert \;=\; 2 \cdot \vert F \vert
$$

Combining:
$$
\vert C \vert \;=\; 2 \cdot \vert F \vert \;\leq\; 2 \cdot \vert OPT \vert
$$

Hence the approximation ratio:
$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{\vert C \vert}{\vert OPT \vert} \;\leq\; 2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **tight**: a complete graph $K_{n}$ has $\vert OPT \vert = n-1$ and the algorithm produces $\vert C \vert = n$ (matching covers $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ disjoint edges, each contributing 2 vertices). Ratio tends to $2$ as $n \to \infty$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Chernoff Bound Application in Randomized Algorithms

Suppose we run a **Monte Carlo algorithm** that errs with probability $p = 1/3$ per run. We amplify correctness by repetition.

**Setup**:
- Each independent run has error probability $p = 1/3$.
- Run the algorithm $t$ times and take a **majority vote**.
- Let $X$ = number of errors in $t$ runs.

$$
X \sim \text{Binomial}(t, 1/3), \quad \mu = t/3
$$

Majority vote fails only if $X > t/2$, i.e., $X \geq t/2$.

We want $\Pr[X \geq t/2] \leq \delta$ for any $\delta > 0$.

By Chernoff Bound with $\delta' = (t/2 - \mu)/\mu = (t/2 - t/3)/(t/3) = 1/2$:

$$
\Pr[X \geq t/2] \;\leq\; 2e^{-\mu \delta'^{2}/3} \;=\; 2e^{-(t/3)(1/4)/3} \;=\; 2e^{-t/36}
$$

For error $\leq 2^{-k}$:
$$
2e^{-t/36} \leq 2^{-k} \;\Longrightarrow\; t \geq 36(k + \log_{2} e) \approx 36k + 25
$$

So **$t = O(k)$ independent runs** reduce error to $2^{-k}$. The cost of amplifiable correctness is *only linear repetition*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Paradigms on Benchmark Problems

| Problem | Approximation | Exact Exp. | Randomized | FPT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertex Cover** | 2-approx (tight) | $O^{*}(1.2738^{k})$ | Random greedy: $O(\log n)$-approx | $O(kn + 1.2738^{k})$ |
| **3-SAT** | $8/7$-approx? (open) | $O^{*}(1.307^{n})$ | Schöning: $O^{*}((4/3)^{n})$ | $O^{*}(2^{k})$ for $k$ vars |
| **TSP (metric)** | $3/2$-approx (Christofides) | $O^{*}(2^{n})$ via DP | — | $O^{*}(2^{k})$ for $k$-path |
| **Independent Set** | $O(n / \log^{2} n)$ | $O^{*}(1.1996^{n})$ | — | $W[1]$-hard in $k$ |
| **Set Cover** | $\ln n$-approx (tight) | $O^{*}(2^{n})$ | — | $O^{*}(2^{k})$ for $k$-cover |
| **Clique** | $O(n / \log^{2} n)$ | $O^{*}(1.1996^{n})$ | — | $W[1]$-hard in $k$ |
| **Min Cut** | Exact in poly-time! | — | Karger: $O(n^{2}\log n)$ | — |

### 3.4 Why FPT is Not the Same as Polynomial

Consider the function $f(k) = 2^{2^{k}}$. Then:
$$
T(n, k) \;=\; 2^{2^{k}} \cdot n^{3}
$$

For $k = 10$, $f(k) = 2^{1024} \approx 10^{308}$ — astronomically large. This is still *formally FPT*, but **practically infeasible** for $k > 5$ or so.

The $W[1]$-hardness conjecture states that certain problems (e.g., Independent Set, Clique) have *no* FPT algorithm. They lie "above" FPT in the parameterized complexity hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hierarchy of Tractability**:
> $$
> \text{P} \;\subseteq\; \text{FPT} \;\subseteq\; W[1] \;\subseteq\; W[2] \;\subseteq\; \cdots
> $$
> Most theoreticians believe **P $\subsetneq$ FPT** (i.e., some FPT problems are not polynomial). For example, the $k$-Clique problem is $W[1]$-complete and believed not FPT.

### 3.5 Comparison of Worst-Case Guarantees

**Time vs. Quality Trade-off** (one slide of insight):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Approximation:} \quad & T = poly(n), \quad \text{quality} = \rho \cdot OPT \\
\text{Exact Exponential:} \quad & T = O^{*}(c^{n}), \quad \text{quality} = OPT \\
\text{Randomized Approx.:} \quad & T = poly(n), \quad \text{quality} = \rho \cdot OPT \text{ w.h.p.} \\
\text{FPT:} \quad & T = f(k) \cdot poly(n), \quad \text{quality} = OPT \text{ if } k \text{ small}
\end{aligned}
$$

The *ideal* algorithm is the one whose **time-vs-quality trade-off** matches the constraints of the application domain.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Tree — Choosing a Paradigm

The following diagram maps the *decision flow* a theoretical computer scientist follows when confronting a new NP-Complete problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
