```mermaid
    startA[New NP-Hard Problem] --> q1{Is n small enough for exact?}
    q1 -- Yes exact feasible --> exPath[Use Exact Exponential Algo]
    q1 -- No n is large --> q2{Is there a small natural parameter k?}
    q2 -- Yes k is small --> fptPath[Use FPT Algorithm]
    q2 -- No k is large --> q3{Need provable quality bound?}
    q3 -- Yes need guarantee --> approxPath[Use Approximation Algorithm]
    q3 -- No average case ok --> randPath[Use Randomized Algorithm]
    exPath --> end1[Output OPT in O*cn]
    fptPath --> end2[Output OPT in fk times n to the c]
    approxPath --> end3[Output rho times OPT in poly n]
    randPath --> end4[Output near OPT in expected poly n]
```

### 4.2 Taxonomy of Complexity Classes

This diagram shows the relationships between the algorithmic paradigms and classical complexity classes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classP[Class P Polynomial]
    classFPT[Class FPT Parameterized]
    classNP[Class NP Decision]
    classNPC[Class NP Complete]
    classW1[Class W 1 Hard]
    classAPX[Class APX Approximable]
    classPTAS[Class PTAS]
    classFPTAS[Class FPTAS]

    classP --> classFPT
    classP --> classAPX
    classFPT --> classW1
    classNP --> classNPC
    classAPX --> classPTAS
    classPTAS --> classFPTAS
    classNPC -. inapproximable .-> classAPX
    classW1 -. believed not in .-> classFPT
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Approximation Algorithm

For problems where approximation is the chosen paradigm, the typical pipeline has four logical blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inBlock[Input Instance I] --> preBlock[Preprocessing Stage]
    preBlock --> algoBlock[Core Heuristic or LP Solver]
    algoBlock --> postBlock[Postprocessing and Rounding]
    postBlock --> outBlock[Output Solution S]
    outBlock --> anaBlock[Analysis Block verifies rho times OPT]
    anaBlock --> certBlock[Certificate of Approximation Ratio]
```

### 4.4 FPT Algorithm Pipeline

The conceptual flow of an FPT algorithm: parameterize, kernelize, then solve the kernel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inInst[Input Instance I and Parameter k] --> param[Parameter Identification]
    param --> kern[Kernelization: reduce to size g of k]
    kern --> boundedSearch[Bounded Search Tree]
    boundedSearch --> solveK[Solve Reduced Kernel in f of k]
    solveK --> lift[Lift Solution back to Original I]
    lift --> outSol[Output Exact Solution with certificate]
```

### 4.5 Randomized Algorithm Control Flow

Shows the difference between Las Vegas (retry loop) and Monte Carlo (vote loop) execution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startR[Algorithm with random bits] --> q1{Always correct?}
    q1 -- Yes Las Vegas --> lv[Run until success]
    q1 -- No Monte Carlo --> mc[Run t times, take majority]
    lv --> outR1[Output: correct, time is random]
    mc --> outR2[Output: correct with probability 1 minus delta]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **alphanumeric node identifiers** and **plain uppercase text** inside labels (no markdown formatting inside Mermaid nodes) per KTU rendering safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define approximation ratio for a minimization problem. What does it mean for an algorithm to be a $\rho$-approximation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
For a minimization problem with instance $I$, the **approximation ratio** is defined as:
$$
\rho(I) = \frac{ALG(I)}{OPT(I)}
$$
where $ALG(I)$ is the cost of the solution produced by the algorithm and $OPT(I)$ is the cost of the optimal solution. An algorithm is called a **$\rho$-approximation algorithm** if for every instance $I$ of the problem, $\rho(I) \leq \rho$. The value $\rho \geq 1$, and **smaller is better**; $\rho = 1$ means the algorithm is optimal. A $\rho$-approximation provides a **provable guarantee** that the algorithm's output is at most $\rho$ times worse than the optimum.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Distinguish between Las Vegas and Monte Carlo randomized algorithms with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The two main types of randomized algorithms differ in their correctness-vs-time guarantees:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Las Vegas:} \quad & \text{Output is always correct, but running time is a random variable.}\\
& \text{Example: Randomized Quicksort — always sorts correctly, expected time } O(n \log n).\\
\text{Monte Carlo:} \quad & \text{Running time is deterministic (polynomial), but output may be incorrect.}\\
& \text{Example: Solovay–Strassen primality test — runs in poly-time, correct w.p. } \geq 1/2.
\end{aligned}
$$
The key trade-off: **Las Vegas trades time certainty for correctness certainty**, while **Monte Carlo trades correctness certainty for time certainty**. Errors in Monte Carlo algorithms can be reduced exponentially by repetition (majority vote).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Approximation + Exact + Randomized**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of a **PTAS** and an **FPTAS**. How do they differ? Give one example problem for which an FPTAS exists and explain why an FPTAS cannot exist for certain problems (e.g., general TSP) under standard complexity assumptions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe **Karger's randomized min-cut algorithm**. What is the probability of success in a single run, and how is it amplified to high probability through repetition? What is the overall expected running time?

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**:

A **PTAS** (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme) is an algorithm that, for any given $\epsilon > 0$, produces a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation to the optimization problem in time polynomial in $n$ (but possibly exponential in $1/\epsilon$):
$$
T(n, \epsilon) = n^{O(1/\epsilon)}
$$

An **FPTAS** (Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme) is a stronger notion: the running time must be polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$:
$$
T(n, \epsilon) = f(1/\epsilon) \cdot poly(n)
$$

**Difference**: PTAS allows $n^{1/\epsilon}$, which is still super-polynomial in $1/\epsilon$; FPTAS does not.

**Example with FPTAS**: The **Knapsack Problem** has an FPTAS based on rounding profits and dynamic programming:
$$
\text{Time: } O(n^{2}/\epsilon)
$$
giving a $(1-\epsilon)$-approximation in truly polynomial time.

**Why FPTAS cannot exist for general TSP**: Sahni and Gonzalez showed that if TSP admits an FPTAS, then P = NP. The intuition is that an FPTAS for TSP could *distinguish* between instances with optimum 0 and those with optimum $\geq 1$, which is precisely the NP-hard Hamiltonian Cycle decision problem. The reduction preserves the approximation gap.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**:

**Karger's Min-Cut Algorithm** (1993):

The algorithm finds a global minimum cut in an undirected multigraph by **randomly contracting edges** that are *not* in the current candidate cut, and recursing.

**Algorithm**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text{1. While } \vert V \vert > 2 \text{ do:} \\
& \text{2. } \quad \text{Pick a random edge } e = (u, v) \\
& \text{3. } \quad \text{Contract } e \text{ (merge } u, v \text{ into a single vertex)} \\
& \text{4. Return the cut between the two remaining vertices}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Probability of Success**: A specific min-cut $C$ of size $k$ has $k$ edges. The total number of edges is at least $kn/2$ (every vertex has degree $\geq k$ in the contracted graph). The probability of *not* contracting an edge of $C$ in a single step is:
$$
\Pr[\text{avoid } C] = 1 - \frac{k}{m} \geq 1 - \frac{2}{n}
$$
Over the $n-2$ contractions, the probability of *surviving* is:
$$
\Pr[C \text{ survives}] = \prod_{i=2}^{n} \left(1 - \frac{2}{i}\right) = \frac{2}{n(n-1)} = \Omega(1/n^{2})
$$

**Amplification**: Run the algorithm $t = n^{2} \ln n$ times independently; by the **union bound**, the probability that *all* runs fail is at most:
$$
(1 - 1/n^{2})^{n^{2} \ln n} \leq e^{-\ln n} = 1/n
$$

**Expected Running Time**:
$$
T(n) = O(n^{2} \cdot n^{2} \ln n) = O(n^{4} \log n)
$$
which is polynomial and gives the *exact* min cut with high probability.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: FPT + Approximation + Randomized**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **Fixed-Parameter Tractability (FPT)**. State the formal running time and explain the concept of **kernelization**. Apply it to the **Vertex Cover problem** with parameter $k$ to derive a kernel of size at most $2k$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the four major approaches to solving NP-Complete problems — **Approximation**, **Exact (Exponential)**, **Randomized**, and **FPT** — using a comparison matrix based on the following criteria: (i) running time, (ii) solution quality, (iii) typical use case, and (iv) theoretical guarantee.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks**:

**Definition**: A parameterized problem $(I, k)$ is in **FPT** if it can be solved in time:
$$
f(k) \cdot n^{c}
$$
where $n = \vert I \vert$, $k$ is a parameter, $c$ is a constant, and $f$ is an arbitrary computable function.

**Kernelization**: A polynomial-time reduction from $(I, k)$ to an equivalent instance $(I', k')$ such that $\vert I' \vert \leq g(k)$ for some function $g$ (and $k' \leq k$). The reduced instance $I'$ is called a **kernel**.

**Vertex Cover Kernel of Size $2k$**:

Given a graph $G$ and a parameter $k$, we want a vertex cover of size $\leq k$.

**Reduction Rules**:
1. If $G$ has a vertex $v$ of degree $> k$: **$v$ must be in any cover of size $\leq k$** (else $k$ vertices cover $\leq k \cdot k < \deg(v)$ edges incident to $v$). So put $v$ in the cover, set $k \leftarrow k-1$, remove $v$ and its incident edges.
2. If $G$ has an isolated edge $(u, v)$ with $k \geq 1$: At least one of $u, v$ must be in the cover. Add both to a kernel list and remove them with $k \leftarrow k-1$.
3. If neither rule applies: every vertex has degree $\leq k$, and there are no isolated edges. Then if $\vert V \vert > k^{2} + k$, by the handshake lemma, the number of edges is at most $k \cdot \vert V \vert / 2$. Each vertex in any cover covers at most $k$ edges, so the cover must have size:
$$
\frac{\vert E \vert}{k} \geq \frac{?}{?}
$$
A careful counting argument shows that if $\vert V \vert > 2k$ and max degree $\leq k$, then we can find a matching of size $> k$, which means *any* vertex cover must have size $> k$. So if $\vert V \vert > 2k$ and no rule applies, **answer is NO**.

After exhaustive application, the kernel has at most $2k$ vertices.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks**:

**Comparison Matrix**:

| Criterion | Approximation | Exact Exp. | Randomized | FPT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Running Time** | $poly(n)$ | $O^{*}(c^{n})$ | $poly(n)$ expected | $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ |
| **(ii) Solution Quality** | Within factor $\rho$ of $OPT$ | Exact $OPT$ | Exact or near-OPT w.h.p. | Exact $OPT$ |
| **(iii) Typical Use Case** | Large $n$, need bounded error | Small/medium $n$ | Average-case hardness | Small parameter $k$ |
| **(iv) Theoretical Guarantee** | Worst-case ratio $\rho$ | Optimality certificate | High-probability bound | Parameterized tractability |
| **Example Problem** | Vertex Cover (2-approx) | 3-SAT $(1.307^{n})$ | Min-Cut (Karger) | $k$-Vertex Cover |
| **Limitation** | APX-hard problems | $n$ must be small | Probabilistic only | $k$ must be small |
| **Class** | APX, PTAS, FPTAS | XP, $O^{*}(c^{n})$ | BPP, ZPP, RP | FPT, $W[1]$-hard |

**Summary Insight**: The four paradigms are **not competing** but **complementary**. The choice depends on:
- The **size of the input** $n$ (favors approximation/randomized if huge)
- The **availability of a natural parameter** $k$ (favors FPT)
- The **need for worst-case guarantees** (favors approximation/FPT)
- The **tolerance for randomness** (favors randomized if acceptable)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams for this Topic**:
>
> 1. **Confusing PTAS and FPTAS (–1 mark)**: Students often state PTAS is "faster" than FPTAS. The opposite is true — FPTAS is *stronger* (polynomial in $1/\epsilon$).
>
> 2. **Mixing up Las Vegas and Monte Carlo (–1 mark)**: Las Vegas = *always* correct, random time. Monte Carlo = *always* polynomial, maybe incorrect.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "min vs max" convention in approximation ratio (–1 mark)**: For minimization, ratio = $ALG/OPT$; for maximization, ratio = $OPT/ALG$. Both must be $\geq 1$.
>
> 4. **Claiming FPT = Polynomial (–2 marks)**: FPT allows $f(k)$ which can be double-exponential. Do not equate the two.
>
> 5. **Omitting the parameter in FPT complexity (–1 mark)**: Always write $f(k) \cdot n^{c}$, not just $n^{c}$.
>
> 6. **Conflating $W[1]$-hard with NP-hard (–1 mark)**: $W[1]$-hard is a *parameterized* notion; NP-hard is classical. They are related but not identical.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **NP-Complete** problems have no known poly-time algorithms. Four paradigms offer escape routes.
- [x] **Approximation Algorithms** give poly-time solutions with provable ratio $\rho$. Classes: **APX, PTAS, FPTAS**.
- [x] **PTAS** runs in $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$; **FPTAS** runs in $f(1/\epsilon) \cdot poly(n)$.
- [x] **Exact Exponential Algorithms** solve optimally in $O^{*}(c^{n})$ with $c < $ brute force.
- [x] **$O^{*}(\cdot)$** notation suppresses polynomial factors; the *base* is the key measure.
- [x] **Randomized Algorithms** come in two flavors: **Las Vegas** (correct, random time) and **Monte Carlo** (random correctness, fixed time).
- [x] **Chernoff Bounds** amplify Monte Carlo correctness via repetition: $O(k)$ runs give error $\leq 2^{-k}$.
- [x] **FPT Algorithms** run in $f(k) \cdot n^{c}$ for small parameter $k$. They give *exact* answers.
- [x] **Kernelization** reduces an FPT instance to size $g(k)$ in polynomial time.
- [x] The **$W$-Hierarchy** classifies problems *not* believed to be FPT: $FPT \subseteq W[1] \subseteq W[2] \subseteq \cdots$.
- [x] **Karger's Min-Cut** is the canonical randomized algorithm: $O(n^{2}\log n)$ expected time.
- [x] **Vertex Cover** has a 2-approximation (tight) and an FPT algorithm in $O(kn + 1.2738^{k})$.
- [x] **Knapsack** has an FPTAS; **general TSP** does not (unless P = NP).
- [x] **Independent Set / Clique** are $W[1]$-hard — no FPT algorithm believed to exist.
- [x] The four paradigms are **complementary**, not competing — choice depends on $n$, $k$, and required guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Approximation Algorithms: Definition and Motivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Approximation Algorithms: Definition and Motivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Approximation Algorithm** is a polynomial-time algorithm that, for an optimization problem $\Pi$, returns a feasible solution whose objective value is provably close to the optimum. Formally, for a minimization problem $\Pi$, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ achieves an **approximation ratio** (or **performance guarantee**) of $\rho \geq 1$ if, for every valid input instance $I$ of size $n$,

$$
\frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \;\leq\; \rho
$$

where $\mathcal{A}(I)$ is the cost of the solution produced by the algorithm and $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ is the cost of an optimal solution. For a maximization problem, the inequality is reversed, and $\rho \in (0, 1]$ (or equivalently, we write the ratio as $1 / \rho \geq 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Definition)**
> An *$\rho$-approximation algorithm* is one whose solution value $S$ satisfies $S \leq \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ for minimization and $S \geq \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ for maximization. The algorithm must run in time polynomial in the input size $n$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving from Kottayam to Thiruvananthapuram during Onam rush. The *perfectly optimal* route (the one your GPS spent 30 minutes computing) is blocked by a massive traffic jam. So your GPS instead suggests a slightly longer but still very good route, maybe 15% longer, but it gets you there in time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> - An **optimal algorithm** = the absolute best possible solution, but may take astronomical time.
> - An **approximation algorithm** = a "good-enough" solution that is guaranteed to be within a small, provable factor of the best, computed in seconds.
> - The closer the approximation ratio $\rho$ is to **1**, the better the algorithm.

This trade-off — sacrificing a tiny amount of solution quality to gain enormous savings in computation time — is the very heart of approximation algorithms.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Them? (The Motivation)

Most natural optimization problems are classified as **NP-hard**, meaning that no known polynomial-time algorithm can solve them to optimality (assuming the widely-believed conjecture that **P $\neq$ NP**). Examples include:

- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)**
- **Vertex Cover**
- **Set Cover**
- **Knapsack** (decision version)
- **Maximum Independent Set**

For such problems, brute-force search requires time exponential in $n$, which becomes infeasible for even modest input sizes (e.g., $n = 60$ gives $\approx 10^{18}$ operations).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Stage Hierarchy of Algorithmic Response to NP-Hardness**
> 1. **Exact algorithms** (branch & bound, ILP) — exponential time, gives OPT.
> 2. **Heuristics** (genetic algorithms, simulated annealing) — fast, but *no guarantee* on solution quality.
> 3. **Approximation algorithms** — fast (polynomial), with a *provable mathematical guarantee* on solution quality.

Approximation algorithms occupy the sweet spot: they are mathematically rigorous *and* computationally efficient.

### 1.4 Performance Guarantee — the Central Promise

The defining feature of an approximation algorithm is its **provable performance guarantee**. For every instance, no matter how adversarial, the solution quality is bounded by the ratio $\rho$. This is a **worst-case guarantee**, which is much stronger than empirical "it worked well on my test cases" claims of heuristics.

A second important metric is the **asymptotic approximation ratio**, which considers the ratio as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\rho^{\infty} \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \sup_{I \,:\, \vert I \vert = n} \frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Categories of Approximation Algorithms

Approximation algorithms are typically classified by the tightness of their guarantee. The hierarchy, from weakest to strongest, is:

- **Constant-factor approximation:** $\rho$ is a fixed constant independent of $n$ (e.g., $\rho = 2$ for Vertex Cover).
- **Logarithmic-factor approximation:** $\rho = O(\log n)$ (e.g., Set Cover).
- **Polynomial-factor approximation:** $\rho = O(n^{c})$ for some constant $c$.
- **Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS):** For every $\epsilon > 0$, produces a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation in time polynomial in $n$ (but the exponent may depend on $1/\epsilon$).
- **Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS):** Same as PTAS, but the running time is polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam**
> The sequence FPTAS $\subset$ PTAS $\subset$ APX $\subset$ NPO forms a strict containment chain (under standard complexity assumptions). FPTAS algorithms are the "gold standard" — Knapsack famously admits an FPTAS, while Vertex Cover only admits a 2-approximation (and no better under Unique Games Conjecture).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Why Approximation Works

1. **Lower Bound Construction:** We establish a lower bound $L(I) \leq \mathrm{OPT}(I)$ using a clever relaxation (e.g., LP relaxation, greedy potential, or local search invariant).
2. **Algorithmic Construction:** We build a feasible solution of cost $C(I)$ using the lower bound as a guide.
3. **Ratio Bound:** We prove that $C(I) / L(I) \leq \rho$, which implies $C(I) \leq \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}(I)$ (since $L \leq \mathrm{OPT}$).
4. **Polynomial Running Time:** We verify that all steps run in $\mathrm{poly}(n)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Metric | Formula or Bound | Problem Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Approximation Ratio (min) | $\rho = \max \dfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}$ | Minimization | $\rho \geq 1$ |
| Approximation Ratio (max) | $\rho = \min \dfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}$ | Maximization | $0 < \rho \leq 1$ |
| Asymptotic Ratio | $\rho^{\infty} = \limsup \dfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)}$ | General | As $n \to \infty$ |
| Additive Gap | $\mathcal{A}(I) - \mathrm{OPT}(I) \leq c$ | Special | Used in scheduling |
| PTAS Running Time | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ | PTAS class | Polynomial in $n$ |
| FPTAS Running Time | $\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)$ | FPTAS class | Strongest scheme |
| Inapproximability | $\rho \geq c$ for some $c$ | Hard problems | PCP-based lower bounds |
| Greedy Bound (Set Cover) | $\rho = H(d) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \tfrac{1}{i}$ | Set Cover | $d$ = max set size |
| Metric TSP Bound | $\rho = 2$ (Christofides) | Metric TSP | Open problem for general |
| Vertex Cover Bound | $\rho = 2$ | Vertex Cover | Tight under UGC |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Design:** Routing, topology control, and fiber-optic link placement use approximations because exact optimization is intractable at scale.
- **Bioinformatics:** Genome assembly and protein folding rely on approximation algorithms for sequence alignment.
- **Operations Research:** Airline crew scheduling, supply chain logistics — problems with millions of variables.
- **Machine Learning:** Inference in graphical models and clustering (e.g., k-means is itself a heuristic with no guarantee, but $k$-median admits a constant-factor approximation).
- **VLSI Chip Design:** Wire routing and circuit partitioning are classical applications of approximation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Generic Approximation Ratio

Let $I$ be any input instance of a minimization problem $\Pi$. Let $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ be the optimum value and $\mathcal{A}(I)$ be the value produced by an approximation algorithm $\mathcal{A}$.

**Step 1: Definition of optimality.**
By definition, no feasible solution has value less than $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$:

$$
\mathcal{A}(I) \;\geq\; \mathrm{OPT}(I)
$$

This is trivial since the optimum is the minimum over all feasible solutions.

**Step 2: Definition of $\rho$-approximation.**
The algorithm is a $\rho$-approximation if it always satisfies:

$$
\mathcal{A}(I) \;\leq\; \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}(I)
$$

**Step 3: Combining the two inequalities.**
The two inequalities together yield the bounded range:

$$
\mathrm{OPT}(I) \;\leq\; \mathcal{A}(I) \;\leq\; \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}(I)
$$

**Step 4: Equivalently, the "gap" is at most a factor of $\rho$.**
Dividing by $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ (which is strictly positive for nontrivial instances):

$$
1 \;\leq\; \frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \;\leq\; \rho
$$

This shows that the *multiplicative gap* between the algorithm and the optimum is at most $\rho$.

**Step 5: Tightness (Lower Bound on $\rho$).**
A lower bound proof (inapproximability) shows that for *any* polynomial-time algorithm $\mathcal{A}'$, there exists an instance $I^*$ such that:

$$
\frac{\mathcal{A}'(I^*)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I^*)} \;\geq\; \rho_{\min}
$$

This is typically proven using **PCP (Probabilistically Checkable Proof) theorems** and gap-producing reductions.

### 3.2 Worked Example: A Naive "Half-Approximation" for a Generic Problem

Consider an optimization problem where a clever observation shows that *any* feasible solution $S$ can be "split" into two complementary halves, one of which is always $\leq \mathrm{OPT}(I)/2$ in cost. We can show:

**Step 1:** Let $S$ be a feasible solution of cost $C(S)$. Decompose $S = S_1 \cup S_2$ with $C(S_1) \leq C(S_2)$.

**Step 2:** Since $C(S_1) + C(S_2) = C(S)$ and $C(S_1) \leq C(S_2)$, we have:

$$
C(S_1) \;\leq\; \frac{C(S)}{2} \;\leq\; \frac{\rho_0 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}(I)}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Returning the cheaper half $S_1$ gives cost at most $\rho_0 / 2$ times the optimum.

**Step 4:** If $\rho_0 = 2$ (i.e., we had a 2-approximation before splitting), then the new algorithm achieves $\rho = 1$, which is *exact*. This is the core idea behind many **local search** and **randomized rounding** arguments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Analogy for the Derivation**
> The "splitting trick" is exactly how Google's *MapReduce shuffle-and-pick-cheaper* reduces cost in distributed computing: it always keeps the better of two parallel computations, halving the worst-case ratio.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Common Approximation Ratios

| Problem | Best Known $\rho$ | Lower Bound (Inapproximability) | Open Gap? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vertex Cover | $2 - o(1)$ | $1.36$ (UGC) | Yes |
| Metric TSP | $3/2$ (Christofides) | $\geq 1.0045$ | Yes |
| Set Cover | $H(d) \approx \ln d$ | $\Omega(\log d)$ (tight) | No (asymptotic) |
| Knapsack | $1 + \epsilon$ (FPTAS) | $1 + o(1)$ | No |
| Max Cut | $0.8785$ (Goemans–Williamson) | $0.9411$ (Håstad) | Yes |
| Facility Location | $1.488$ | $1.463$ | Yes |
| Steiner Tree | $\ln(4) \approx 1.39$ | $1.01$ | Yes |
| Satisfiability (MAX-3SAT) | $7/8$ | $7/8$ (tight) | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Choosing an Algorithmic Strategy

The diagram below shows how a practitioner decides whether to use an exact algorithm, an approximation algorithm, or a heuristic, based on the problem class and the required guarantee.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Optimization Problem] --> B{Is the problem in P?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Exact Polynomial Algorithm]
    B -- No --> D{Is the problem NP-hard?}
    D -- No --> E[Use Exact Algorithm with Exponential Worst Case]
    D -- Yes --> F{Is a small provable error acceptable?}
    F -- No --> G[Use Heuristic with Empirical Validation]
    F -- Yes --> H{Is epsilon-parameterization meaningful?}
    H -- No --> I[Use rho-Approximation Algorithm]
    H -- Yes --> J{Need runtime poly in 1/epsilon?}
    J -- No --> K[Use PTAS]
    J -- Yes --> L[Use FPTAS]
    I --> M[Output: Feasible Solution with Bounded Ratio]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    C --> N[Output: Optimal Solution]
    E --> N
    G --> O[Output: Best Effort Solution]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Approximation Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Input Instance I] --> nodeB[Phase 1: Relaxation Module]
    nodeB --> nodeC[LP Relaxation or Combinatorial Lower Bound]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Phase 2: Rounding Module]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Convert Fractional to Integral Solution]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Phase 3: Feasibility Repair]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Ensure All Constraints Satisfied]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Output: Feasible Solution S]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Verification: Compute S and Compare to OPT]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Performance Ratio Check: S divided by OPT is less than or equal to rho]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Ratio within bound?}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeL[Accept and Return]
    nodeK -- No --> nodeM[Reject or Refine]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: The Hierarchy of Approximation Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Optimization Problems NPO] --> nodeQ[APX: Constant Factor]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[PTAS: 1 plus epsilon for any epsilon]
    nodeR --> nodeS[FPTAS: Fully Polynomial Time]
    nodeP --> nodeT[Poly-APX: Polynomial Factor]
    nodeP --> nodeU[Log-APX: Logarithmic Factor]
    nodeQ --> nodeV[Tight Lower Bounds from PCP Theorem]
    nodeR --> nodeV
    nodeS --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeW[Inapproximability Frontier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**
> - FPTAS is the *strongest* class: the runtime is polynomial in both $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.
> - APX contains problems with constant-factor approximations (e.g., Vertex Cover, Metric TSP).
> - Log-APX and Poly-APX represent weaker guarantees for problems where constant-factor approximations are impossible.
> - PCP-based lower bounds (e.g., Håstad's 3-bit PCP theorem) define the **inapproximability frontier** — the boundary below which no polynomial-time approximation can exist (assuming P $\neq$ NP).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*Define an approximation algorithm. What is meant by the approximation ratio?*

**Model Answer:**

An approximation algorithm is a polynomial-time algorithm that produces a feasible solution for an optimization problem whose objective value is provably close to the optimum.

The approximation ratio $\rho$ is the multiplicative factor bounding the gap between the algorithm's output and the optimum:

- For minimization: $\rho = \max_{I} \dfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \geq 1$.
- For maximization: $\rho = \min_{I} \dfrac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\mathrm{OPT}(I)} \leq 1$.

A smaller $\rho$ (closer to 1) indicates a better approximation.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for ratio definition, 1 for direction of inequality.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

*Distinguish between PTAS and FPTAS. Give one example of a problem admitting an FPTAS.*

**Model Answer:**

- **PTAS (Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme):** For any $\epsilon > 0$, the algorithm returns a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation in time polynomial in $n$, but the exponent of $n$ may depend on $1/\epsilon$.
- **FPTAS (Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme):** The runtime is polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$ — i.e., $T(n, \epsilon) = \mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)$.

**Example:** The **0/1 Knapsack** problem admits an FPTAS based on dynamic programming with rounded profit values.

*(3 marks — 1 for each distinction, 1 for example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define an approximation algorithm rigorously. Explain with a neat diagram the relationship between P, NP, NP-hard, and NP-complete classes, and indicate where approximation algorithms fit into this landscape.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For the **Vertex Cover** problem, describe the standard 2-approximation algorithm based on maximal matching. Prove formally that it achieves a ratio of exactly 2.

**Model Solution (a):**

A $\rho$-approximation algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ for a minimization problem $\Pi$ is a polynomial-time algorithm such that for every instance $I$:

$$
\mathcal{A}(I) \;\leq\; \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}(I)
$$

where $\mathcal{A}(I)$ is the cost of the algorithm's solution and $\mathrm{OPT}(I)$ is the optimum cost.

*Landscape diagram (textual rendering):*

- **P** (polynomial-time solvable) — sits at the base; for these, we use exact algorithms.
- **NP** — decision problems with polynomial-time verifiable certificates; includes P.
- **NP-complete** — hardest problems in NP (Cook–Levin theorem: SAT is NP-complete).
- **NP-hard** — at least as hard as NP-complete, but may not be in NP (includes all optimization versions of NP-complete problems).
- **Approximation algorithms** target the **NP-hard optimization problems** lying *above* P but *within* feasible reach of polynomial-time computable guarantees.

The approximation hierarchy **FPTAS $\subseteq$ PTAS $\subseteq$ APX $\subseteq$ NPO** describes increasingly weaker classes of approximability.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Algorithm (Maximal Matching for Vertex Cover):**

1. Start with $C \leftarrow \emptyset$.
2. While there is an uncovered edge $(u, v)$:
   - Add both $u$ and $v$ to $C$.
   - Remove all edges incident to $u$ or $v$.
3. Return $C$.

**Proof of 2-Approximation:**

- Let $M$ be the set of edges added in Step 2. Since $M$ is a **maximal matching** (no edge can be added without violating the matching property), every edge of the graph has at least one endpoint matched in $M$.
- The algorithm picks $2 \cdot \vert M \vert$ vertices, so $\vert C \vert = 2 \vert M \vert$.
- Any vertex cover must contain **at least one endpoint** of every edge in $M$ (since edges in a matching share no vertices). Thus $\mathrm{OPT} \geq \vert M \vert$.
- Combining: $\vert C \vert = 2 \vert M \vert \leq 2 \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$.
- Therefore the ratio is exactly 2.

*(14 marks: 7 for part (a) — 2 definition, 2 diagram, 3 landscape explanation; 7 for part (b) — 2 algorithm, 5 proof.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* What is a Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS)? Explain the difference between PTAS and FPTAS. Show that the knapsack problem admits an FPTAS using profit-rounding dynamic programming.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Define the approximation ratio formally. State and prove the inapproximability result for the **General TSP** (i.e., TSP without triangle inequality).

**Model Solution (a):**

A **PTAS** is a family of algorithms $\{\mathcal{A}_\epsilon\}_{\epsilon > 0}$ such that for every $\epsilon > 0$, $\mathcal{A}_\epsilon$ is a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation running in time polynomial in $n$.

An **FPTAS** is a PTAS whose runtime is bounded by a polynomial in $n$ **and** $1/\epsilon$, i.e., $T(n, \epsilon) = \mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)$.

**Knapsack FPTAS Sketch:**

Given items with profits $p_i$ and weights $w_i$, capacity $W$, and target $\epsilon > 0$:

1. Let $P = \sum_i p_i$. Scale profits: $p'_i = \lfloor p_i \cdot n / (\epsilon P) \rfloor$.
2. Run dynamic programming on the scaled profits (table size $O(n^2 / \epsilon)$).
3. Recover the original solution and return it.

The rounding error is at most $n \cdot (\epsilon P / n) = \epsilon P \leq \epsilon \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$, giving a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation. Runtime is $O(n^2 / \epsilon)$, polynomial in both $n$ and $1/\epsilon$.

**Model Solution (b):**

The **approximation ratio** is defined as in Part A.

**Inapproximability of General TSP:**

- *Claim:* For any polynomial-time computable function $\rho(n)$, there is no polynomial-time $\rho(n)$-approximation algorithm for the General TSP, unless P $=$ NP.
- *Proof Sketch:* Reduce from the **Hamiltonian Cycle** problem. Given a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices, construct a TSP instance where edge weights are $1$ for edges in $G$ and $2 \cdot n$ otherwise.
  - If $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle, then $\mathrm{OPT} = n$.
  - Otherwise, every tour must use at least one "expensive" edge, so $\mathrm{OPT} \geq 2n - 1$.
- A $\rho(n)$-approximation algorithm with $\rho(n) < 2$ would distinguish these two cases, solving Hamiltonian Cycle in polynomial time, contradicting P $\neq$ NP.
- Hence, General TSP is **not approximable within any constant factor**.

*(14 marks: 7 for part (a) — 2 PTAS, 2 FPTAS, 3 Knapsack; 7 for part (b) — 2 ratio, 5 inapproximability proof.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Direction of the inequality is the #1 mistake.** Many students write $\mathcal{A}(I) \geq \rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ for minimization. *Always verify* whether the problem is minimization or maximization before writing the bound.
> 2. **Confusing $\rho$ with $1/\rho$.** Some textbooks use the convention $\rho = 1.5$ meaning "within 50% of optimum," while others use $0.67$ meaning "achieves 67% of optimum." Stick to the KTU convention: $\rho \geq 1$ for minimization, $\rho \leq 1$ for maximization.
> 3. **Forgetting to state polynomial running time.** An algorithm is *not* an approximation algorithm unless it runs in $\mathrm{poly}(n)$. Exponential-time "approximations" are just heuristics.
> 4. **PCP-based inapproximability proofs are easy to hand-wave incorrectly.** Be careful to clearly state the reduction and the gap it produces.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A $\rho$-approximation algorithm runs in polynomial time and produces a solution of cost at most $\rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ (min) or at least $\rho \cdot \mathrm{OPT}$ (max).
- **Motivation:** NP-hard problems lack polynomial-time exact algorithms; approximation offers a mathematically rigorous way to obtain near-optimal solutions efficiently.
- **Approximation Ratio** is the multiplicative bound on solution quality; smaller (closer to 1) is better.
- **PTAS** = $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation in $\mathrm{poly}(n)$ time.
- **FPTAS** = PTAS with runtime $\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)$ — the strongest scheme.
- **Constant-factor approximations** are achievable for many problems (Vertex Cover: 2, Metric TSP: 3/2, Set Cover: $H(d)$).
- **Inapproximability** (PCP-based) shows that some problems cannot be approximated within any constant factor (e.g., General TSP, Independent Set).
- **Hierarchy:** FPTAS $\subset$ PTAS $\subset$ APX $\subset$ NPO (under standard assumptions).
- **Key algorithms to know for KTU exams:** Vertex Cover via maximal matching (2-approx), Set Cover via greedy ($H(d)$-approx), Christofides for Metric TSP (3/2-approx), Goemans–Williamson for Max Cut (0.878-approx), Knapsack FPTAS via profit-rounding.
- **Worst-case guarantee** distinguishes approximation from heuristics — never say "works well in practice" without a proof.
- **Asymptotic vs. absolute ratio:** Asymptotic considers $n \to \infty$; absolute holds for *every* instance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximation Ratio: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Approximation Ratio: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the theory of approximation algorithms, the **Approximation Ratio** (also called the *performance guarantee* or *approximation factor*) is a formal measure of how close the solution produced by a polynomial-time algorithm is to the optimal solution of an NP-hard optimization problem.

Let $\Pi$ be an optimization problem. For any instance $I \in \mathcal{I}$ of $\Pi$ with input size $n = \vert I \vert$:

- Let $A(I)$ denote the objective function value (cost or profit) of the solution produced by algorithm $A$ on instance $I$.
- Let $OPT(I)$ denote the value of the truly optimal solution.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form)**
> An algorithm $A$ is said to be a **$\rho(n)$-approximation algorithm** (or to have an **approximation ratio of $\rho(n)$**) if, for every input instance $I$ of size $n$, the following inequality holds:
>
> $$\rho(n) \geq \max\left\{\frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)},\ \frac{OPT(I)}{A(I)}\right\}$$

The function $\rho : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 1}$ is called the **approximation ratio** (or bound) of algorithm $A$.

## 1.2 Separate Forms for Minimization vs. Maximization

To avoid ambiguity, KTU examiners expect students to write the definition separately based on problem type:

**For a Minimization Problem (e.g., TSP, Vertex Cover, Set Cover):**

$$A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I) \quad \text{for all } I \in \mathcal{I}$$

**For a Maximization Problem (e.g., Max-Cut, Knapsack, Max-Clique):**

$$A(I) \geq \frac{1}{\rho(n)} \cdot OPT(I) \quad \text{for all } I \in \mathcal{I}$$

In both cases, $\rho(n) \geq 1$, and **smaller values of $\rho(n)$ indicate a better algorithm**. A value of $\rho(n) = 1$ implies that the algorithm always produces an optimal solution.

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Interpretation**
> The approximation ratio tells us the *worst-case multiplicative blow-up* (for minimization) or *worst-case multiplicative shortfall* (for maximization) that the algorithm can incur, measured against the optimum. It is a *guarantee* that holds for *every* instance — not just the average or typical case.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Plain-English Analogy: The Taxi Fare Problem**
> Imagine you are in a new city and need to reach the airport. You have two options:
> 1. Ask a local expert (the *oracle*) who knows the absolute shortest route, but charges you ₹1,000 just for the consultation — and you cannot afford him.
> 2. Use a ride-hailing app (the *approximation algorithm*) that is free and fast, but may give a slightly longer route.
>
> If the app *guarantees* that its suggested route is **at most 1.4 times longer** than the expert's optimal route, we say the app has an **approximation ratio of 1.4**. The closer this number is to **1.0**, the better the app mimics the expert. If the ratio is 1.0, the app is *itself* an expert — it always finds the shortest route.
>
> In NP-hard problems, the "expert" (OPT) cannot be computed in polynomial time for large inputs, so we settle for the "app" (algorithm $A$) with a provable ratio $\rho$.

## 1.4 Why the Ratio Must Be $\geq 1$

Since $A(I)$ is a *feasible* solution, it can never be *better* than the optimal one:

- For minimization: $A(I) \geq OPT(I)$, hence $A(I)/OPT(I) \geq 1$.
- For maximization: $A(I) \leq OPT(I)$, hence $OPT(I)/A(I) \geq 1$.

Therefore, $\rho(n) \geq 1$ is a *necessary property*, not a convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Definition

Let us dissect the definition layer-by-layer for crystal-clear KTU-board-ready understanding:

- **Instance space $\mathcal{I}$**: The set of all legal inputs to the problem (e.g., all graphs, all knapsack instances). The bound must hold for *every* $I \in \mathcal{I}$, not just selected ones.
- **Size function $n = \vert I \vert$**: The bit-length of the encoding. The ratio is allowed to depend on $n$ (e.g., $\rho(n) = 1 + 1/n$ or $\rho(n) = O(\log n)$).
- **Algorithm $A$**: A deterministic or randomized polynomial-time procedure. The time complexity $T(n)$ must be polynomial in $n$.
- **Worst-case quantification**: The "for all $I$" quantifier is the source of the guarantee. We say $A$ has ratio $\rho$ if the *worst* instance still satisfies the inequality.

## 2.2 Key Design Principles Behind the Definition

- **Why maximum?** The $\max$ operator unifies both problem types into one inequality — the ratio captures the worst deviation in *either* direction.
- **Why multiplicative, not additive?** An additive guarantee (e.g., $A(I) \leq OPT(I) + c$) is not *scale-invariant*. On large instances, a constant $c$ becomes negligible, but the optimality gap should grow with input size. Multiplicative ratios are dimensionless and scale properly.
- **Asymptotic variant**: Sometimes authors define the asymptotic approximation ratio $\rho_{\infty}$ for problems where the *constant* additive gap is unavoidable. We write $A(I) \leq \rho_{\infty} \cdot OPT(I) + o(OPT(I))$.

## 2.3 Common Special Cases Students Must Know

- **$\rho = 1$ (Exact):** Algorithm is optimal (e.g., Kruskal/Prim for MST, Dijkstra for shortest path, Bellman-Ford). For NP-hard problems, this is impossible unless P = NP.
- **$\rho = 2$:** 2-approximation (e.g., Vertex Cover via maximal matching, Metric TSP via MST doubling).
- **$\rho = 1.5$:** A tight 3/2-approximation (e.g., Metric TSP via Christofides' algorithm).
- **$\rho = O(\log n)$:** Logarithmic approximation (e.g., Set Cover via greedy).
- **$\rho = (1 + \epsilon)$ for any fixed $\epsilon > 0$:** Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS), e.g., Euclidean TSP.
- **$\rho = (1 + \epsilon)$ with runtime polynomial in *both* $n$ and $1/\epsilon$:** Fully PTAS (FPTAS), e.g., Knapsack.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Meaning | Constraint | Typical Form in KTU Papers |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A(I)$ | Cost of algorithm's solution on instance $I$ | — | Numeric value |
| $OPT(I)$ | Cost of the truly optimal solution | $\leq A(I)$ (min) / $\geq A(I)$ (max) | Numeric value |
| $\rho(n)$ | Approximation ratio as a function of input size $n$ | $\rho(n) \geq 1$ | Real number $\geq 1$ |
| Minimization ratio | $A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$ | $\rho \geq 1$ | Upper bound form |
| Maximization ratio | $A(I) \geq \dfrac{OPT(I)}{\rho(n)}$ | $\rho \geq 1$ | Lower bound form |
| Unified bound | $\max\!\left\{\dfrac{A(I)}{OPT(I)},\ \dfrac{OPT(I)}{A(I)}\right\} \leq \rho(n)$ | $\rho \geq 1$ | Always valid |
| Asymptotic ratio | $A(I) \leq \rho_{\infty} \cdot OPT(I) + c$ | $c$ is a constant | Used when additive gap is unavoidable |
| PTAS condition | $\rho = 1 + \epsilon$, runtime poly$(n)$ for fixed $\epsilon$ | $\epsilon > 0$ fixed | $n^{O(1/\epsilon)}$ |
| FPTAS condition | $\rho = 1 + \epsilon$, runtime poly$(n, 1/\epsilon)$ | $\epsilon > 0$ | $O(n^{3}/\epsilon)$ or similar |
| Tightness | $\rho$ is the *smallest* value satisfying the bound | — | "Tight" or "best possible" |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility of Approximation Ratios**
> In real engineering — VLSI chip routing, airline scheduling, 5G network slicing, cloud resource allocation, and logistics — exact NP-hard optimization is computationally infeasible. Engineers use approximation algorithms whose *provable ratio* translates directly into a **performance Service-Level Agreement (SLA)**. For example, a $\rho = 1.1$ guarantee in a data-center scheduler ensures that the latency is at most 10% above the theoretical optimum, a critical bound for real-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From "Algorithm Cost" to "Approximation Ratio"

The approximation ratio is not "derived" in the calculus sense, but it is *constructed* from the more elementary notion of *relative error*. We show this construction rigorously.

### Step 1: Define the absolute error

For a minimization problem, the absolute error of algorithm $A$ on instance $I$ is:

$$E_{abs}(A, I) = A(I) - OPT(I)$$

Since $A(I) \geq OPT(I)$ always, we have $E_{abs}(A, I) \geq 0$.

### Step 2: Define the relative error

The relative error normalizes the absolute error by the optimum:

$$E_{rel}(A, I) = \frac{A(I) - OPT(I)}{OPT(I)} = \frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)} - 1$$

### Step 3: Take the worst-case over all instances of size $n$

The "guaranteed" relative error of $A$ is the supremum:

$$E_{rel}^{\max}(n) = \sup_{I \in \mathcal{I}\,:\,\vert I \vert = n} E_{rel}(A, I) = \sup_{I\,:\,|I|=n} \left[\frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)} - 1\right]$$

### Step 4: Convert to the approximation ratio

By the equivalence

$$\frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)} = 1 + E_{rel}(A, I) \leq 1 + E_{rel}^{\max}(n) = \rho(n)$$

we obtain the final form:

$$\boxed{\ A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)\ \text{ for all } I \text{ with } \vert I \vert = n\ }$$

This shows that the **approximation ratio is exactly $1 +$ worst-case relative error**.

### Step 5: Derivation of the unified (min/max) form

Consider the two possible directions:

$$\text{Minimization:}\quad \frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)} \geq 1$$

$$\text{Maximization:}\quad \frac{OPT(I)}{A(I)} \geq 1$$

A *single* inequality that subsumes both is:

$$\max\!\left\{\frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)},\ \frac{OPT(I)}{A(I)}\right\} \leq \rho(n)$$

For minimization, the second argument is $\leq 1$, so the $\max$ is the first. For maximization, the first is $\leq 1$, so the $\max$ is the second. Hence this single expression is **problem-agnostic** and is the most general form a KTU answer should include.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: Verifying a Claimed Ratio

> [!NOTE] **Worked Example (Proof of $\rho = 2$ for the Greedy Vertex Cover)**
> **Claim:** The greedy algorithm that repeatedly picks an arbitrary edge and adds both its endpoints to the cover achieves $\rho = 2$.
>
> **Proof outline:**
> 1. Let $M$ be the set of edges picked by the greedy algorithm; these edges form a *maximal matching*.
> 2. The size of the cover produced is $A(I) = 2 \vert M \vert$ (each edge contributes 2 vertices).
> 3. Every edge of the graph is incident to at least one vertex in $M$ (by maximality). Therefore, *any* vertex cover must contain at least one endpoint of every edge in $M$, so $OPT(I) \geq \vert M \vert$.
> 4. Combining: $A(I) = 2 \vert M \vert \leq 2 \cdot OPT(I)$, giving $\rho = 2$.

## 3.3 Summary Table: Where Each Inequality Comes From

| Problem Class | Inequality Used | Lower Bound on $A(I)$ | Upper Bound on $A(I)$ | Ratio Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Minimization | $A \leq \rho \cdot OPT$ | $A \geq OPT$ | $A \leq \rho \cdot OPT$ | $\rho \geq A/OPT$ |
| Maximization | $A \geq OPT / \rho$ | $A \geq OPT/\rho$ | $A \leq OPT$ | $\rho \geq OPT/A$ |
| Unified | $\max(A/OPT, OPT/A) \leq \rho$ | $\rho \geq 1$ | — | Symmetric |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Approximation Ratio Definition

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how the *instance*, the *algorithm*, and the *optimizer* interact to produce the approximation ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Instance I of size n] --> B1[Run Algorithm A in polynomial time]
    A0 --> B2[Invoke Optimal Solver OPT exponential time]
    B1 --> C1[Algorithm Output A of I]
    B2 --> C2[Optimal Value OPT of I]
    C1 --> D1[Compute Ratio R equals A of I divided by OPT of I]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1{Is R less than or equal to rho of n for all instances?}
    E1 -->|Yes| F1[Algorithm A is a rho of n approximation]
    E1 -->|No| F2[Algorithm A is NOT a rho of n approximation]
    F1 --> G1[Smaller rho means better approximation]
    F2 --> H1[Need a larger rho or a different algorithm]
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy Diagram: Hierarchy of Approximation Qualities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start0[Approximation Algorithm Quality Hierarchy] --> L1[rho equals 1 EXACT OPTIMAL]
    start0 --> L2[rho equals 1 plus epsilon PTAS]
    start0 --> L3[rho constant FACTOR APPROXIMATION]
    start0 --> L4[rho equals O log n LOGARITHMIC]
    start0 --> L5[rho equals O n POLYNOMIAL]
    L2 --> L2a[FPTAS if runtime poly in n and 1 by epsilon]
    L1 --> note1[Achievable only in P class problems]
    L3 --> note2[Example rho equals 2 for Vertex Cover]
    L4 --> note3[Example Set Cover greedy]
    L5 --> note4[Example general graph Independent Set]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: How the Worst-Case Bound Arises

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inst1[Instance I1] --> alg1[A of I1]
    inst2[Instance I2] --> alg2[A of I2]
    inst3[Instance I3] --> alg3[A of I3]
    instDots[...] --> algD
    instN[Instance IN] --> algN[A of IN]
    alg1 --> sup[Take supremum over all instances of size n]
    alg2 --> sup
    alg3 --> sup
    algD --> sup
    algN --> sup
    sup --> final[Smallest rho of n such that bound holds for all I]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the diagrams**: In Diagram 4.1, observe the *parallel* execution of $A$ and $OPT$ on the same instance $I$. The ratio $R = A(I)/OPT(I)$ is computed and then checked against $\rho(n)$ over *all* instances. Diagram 4.2 places your algorithm in the quality hierarchy: aim for the *highest* box reachable for your problem. Diagram 4.3 emphasizes that $\rho(n)$ is defined via a *supremum* over infinitely many instances, which is why counter-example constructions are central to proving tightness in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]`**
> **Q1.** Define the *approximation ratio* of an algorithm for an optimization problem. Why is it always $\geq 1$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Definition: An algorithm $A$ is a $\rho(n)$-approximation if for every instance $I$ of size $n$, the cost $A(I)$ satisfies $A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$ (minimization) or $A(I) \geq OPT(I)/\rho(n)$ (maximization).
- **(1 mark)** Unified form: $\max\{A(I)/OPT(I),\, OPT(I)/A(I)\} \leq \rho(n)$.
- **(1 mark)** Reason for $\rho \geq 1$: Since $A$ returns a *feasible* solution, it cannot beat $OPT$, so $A(I)/OPT(I) \geq 1$ (min) or $OPT(I)/A(I) \geq 1$ (max). Hence the ratio is always at least 1.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Q1]`**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between the approximation ratio for a *minimization* problem and a *maximization* problem. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Minimization: $A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$, e.g., **Metric TSP** (Christofides' algorithm with $\rho = 1.5$) or **Vertex Cover** (greedy with $\rho = 2$).
- **(1 mark)** Maximization: $A(I) \geq OPT(I) / \rho(n)$, e.g., **Max-Cut** (randomized algorithm with $\rho = 2$, i.e., expected value at least $OPT/2$) or **Knapsack** (FPTAS with $\rho = 1 + \epsilon$).
- **(1 mark)** Commonality: $\rho(n) \geq 1$; smaller $\rho$ = better algorithm; $\rho = 1$ = optimal.

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 (Choice A)]`**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 marks] Define the *approximation ratio* $\rho(n)$ of an algorithm $A$ for a minimization problem $\Pi$. State and briefly justify the condition that the ratio is always $\geq 1$. Explain with a small example how the value of $\rho$ is computed from $A(I)$ and $OPT(I)$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Consider the **Vertex Cover** problem. Suppose on a graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, the optimal vertex cover has size $OPT(G) = 10$ and a particular heuristic algorithm $A$ returns a cover of size $A(G) = 18$.
- (i) Compute the approximation ratio of $A$ on this instance.
- (ii) If the algorithm is *guaranteed* to return a cover of size at most $2 \cdot OPT(G)$ for *every* graph, what is its approximation ratio $\rho$?
- (iii) Suppose another algorithm has $\rho(n) = 1 + 1/n$. Does it always beat the $\rho = 2$ algorithm asymptotically? Justify.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
- **(2 marks)** *Definition:* An algorithm $A$ is a $\rho(n)$-approximation for a minimization problem $\Pi$ if for every instance $I$ of $\Pi$ with input size $n = \vert I \vert$:
$$A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$$
- **(2 marks)** *Why $\rho \geq 1$:* Since $A(I)$ is a feasible solution and $OPT(I)$ is the minimum possible, $A(I) \geq OPT(I)$, hence $A(I)/OPT(I) \geq 1$. The inequality $A(I) \leq \rho \cdot OPT(I)$ can only hold if $\rho \geq A(I)/OPT(I) \geq 1$.
- **(2 marks)** *Computation example:* If $A(I) = 12$ and $OPT(I) = 4$, then $\rho(I) = 12/4 = 3$, meaning the algorithm's solution is 3 times the optimum on instance $I$. The approximation ratio of the algorithm is the supremum of $\rho(I)$ over all instances.
- **(1 mark)** Mention that smaller $\rho$ = better algorithm, $\rho = 1$ = optimal.

**(b) [7 marks]**
- **(i) [2 marks]** $\rho = A(G)/OPT(G) = 18/10 = 1.8$. So on this particular instance, the algorithm is a 1.8-approximation.
- **(ii) [2 marks]** If the guarantee $A(G) \leq 2 \cdot OPT(G)$ holds for *every* graph, then $\rho = 2$ is a valid (and tight, up to constant factors) approximation ratio.
- **(iii) [3 marks]** Yes. For $\rho_1(n) = 1 + 1/n$: as $n \to \infty$, $\rho_1(n) \to 1$, while $\rho_2 = 2$ remains constant. For any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$, $\rho_1(n) < 1 + \epsilon < 2 = \rho_2$. Therefore the $\rho = 1 + 1/n$ algorithm *asymptotically dominates* the $\rho = 2$ algorithm in the worst-case sense. However, note that this does not automatically mean it is "better" overall — we must also consider its *running time* and *practical tightness*.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 1, Q2 (Choice B)]`**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 marks] Define the *unified* form of the approximation ratio that works for both minimization and maximization problems. Show algebraically that this unified inequality reduces to the correct form for each problem class.

**(b)** [7 marks] A maximization problem has three test instances with the following data:

| Instance $I$ | $A(I)$ | $OPT(I)$ |
|---|---|---|
| $I_1$ | 20 | 50 |
| $I_2$ | 15 | 30 |
| $I_3$ | 8 | 10 |

- (i) Compute $A(I)/OPT(I)$ and $OPT(I)/A(I)$ for each instance.
- (ii) Determine the smallest $\rho$ such that the algorithm is a $\rho$-approximation under the unified definition.
- (iii) If the same algorithm is run on an *unknown* instance $I_4$ of the same size and returns $A(I_4) = 7$, what can you conclude about $OPT(I_4)$?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

- **(2 marks)** *Unified definition:* An algorithm $A$ has approximation ratio $\rho(n) \geq 1$ if, for every instance $I$ of size $n$:
$$\max\!\left\{\frac{A(I)}{OPT(I)},\ \frac{OPT(I)}{A(I)}\right\} \leq \rho(n)$$
- **(2 marks)** *For minimization:* Since $A(I) \geq OPT(I)$, the first term $A(I)/OPT(I) \geq 1$ and the second term $OPT(I)/A(I) \leq 1$. The $\max$ equals the first term, giving $A(I)/OPT(I) \leq \rho(n)$, i.e., $A(I) \leq \rho \cdot OPT(I)$.
- **(2 marks)** *For maximization:* Since $A(I) \leq OPT(I)$, the first term $\leq 1$ and the second term $\geq 1$. The $\max$ equals the second term, giving $OPT(I)/A(I) \leq \rho(n)$, i.e., $A(I) \geq OPT(I)/\rho(n)$.
- **(1 mark)** Conclude that the unified form is problem-agnostic and is the form recommended in KTU 2024 answers for full marks.

**(b) [7 marks]**

- **(i) [2 marks]**
  - $I_1$: $A/OPT = 20/50 = 0.4$; $OPT/A = 50/20 = 2.5$.
  - $I_2$: $A/OPT = 15/30 = 0.5$; $OPT/A = 30/15 = 2.0$.
  - $I_3$: $A/OPT = 8/10 = 0.8$; $OPT/A = 10/8 = 1.25$.
- **(ii) [2 marks]** The unified ratio per instance is $\max\{A/OPT, OPT/A\}$:
  - $I_1$: $\max\{0.4, 2.5\} = 2.5$.
  - $I_2$: $\max\{0.5, 2.0\} = 2.0$.
  - $I_3$: $\max\{0.8, 1.25\} = 1.25$.
  - Worst case: $\rho = \max\{2.5, 2.0, 1.25\} = 2.5$.
  - The smallest such $\rho$ is $\rho = 2.5$.
- **(iii) [3 marks]** Since the algorithm has $\rho = 2.5$, we know $A(I_4)/OPT(I_4) \leq 2.5$, i.e., $OPT(I_4) \geq A(I_4)/2.5 = 7/2.5 = 2.8$. So we can guarantee $OPT(I_4) \geq 2.8$. We cannot bound $OPT(I_4)$ from above using the ratio alone (the ratio only gives a lower bound on the optimum relative to $A$).

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
> 1. **Forgetting the "for all $I$" quantifier:** A ratio computed on *one* instance is **not** the algorithm's approximation ratio. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this. Always state "for *every* instance of size $n$".
> 2. **Mixing up min and max forms:** Writing $A(I) \leq \rho \cdot OPT(I)$ for a *maximization* problem will fetch zero credit on the bound.
> 3. **Omitting $\rho \geq 1$ justification:** Examiners often allocate a separate mark for explaining *why* the ratio is bounded below by 1. A bare inequality without this reasoning loses a mark.
> 4. **Confusing approximation ratio with running time:** The ratio measures *solution quality*, not algorithmic speed. Do not write "the algorithm has approximation ratio $O(n^2)$" — that mixes two unrelated complexity measures.
> 5. **Confusing "tight" with "best":** A $\rho$-approximation algorithm is *tight* if there exists an instance achieving $\rho$ *and* a matching hardness lower bound. "Best possible" requires citing a known inapproximability result (e.g., PCP-based).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Formula (Minimization):** $A(I) \leq \rho(n) \cdot OPT(I)$.
- **Core Formula (Maximization):** $A(I) \geq \dfrac{OPT(I)}{\rho(n)}$.
- **Unified Formula:** $\max\!\left\{\dfrac{A(I)}{OPT(I)},\ \dfrac{OPT(I)}{A(I)}\right\} \leq \rho(n)$.
- **Constraint:** $\rho(n) \geq 1$ always; $\rho(n) = 1$ implies optimal.
- **Worst-Case Guarantee:** The bound must hold *for every instance* of size $n$, not on average.
- **Quality Hierarchy:** $\rho = 1$ (optimal) $>$ $\rho = 1 + \epsilon$ (PTAS) $>$ $\rho = O(1)$ (constant factor) $>$ $\rho = O(\log n)$ (log) $>$ $\rho = O(n)$ (polynomial).
- **FPTAS Upgrade:** A PTAS whose runtime is polynomial in $1/\epsilon$ is an FPTAS, the strongest guarantee.
- **Asymptotic Form:** $A(I) \leq \rho_\infty \cdot OPT(I) + c$ is used when a constant additive gap is unavoidable (rare in KTU but worth knowing).
- **Common 2-Approximations:** Vertex Cover, Metric TSP via MST-doubling, Max-Cut (randomized).
- **Tightness:** A ratio is *tight* if there exists an instance for which the bound is *attained* (equality) or matched by a known lower bound.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state (a) the problem class (min/max), (b) the inequality form, (c) the domain of $I$, and (d) the condition $\rho \geq 1$. This four-part structure is what KTU 2024 examiners look for in a complete answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Techniques: Greedy Algorithms and Local Search - Basic Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Techniques: Greedy Algorithms and Local Search - Basic Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Approximation Algorithms — The Foundational Premise

An **Approximation Algorithm** is a polynomial-time algorithm that finds a solution to an NP-hard optimization problem whose value is provably close to the optimal value. Formally, for a minimization problem $\Pi$, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ has an **approximation ratio** (or performance guarantee) of $\rho \geq 1$ if, for every instance $I$ of $\Pi$:

$$\frac{\mathcal{A}(I)}{\text{OPT}(I)} \leq \rho$$

For a maximization problem, the ratio is defined as $\frac{\text{OPT}(I)}{\mathcal{A}(I)} \leq \rho$. The value $\rho$ is the **approximation factor**, and we say $\mathcal{A}$ is a **$\rho$-approximation algorithm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEITT751 — Module 1):** The two foundational design paradigms for constructing approximation algorithms are **(i) Greedy Algorithms** and **(ii) Local Search**. Mastery of both is mandatory for ESE and Module Internal assessments.

---

### 1.2 Greedy Algorithms — Definition and Intuition

A **Greedy Algorithm** builds a solution incrementally, making a sequence of irrevocable, locally optimal choices with the hope (and provable guarantee) that this sequence yields a globally near-optimal solution.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine you are packing a knapsack for a mountain trek. You have many items of different weights and values. A *greedy* hiker picks the item with the best *value-to-weight ratio* first, then the next best, and so on — without ever reconsidering earlier picks. The hiker never steps back to swap a low-value item out, even if a different combination could fit better. This mirrors exactly how greedy algorithms operate: **locally optimal, myopic, and irrevocable**.

Formally, a greedy algorithm is specified by two components:
- A **greedy choice property**: a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal (or near-optimal) solution.
- An **optimal substructure**: an optimal solution to the whole problem contains within it optimal solutions to subproblems.

---

### 1.3 Local Search — Definition and Intuition

**Local Search** starts from an arbitrary (often infeasible or poor) initial solution and iteratively improves it by moving to a "neighboring" solution that is strictly better, according to an objective function, until no such improvement is possible. The final solution is called a **local optimum**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a blindfolded hiker descending a foggy mountain. At every step, the hiker feels the ground beneath their feet and takes a step *downhill* in the steepest direction they can sense. Eventually, the hiker stops at the bottom of *some* dip or bowl — they might be at the lowest point in their immediate surroundings, but they cannot see whether a deeper valley exists beyond the fog. That resting point is the **local minimum**. The hiker's strategy is **local search**.

Formally, a local search algorithm is defined by:
- A **solution space** $S$ of feasible (or sometimes infeasible) solutions.
- A **neighborhood function** $N : S \to 2^{S}$ that maps each solution to a set of "nearby" solutions.
- An **objective function** $f : S \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$.

The algorithm moves from $s \in S$ to some $s' \in N(s)$ with $f(s') < f(s)$ (for minimization) until $f(s') \geq f(s)$ for all $s' \in N(s)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Greedy:** Greedy is *constructive* (builds the solution piece by piece from scratch). Local Search is *improvement-based* (starts with a complete but possibly poor solution and refines it).

---

### 1.4 Why Approximation? The Inefficiency of Exact Solutions

Many combinatorial optimization problems (Vertex Cover, Traveling Salesman, Set Cover, Knapsack) are **NP-hard**. This means, under the standard conjecture $P \neq NP$, no polynomial-time algorithm can solve them exactly. Hence, we settle for **approximation algorithms** that run in polynomial time and return provably near-optimal answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metric — Approximation Ratio $\rho$:**
> For minimization: $\mathcal{A}(I) \leq \rho \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$
> For maximization: $\text{OPT}(I) \leq \rho \cdot \mathcal{A}(I)$
> The smaller (or tighter) the $\rho$, the better the algorithm. A $\rho = 1$ algorithm is exact; a $\rho = 2$ algorithm is a *2-approximation*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Greedy Algorithm

A greedy algorithm is built on three pillars:

1. **Greedy Choice Property.** A global optimum can be reached by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice. We prove this typically by an *exchange argument* — we show that any optimal solution can be transformed to include the greedy choice without increasing cost.
2. **Optimal Substructure.** After making the greedy choice, the remaining subproblem is itself an instance of the same problem.
3. **Feasibility Invariant.** Each greedy step maintains a feasible (partial) solution.

**Operational Steps of a Generic Greedy Algorithm:**

- **Step 1:** Sort or rank candidates by a carefully chosen greedy criterion (weight, ratio, degree, profit, etc.).
- **Step 2:** Initialize the solution set as empty.
- **Step 3:** Scan candidates in order. For each candidate, if adding it preserves feasibility, **commit irrevocably**; otherwise, discard it permanently.
- **Step 4:** Return the accumulated solution.

The *irrevocability* is what distinguishes greedy from dynamic programming — there is no backtracking.

---

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Local Search Algorithm

A local search algorithm revolves around four design choices:

- **Initial Solution Generation:** Random, greedy-constructed, or problem-specific heuristic.
- **Neighborhood Structure $N(s)$:** The set of solutions reachable from $s$ by a small, well-defined perturbation (e.g., flipping one bit, swapping two elements, adding/removing one item).
- **Pivot Rule:** Determines the next move when multiple improving neighbors exist. Common rules:
  - *Best Improvement:* Pick $s' \in N(s)$ that minimizes $f(s')$.
  - *First Improvement:* Pick the first $s' \in N(s)$ found with $f(s') < f(s)$.
  - *Random Improvement:* Pick a random improving neighbor.
- **Stopping Criterion:** A local optimum is reached when no neighbor improves the objective. For some variants, a time bound or iteration cap is used.

**Key Limitation:** A local optimum is not necessarily a global optimum. Escaping local optima is the central challenge and motivates advanced variants (simulated annealing, tabu search, etc.), which are beyond the scope of Module 1.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Domain / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Approximation ratio | $\rho \geq 1$; smaller is better for minimization |
| $\mathcal{A}(I)$ | Cost of algorithm's solution on instance $I$ | Polynomial in $\vert I \vert$ |
| $\text{OPT}(I)$ | Optimal (exact) cost on instance $I$ | Possibly exponential to compute |
| $N(s)$ | Neighborhood of solution $s$ | Subset of solution space $S$ |
| $f(s)$ | Objective function value at $s$ | To be minimized (or maximized) |
| $\alpha(G)$ | Size of a minimum vertex cover of $G$ | Used in Vertex Cover examples |
| $\tau(G)$ | Size of a maximum matching of $G$ | Used in matching-based bounds |
| $\vert E \vert$, $\vert V \vert$ | Edge and vertex counts of graph $G$ | For graph problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pivotal Inequality Used Extensively in Module 1:**
> For the greedy Vertex Cover algorithm, the **lower bound** $\text{OPT}(I) \geq \tau(G)$ (size of a maximum matching) is the workhorse. It is a $\vert V \vert / 2$ lower bound on the optimal cover for any graph.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Techniques

- **Greedy algorithms** power **Huffman coding** (data compression), **Dijkstra's shortest path**, **Kruskal's and Prim's MST**, and **scheduling heuristics** in operating systems.
- **Local search** underpins modern **SAT solvers** (e.g., WalkSAT), **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **logistics routing** (UPS ORION, Google Maps refinement layers), and **neural network training** (stochastic gradient descent is, in essence, a local search in weight space).
- **Approximation algorithms in practice:** Google's MapReduce scheduling, airline crew pairing, network design (minimum spanning tree variants), and bioinformatics (genome assembly).

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, Module 1 expects students to (a) define these paradigms, (b) state the approximation ratio, and (c) demonstrate the analysis on a canonical problem such as **Vertex Cover** or **Set Cover**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Greedy Vertex Cover

**Problem Statement (Vertex Cover):** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, find a minimum-size set $C \subseteq V$ such that every edge in $E$ has at least one endpoint in $C$.

**Greedy Algorithm (max-degree greedy):**

- **Step 1:** Set $C \leftarrow \emptyset$, $E' \leftarrow E$.
- **Step 2:** While $E' \neq \emptyset$:
  - Pick the vertex $v$ in $V$ with the **maximum degree** in $E'$.
  - Add $v$ to $C$.
  - Remove from $E'$ all edges incident to $v$.
- **Step 3:** Return $C$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Approximation Ratio

**Goal:** Show that the greedy vertex cover returns a cover of size at most $2 \cdot \text{OPT}(G)$.

**Lower Bound on OPT:** Let $M$ be a **maximum matching** in $G$. By definition, the edges of $M$ are pairwise non-adjacent, and any vertex cover $C$ must contain at least one endpoint of each edge in $M$ (otherwise that edge would be uncovered). Since the edges of $M$ are disjoint, we need at least $\vert M \vert$ distinct vertices to cover them:

$$\text{OPT}(G) \geq \vert M \vert = \tau(G)$$

**Upper Bound on Greedy Output:** Observe the following invariant — at every step of the greedy algorithm, the vertex $v$ selected has *at least one* edge remaining in $E'$, namely the edge that justified its selection. We charge this edge to $v$. Since each edge is removed at most once, and each vertex added to $C$ is charged by at least one edge that gets removed, the number of vertices added is at most the number of edges removed before $E'$ becomes empty.

More precisely, the greedy algorithm returns a set $C$ such that $C$ is itself a vertex cover. A sharper bound uses the matching: the set of edges "killed" by each selected vertex contains a matching of size equal to the number of times the algorithm increased $C$ in the worst case. A clean bound is:

$$\vert C_{\text{greedy}} \vert \leq 2 \cdot \tau(G) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(G)$$

**Combining the inequalities:**

$$\vert C_{\text{greedy}} \vert \leq 2 \cdot \tau(G) \leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}(G)$$

Hence, the greedy vertex cover is a **2-approximation algorithm**.

$$\boxed{\rho = 2}$$

**Remark (Tightness):** This bound is tight. Consider a star graph $K_{1,n}$: one central vertex connected to $n$ leaves. The optimum cover is the single central vertex ($\text{OPT} = 1$). The greedy algorithm, on the other hand, may pick a leaf, then a leaf, etc., giving $\vert C \vert = n$. Wait — in the *max-degree* greedy, the central vertex is picked first (it has degree $n$), so this example is not tight for max-degree. The max-degree greedy is actually a 2-approximation and provably tight on bipartite graphs with certain structures. (A simpler *edge-pick* greedy that picks any edge and adds both endpoints is also 2-approximate and tight on disjoint edges.)

---

### 3.3 Local Search Analysis — Generic Bound

For a minimization problem with a polynomial-time-checkable neighborhood $N(s)$ and the property that every solution $s$ has $\text{OPT} \leq f(s) \leq \rho \cdot \text{OPT}$, the local search terminates in polynomial time. The bound on the approximation ratio is derived case-by-case.

**Generic Termination Argument:**

$$f(s_{t+1}) \leq f(s_t) - \delta$$

where $\delta > 0$ is the minimum improvement (or at least $\frac{1}{p(\vert I \vert)}$ for some polynomial $p$). Since $f$ is bounded below by $\text{OPT}$, after at most $p(\vert I \vert) \cdot (f(s_0) - \text{OPT})$ steps, no improvement is possible and we are at a local optimum.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Greedy vs. Local Search

| Aspect | Greedy Algorithm | Local Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution Construction** | Builds from scratch, one element at a time | Starts from a complete (often arbitrary) solution and refines |
| **Decision Finality** | Irrevocable — no backtracking | Revocable — can move to any neighbor |
| **Time Complexity** | Typically very fast (often $O(n \log n)$) | Depends on neighborhood size; can be exponential in worst case |
| **Quality Guarantee** | Proven via exchange argument or matroid theory | Proven by relating local optimum to global optimum |
| **Sub-problem Use** | Optimal substructure required | Not required — pure black-box improvement |
| **Typical Approximation Ratio** | Often 2, $O(\log n)$, or even better | Often 2 or problem-specific |
| **Example Problems** | Set Cover ($\ln n + 1$), Knapsack (PTAS), MST | TSP (2-opt, 1.5-opt via Christofides), Max-Cut, k-means |
| **Pitfall** | Greedy choice may lock in bad decisions | Local optima may be far from global optimum |

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Numerical Trace — Greedy Vertex Cover on $K_3$

Consider the triangle graph $G = K_3$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $E = \{(1,2), (2,3), (1,3)\}$.

- **Initial state:** $C = \emptyset$, $E' = \{(1,2), (2,3), (1,3)\}$.
- **Iteration 1:** All vertices have degree $2$. Pick vertex $1$ (any tie-break). $C = \{1\}$. Remove edges $(1,2), (1,3)$. $E' = \{(2,3)\}$.
- **Iteration 2:** Degrees: vertex 2 has degree 1, vertex 3 has degree 1. Pick vertex 2. $C = \{1, 2\}$. Remove $(2,3)$. $E' = \emptyset$.
- **Stop.** Return $C = \{1, 2\}$.

**Verification:** $\vert C \vert = 2$, $\text{OPT}(K_3) = 2$ (any two vertices cover all three edges). Hence the ratio is:

$$\frac{\vert C \vert}{\text{OPT}} = \frac{2}{2} = 1$$

Optimal in this case. The worst-case bound of 2 is only attained on adversarial instances.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Greedy Algorithm — Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Instance I] --> B[Initialize Solution S as empty set]
    B --> C[Sort or Rank Candidates by Greedy Criterion]
    C --> D{Candidates Remaining?}
    D -- No --> Z[Return Solution S and Stop]
    D -- Yes --> E[Pick Next Best Candidate c]
    E --> F{Adding c Preserves Feasibility?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Commit c: Add c to S Irrevocably]
    G --> D
    F -- No --> H[Discard c Permanently]
    H --> D
```

**Figure 4.1 — High-level control flow of a generic greedy approximation algorithm.** The crucial property is the **one-way arrow** from "Discard" back to "Candidates Remaining" — once discarded, a candidate is never reconsidered. This irrevocability is what defines the greedy paradigm.

---

### 4.2 Local Search — Neighborhood Traversal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Generate Initial Solution s0] --> Q[Evaluate Objective f of s0]
    Q --> R[Enumerate Neighbors N of s]
    R --> S{Any Neighbor s Prime with f s Prime less than f s?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Select s Prime using Pivot Rule]
    T --> U[Move: s becomes s Prime]
    U --> Q
    S -- No --> V[Local Optimum Reached]
    V --> W[Return s as Final Solution]
```

**Figure 4.2 — Local search traversal of the solution space.** Each node represents a candidate solution; the algorithm walks through the neighborhood graph $N$ until it reaches a sink (local optimum). The "Pivot Rule" choice inside node T critically affects both speed and final solution quality.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Combining Both Paradigms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Problem Instance I]
    end
    subgraph GREEDY [GREEDY STAGE]
        G1[Sort Candidates by Criterion]
        G2[Build Feasible Solution S]
    end
    subgraph LOCSEARCH [LOCAL SEARCH STAGE]
        L1[Define Neighborhood N of S]
        L2[Improve via Pivot Rule]
        L3[Iterate Until Local Optimum]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[Final Approximate Solution]
        O2[Bound: f less than or equal to rho times OPT]
    end
    I1 --> G1 --> G2 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> O1 --> O2
```

**Figure 4.3 — Hybrid architecture.** Many real-world approximation algorithms use a **greedy construction** to obtain a strong initial solution, then apply **local search** to refine it. Examples include Lin-Kernighan TSP heuristics and GRASP (Greedy Randomized Adaptive Search Procedures). The output layer is responsible for the formal approximation guarantee $\rho$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "design an approximation algorithm" in ESE, students who sketch both stages — greedy for initialization and local search for refinement — typically earn higher marks because it demonstrates awareness of practical engineering trade-offs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define an approximation algorithm. What is meant by an approximation ratio of $\rho$ for a minimization problem? Give one example.*

**Model Answer:**

An **approximation algorithm** is a polynomial-time algorithm that, for an NP-hard optimization problem, returns a solution whose cost is provably close to the optimum.

For a minimization problem, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ achieves **approximation ratio $\rho \geq 1$** if for every instance $I$:

$$\mathcal{A}(I) \leq \rho \cdot \text{OPT}(I)$$

**Example:** The greedy 2-approximation algorithm for the **Vertex Cover** problem always returns a cover of size at most $2 \cdot \text{OPT}(G)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a greedy algorithm and a local search algorithm. Mention one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Greedy Algorithm | Local Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Constructive, builds from scratch | Improvement-based, refines an existing solution |
| **Reversibility** | Irrevocable choices | Reversible moves within neighborhood |
