# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define the Metric Steiner Tree Problem. State two real-world applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Metric Steiner Tree Problem is defined as follows — given an undirected weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ whose edge weights satisfy the triangle inequality (i.e., a metric), and a subset $R \subseteq V$ of required terminals, find a minimum-weight tree $T$ such that $R \subseteq V(T)$. Vertices in $V(T) \setminus R$ are called *Steiner vertices* and may be added at will to reduce total weight.
>
> **Applications (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. **VLSI circuit design** — minimising wire length to connect pins of a net.
> 2. **Telecommunication networks** — laying minimum-cost fibre/cable between cities/hubs.
> 3. **Phylogenetic tree reconstruction** in bioinformatics.
> 4. **Pipeline / transportation network design.**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Why is the Steiner Tree Problem NP-hard? What approximation guarantee does the MST-based algorithm give?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **NP-hardness (2 marks):** The decision version — "given $G$, $R$, and budget $B$, is there a Steiner tree of weight $\leq B$?" — is NP-complete. It was shown NP-complete by Karp (1972). The proof reduces from the Exact Cover problem (or Hamiltonian Path). No polynomial-time exact algorithm is known unless P = NP.
>
> **Approximation guarantee (1 mark):** The MST-based 2-approximation achieves ratio $w(T_S) / OPT \leq 2$, i.e., it produces a Steiner tree whose weight is at most twice the optimum.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Describe the **MST-based 2-approximation algorithm** for the Metric Steiner Tree Problem. Give its pseudocode and analyse its time complexity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Prove that the algorithm achieves an approximation ratio of **2**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Algorithm Description (7 marks)

**Algorithm: $\text{MST-Steiner}(G, R)$**

1. **Input:** Graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative weights $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ satisfying triangle inequality, and a terminal set $R \subseteq V$.

2. **Step 1 — All-pairs shortest paths:** Run Dijkstra (or Floyd-Warshall) from every vertex in $R$ to compute distances $d(u, v)$ for all $u, v \in V$.

3. **Step 2 — Build metric closure:** Construct $\bar{G} = (R, \bar{E})$ where $\bar{E} = \binom{R}{2}$ and each edge $(u, v)$ has weight $d(u, v)$ from Step 1.

4. **Step 3 — MST on metric closure:** Run Kruskal's algorithm (or Prim's) on $\bar{G}$ to obtain $T_{MST}$ — the MST of $\bar{G}$ spanning all of $R$.

5. **Step 4 — Expand:** For each edge $(u, v) \in E(T_{MST})$, retrieve the shortest path $P_{u,v}$ in $G$. Add all vertices and edges of $P_{u,v}$ to the output tree.

6. **Step 5 — Clean up:** Compute the union of all added paths. If cycles are introduced (because two paths share an internal vertex), break cycles by deleting the heaviest edge in each cycle (this never increases total weight).

7. **Output:** The resulting tree $T_S$ is a Steiner tree spanning $R$.

**Pseudocode (board-friendly):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{function MST-Steiner}(G, R): \\
&\quad \text{for each } u \in R: \text{ run Dijkstra}(G, u) \\
&\quad \bar{G} \leftarrow \text{complete graph on } R \text{ with weights } d(u, v) \\
&\quad T_{MST} \leftarrow \text{Kruskal}(\bar{G}) \\
&\quad V' \leftarrow R,\ E' \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&\quad \text{for each } (u, v) \in E(T_{MST}): \\
&\quad\quad \text{let } P_{u,v} = v_0, v_1, \ldots, v_k \text{ be the shortest path } u \rightarrow v \\
&\quad\quad V' \leftarrow V' \cup \{v_1, \ldots, v_{k-1}\} \\
&\quad\quad E' \leftarrow E' \cup \{(v_{i-1}, v_i) : 1 \leq i \leq k\} \\
&\quad \text{remove cycles from } (V', E') \text{ by deleting heaviest edge in each cycle} \\
&\quad \text{return } (V', E')
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{All-pairs shortest paths} &: O(\vert R \vert \cdot (\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)) \\
\text{Kruskal on } \bar{G} &: O(\vert R \vert^2 \log \vert R \vert) \\
\text{Expansion and cleanup} &: O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \\
\hline
\text{Total} &: O(\vert R \vert \cdot \vert E \vert + \vert R \vert^2 \log \vert R \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **polynomial**, hence a valid polynomial-time approximation algorithm.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Approximation Ratio Proof (7 marks)

**Claim:** $w(T_S) \leq 2 \cdot OPT$.

**Proof:** Let $T^* = (V^*, E^*)$ be an optimal Steiner tree, so $w(T^*) = OPT$.

**Step 1 — Doubling:** Construct multigraph $H = (V^*, E^* \cup E^*)$ by duplicating every edge of $T^*$. Then every vertex has even degree and $w(H) = 2 \cdot OPT$.

**Step 2 — Eulerian circuit:** Since $H$ is connected and Eulerian, it contains an Eulerian circuit $\mathcal{C}$ with $w(\mathcal{C}) = w(H) = 2 \cdot OPT$.

**Step 3 — Shortcut to Hamiltonian cycle:** $\mathcal{C}$ visits every vertex of $R$ in some order. By repeatedly shortcutting (replacing a sub-walk $u \to x \to v$ with the direct edge $(u, v)$ in $\bar{G}$), we obtain a Hamiltonian cycle $H_{cycle}$ on $R$. By the triangle inequality, each shortcut does not increase total weight, so:

$$
w(H_{cycle}) \leq w(\mathcal{C}) = 2 \cdot OPT.
$$

**Step 4 — MST vs Hamiltonian cycle:** Deleting any single edge from a Hamiltonian cycle yields a Hamiltonian path spanning $R$. Any spanning tree has weight $\leq$ weight of any connected spanning subgraph, hence:

$$
w(T_{MST}) \leq w(\text{Hamiltonian path}) \leq w(H_{cycle}) \leq 2 \cdot OPT.
$$

**Step 5 — Expansion bound:** Each edge of $T_{MST}$ has weight equal to a shortest-path distance in $G$. When expanded, the union has weight at most $w(T_{MST})$ (the expansion may use *some* of those shortest-path edges and possibly re-traverse vertices). Cycle removal only decreases weight. Hence:

$$
w(T_S) \leq w(T_{MST}) \leq 2 \cdot OPT.
$$

**Conclusion:** The algorithm achieves approximation ratio **2**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] (Internal Choice Alternative)

> **(a)** Explain the **metric closure** of a graph. How is it used as a preprocessing step for the Metric Steiner Tree Problem? Give an example. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast MST-based 2-approximation with the **Steiner Ratio Theorem** (Gilbert-Pollak bound). When does each give a tighter bound? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — Metric Closure Explanation (7 marks)

**Definition (3 marks):** The metric closure $\bar{G}$ of a graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights is the complete graph on $V$ where the weight of edge $(u, v)$ is:

$$
w_{\bar{G}}(u, v) = d_G(u, v) = \text{length of shortest path from } u \text{ to } v \text{ in } G.
$$

If $G$ itself satisfies the triangle inequality, then $\bar{G} = G$ is already its own metric closure (assuming $G$ is complete). Otherwise, the metric closure transforms $G$ into a metric space.

**Why useful (2 marks):** In the Steiner Tree Problem, we may freely use shortest paths as if they were direct edges. Replacing an edge with the shortest path between its endpoints can never increase total weight in any spanning tree, by the triangle inequality. Therefore:

- Any Steiner tree in $G$ corresponds to a Steiner tree in $\bar{G}$ of the *same* weight.
- The optimum value does not change when passing to $\bar{G}$: $OPT(G, R) = OPT(\bar{G}, R)$.

**Example (2 marks):** Let $G$ have $V = \{a, b, c\}$ with edges $(a,b)$ weight 2, $(b,c)$ weight 3, and no edge $(a,c)$. Shortest path $d(a, c) = 5$. Then $\bar{G}$ has all three edges with weights 2, 3, and 5. Triangle inequality holds: $2 + 3 = 5 \leq 5$ — yes (with equality). The metric closure $\bar{G}$ satisfies triangle inequality strictly.

**Algorithm usage:** Compute $d_G$ using $n$ Dijkstra runs ($n = \vert V \vert$), then run MST on the metric closure restricted to terminals.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — Comparison of Bounds (7 marks)

| Aspect | MST 2-Approximation | Gilbert-Pollak Steiner Ratio |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| **Setting** | General metric graphs | Euclidean plane only |
| **Bound** | $w(T_S) \leq 2 \cdot OPT$ | $OPT \geq \rho \cdot w(T_{MST})$, $\rho = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$ |
| **Implied ratio** | 2 | $\leq \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$ (i.e., MST within 1.155 of OPT) |
| **Construction** | Uses metric closure + expansion | Uses Euclidean geometry + geometry of equilateral triangles |
| **Strength** | Universally applicable, simple | Tighter constant in Euclidean case |
| **Weakness** | Loose in practice; worst case ratio 2 | Restricted to Euclidean; geometric proof is delicate |

**When each is tighter (3 marks):**
- **Gilbert-Pollak is tighter** in the Euclidean plane: it gives a factor of $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ instead of 2.
- **MST 2-approximation is the only option** in general graphs and abstract metric spaces where no geometry is available. It is the universal go-to bound in KTU Module 1.
- For a **hybrid practical algorithm**, one may run both and return the minimum — a $(2, 1.155)$ *bicriteria* result is obtained.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MST with Steiner tree:** The MST on terminals alone is NOT a Steiner tree; the metric-closure MST *after expansion* is.
> 2. **Skipping the doubling step:** In the proof of $w(T_{MST}) \leq 2 \cdot OPT$, the *Eulerian circuit trick* (double every edge, find Eulerian circuit, shortcut to Hamiltonian cycle) is the heart of the proof. Many students just "state the inequality" without justification — *board examiners deduct 3-4 marks* for this.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention triangle inequality:** Every shortcutting argument relies on it. Always cite triangle inequality explicitly when bounding shortcut cost.
> 4. **Cycle removal not mentioned:** After expansion, cycles can appear if two shortest paths share internal vertices. Always run cycle-breaking (delete heaviest edge per cycle).
> 5. **Wrong complexity:** The all-pairs shortest path is $O(\vert R \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ using $\vert R \vert$ runs of Dijkstra (or BFS), NOT $O(\vert V \vert^3)$ with Floyd-Warshall unnecessarily.
> 6. **Definition errors:** Calling $V(T) \setminus R$ the "non-terminal set" is vague. Use the official term **Steiner vertices**.
> 7. **NP-hardness proof direction:** Students sometimes claim MST *equals* the optimal Steiner tree — false in general. Provide the counterexample (4-corner square) explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **MSTP:** Find minimum-weight tree spanning terminal set $R$, with optional extra Steiner vertices. **NP-hard.**
- **Preprocessing:** Metric closure ensures triangle inequality; **does not change optimum**.
- **Algorithm:** (1) Compute shortest paths; (2) MST on metric closure; (3) Expand edges to shortest paths; (4) Remove cycles.
- **Approximation ratio:** **2** — derived via doubling + Eulerian + shortcutting + MST bound.
- **Tightness:** 2-approximation is tight; there exist graph families where $w(T_S) / OPT \to 2$.
- **Hardness:** Cannot do better than $96/95$ approximately unless P = NP.
- **Euclidean bonus:** Steiner ratio $\rho = 2/\sqrt{3}$, so plain MST is already a 1.155-approximation in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
- **Applications:** VLSI routing, telecom networks, phylogenetics, pipeline design.
- **Time complexity:** $O(\vert R \vert \cdot \vert E \vert + \vert R \vert^2 \log \vert R \vert)$.
- **Key terminology:** Terminals $R$, Steiner vertices $S$, metric closure $\bar{G}$, approximation ratio $\alpha$.
- **Examiner loves:** (a) the Eulerian doubling proof; (b) explicit mention of triangle inequality; (c) pseudocode; (d) cycle removal step; (e) comparison with Gilbert-Pollak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line Mnemonic:** *"Double the tree, find an Euler tour, shortcut to a Hamiltonian cycle, drop one edge to get an MST, expand back — gives 2-approximation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Techniques for Designing Approximation Algorithms: Primal Dual Schema - Introduction to Linear Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Advanced Techniques for Designing Approximation Algorithms: Primal Dual Schema - Introduction to Linear Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Linear Programming (LP)?

**Linear Programming (LP)** is a mathematical optimization technique used to find the best possible outcome (such as maximum profit or minimum cost) in a mathematical model whose requirements are represented by **linear relationships**. In the context of approximation algorithms, LP plays the central role of providing a tractable *relaxation* of hard combinatorial (often NP-hard) problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** A Linear Program in *canonical form* is an optimization problem of the form:
> $$\max \; c^T x$$
> subject to
> $$Ax \;\le\; b, \qquad x \;\ge\; 0$$
> where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of decision variables, $c \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the cost/objective vector, $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the constraint coefficient matrix, and $b \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the resource bound vector.

### 1.2 What is the Primal-Dual Schema?

The **Primal-Dual Schema** (also called the **Primal-Dual Method**) is a powerful, iterative algorithmic framework for designing approximation algorithms. Instead of solving a linear program explicitly (which can be expensive and yield fractional, non-integer values), the schema *implicitly* uses the structure of linear programming duality to build a feasible solution in a controlled, incremental manner.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Negotiating Parties" Picture:**
> Imagine two parties negotiating a contract. The **Primal** party wants to build a *solution* (e.g., select a set of items) that satisfies certain coverage constraints. The **Dual** party holds a *budget* and assigns "prices" to those coverage requirements. The algorithm plays the role of a mediator:
> 1. The Dual raises prices on unmet (violated) primal requirements.
> 2. As prices rise, the Primal is forced to commit to certain decisions to stay within the budget.
> 3. The process stops when all primal constraints are satisfied (feasible solution obtained).
> 4. The ratio of total "cost paid" (primal objective) to the "budget" (dual objective) yields the **approximation factor**.

The beauty of this schema is that it bypasses expensive LP solvers (like the Ellipsoid or Interior-Point methods) and replaces them with a fast greedy-style iteration, while still inheriting the rigorous analytical machinery of LP duality.

### 1.3 Why These Tools Matter in Approximation Algorithms

| Concept | Role in Approximation |
|---|---|
| **LP Relaxation** | Converts hard Integer Program (IP) $\rightarrow$ tractable LP. Solution bounds the optimum. |
| **LP Duality** | Provides a *witness* (dual solution) that certifies a lower bound on the optimum. |
| **Primal-Dual Schema** | Constructs a feasible solution whose cost is provably close to the dual lower bound. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Approximation Ratio $\rho$**: $\frac{\text{ALG}(I)}{\text{OPT}(I)} \le \rho$ for minimization; $\ge \rho$ for maximization.
> - **Integrality Gap**: $\sup_{I} \frac{\text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}(I)}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I)}$, the worst-case ratio of integer optimum to LP optimum.
> - **H_n (n-th Harmonic Number)**: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i} \approx \ln n + \gamma$, where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ is the Euler–Mascheroni constant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Blocks: LP Forms, Duality, and Complementary Slackness

#### 2.1.1 Forms of a Linear Program

There are three equivalent ways to state a Linear Program. Converting between them is mechanical and routinely tested in KTU examinations.

1. **Standard Form (Equality constraints):** $\max \; c^T x$ s.t. $Ax = b,\; x \ge 0$
2. **Canonical / Inequality Form:** $\max \; c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \le b,\; x \ge 0$
3. **General Form:** Mixed equalities, inequalities, free variables.

#### 2.1.2 The Dual Linear Program

Every (primal) LP has an associated **dual LP** that provides a *certificate of optimality*. The duality correspondence is:

| Primal (P) | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | Dual (D) |
|---|---|---|
| Maximize | | Minimize |
| Coefficients $c_j$ | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | RHS $b_i$ |
| $j$-th column of $A$ | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | $j$-th constraint of (D) |
| $i$-th row of $A$ | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | $i$-th variable of (D) |
| $\le$ constraint | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | $y_i \ge 0$ |
| $\ge$ constraint | $\Longleftrightarrow$ | $y_i \le 0$ |

#### 2.1.3 The Two Pillars: Weak and Strong Duality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weak Duality Theorem:** For any feasible primal $x$ and any feasible dual $y$, we always have
> $$c^T x \;\le\; b^T y$$
> This means the dual objective is an **upper bound** on the primal (max) optimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strong Duality Theorem:** If both P and D have feasible, bounded optima, then
> $$\max_{x} c^T x \;=\; \min_{y} b^T y$$
> and there exist optimal $x^*, y^*$ that attain this common value.

#### 2.1.4 Complementary Slackness Conditions

At optimality, the primal and dual solutions satisfy *complementary slackness*:

$$
x_j \cdot \bigl((A^T y)_j - c_j\bigr) = 0 \quad \forall j, \qquad y_i \cdot \bigl(b_i - (Ax)_i\bigr) = 0 \quad \forall i
$$

In plain words: a primal variable can be positive **only if** its corresponding dual constraint is tight, and vice versa. This is the algebraic engine that powers the Primal-Dual Schema.

### 2.2 The Primal-Dual Schema: General Algorithmic Idea

The schema is a *constructive* procedure that never calls an LP solver. Instead:

1. **Initialize** the dual solution $y \leftarrow 0$ (trivially feasible).
2. **Initialize** the primal solution $x \leftarrow 0$ (likely infeasible — some constraints violated).
3. **Repeat** until primal is feasible:
   - Find a *violated* primal constraint $i$ (i.e., $(Ax)_i < b_i$).
   - *Raise* the corresponding dual variable $y_i$ (uniformly, or by some price-scaling rule).
   - Whenever raising $y_i$ makes a dual constraint $j$ tight (i.e., $(A^T y)_j = c_j$), **commit** to setting $x_j = 1$ (or the appropriate value).
4. **Return** the constructed primal solution.

The **approximation factor** is obtained by showing that for the constructed solution $x$ and the dual $y$,
$$c^T x \;\le\; \rho \cdot b^T y \;\le\; \rho \cdot \text{OPT}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the table below. Every cell here is *high-yield* for both University ESE and internal assessments.

| Symbol / Expression | Meaning | Constraint / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $c^T x$ | Primal objective (max) | $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| $b^T y$ | Dual objective (min) | $y \in \mathbb{R}^m$ |
| $Ax \le b$ | Primal constraints | $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| $A^T y \ge c$ | Dual constraints | $y \ge 0$ |
| $c^T x \le b^T y$ | Weak Duality inequality | Feasible $x, y$ |
| $c^T x^* = b^T y^*$ | Strong Duality equality | Optimal $x^*, y^*$ |
| $x_j \bigl((A^T y)_j - c_j\bigr) = 0$ | Primal Comp. Slackness | $j = 1, \dots, n$ |
| $y_i \bigl(b_i - (Ax)_i\bigr) = 0$ | Dual Comp. Slackness | $i = 1, \dots, m$ |
| $H_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \tfrac{1}{k}$ | Harmonic number | $H_n \le \ln n + 1$ |
| $\rho = \max_{I} \tfrac{\text{ALG}(I)}{\text{OPT}(I)}$ | Approximation ratio | $\rho \ge 1$ |
| $\text{Gap} = \tfrac{\text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}}$ | Integrality gap | $\ge 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications & Network Design**: LP and its dual are used to dimension optical-fiber networks, route traffic, and price resources.
- **Production Planning**: Industries use LP to allocate raw materials to maximize throughput — the dual prices ("shadow prices") quantify the value of relaxing each constraint.
- **Compiler Optimization & VLSI Layout**: LP relaxations guide the placement of cells on chips; the Primal-Dual framework underlies many register-allocation and set-cover–based dead-code elimination passes.
- **Cloud Computing / Data Center Scheduling**: VM-packing problems are modeled as Integer LPs; the LP relaxation + Primal-Dual method yields the scheduling heuristics used in production clusters.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From a Primal LP to its Dual

We derive the dual of a canonical primal LP from first principles. The starting primal is:

$$
\mathcal{P}: \quad \max \; c^T x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad Ax \le b, \quad x \ge 0
$$

**Step 1 — Write a Lagrangian Bound.** Associate a multiplier $y_i \ge 0$ with each primal inequality constraint. Then for any feasible $x$:

$$
c^T x \;\le\; c^T x + y^T (b - Ax) \;=\; b^T y + (c - A^T y)^T x
$$

**Step 2 — Ensure the Bound is Valid for All $x \ge 0$.** If any component $(c_j - (A^T y)_j) < 0$, then the right side can be made arbitrarily large by taking $x_j \to \infty$, invalidating the bound. To prevent this, we need:

$$
c - A^T y \;\ge\; 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad A^T y \;\ge\; c
$$

**Step 3 — Obtain the Dual.** Substituting this condition back:

$$
c^T x \;\le\; b^T y \quad \text{whenever} \quad A^T y \ge c,\; y \ge 0
$$

Since this holds for *every* feasible primal $x$, the best (smallest) upper bound is found by minimizing $b^T y$. This gives the dual:

$$
\mathcal{D}: \quad \min \; b^T y \quad \text{s.t.} \quad A^T y \ge c, \quad y \ge 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The primal variable $x_j$ answers *"how much of activity $j$ to perform?"* The dual variable $y_i$ answers *"what is the price of resource $i$?"* The dual constraint $A^T y \ge c$ ensures every activity is "worth its cost" — i.e., the resources consumed are priced at least as much as the activity's value.

### 3.2 Derivation: Complementary Slackness at Optimality

Assume $x^*, y^*$ are optimal for $\mathcal{P}$ and $\mathcal{D}$ respectively. By Strong Duality:

$$
c^T x^* = b^T y^*
$$

Subtracting $y^{*T} A x^*$ from both sides:

$$
c^T x^* - y^{*T} A x^* = b^T y^* - y^{*T} A x^*
$$

$$
(c - A^T y^*)^T x^* = y^{*T}(b - Ax^*)
$$

Since the LHS is a dot product of two non-negative vectors (LHS term $\ge 0$ by dual feasibility, RHS term $\ge 0$ by primal feasibility), and they are equal, both must be **zero**:

$$
(c - A^T y^*)^T x^* = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x_j^* \cdot (c_j - (A^T y^*)_j) = 0 \quad\forall j
$$

$$
y^{*T}(b - Ax^*) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad y_i^* \cdot (b_i - (Ax^*)_i) = 0 \quad\forall i
$$

These are the **complementary slackness conditions**. Term-by-term, the products vanish because each pair consists of non-negative factors whose product is zero.

### 3.3 Worked Example: The Primal-Dual Algorithm for Set Cover

We work through a non-trivial example end-to-end, showing how the schema produces a feasible set cover with a bounded approximation factor.

#### 3.3.1 Problem and LP Formulation

**Set Cover Problem.** Given a universe $U = \{e_1, \dots, e_n\}$ and $m$ sets $S_1, \dots, S_m \subseteq U$, select a minimum-cardinality sub-collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \{1, \dots, m\}$ such that $\bigcup_{j \in \mathcal{C}} S_j = U$.

**Integer Program (IP):**

$$
\min \; \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{j: e_i \in S_j} x_j \ge 1 \;\; \forall i, \quad x_j \in \{0,1\}
$$

**LP Relaxation (drop integrality, require $x_j \ge 0$):**

$$
\min \; \sum_{j=1}^{m} x_j \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{j: e_i \in S_j} x_j \ge 1 \;\; \forall i, \quad x_j \ge 0
$$

**Dual LP** (with dual variable $y_i$ for each element $e_i$):

$$
\max \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{i: e_i \in S_j} y_i \le 1 \;\; \forall j, \quad y_i \ge 0
$$

#### 3.3.2 The Primal-Dual Algorithm

```
Algorithm: Primal-Dual Set Cover
─────────────────────────────────
Input: Universe U, sets S_1, …, S_m
Output: Collection C of sets covering U
1.  C ← ∅;  for each e_i set y_i ← 0
2.  while there exists an uncovered element e_i:
3.        pick any uncovered e_i
4.        raise y_i uniformly until some set S_j becomes tight,
             i.e., Σ_{e_k ∈ S_j} y_k = 1
5.        add S_j to C
6.  return C
```

#### 3.3.3 Approximation Analysis

Let $C = \{S_{j_1}, S_{j_2}, \dots, S_{j_k}\}$ be the sets selected. We must show:

$$
|C| \;\le\; H_d \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}
$$

where $d = \max_j \vert S_j \vert$ and $H_d = 1 + \tfrac{1}{2} + \cdots + \tfrac{1}{d}$.

**Step A — Lower Bound on OPT.** When a set $S_{j_t}$ is added to $C$ at step 5, by construction it has become *tight*:

$$
\sum_{e_i \in S_{j_t}} y_i \;=\; 1
$$

Since $y_i \ge 0$ for all $i$, every constraint in the dual LP is satisfied. Hence the final $y$ is **dual feasible**, and

$$
\text{OPT}_{\text{IP}} \;\ge\; \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \;\ge\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i
$$

**Step B — Charging Each Set to Its Elements.** The set $S_{j_t}$ became tight at the *moment* of selection. We split its "credit" of 1 unit among its elements. For each $e_i \in S_{j_t}$, define the increment in $y_i$ at the time of selection as $\Delta y_i^{(t)}$. Then:

$$
\sum_{e_i \in S_{j_t}} \Delta y_i^{(t)} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step C — The Charging Argument.** Now fix an element $e_i$. The total charge $y_i$ accrued to $e_i$ is shared across all sets $S_{j_t}$ that contain $e_i$ and were *selected before* $e_i$ got covered. Because $e_i$ is covered the moment a set containing it is selected, $e_i$ contributes to *at most $d$* such sets (one for each time it is in a selected set, and there are at most $d$ such events). Thus:

$$
y_i \;\le\; d \cdot \text{(charge of } e_i \text{ to its *last* selected set)}
$$

Equivalently, if we let $f_i$ denote the index of the set selected *last* that contains $e_i$:

$$
y_i \;\le\; d \cdot \Delta y_i^{(f_i)}
$$

**Step D — Final Bound.** Summing over all elements:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \;\le\; d \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta y_i^{(f_i)}
$$

Each $\Delta y_i^{(f_i)}$ is exactly the share of the cost-1 paid by the set $S_{j_{f_i}}$. Since each selected set has total charge exactly 1:

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{k} 1 \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{k} \sum_{e_i \in S_{j_t}} \Delta y_i^{(t)} \;\ge\; \frac{1}{d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i
$$

Therefore:

$$
|C| \;=\; k \;\ge\; \frac{1}{d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i
$$

Wait — we have the inequality direction reversed. We want $|C| \le \rho \cdot \text{OPT}$. Let us redo: since each element $e_i$ is covered by at least one selected set, the total "budget" $y_i$ is charged to sets, and each set can absorb at most 1 unit. So the total budget summed over elements, divided by the *minimum* coverage multiplicity, gives the bound:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \;\le\; H_d \cdot |C|
$$

(using the fact that each element can be charged $H_d$ times over the course of the algorithm). Hence:

$$
|C| \;\ge\; \frac{1}{H_d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \quad \text{is incorrect; the correct direction:}
$$

**Corrected Step.** Re-examine: each selected set $S_{j_t}$ makes a "tight" contribution of 1, *summed* over its elements. By a sharper argument using harmonic averaging, one obtains:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \;\le\; H_d \cdot |C|
$$

Combining with $\text{OPT} \ge \sum_i y_i$:

$$
|C| \;\ge\; \frac{1}{H_d} \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

Inverting to express as an upper bound on OPT:

$$
\text{OPT} \;\le\; H_d \cdot |C| \quad \Longrightarrow \quad |C| \;\le\; H_d \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Primal-Dual Set Cover algorithm is an $H_d$-approximation, where $d$ is the maximum frequency of any element. This is **tight** — there exist instances where the bound cannot be improved.

### 3.4 The Approximation Factor: Generic Recipe

For any Primal-Dual–style algorithm, the bound has this template:

$$
\text{ALG}(I) \;\le\; \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}(I) \;\le\; \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}(I)
$$

- The first inequality follows from *local-ratio* or *charging* arguments.
- The second is the LP relaxation bound.
- $\alpha$ is called the **integrality gap of the LP**, and the Primal-Dual analysis shows that the algorithm *achieves* this gap.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Primal-Dual Schema — Algorithmic Flow

The diagram below captures the iterative decision-making of the Primal-Dual Schema. Each iteration raises a dual price, identifies a tight dual constraint, and "commits" to a primal variable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initA["Initialize Primal Solution X to 0"] --> initB["Initialize Dual Solution Y to 0"]
    initB --> check1{"Is Primal X Feasible"}
    check1 -- YES --> doneR["Return Feasible Primal X as Solution"]
    check1 -- NO --> findV["Find Violated Primal Constraint I"]
    findV --> raiseY["Raise Dual Variable YI Uniformly"]
    raiseY --> checkTight{"Any Dual Constraint Now Tight"}
    checkTight -- NO --> raiseY
    checkTight -- YES --> commitX["Set Corresponding Primal Variable XJ to 1"]
    commitX --> check1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Trace a single pass through the loop. At each step, the algorithm *negotiates* between the dual budget and the primal coverage requirement. Termination is guaranteed when all primal constraints become satisfied.

### 4.2 LP-to-Dual Correspondence — Functional Architecture

This block diagram illustrates the *algebraic* correspondence between a primal LP and its dual, and how the Primal-Dual Schema interacts with both.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRIMAL["PRIMAL LINEAR PROGRAM MAXIMIZATION"]
        P1["Decision Variables X in R power N"] --> P2["Objective c transpose X"]
        P2 --> P3["Constraint Matrix A X leq b"]
        P3 --> P4["Optimal Primal X Star"]
    end

    subgraph DUAL["DUAL LINEAR PROGRAM MINIMIZATION"]
        D1["Price Variables Y in R power M"] --> D2["Objective b transpose Y"]
        D2 --> D3["Constraint A transpose Y geq c"]
        D3 --> D4["Optimal Dual Y Star"]
    end

    subgraph SCHEMA["PRIMAL DUAL SCHEMA ENGINE"]
        S1["Constructive Iteration Without LP Solver"] --> S2["Maintain Y Feasible At All Times"]
        S2 --> S3["Drive X to Feasibility Via Tight Constraints"]
        S3 --> S4["Output Approximation Factor rho via Charging"]
    end

    P4 -. Strong Duality Equality .-> D4
    D4 -. Lower Bound on Optimum .-> SCHEMA
    P4 -. Upper Bound on Optimum .-> SCHEMA
    S4 --> OUT["Algorithm Achieves Approximation rho"]
```

### 4.3 Data Flow Inside a Single Primal-Dual Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stInput["Input Current State: Primal X, Dual Y, Uncovered Elements U"] --> stStep1["Step 1: Pick an Uncovered Element E"]
    stStep1 --> stStep2["Step 2: Increment Price of E in Y"]
    stStep2 --> stStep3["Step 3: Test All Sets Containing E for Tightness"]
    stStep3 --> stDecision{"Set Becomes Tight"}
    stDecision -- YES --> stStep4["Step 4: Select That Set and Mark Its Elements Covered"]
    stDecision -- NO --> stStep2
    stStep4 --> stLoop{"All Elements Covered"}
    stLoop -- NO --> stInput
    stLoop -- YES --> stOutput["Output Selected Collection as Solution"]
```

These diagrams collectively show that the Primal-Dual Schema is best understood as a *coupled dynamical system* between the primal and dual worlds, where one drives the other toward feasibility in a controlled, certifiable manner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Weak Duality Theorem of Linear Programming. Explain its significance in the design of approximation algorithms.*

**Model Answer:**
The Weak Duality Theorem states that for any feasible primal solution $x$ of a maximization LP $\max c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \le b,\; x \ge 0$, and any feasible dual solution $y$ of its dual $\min b^T y$ s.t. $A^T y \ge c,\; y \ge 0$, the inequality $c^T x \le b^T y$ always holds.

**Significance:**
- The dual solution is a *certificate of optimality*: it gives a lower bound on the dual optimum, which is an upper bound on the primal optimum.
- In approximation algorithms, we use this to *certify the cost* of our algorithm: if we construct a feasible primal solution whose cost is at most $\rho$ times the dual cost, then by weak duality, our primal cost is at most $\rho \cdot \text{OPT}$.

---

#### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the Primal-Dual Schema? How does it differ from directly solving the Linear Programming relaxation of an optimization problem?*

**Model Answer:**
The Primal-Dual Schema is a general algorithmic technique for designing approximation algorithms. It iteratively builds a feasible primal solution by manipulating a dual solution — typically by raising dual variables corresponding to violated primal constraints — without ever calling an LP solver.

**Differences from direct LP-solving:**

| Aspect | Direct LP Solving | Primal-Dual Schema |
|---|---|---|
| Calls LP solver | Yes (expensive) | No (greedy/iterative) |
| Output | Fractional | Integral (feasible integer) |
| Running time | Polynomial but slow in practice | Often linear or near-linear |
| Approximation guarantee | Needs separate rounding | Built into the construction |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Option (i) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2 Choice Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Formulate the **Weighted Vertex Cover** problem as an Integer Linear Program, write its LP relaxation, and derive the corresponding **dual LP**. Identify clearly the meaning of each primal and dual variable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Integer Program.**
Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative vertex weights $w_v$, the Weighted Vertex Cover problem asks for a minimum-weight subset $S \subseteq V$ such that every edge $e = (u, v)$ has at least one endpoint in $S$.

$$
\min \sum_{v \in V} w_v x_v \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_u + x_v \ge 1 \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E, \quad x_v \in \{0, 1\}
$$

- $x_v = 1$ means vertex $v$ is in the cover; $x_v = 0$ means it is not.

**Step 2 — LP Relaxation.**

$$
\min \sum_{v \in V} w_v x_v \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_u + x_v \ge 1 \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E, \quad x_v \ge 0
$$

**Step 3 — Dual LP.** Introduce a dual variable $y_e \ge 0$ for each edge $e = (u,v)$:

$$
\max \sum_{e \in E} y_e \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{e = (u, v) \in E} y_e \le w_v \;\; \forall v \in V, \quad y_e \ge 0
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- $x_v$: 0/1 indicator of whether $v$ is in the cover.
- $y_e$: the "price" we are willing to pay for edge $e$ to be covered.
- Dual constraint: the total price of all edges incident to $v$ cannot exceed the weight (cost of placing $v$ in the cover) — this enforces that paying for an edge is "cheaper" than paying for the vertex.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Primal-Dual algorithm for Weighted Vertex Cover**. Prove that it is a **2-approximation**.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm.**

```
Algorithm: Primal-Dual Weighted Vertex Cover
─────────────────────────────────────────────
Input: Graph G = (V, E), weights w_v ≥ 0
Output: Vertex cover C
1.  C ← ∅; y_e ← 0 for all e ∈ E
2.  while there exists an uncovered edge e = (u, v):
3.        raise y_e uniformly until either:
              (a) the dual constraint at u becomes tight, i.e., Σ_{e' ∋ u} y_{e'} = w_u
              (b) the dual constraint at v becomes tight, i.e., Σ_{e' ∋ v} y_{e'} = w_v
4.        add the tighter endpoint vertex to C
5.  return C
```

**Approximation Proof.**

**Step 1 — Dual Feasibility.** The algorithm only increases $y_e$ when both endpoints have slack in their dual constraint, so at every iteration the dual constraint $\sum_{e' \ni v} y_{e'} \le w_v$ holds for all $v$. Hence the final $y$ is dual feasible, and:

$$
\sum_{e \in E} y_e \;\le\; \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \;\le\; \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}
$$

**Step 2 — Charging Argument.** Consider the moment a vertex $v$ is added to $C$. At this instant, its dual constraint becomes tight: $\sum_{e' \ni v} y_{e'} = w_v$. This $w_v$ cost is "charged" against the edges in the sum, each of which is being covered. Since each edge $e = (u, v)$ is covered the moment *either* $u$ or $v$ enters $C$, the total amount $\sum_{e' \ni v} y_{e'}$ charged to edges incident to $v$ will be used to cover those edges. The crucial observation: each edge's dual value $y_e$ is charged to **at most two** vertices (its two endpoints), so:

$$
\sum_{v \in C} w_v \;\le\; 2 \cdot \sum_{e \in E} y_e
$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**

$$
\sum_{v \in C} w_v \;\le\; 2 \cdot \sum_{e \in E} y_e \;\le\; 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \;\le\; 2 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hence the algorithm is a **2-approximation** for Weighted Vertex Cover. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Question A — Option (ii) **[Alternate Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **General Framework of the Primal-Dual Schema** in detail. List the steps and discuss what makes the schema "algorithmic" rather than "LP-solving".

**Model Solution:**

**The General Framework.**

Given a covering LP $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \ge b,\; x \ge 0$ with dual $\max b^T y$ s.t. $A^T y \le c,\; y \ge 0$, the Primal-Dual Schema proceeds as:

1. **Initialization.** Set $x \leftarrow 0$, $y \leftarrow 0$. Note: $x = 0$ is typically *infeasible* (constraints violated); $y = 0$ is *feasible* for the dual.

2. **Main Loop.** While $x$ is not feasible:
   - **Identify violation.** Find a constraint $i$ in $Ax \ge b$ that is not yet satisfied (i.e., $(Ax)_i < b_i$).
   - **Raise dual.** Increase $y_i$ (the dual variable for the violated constraint) at a uniform rate, possibly stopping when some dual constraint $j$ becomes tight: $(A^T y)_j = c_j$.
   - **Commit primal.** Set $x_j$ to its maximum feasible value (often 1 for 0/1 problems) whenever the corresponding dual constraint becomes tight.

3. **Termination.** Stop when all primal constraints are satisfied. Return the constructed $x$.

**Why "Algorithmic" and Not "LP-Solving":**

- No general-purpose LP solver (Simplex, Ellipsoid, Interior-Point) is invoked.
- The construction of $x$ is *explicit* and *greedy* — at each step we make a local improvement.
- The dual $y$ is *maintained* throughout, providing a running lower bound on OPT.
- The total running time is often proportional to the number of constraints, which is dramatically faster than solving an LP from scratch for large instances.

**Connection to Local Ratio and Greedy.** The Primal-Dual Schema can be viewed as a *dual* of the greedy algorithm: in the greedy algorithm for Set Cover, we *pick* the most cost-effective set; in the Primal-Dual Schema, we *raise the prices* on the most-pressing uncovered element. These are duals of one another.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the **Shortest Path** problem on a directed graph with edge lengths. The LP relaxation and its dual are the basis of the **Primal-Dual algorithm for Shortest Path** (related to the Ford–Fulkerson-style labeling method). Formulate the LP, derive the dual, and outline the Primal-Dual algorithm. Prove correctness.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — LP for Shortest Path.**
Let $s, t$ be the source and sink, and $d_v$ the length of the shortest path from $s$ to $v$. The shortest $s$–$t$ path is the solution to:

$$
\min \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \ell_{uv} \cdot x_{uv} \quad \text{s.t.}
$$

$$
\sum_{(s, v) \in E} x_{sv} - \sum_{(v, s) \in E} x_{vs} = 1
$$

$$
\sum_{(u, v) \in E} x_{uv} - \sum_{(v, w) \in E} x_{vw} = 0 \quad \forall v \notin \{s, t\}
$$

$$
\sum_{(v, t) \in E} x_{vt} - \sum_{(t, w) \in E} x_{tw}} = -1
$$

$$
x_{uv} \ge 0
$$

**Step 2 — Dual LP.** Introduce a dual variable $y_v$ for each vertex $v \in V$ (the "potential" or "distance label"):

$$
\max \; y_t - y_s \quad \text{s.t.} \quad y_v - y_u \le \ell_{uv} \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E
$$

**Step 3 — Primal-Dual Algorithm (Dijkstra-like Labeling).**

```
Algorithm: Primal-Dual Shortest Path
─────────────────────────────────────
Input: Directed graph G, source s, sink t, edge lengths ℓ
Output: Shortest s-t path
1.  y_v ← 0 for all v; pick s, label y_s = 0
2.  while vertex t is not yet finalized:
3.        find an edge (u, v) with u finalized, v not finalized
4.        set y_v ← min{y_v, y_u + ℓ_{uv}}  (relaxation)
5.        mark v as finalized when y_v equals the minimum over all such u
6.  reconstruct path by tracing predecessors
```

**Step 4 — Proof of Correctness.**

- **Dual feasibility:** We maintain $y_v - y_u \le \ell_{uv}$ for all edges $(u, v)$ — this is enforced by the relaxation step.
- **At termination:** $y_t - y_s$ is the length of the shortest path. By Strong Duality, the primal and dual optima are equal.
- **Reconstructed path:** Predecessors are recorded when a vertex is finalized, yielding the shortest $s$–$t$ path.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the dual of the LP; the Primal-Dual Schema's *algorithmic essence* is precisely the iterative label-setting procedure, which is equivalent to running a primal-dual method without explicit LP calls. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign errors in dual construction:** A *very* common mistake is forgetting to flip the inequality direction when moving from a max primal to a min dual. Rehearse the duality table until it is automatic.
> 2. **Mixing up strong and weak duality:** Weak duality is an *inequality*; strong duality is an *equality* at optimality. Examiners will deduct marks if you state the wrong direction.
> 3. **Charging argument gaps:** In approximation proofs, the charging step must explicitly identify *which* primal cost is being charged to *which* dual quantity. A vague "therefore the algorithm is a 2-approximation" without the charging argument is a guaranteed zero for the proof sub-part.
> 4. **Forgetting the integrality of the primal in the Primal-Dual Schema:** A common pitfall is to set $x_j$ to the *fractional* value that makes the dual tight, instead of the integer 1 (for 0/1 problems). The schema is a rounding rule — make sure you pick $x_j \in \{0, 1\}$.
> 5. **Not invoking strong duality in the bound:** After showing $c^T x \le \rho \cdot b^T y$ for *feasible* (not necessarily optimal) $y$, you must explicitly cite that $b^T y \le \text{OPT}_{\text{LP}} \le \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}}$ to close the chain.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LP Standard Form** is $\max c^T x$ s.t. $Ax \le b,\; x \ge 0$; the **Dual** is $\min b^T y$ s.t. $A^T y \ge c,\; y \ge 0$.
- **Weak Duality** always gives $c^T x \le b^T y$ for feasible $(x, y)$. **Strong Duality** gives equality at optima.
- **Complementary Slackness** is the key tool: $x_j (A^T y - c)_j = 0$ and $y_i (b - Ax)_i = 0$.
- The **Primal-Dual Schema** is an *algorithmic* (not LP-solving) method: it iteratively raises dual variables to satisfy violated primal constraints, and commits primal variables when dual constraints become tight.
- The **approximation factor** of a Primal-Dual algorithm is established via a **charging argument**: each unit of primal cost is matched to a constant $\rho$ units of dual value, which in turn is bounded above by OPT.
- **Set Cover** has an $H_d$-approximation (Primal-Dual) where $d$ is the maximum set size — this is *tight*.
- **Weighted Vertex Cover** has a clean **2-approximation** via Primal-Dual, and this is also tight.
- **Shortest Path** can be solved *exactly* in polynomial time, and the Primal-Dual view recovers Dijkstra's algorithm.
- The Primal-Dual Schema is the **conceptual bridge** between LP-based bounds (which are loose in general) and constructive approximation algorithms (which produce integral solutions).
- The **integrality gap** of the LP equals the approximation factor achieved by the best Primal-Dual algorithm for that LP — this is a deep and useful rule of thumb.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Primal Dual Schema: LP Duality Theorem, Complementary Slackness Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Primal Dual Schema: LP Duality Theorem, Complementary Slackness Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Primal-Dual Schema — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primal-Dual Schema (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> The **Primal-Dual Schema** is an algorithmic paradigm for the design and analysis of approximation algorithms in which an **LP relaxation** (the *primal*) and its corresponding **dual linear program** are jointly exploited. The algorithm maintains a **feasible dual solution** throughout, and incrementally builds a **feasible primal integral solution** by examining which primal constraints are *tight* with respect to the current dual solution, as dictated by the **Complementary Slackness Theorem**.

This schema is the conceptual bridge between **continuous optimization** (LP duality) and **combinatorial approximation** (integrality guarantees). It is the canonical engine behind classical approximation algorithms such as:
- **Set Cover** (greedy primal-dual)
- **Shortest Path / Dijkstra's** (dual variables as potentials)
- **Prize-Collecting Steiner Tree**
- **Facility Location & k-Median**
- **Network Design** (Rakesh–Williamson framework)
- **Vertex Cover** (factor-2 algorithm)

### 1.2 LP Duality Theorem — Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LP Duality Theorem (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Every Linear Program (called the *Primal*) has an associated Linear Program (called the *Dual*) such that the optimal value of one equals or bounds the optimal value of the other. The **Strong LP Duality Theorem** asserts that if either the primal or dual has a finite optimum, then *both* have finite optima of **equal value**.

### 1.3 Complementary Slackness Theorem — Formal Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary Slackness Theorem (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Let $x^*$ be feasible for the primal and $y^*$ be feasible for the dual. Then $x^*$ and $y^*$ are *both optimal* if and only if for **every** primal variable $x_j^*$ and **every** dual variable $y_i^*$:
> $$x_j^* \cdot (c_j - \sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad y_i^* \cdot (\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i) = 0$$
> In plain words: a variable may be strictly positive **only** if its corresponding constraint is **tight** (satisfied with equality).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Auction House

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Auction House":**
> Imagine a **primal** problem as an *auctioneer* trying to **maximize revenue** by selecting a bundle of items to sell (primal variables $x_j \ge 0$). Each buyer (dual constraint $i$) has a *willingness to pay* for each item ($a_{ij}$), capped by their budget $b_i$.
>
> - The **dual** problem represents a *group of buyers* cooperating to **minimize the total budget needed** to "out-bid" the auctioneer's per-item prices $c_j$.
> - **Weak Duality** = "the auctioneer's declared revenue can never exceed the buyers' committed budget."
> - **Strong Duality** = "at the equilibrium, *exactly* the auctioneer's revenue equals the buyers' budget — nothing is wasted."
> - **Complementary Slackness** = "a buyer pays (positive $y_i^*$) **only if** the auctioneer's selected bundle forces their budget constraint to be exactly met (tight)."
>
> The **Primal-Dual Schema** is the iterative negotiation process: start with all buyers offering zero, raise offers greedily, and whenever a buyer's offer matches the auctioneer's price, lock in that item.

### 1.5 Why This Matters in Approximation Algorithms

The Primal-Dual Schema allows algorithm designers to:
1. **Avoid solving the LP explicitly** — only LP-duality-theoretic arguments are needed.
2. **Produce integral solutions** with a provable approximation ratio by analyzing how much the dual cost grows relative to the primal cost.
3. **Run in polynomial time** — the dual is updated via simple, often greedy, rules.

The **approximation ratio** is typically bounded as:
$$\text{Approximation Ratio} = \frac{\text{Primal Cost (integral solution)}}{\text{Optimal LP Cost}}$$
and by **Weak Duality** we always have:
$$\text{Optimal LP Cost} \le \text{Optimal Dual Cost}$$

so we compare our algorithm's primal cost against the LP optimum (a lower bound on the true integer optimum).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Canonical LP Pairs — Standard Forms

#### 2.1.1 The Primal LP (Standard Form — Primal Maximization)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)} \quad \max \quad & \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b} \\
& \mathbf{x} \ge \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

#### 2.1.2 The Dual LP (Derived by Canonical Rules)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)} \quad \min \quad & \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & A^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} \ge \mathbf{c} \\
& \mathbf{y} \ge \mathbf{0}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2 Transformation Rules — "The Three Golden Rules of LP Duality"

| # | Primal (max) | Dual (min) | Comment |
|---|--------------|------------|---------|
| **R1** | Constraint $i$: $\sum_j a_{ij} x_j \le b_i$ | Dual variable $y_i \ge 0$ | One dual var per primal constraint |
| **R2** | Variable $x_j \ge 0$ | Dual constraint $j$: $\sum_i a_{ij} y_i \ge c_j$ | One dual constraint per primal variable |
| **R3** | Objective coefficients $c_j$ | RHS of dual constraints |  |
| **R4** | RHS $b_i$ | Objective coefficients of dual |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "*The transpose of the constraint matrix $A$ becomes the constraint matrix of the dual, while the roles of the objective and the RHS are interchanged.*"

### 2.3 The Three Foundational Theorems

#### 2.3.1 Weak Duality Theorem

> For **any** primal-feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual-feasible $\mathbf{y}$:
> $$\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \;\le\; \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$$

**Implication:** The dual gives an **upper bound** on every primal feasible solution's objective. The minimum dual value is **at least** the maximum primal value.

#### 2.3.2 Strong Duality Theorem

> If the primal (P) has an optimal solution $\mathbf{x}^*$, and the dual (D) has an optimal solution $\mathbf{y}^*$, then:
> $$\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* \;=\; \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$$

**Implication:** The duality gap **closes completely**. (Note: requires mild regularity such as LP feasibility or Slater's condition for the general case.)

#### 2.3.3 Complementary Slackness Theorem (Full Statement)

> Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be primal-feasible and $\mathbf{y}^*$ be dual-feasible. Then $\mathbf{x}^*$ and $\mathbf{y}^*$ are *jointly optimal* **iff**:
> $$x_j^* \cdot \left(c_j - \sum_{i} a_{ij} y_i^*\right) = 0 \quad \forall \, j$$
> $$y_i^* \cdot \left(\sum_{j} a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i\right) = 0 \quad \forall \, i$$

**Intuition:** If a variable is *strictly positive*, its matching dual constraint must be **tight** (and vice versa).

### 2.4 The Primal-Dual Algorithm — Generic Schema

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm Skeleton (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. **Initialize:** Set $\mathbf{y} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$ (or any feasible dual); $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$ (empty primal solution).
> 2. **Repeat** until primal solution is feasible:
>    - Find an *unsatisfied* primal constraint $i$ (i.e., $\sum_j a_{ij} x_j < b_i$).
>    - **Raise** the dual variable $y_i$ **uniformly / greedily** until some dual constraint becomes tight.
>    - Whenever a dual constraint $j$ becomes tight, set $x_j \leftarrow 1$ (or increment by 1).
> 3. **Output:** Integral primal $\mathbf{x}$ and final dual $\mathbf{y}$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for PEITT751 — Module 2.

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Bound / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| F1 | Weak Duality | $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$ | $\forall$ feasible $(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y})$ |
| F2 | Strong Duality | $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$ | When both optima exist |
| F3 | CS — Primal side | $x_j^* \left( c_j - \sum_i a_{ij} y_i^* \right) = 0$ | $\forall j$ |
| F4 | CS — Dual side | $y_i^* \left( \sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i \right) = 0$ | $\forall i$ |
| F5 | Duality Gap | $\Delta = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}$ | Always $\Delta \ge 0$; $=0$ at optimum |
| F6 | Approx. Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{ALG}_{\text{integral}}}{\text{OPT}_{\text{LP}}} \le \dfrac{\text{ALG}_{\text{integral}}}{\text{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}}$ | By Weak Duality |
| F7 | Symmetry | Dual of Dual $\equiv$ Primal | $\text{LP}$ is self-dual under transposition |
| F8 | Perturbation | If $\mathbf{x}^*$ unique optimal & CS holds $\Rightarrow \mathbf{y}^*$ unique optimal | Strict complementarity |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Primal-dual schemas power network-flow based routing and bandwidth allocation (e.g., max-flow min-cut is a primal-dual pair).
- **Operations Research:** Vehicle routing, facility location, supply chain network design.
- **VLSI Design:** Steiner-tree approximations for minimal wire routing on chips.
- **Computational Biology:** Cover-based formulations for motif finding and genome assembly.
- **Cloud / Distributed Systems:** Job assignment and load balancing via dual-based price mechanisms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weak Duality Theorem

**Claim:** For every primal-feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual-feasible $\mathbf{y}$,
$$\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \;\le\; \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}.$$

**Proof (Exhaustive):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\
&\le \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} a_{ij} y_i \right) x_j \quad \text{(since } y \ge 0,\; a_{ij} y_i \text{ sum} \ge c_j\text{)} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \right) \\
&\le \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i \quad \text{(since } x \ge 0,\; \sum_j a_{ij} x_j \le b_i\text{)} \\
&= \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}.
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic Line-by-Line:**
- **Line 1:** Expand the inner product.
- **Line 2:** Apply the dual feasibility $A^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} \ge \mathbf{c}$ term-by-term. Because $x_j \ge 0$, multiplying an inequality by $x_j$ preserves direction.
- **Line 3:** Swap the order of summation (finite sums commute).
- **Line 4:** Factor out $y_i$ and apply primal feasibility $A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}$. Because $y_i \ge 0$, multiplying by $y_i$ preserves direction.
- **Line 5:** Reassemble into the dual inner product. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Complementary Slackness Theorem

**Claim:** $\mathbf{x}^*$ optimal for (P) and $\mathbf{y}^*$ optimal for (D) **iff**
$$x_j^*(c_j - \textstyle\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*) = 0 \; (\forall j), \quad y_i^*(\textstyle\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i) = 0 \; (\forall i).$$

**Proof (Necessity — $(\Rightarrow)$):**

By Strong Duality, $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$. Subtracting the Weak-Duality equality:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^* - \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* \\
&= \sum_{i} y_i^* b_i - \sum_{j} c_j x_j^* \\
&= \sum_{i} y_i^* b_i - \sum_{j} \left(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*\right) x_j^* + \sum_{j} \left(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*\right) x_j^* - \sum_j c_j x_j^* \\
&= \sum_i y_i^* \left( b_i - \sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* \right) + \sum_j x_j^* \left( \sum_i a_{ij} y_i^* - c_j \right).
\end{aligned}$$

Each term in the two sums is **non-negative** (because both $y_i^*, x_j^* \ge 0$ and primal/dual feasibility hold). A sum of non-negatives equal to zero forces each term to be zero:

$$y_i^*\bigl(b_i - \textstyle\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^*\bigr) = 0, \quad x_j^*\bigl(\textstyle\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^* - c_j\bigr) = 0 \;\; \forall i,j. \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Proof (Sufficiency — $(\Leftarrow)$):**

Assume $\mathbf{x}$ feasible for (P), $\mathbf{y}$ feasible for (D), and CS holds. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} &= \sum_i y_i b_i = \sum_i y_i \sum_j a_{ij} x_j \quad \text{(since } y_i(b_i - \sum_j a_{ij} x_j) = 0) \\
&= \sum_j \left(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i\right) x_j = \sum_j c_j x_j \quad \text{(since } x_j(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i - c_j) = 0) \\
&= \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}.
\end{aligned}$$

By Weak Duality, this forces both to be optimal. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Example — A Canonical Primal-Dual Pair

**Primal:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & 4 x_1 + 3 x_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \le 5 \\
& 2 x_1 + x_2 \le 8 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Dual** (variables $y_1, y_2 \ge 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & 5 y_1 + 8 y_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & y_1 + 2 y_2 \ge 4 \\
& y_1 + y_2 \ge 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step solution of the primal:**

Vertices of the feasible region $\{(x_1,x_2): x_1+x_2 \le 5,\; 2x_1+x_2 \le 8,\; x_1,x_2 \ge 0\}$:

| Vertex | $4x_1+3x_2$ |
|--------|-------------|
| $(0,0)$ | $0$ |
| $(4,0)$ | $16$ |
| $(0,5)$ | $15$ |
| $(3,2)$ (intersection) | $18$ |

Optimal primal: $\mathbf{x}^* = (3,2)$, value $= 18$.

**Step-by-step solution of the dual by vertex enumeration:**

| Vertex $(y_1,y_2)$ | $5y_1+8y_2$ |
|--------------------|-------------|
| $(0,4)$ from $y_1+2y_2=4$ alone | $32$ |
| $(3,0)$ from $y_1+y_2=3$ alone | $15$ |
| Intersection $y_1+2y_2=4$ & $y_1+y_2=3$ $\Rightarrow (2,1)$ | $18$ |

Optimal dual: $\mathbf{y}^* = (2,1)$, value $= 18$. **Strong Duality verified.**

**Complementary Slackness check:**

- Primal constraints:
  - Constraint 1: $x_1^*+x_2^* = 3+2 = 5 = b_1$ ✓ (tight, so $y_1^*$ may be $>0$; indeed $y_1^*=2$).
  - Constraint 2: $2x_1^*+x_2^* = 6+2 = 8 = b_2$ ✓ (tight, so $y_2^*$ may be $>0$; indeed $y_2^*=1$).
- Dual constraints:
  - $y_1^*+2y_2^* = 2+2 = 4 = c_1$ ✓ (tight, so $x_1^*$ may be $>0$; indeed $x_1^*=3$).
  - $y_1^*+y_2^* = 2+1 = 3 = c_2$ ✓ (tight, so $x_2^*$ may be $>0$; indeed $x_2^*=2$).

All four conditions are tight — Strong Complementary Slackness holds, and both solutions are **strictly complementary** (every constraint active). $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Example — Primal-Dual Algorithm on Vertex Cover

**Instance:** Graph $G = (V,E)$, $V = \{1,2,3,4\}$, $E = \{12, 23, 34, 14\}$.
**Goal:** Find min-cardinality vertex cover (a set $S \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $S$).

**LP Relaxation (Primal):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \ge 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& x_v \ge 0 \quad \forall v
\end{aligned}$$

**Dual (one var $y_e$ per edge):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{e \in E} y_e \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{e \ni v} y_e \le 1 \quad \forall v \in V \\
& y_e \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Algorithm Trace:**

| Step | Action | Dual vars after | Tight vertices | Picked primal |
|------|--------|-----------------|----------------|---------------|
| 0    | Init $y_e = 0$ | all 0 | none | $S = \{\}$ |
| 1    | Raise $y_{12}$ until vertex $1$ or $2$ tight | $y_{12} = 1$ | $\{1,2\}$ | add 1 & 2 → $S = \{1,2\}$ |
| 2    | Edges 23 and 14 still uncovered | — | — | need more |
| 3    | Raise $y_{34}$ until vertex 3 or 4 tight | $y_{34} = 1$ | $\{3,4\}$ | add 3 & 4 → $S = \{1,2,3,4\}$ |
| End  | All edges covered | dual cost $= 2$ | — | primal cost $= 4$ |

**Ratio bound:** $\dfrac{4}{2} = 2$ — this is the famous **factor-2 approximation** for Vertex Cover. $\blacksquare$

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Key Logical Implications

| Theorem | Hypotheses | Conclusion | Use in Approximation |
|---------|------------|------------|----------------------|
| Weak Duality | $\mathbf{x}$ P-feas, $\mathbf{y}$ D-feas | $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$ | Lower bound on OPT |
| Strong Duality | Both LPs feasible, bounded | $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$ | Certifies optimality |
| Complementary Slackness | $\mathbf{x}^*,\mathbf{y}^*$ jointly feasible | CS $\Leftrightarrow$ joint optimality | Algorithmic certificate |
| Farkas' Lemma | Linear system $A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}$ | Exactly one of $\{$feasible$\}$, $\{$cert of infeasibility$\}$ holds | Separating hyperplane |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Primal-Dual Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: Integer Program IP with combinatorial constraints"] --> B["Step 1 - Relax IP to Linear Program LP - fractional x in Rplus"]
    B --> C["Step 2 - Form Dual LP - variable y per primal constraint"]
    C --> D["Step 3 - Initialize dual y equals zero - primal x equals zero"]
    D --> E{"Step 4 - Primal feasible?"}
    E -- "No" --> F["Step 5 - Find unsatisfied primal constraint i"]
    F --> G["Step 6 - Raise yi uniformly or greedily"]
    G --> H["Step 7 - Check Complementary Slackness - any tight dual constraint j?"]
    H -- "Yes - constraint j tight" --> I["Step 8 - Set xj equals 1 or increment xj"]
    H -- "No" --> G
    I --> E
    E -- "Yes" --> J["OUTPUT: Integral primal x and dual y - prove ratio via CS and Weak Duality"]
    J --> K["Compute approximation ratio = primal cost divided by b transpose y"]
```

### 4.2 Logical Map — Duality & Complementary Slackness Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Primal LP max cT x"] -- "A transpose y geq c" --> D["Dual LP min bT y"]
    D -- "A x leq b" --> P
    P -- "Strong Duality at optimum" --> SD["cT x star equals bT y star"]
    SD --> CS["Complementary Slackness holds"]
    CS -. "if and only if" .- SD
    CS --> PA["xj star times reduced cost equals 0"]
    CS --> DA["yi star times primal slack equals 0"]
    PA --> OUT["Optimality Certificate"]
    DA --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Primal-Dual Iteration — Conceptual State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateInit: Initialize y = 0, x = 0
    StateInit --> StateScan: Identify unsatisfied primal constraint
    StateScan --> StateRaiseDual: Increase dual variable yi
    StateRaiseDual --> StateCheckCS: Any dual constraint j become tight?
    StateCheckCS --> StateFixPrimal: YES - set xj to 1
    StateCheckCS --> StateRaiseDual: NO - keep raising
    StateFixPrimal --> StateScan: Continue
    StateScan --> StateFinal: All primal constraints satisfied
    StateFinal --> [*]: Output x, y - prove ratio
```

### 4.4 Information-Flow Block Diagram — How CS Drives Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TheoreticCore["Theoretic Foundation"]
        T1["LP Relaxation provides lower bound on OPT"] --> T2["Dual variables y act as resource prices"]
        T2 --> T3["Complementary Slackness certifies optimality"]
    end
    subgraph AlgorithmicCore["Algorithmic Engine"]
        A1["Initialize y = 0"] --> A2["Iteratively raise y on tight primal constraints"]
        A2 --> A3["When dual constraint becomes tight - pick primal variable"]
        A3 --> A4["Repeat until primal solution is feasible"]
    end
    subgraph AnalysisCore["Approximation Analysis"]
        R1["Integral primal cost = sum xj"] --> R2["Dual cost = b transpose y upper bounds OPT"]
        R2 --> R3["Ratio = primal cost divided by dual cost is approximation factor"]
    end
    TheoreticCore --> AlgorithmicCore
    AlgorithmicCore --> AnalysisCore
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**State the Weak Duality Theorem for a pair of primal-dual linear programs. Show, using the theorem, that the optimal value of the primal is at most the optimal value of the dual.**

**Model Answer:**

> Consider the canonical pair: primal (P): $\max \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $A\mathbf{x}\le \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x}\ge \mathbf{0}$; dual (D): $\min \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$ s.t. $A^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}\ge \mathbf{c},\ \mathbf{y}\ge \mathbf{0}$.
>
> **Weak Duality Theorem:** For any primal-feasible $\mathbf{x}$ and dual-feasible $\mathbf{y}$,
> $$\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \;\le\; \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}.$$
>
> Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be any optimal primal solution and $\mathbf{y}^*$ any optimal dual solution. Then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is primal-feasible and $\mathbf{y}^*$ is dual-feasible, so the inequality applies:
> $$\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = \text{OPT}(P) \;\le\; \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^* = \text{OPT}(D).$$
>
> Hence $\text{OPT}(P) \le \text{OPT}(D)$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Write the statement of the Complementary Slackness Theorem. Explain in one sentence why it is useful in primal-dual approximation algorithms.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Complementary Slackness Theorem:** Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be feasible for the primal and $\mathbf{y}^*$ feasible for the dual. Then $\mathbf{x}^*$ and $\mathbf{y}^*$ are *both optimal* **iff**
> $$x_j^* \cdot \bigl(c_j - \textstyle\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*\bigr) = 0 \;\; \forall j,\qquad y_i^* \cdot \bigl(\textstyle\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i\bigr) = 0 \;\; \forall i.$$
>
> **Utility (1 line):** It provides a *certificate of optimality* that the algorithm can maintain incrementally, allowing it to construct an integral primal solution while only raising dual variables — enabling factor-bounded approximations **without solving the LP**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice 1
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** Consider the linear program pair:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)} \quad \max \quad & 5 x_1 + 4 x_2 + 3 x_3 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 2 x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \le 6 \\
& x_1 + 2 x_2 + x_3 \le 5 \\
& x_1, x_2, x_3 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

Write the dual LP and verify Strong Duality by finding the optimal solutions of both LPs.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** State and prove the Complementary Slackness Theorem for this primal-dual pair. Apply it to verify that the optimal primal and dual solutions you obtained in part (a) satisfy the conditions.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Dual variables: $y_1$ for constraint 1, $y_2$ for constraint 2.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)} \quad \min \quad & 6 y_1 + 5 y_2 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & 2 y_1 + y_2 \ge 5 \quad (\text{from } x_1) \\
& y_1 + 2 y_2 \ge 4 \quad (\text{from } x_2) \\
& y_1 + y_2 \ge 3 \quad (\text{from } x_3) \\
& y_1, y_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Solving the primal by vertex enumeration:**

| Vertex $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ | $5x_1+4x_2+3x_3$ |
|---------------------------|------------------|
| $(0,0,0)$ | $0$ |
| $(3,0,0)$ from $2x_1=6$ | $15$ |
| $(0,2.5,0)$ from $2x_2=5$ | $10$ |
| $(0,0,5)$ from $x_3=5$? But constraint 1 limits: $x_3 \le 6$, so $(0,0,5)$ feasible | $15$ |
| Intersection of constraints 1 & 2: $(7/3, 4/3, 0)$ | $5(7/3)+4(4/3) = 35/3+16/3 = 51/3 = 17$ |
| Check constraint 2: $7/3 + 8/3 = 15/3 = 5$ ✓ |

Thus primal optimum $\mathbf{x}^* = (7/3, 4/3, 0)$ with value $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = 17$.

**Solving the dual:**

The active dual constraints at optimum correspond to $x_1^*, x_2^* > 0$, so dual constraints 1 and 2 are tight:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 y_1 + y_2 &= 5 \\
y_1 + 2 y_2 &= 4
\end{aligned}$$

Solving: multiply first by 2: $4y_1 + 2y_2 = 10$; subtract: $3y_1 = 6 \Rightarrow y_1 = 2$; then $y_2 = 5 - 4 = 1$.

Check constraint 3: $y_1 + y_2 = 3$ ✓ (just tight, but $x_3^* = 0$ so CS allows this).

**Dual optimum $\mathbf{y}^* = (2, 1)$ with value $6(2) + 5(1) = 17$.**

**Strong Duality verified:** $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = 17 = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$. $\blacksquare$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Statement of CS Theorem (recap):** For joint optimality $\mathbf{x}^*, \mathbf{y}^*$:
$$x_j^* \bigl(c_j - \textstyle\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*\bigr) = 0 \;\; \forall j,\qquad y_i^* \bigl(\textstyle\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i\bigr) = 0 \;\; \forall i.$$

**Proof sketch:** By Strong Duality, $0 = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^* - \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^*$. Adding and subtracting the dual constraint weighted by $x_j^*$ and primal constraint weighted by $y_i^*$ gives a sum of non-negative terms, each of which must vanish:

$$0 = \sum_i y_i^*\Bigl(b_i - \sum_j a_{ij} x_j^*\Bigr) + \sum_j x_j^*\Bigl(\sum_i a_{ij} y_i^* - c_j\Bigr).$$

Both bracket terms are $\ge 0$ by feasibility, and $x_j^*, y_i^* \ge 0$. Hence each product term is zero. $\blacksquare$

**Verification on our solutions $\mathbf{x}^*=(7/3, 4/3, 0),\; \mathbf{y}^*=(2,1)$:**

*Primal-side CS:*
- $j=1$: $x_1^* = 7/3 > 0$, need $c_1 - (2y_1^*+y_2^*) = 5 - (4+1) = 0$ ✓
- $j=2$: $x_2^* = 4/3 > 0$, need $c_2 - (y_1^*+2y_2^*) = 4 - (2+2) = 0$ ✓
- $j=3$: $x_3^* = 0$, CS trivially holds ✓

*Dual-side CS:*
- $i=1$: $y_1^* = 2 > 0$, need $2x_1^*+x_2^*+x_3^* = 14/3+4/3+0 = 18/3 = 6 = b_1$ ✓
- $i=2$: $y_2^* = 1 > 0$, need $x_1^*+2x_2^*+x_3^* = 7/3+8/3 = 5 = b_2$ ✓

All CS conditions verified. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** **(7 Marks)** Describe the **Primal-Dual Schema** for designing approximation algorithms. List the generic algorithmic steps and explain the role of Complementary Slackness in the algorithm.

**(b)** **(7 Marks)** Apply the Primal-Dual Schema to design a factor-2 approximation algorithm for the **Vertex Cover** problem. Prove the approximation ratio rigorously using LP duality.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Primal-Dual Schema — Conceptual Description:**

The Primal-Dual Schema is an iterative algorithm-design framework for combinatorial optimization problems. The key idea is:
1. Formulate the problem as an Integer Program (IP).
2. Relax to an LP (the *primal*).
3. Construct the dual LP — its variables $y_i$ act as "prices" or "potentials."
4. Run an algorithm that maintains a **feasible dual** $\mathbf{y}$ throughout, and incrementally constructs an **integral primal** $\mathbf{x}$.
5. Bound the ratio of the integral primal cost to the dual cost (which upper-bounds OPT by Weak Duality).

**Generic Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** $\mathbf{y} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$; $\mathbf{x} \leftarrow \mathbf{0}$.
2. **Repeat** while $\mathbf{x}$ is not primal-feasible:
   - Find a *violated* primal constraint $i$ (i.e., $\sum_j a_{ij} x_j < b_i$).
   - **Raise** $y_i$ uniformly (or by the largest amount that keeps dual feasibility) until at least one dual constraint becomes tight.
   - Whenever a dual constraint $j$ becomes tight, **set $x_j = 1$** (or increment $x_j$).
3. **Output:** $\mathbf{x}$ (integral primal) and $\mathbf{y}$ (dual).

**Role of Complementary Slackness:**

CS provides the **algorithmic certificate** for optimality: it dictates *exactly when* a primal variable may be safely set. By only setting $x_j = 1$ when the $j$-th dual constraint is tight, the algorithm mimics the optimality condition. After termination, the algorithm computes its approximation ratio as $\frac{\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}}{\mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}}$, which by Weak Duality is $\ge \frac{\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}}{\text{OPT}}$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Vertex Cover — LP Formulation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(P)} \quad \min \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_u + x_v \ge 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& x_v \ge 0 \quad \forall v
\end{aligned}$$

**Dual (one variable $y_e$ per edge):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)} \quad \max \quad & \sum_{e \in E} y_e \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{e \ni v} y_e \le 1 \quad \forall v \in V \\
& y_e \ge 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Primal-Dual Algorithm:**

```
1. Set x_v = 0 for all v, y_e = 0 for all e.
2. While there exists an edge e = (u,v) with x_u + x_v < 1:
       (a) Raise y_e uniformly until some vertex w becomes tight:
           i.e., ∑_{e'∋w} y_{e'} = 1.
       (b) Add all newly-tight vertices to S (set x_w = 1 for each).
3. Output S = {v : x_v = 1}.
```

**Approximation Ratio Proof:**

Let $S$ be the algorithm's output, and let $E_S$ be the set of edges whose dual variable was raised during execution. Observe:
- Each vertex $v \in S$ was added because $\sum_{e \ni v} y_e = 1$ (tight in the dual).
- The edges in $E_S$ are pairwise? — *No*: edges share vertices, so each $y_e$ raised may affect both endpoints.

**Key Lemma:** *For every edge $e = (u,v) \in E_S$, at least one of $u,v$ lies in $S$.*

**Proof of Lemma:** Edges are processed only when uncovered; when edge $e$ is processed, both $u,v$ are not yet in $S$, so the algorithm raises $y_e$ until either $u$ or $v$ (or both) becomes tight — at which point that vertex is added to $S$. $\square$

**Main Proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
|S| &= \sum_{v \in S} 1 = \sum_{v \in S} \sum_{e \ni v} y_e \quad (\text{by tightness at every } v \in S) \\
&= \sum_{e \in E} y_e \cdot (\text{number of endpoints of } e \text{ in } S) \\
&\le 2 \sum_{e \in E} y_e \quad (\text{since each edge has at most 2 endpoints in } S) \\
&= 2 \cdot \text{(dual objective)}.
\end{aligned}$$

By Weak Duality, $\text{(dual objective)} \le \text{OPT}$. Hence:
$$|S| \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}.$$

The algorithm is a **factor-2 approximation** for Vertex Cover. $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting non-negativity in Weak Duality proof (≈2 marks):** The proof relies on $x_j \ge 0$ to flip inequality signs. Writing $\sum c_j x_j \le \sum (\sum a_{ij} y_i) x_j$ *without* justifying via $x_j \ge 0$ costs full marks.
>
> 2. **Mixing up primal and dual constraints in CS (≈2 marks):** Always remember: $x_j^* \cdot (\text{dual slack}) = 0$ (variable vs. dual constraint) and $y_i^* \cdot (\text{primal slack}) = 0$ (variable vs. primal constraint). Swapping these is the #1 mistake.
>
> 3. **Not checking ALL CS conditions (≈1 mark):** Even if the active constraints are tight, you must verify both primal-side and dual-side, including the *trivial* conditions ($x_j^*=0$ or $y_i^*=0$).
>
> 4. **Omitting the duality gap argument in sufficiency proof (≈1 mark):** In the "$\Leftarrow$" direction of CS, students forget to invoke Weak Duality to conclude that equality of objectives $\Rightarrow$ both are optimal.
>
> 5. **Confusing Strong Duality with the LP being unbounded:** Strong Duality holds when *both* LPs are bounded and feasible. Do not state it as unconditional.
>
> 6. **Algorithm steps in wrong order for Vertex Cover (≈1 mark):** The "raise $y_e$ until a vertex becomes tight, *then* add that vertex" order is essential. Reversing it (add vertex first, then raise $y_e$) breaks the factor-2 argument.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Primal-Dual Schema**

- **Three foundational theorems** to memorize in order: **(1)** Weak Duality, **(2)** Strong Duality, **(3)** Complementary Slackness.
- **Canonical LP pair to remember by heart:**
  Primal: $\max \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}$ s.t. $A\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b},\ \mathbf{x}\ge 0$.
  Dual: $\min \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$ s.t. $A^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y} \ge \mathbf{c},\ \mathbf{y}\ge 0$.
- **Duality transformation rules:** Constraints ↔ Variables, Maximize ↔ Minimize, $\le$ ↔ $\ge$, RHS ↔ objective coefficients, matrix transposes.
- **Weak Duality:** $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x} \le \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}$ — the most-used inequality in approximation ratio proofs.
- **Strong Duality:** $\mathbf{c}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}^*$ at optimum (gap = 0).
- **Complementary Slackness in two equations:**
  $x_j^*(c_j - \sum_i a_{ij} y_i^*) = 0$ and $y_i^*(\sum_j a_{ij} x_j^* - b_i) = 0$.
- **Primal-Dual Schema algorithm structure:** Init $\mathbf{y}=0$, $\mathbf{x}=0$ → find violated primal constraint → raise dual → set primal when dual becomes tight → repeat.
- **Approximation ratio formula:** $\rho = \frac{\text{ALG (integral primal cost)}}{\text{b}^{\mathsf T}\mathbf{y}_{\text{final}}} \le \frac{\text{ALG}}{\text{OPT}}$.
- **Canonical applications:** Set Cover ($\rho = H_n$), Vertex Cover ($\rho = 2$), Shortest Path (exact via Dijkstra as a primal-dual process), Prize-Collecting Steiner Tree.
- **Key insight:** Complementary Slackness turns optimality from a *post-hoc verification* into a *constructive design principle*.
- **Self-duality:** The dual of the dual is the primal — a fundamental symmetry.
- **Strict complementarity:** When LP is non-degenerate, $\mathbf{x}^* > 0$ everywhere $\mathbf{y}^*$ constraint is tight and vice versa — important for uniqueness.
- **Exam tip:** Always state the dual explicitly, label variables, then verify Strong Duality numerically before applying CS — this earns full marks for both parts of a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
