<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Linear Programming (LP) Relaxation — The Foundation

A **Linear Program (LP)** is an optimization problem where we minimize (or maximize) a linear objective function subject to linear equality and inequality constraints. In the context of approximation algorithms, LP plays a **central, indispensable role** because most NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems can be naturally expressed as **Integer Linear Programs (ILPs)**, and then "relaxed" by replacing integrality constraints with continuous bounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LP Relaxation** = Drop the constraint that variables must be integers. Allow them to take any real value within [0, 1]. The resulting fractional solution is then *rounded* to obtain a near-optimal integer solution. This is the cornerstone of approximation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology**: When the KTU paper asks for "LP formulation," it means writing the canonical linear program with:
> 1. **Decision variables** (clearly defined)
> 2. **Objective function** (minimization, since all problems here are minimization)
> 3. **Constraints** (covering, capacity, integrality, etc.)

## 1.2 Problem 1 — Set Cover (SC)

### Formal Definition
Given a **universe** $U$ of $n$ elements and a collection $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$ of $m$ subsets of $U$ such that $\bigcup_{i=1}^{m} S_i = U$, the **Set Cover problem** asks to find a sub-collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ of **minimum cardinality** that covers every element of $U$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Fire Station Placement**
> Imagine you are the **District Collector of Kerala** after the 2018 floods. You have a list of $m$ proposed fire-station locations. Each location $S_i$ can "cover" (serve within reachable distance) a certain set of villages. You must pick the **fewest possible locations** so that every village has at least one nearby fire station. That is the **Set Cover** problem in disguise!

## 1.3 Problem 2 — Vertex Cover (VC)

### Formal Definition
Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, the **Vertex Cover problem** asks to find a subset $C \subseteq V$ of **minimum size** such that every edge $(u, v) \in E$ has at least one endpoint in $C$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Security Camera Network**
> You own a museum with corridors connecting rooms. Each corridor is an *edge*, and you must place a security guard (vertex) such that every corridor is monitored. The minimum number of guards needed to monitor every corridor is the **Vertex Cover**.

## 1.4 Problem 3 — Weighted Vertex Cover (WVC)

### Formal Definition
A generalization of Vertex Cover. Each vertex $v \in V$ has a **non-negative weight** $w_v$, and we wish to find a vertex cover $C$ that **minimizes the total weight** $\sum_{v \in C} w_v$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Hire Guards with Different Salaries**
> Instead of identical guards, you have a *star security expert* (cost = ₹50,000) and a *junior guard* (cost = ₹10,000). The *Weighted Vertex Cover* minimizes your total payroll while still monitoring every corridor.

## 1.5 Problem 4 — Multiway Cut (MWC)

### Formal Definition
Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $c_e \geq 0$ and a set of **terminal vertices** $T = \{t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_k\} \subseteq V$, the **Multiway Cut problem** asks to find a subset of edges $F \subseteq E$ of **minimum total weight** such that removing $F$ disconnects every pair of terminals — i.e., no $t_i$ and $t_j$ ($i \neq j$) remain in the same connected component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Disconnect Competitor Offices**
> A telecom company has $k$ regional headquarters. Cutting some communication links to ensure no two headquarters can directly communicate incurs costs proportional to the link capacities. **Multiway Cut** = minimum cost to isolate every regional HQ from every other.

## 1.6 Problem 5 — Minimum Multicut (MMC)

### Formal Definition
Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge capacities $c_e \geq 0$ and a list of $k$ **source-sink pairs** $(s_1, t_1), (s_2, t_2), \ldots, (s_k, t_k)$ where all $s_i, t_i$ are distinct, the **Minimum Multicut problem** asks to find a subset of edges $F \subseteq E$ of **minimum total capacity** such that every pair $(s_i, t_i)$ is disconnected in the graph $G \setminus F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Block Specific Phone Calls**
> A network operator has $k$ specific customers $(s_i, t_i)$ whose calls must NOT connect. They can disable certain trunk lines (edges) at a cost. The operator wants the *cheapest* set of lines to disable while ensuring all these specific pairs cannot talk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested)**:
> - **Multiway Cut** separates *every* pair from a set $T$.
> - **Minimum Multicut** separates *specific* given pairs $(s_i, t_i)$.
> Multiway Cut is a special case of Minimum Multicut when the pair set is the *Cartesian product* $T \times T \setminus \{(t,t)\}$.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Universal Recipe for LP Formulation

Every problem in this module follows the same 4-step recipe:

1. **Define decision variables** $x \in \{0, 1\}$ indicating selection.
2. **Write the objective function** (sum of selected variable costs).
3. **Write the covering/connectivity constraints** (linear inequalities).
4. **Apply LP relaxation** by replacing $x \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x \leq 1$.

## 2.2 LP Formulation — Set Cover (SC)

**Given:** Universe $U = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}$, subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \quad \forall \, e_j \in U \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall \, i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Relaxation of SC:**
$$
0 \leq x_i \leq 1
$$

**Intuition:** $x_i = 1$ means "include subset $S_i$ in the cover"; the constraint ensures each element $e_j$ is in at least one chosen subset.

## 2.3 LP Formulation — Vertex Cover (VC)

**Given:** Undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ where $|V| = n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall \, (u, v) \in E \\
& x_v \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall \, v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Relaxation of VC:**
$$
0 \leq x_v \leq 1
$$

**Intuition:** The constraint $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ says *"for every edge, at least one endpoint must be in the cover."*

## 2.4 LP Formulation — Weighted Vertex Cover (WVC)

**Given:** Vertex weights $w_v \geq 0$ for each $v \in V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{v \in V} w_v \cdot x_v \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall \, (u, v) \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 \quad \forall \, v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** The LP relaxation for WVC is itself the formulation; integrality constraints are dropped.

## 2.5 LP Formulation — Multiway Cut (MWC)

**Given:** Graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge costs $c_e \geq 0$, terminal set $T = \{t_1, \ldots, t_k\}$.

**Variables:**
- $x_e \in \{0, 1\}$ for each $e \in E$ (1 if $e$ is cut).
- $d_v^t \geq 0$ for each $v \in V$ and $t \in T$ (a "labeling distance" from $v$ to terminal $t$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{e = (i,j) \in E} c_e \cdot x_{ij} \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0 \quad \forall \, i \\
& d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1 \quad \forall \, i \neq j \\
& d_v^{t} \leq d_u^{t} + x_{uv} \quad \forall \, (u,v) \in E, \, \forall \, t \in T \\
& 0 \leq x_e \leq 1, \quad d_v^t \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** Think of $d_v^t$ as the *distance label* from vertex $v$ to terminal $t$ in a *virtual* graph where cutting an edge adds 1 to the distance. The constraints force different terminals to have different effective "regions" in the labeled space.

## 2.6 LP Formulation — Minimum Multicut (MMC)

**Given:** Graph $G = (V, E)$, edge capacities $c_e \geq 0$, source-sink pairs $(s_1, t_1), \ldots, (s_k, t_k)$.

**Variables:**
- $d_v$ — a "distance label" for each vertex $v \in V$ (no per-pair labels, single value).
- $x_e \in \{0, 1\}$ — edge selection variable.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & \sum_{e \in E} c_e \cdot x_e \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1 \quad \forall \, i = 1, \ldots, k \\
& d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv} \quad \forall \, (u, v) \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_e \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** Assign each vertex a real "label" $d_v$. Two vertices connected by a path in the surviving graph (where $x_e = 0$) must have similar labels (difference $\leq 0$). The constraint $d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1$ ensures each pair is *separated*.

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| **Problem** | **Decision Variable** | **Objective** | **Key Constraint** | **Relaxation Bound** |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Set Cover | $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ for each subset $S_i$ | $\sum x_i$ (min) | $\sum_{i : e \in S_i} x_i \geq 1$ for every element $e$ | $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$ |
| Vertex Cover | $x_v \in \{0,1\}$ for each vertex $v$ | $\sum x_v$ (min) | $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ for every edge $(u,v)$ | $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$ |
| Weighted VC | $x_v \in [0,1]$ | $\sum w_v x_v$ (min) | $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ for every edge | $0 \leq x_v \leq 1$ |
| Multiway Cut | $x_e \in [0,1]$, $d_v^t \geq 0$ | $\sum c_e x_e$ (min) | $d_v^t \leq d_u^t + x_{uv}$ for all $e, t$ | Replace integrality with $[0,1]$ |
| Minimum Multicut | $x_e \in [0,1]$, $d_v \in \mathbb{R}$ | $\sum c_e x_e$ (min) | $d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv}$, $d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1$ | Replace integrality with $[0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**:
> - **Telecommunications:** Network design and link removal to disconnect traffic.
> - **VLSI Design:** Separating circuit components on a chip.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Identifying minimal gene sets covering pathways.
> - **Database Systems:** Selecting minimum index set covering all query attributes.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation Walkthrough — Why These Constraints?

### 3.1.1 Deriving the Set Cover LP

**Step 1 — Identify decision variables.**
We must decide whether to *include* each subset $S_i$ in the cover. Therefore define:
$$
x_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } S_i \text{ is selected} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the objective.**
The cost is the number of subsets selected (unweighted SC), so:
$$
\text{Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the covering constraint.**
For an element $e_j \in U$, consider the set of all subsets that *contain* $e_j$:
$$
I(e_j) = \{ i : e_j \in S_i \}
$$
Then $e_j$ is covered if and only if at least one of these subsets is selected:
$$
\sum_{i \in I(e_j)} x_i \geq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the canonical ILP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ILP:} \quad & \min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \\
& \text{s.t. } \sum_{i \in I(e_j)} x_i \geq 1 \quad \forall e_j \in U \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply LP relaxation:**
Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$. This is the standard form used for rounding.

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Vertex Cover LP

**Step 1 — Decision variable:**
$$
x_v = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if vertex } v \text{ is in the cover} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Objective:**
$$
\text{Cost} = \sum_{v \in V} x_v
$$

**Step 3 — Constraint for each edge $(u, v)$:**
For the edge to be "covered," at least one of $u, v$ must be in $C$:
$$
x_u + x_v \geq 1
$$

**Step 4 — Full LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LP:} \quad & \min \sum_{v \in V} x_v \\
& \text{s.t. } x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** For bipartite graphs, the LP relaxation is **integral** (totally unimodular constraint matrix). For general graphs, half-integrality holds: $x_v \in \{0, 1/2, 1\}$.

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Weighted Vertex Cover LP

**Step 1 — Same as above, but multiply each variable by its weight in the objective:**

$$
\min \sum_{v \in V} w_v x_v
$$

**Step 2 — Constraints identical to unweighted VC.**

**Step 3 — Final LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WVC-LP:} \quad & \min \sum_{v \in V} w_v x_v \\
& \text{s.t. } x_u + x_v \geq 1 \quad \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& 0 \leq x_v \leq 1 \quad \forall v \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Deriving the Multiway Cut LP (Shiloach-Vazirani Style)

**Step 1 — Edge variable:**
$$
x_e = \text{fraction of edge } e \text{ that is cut}
$$

**Step 2 — Distance variable:**
For each vertex $v$ and terminal $t$, define $d_v^t$ as the *shortest path distance* from $v$ to $t$ in a graph where cutting an edge adds 1 to the distance.

**Step 3 — Self-distance constraint:**
For any terminal $t_i$ and the same terminal: $d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Cross-distance constraint:**
For $t_i \neq t_j$: $d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1$. This ensures terminals are *visibly different* after the cut.

**Step 5 — Triangular inequality (per edge, per terminal):**
For every edge $(u, v)$ and terminal $t$:
$$
d_v^t \leq d_u^t + x_{uv}
$$
This says: the distance from $v$ to $t$ is at most the distance from $u$ to $t$ plus the cost of crossing edge $(u, v)$.

**Step 6 — Final LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MWC-LP:} \quad & \min \sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij} \\
& \text{s.t. } d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0, \; d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1 \;\; (i \neq j) \\
& \quad d_v^t \leq d_u^t + x_{uv} \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E, \, t \in T \\
& \quad x_e \in [0, 1], \; d_v^t \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Deriving the Minimum Multicut LP

**Step 1 — Edge variable:** $x_e$ (fraction of edge to cut).

**Step 2 — Vertex "potential" variable:** $d_v$ is a real label for each vertex $v$.

**Step 3 — Separation constraint:** For each pair $(s_i, t_i)$:
$$
d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1
$$
This forces the labels of $s_i$ and $t_i$ to differ by at least 1.

**Step 4 — Edge smoothing constraint:** For each edge $(u, v)$:
$$
d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv}
$$
This means: if the edge is *not* cut ($x_{uv} = 0$), then $d_v = d_u$, so the label cannot "jump" across an uncut edge. This effectively forces $s_i$ and $t_i$ to have a sequence of close labels if a path of uncut edges connects them.

**Step 5 — Final LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MMC-LP:} \quad & \min \sum_{e \in E} c_e x_e \\
& \text{s.t. } d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1 \;\; \forall i = 1, \ldots, k \\
& \quad d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv} \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E \\
& \quad x_e \in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrality Gap & Rounding**: The LP relaxation value is a *lower bound* on the true optimal. After solving, the fractional solution is rounded (e.g., randomized rounding, threshold rounding) to obtain a feasible integer solution whose cost is within a factor (the *approximation ratio*) of the LP optimum.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Set Cover LP Relaxation

**Instance:** $U = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $S_1 = \{1, 2\}$, $S_2 = \{2, 3\}$, $S_3 = \{1, 3\}$.

**ILP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_3 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 1) \\
& x_1 + x_2 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 2) \\
& x_2 + x_3 \geq 1 \quad (\text{cover } 3) \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Relaxation Optimal:** $x_1 = x_2 = x_3 = 1/2$, giving cost = $3/2$. The optimal integer solution has cost 2 (e.g., $\{S_1, S_2\}$). Hence, integrality gap = $2 / (3/2) = 4/3$.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Vertex Cover LP on $K_3$

Graph: Triangle with vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$ and edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (1,3)\}$.

**LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \geq 1, \; x_2 + x_3 \geq 1, \; x_1 + x_3 \geq 1 \\
& 0 \leq x_i \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Optimal:** $x_1 = x_2 = x_3 = 1/2$, cost = $3/2$. **Integer Optimal:** cost = 2 (any two vertices form a vertex cover). **Integrality Gap:** $2/(3/2) = 4/3$.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Weighted VC on a 4-Vertex Path

Graph: $1 - 2 - 3 - 4$. Weights: $w_1 = w_4 = 1$, $w_2 = w_3 = 10$.

**LP:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & x_1 + 10x_2 + 10x_3 + x_4 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \geq 1, \; x_2 + x_3 \geq 1, \; x_3 + x_4 \geq 1 \\
& 0 \leq x_i \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Optimal:** $x_1 = x_4 = 1$, $x_2 = x_3 = 0$, cost = $2$. This is integral and optimal! (Pick the cheap endpoints instead of the expensive middle.)

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Architecture: LP-Based Approximation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Problem Instance (SC, VC, WVC, MWC, MMC)"] --> B["Identify Decision Variables"]
    B --> C["Write Objective Function (Min Sum)"]
    C --> D["Encode Logical Constraints (Covering / Separation)"]
    D --> E["Integer Linear Program (ILP)"]
    E --> F["Apply LP Relaxation: Replace Binary with Continuous Bounds 0 to 1"]
    F --> G["Solve LP using Simplex / Ellipsoid / Interior-Point"]
    G --> H["Obtain Fractional Optimal x* and LP Optimum ZLP"]
    H --> I{"Rounding Scheme"}
    I --> J["Deterministic Threshold Rounding"]
    I --> K["Randomized Rounding + Derandomization"]
    I --> L["Region-growing / Multiway Rounding"]
    J --> M["Integer Solution Z Integer"]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N["Performance Guarantee: Z Integer / ZLP leq c (approximation ratio)"]
    N --> O["Report Algorithm and Ratio"]
```

```mermaid

        scA["Define xi for each subset Si"]
        scB["Sum xi to Minimize"]

    end
    subgraph probB["VC Formulation"]

        vcB["Sum xv to Minimize"]
        vcC["Constraint: For each edge u,v, xu plus xv ge 1"]
    end
    subgraph probC["WVC Formulation"]
        wvcA["Define xv in 0 to 1"]
        wvcB["Sum wv xv to Minimize"]

    end
    subgraph probD["MWC Formulation"]
        mwcA["Variables xe and dvt"]
        mwcB["Sum ce xe to Minimize"]

    end
    subgraph probE["MMC Formulation"]
        mmcA["Variables xe and dv"]
        mmcB["Sum ce xe to Minimize"]

    end
    scC --> relA["LP Relax: 0 le xi le 1"]

    mwcC --> relD["LP Relax: 0 le xe le 1, dvt ge 0"]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the LP relaxation of the Vertex Cover problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Vertex Cover problem seeks a minimum-size set $C \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $C$.

**ILP Formulation (1 mark):**
$$
\min \sum_{v \in V} x_v \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_u + x_v \geq 1 \;\; \forall (u, v) \in E, \quad x_v \in \{0, 1\}
$$

**LP Relaxation (1 mark):** Replace the integrality constraint with continuous bounds:
$$
0 \leq x_v \leq 1 \quad \forall v \in V
$$
This is the LP relaxation, and its optimal value is a lower bound on the integer optimum. For bipartite graphs, the LP relaxation has an integral optimal solution.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Write the ILP formulation of the Set Cover problem. What is the role of LP relaxation in approximation algorithms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ILP Formulation (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \quad \forall e_j \in U \\
& x_i \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of LP Relaxation (1 mark):** The LP relaxation drops integrality and yields a lower bound on the optimum. By solving the LP efficiently (polynomial time) and then rounding the fractional solution to integers, we obtain near-optimal integer solutions — this is the *foundation* of LP-based approximation algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Formulate the **Multiway Cut** problem as a linear program. Clearly state all variables, the objective, and every constraint. Explain the role of the distance variables $d_v^t$.

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Variables (2 marks):**
- $x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ for each edge $(i, j) \in E$ — equals 1 if the edge is cut.
- $d_v^t \geq 0$ for each vertex $v \in V$ and each terminal $t \in T$ — a "labeling distance."

**Objective (1 mark):**
$$
\min \sum_{(i,j) \in E} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$

**Constraints (3 marks):**
1. **Self-anchor:** $d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0$ for each terminal $t_i$.
2. **Cross-terminal:** $d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1$ for all $i \neq j$.
3. **Edge triangular inequality:** $d_v^t \leq d_u^t + x_{uv}$ for all $(u, v) \in E$ and all $t \in T$.
4. **Bounds:** $0 \leq x_{ij} \leq 1$, $d_v^t \geq 0$.

**Role of $d_v^t$ (1 mark):** The distance variable $d_v^t$ models the *shortest-path distance* from $v$ to terminal $t$ in a graph where cutting an edge adds 1 unit to the path length. This auxiliary variable enforces consistency of edge-cuts with the requirement that terminals end up in *separated regions*.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with terminal set $T = \{t_1, t_2, t_3\}$ and edge costs $c_e$, write the explicit LP for the Multiway Cut problem. Suppose the LP solver returns the fractional solution $x_e = 0.4$ for every edge, and all $d_v^t$ are computed accordingly. Suggest a **rounding scheme** and state the approximation ratio it achieves.

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Explicit LP (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & \sum_{e \in E} c_e x_e \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0, \; d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1 \;\; (i \neq j, \; i,j \in \{1,2,3\}) \\
& d_v^t \leq d_u^t + x_{uv} \;\; \forall (u,v) \in E, \; t \in \{t_1, t_2, t_3\} \\
& 0 \leq x_e \leq 1, \; d_v^t \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rounding Scheme (3 marks):** The **Călinescu-Karloff-Rabani (CKR) rounding** uses a *region-growing* technique:
1. Pick a random permutation of terminals.
2. Grow balls around each terminal in random order; add an edge to the cut only when it is the *first edge* in the random permutation that connects two growing regions.
3. Expected cut size $\leq (2 - 2/k) \cdot Z_{LP}$, giving a $(2 - 2/k)$-approximation.

**Approximation Ratio (1 mark):** For $k = 3$ terminals, the ratio is $2 - 2/3 = 4/3$.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Formulate the **Minimum Multicut** problem as a linear program. Explain how the formulation enforces disconnection of all source-sink pairs. Write the constraints in full.

**Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Variables (2 marks):**
- $x_e \in [0, 1]$ — fractional selection variable for each edge.
- $d_v$ — a real-valued "potential" for each vertex $v \in V$.

**Objective (1 mark):**
$$
\min \sum_{e \in E} c_e x_e
$$

**Constraints (3 marks):**
1. **Source-sink separation:** $d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1$ for every pair $(s_i, t_i)$.
2. **Edge smoothing:** $d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv}$ for every edge $(u, v) \in E$.
3. **Bounds:** $0 \leq x_e \leq 1$.

**Explanation of Disconnection (1 mark):** If a path of uncut edges ($x_{uv} = 0$) connects $s_i$ to $t_i$, then summing the smoothing constraints along the path gives $d_{s_i} \leq d_{t_i}$, *violating* the separation constraint. Therefore, every $s_i$-$t_i$ path must contain at least one cut edge, ensuring disconnection.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Formulate the **Weighted Vertex Cover** problem as an LP. Given a graph with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ forming a 4-cycle $1-2-3-4-1$ with weights $w_1 = 5, w_2 = 1, w_3 = 1, w_4 = 5$, solve the LP and identify the integrality gap.

**Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**LP Formulation (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\min \quad & 5x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + 5x_4 \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \geq 1 \; (e_{12}) \\
& x_2 + x_3 \geq 1 \; (e_{23}) \\
& x_3 + x_4 \geq 1 \; (e_{34}) \\
& x_4 + x_1 \geq 1 \; (e_{41}) \\
& 0 \leq x_i \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solving the LP (2 marks):** By symmetry of cheap and expensive vertices, set $x_1 = x_4 = a$ and $x_2 = x_3 = b$. Then $a + b \geq 1$. Minimize $5a + b \cdot 2$ with $a + b = 1$, i.e., $b = 1 - a$. Cost = $5a + 2(1-a) = 3a + 2$. To minimize, set $a = 0, b = 1$, giving **LP optimum = 2** with $x_2 = x_3 = 1$, $x_1 = x_4 = 0$.

**Integrality Gap (2 marks):** This solution is integral and gives an integer optimum of **2**. Hence, the integrality gap is $2/2 = 1$ for this instance. (For Weighted VC, the integrality gap is always $\leq 2$, since a simple 2-approximation can be obtained by taking all vertices with $x_v \geq 1/2$.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the integrality constraint:** Many students write $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$ and call it the ILP. **NO** — the ILP requires $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. The relaxation is a *separate* artifact.
> 2. **Confusing the directions of inequalities:** Set Cover and Vertex Cover constraints use $\geq 1$ (covering constraints), not $\leq$. Using the wrong direction is a guaranteed **−1 to −2 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Missing the cross-terminal constraint in Multiway Cut:** Only writing $d_{t_i}^{t_i} = 0$ without the $d_{t_i}^{t_j} \geq 1$ for $i \neq j$ is incomplete; the formulation will not enforce separation.
> 4. **Omitting the edge-smoothing constraint in Minimum Multicut:** Without $d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv}$, the LP does not enforce the disconnection property.
> 5. **Mixing up Multiway Cut and Minimum Multicut definitions:** Multiway Cut uses *all* pairs from a *terminal set*; Minimum Multicut uses a *specific list* of pairs. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Not defining variables explicitly:** Always declare variables before writing the LP. Skipping this loses **1 mark** in most KTU valuations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 LP Formulations**
> - **Set Cover:** Variables for subsets; constraint: every element covered ≥ 1.
> - **Vertex Cover:** Variables for vertices; constraint: $x_u + x_v \geq 1$ for every edge.
> - **Weighted VC:** Same as VC, but objective has $w_v x_v$.
> - **Multiway Cut:** Variables: $x_e$ and $d_v^t$; constraints include self-anchor, cross-terminal ($\geq 1$), and edge-triangular.
> - **Minimum Multicut:** Variables: $x_e$ and $d_v$; constraints: $d_{s_i} - d_{t_i} \geq 1$ and $d_v - d_u \leq x_{uv}$.
> - **LP Relaxation = Drop integrality**, replace with $0 \leq x \leq 1$.
> - **Integrality Gap = (Integer Optimum) / (LP Optimum) $\geq 1$.**
> - **Approximation Ratio** is computed by bounding (cost of rounded solution) / (LP optimum).
> - **Bipartite VC** has integral LP relaxation.
> - **General graph VC** has half-integral LP optimum.
> - **Set Cover** has the classical $H_n$-approximation via LP rounding, where $H_n = 1 + 1/2 + \ldots + 1/n$.
> - **Multiway Cut** has a $(2 - 2/k)$-approximation via CKR rounding.
> - **Minimum Multicut** has an $O(\log k)$-approximation in general graphs (Garg-Vazirani-Yannakakis).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximation Algorithm for the Set Cover Problem Using Simple Rounding Technique and Primal Dual Schema - Analysis of Approximation Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Approximation Algorithm for the Set Cover Problem Using Simple Rounding Technique and Primal Dual Schema - Analysis of Approximation Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Set Cover Problem (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The **Set Cover Problem** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem. Given a universe $U$ of $n$ elements, a family $\mathcal{F} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$ of $m$ subsets whose union is $U$, and a cost function $c : \mathcal{F} \rightarrow \mathbb{Q}^+$, the goal is to find a minimum-cost subfamily $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$ such that every element $e \in U$ belongs to at least one set in $\mathcal{C}$.

Formally, the optimization problem is:

$$
\mathcal{C}^* = \arg\min_{\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}} \left\{ \sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) \;:\; \bigcup_{S \in \mathcal{C}} S = U \right\}
$$

The **unweighted Set Cover** problem is the special case where $c(S) = 1$ for all $S \in \mathcal{F}$; the objective then reduces to minimizing the number of chosen sets $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert$.

### Decision Version (NP-Completeness)

> [!NOTE]
> The decision version — "Does there exist a cover of size at most $k$?" — was one of **Karp's 21 NP-complete problems** (1972). Hence the optimization version is **NP-hard**, and unless $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$, no polynomial-time exact algorithm exists. This is precisely why we study **approximation algorithms**.

### Two Variants Studied in This Module

| Variant | Cost Function | Objective |
|---|---|---|
| Unweighted Set Cover | $c(S) = 1, \forall S$ | Minimize $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert$ |
| Weighted Set Cover | $c(S) \geq 1$ arbitrary | Minimize $\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S)$ |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are the **Fire Chief of Kerala** planning fire station placements. The state has $n = 100$ villages (universe $U$). You have $m = 30$ proposed station locations, each location $S_i$ covers a specific group of nearby villages. Each station costs a certain amount to build.

> **Your Task:** Choose the **cheapest collection** of stations such that **every village is within reach of at least one station**.

- The "frequency" $f$ = the maximum number of stations that can cover any single village. If a village is reachable by 8 stations, then $f \geq 8$.
- A village covered by many stations gives us more "flexibility" — but it also means our rounding algorithm may have to be generous.
- This $f$ is exactly the **approximation factor** of the rounding algorithm we will derive!

## 1.3 Why Approximation? The Big Picture

Since Set Cover is NP-hard, we settle for a polynomial-time algorithm whose output cost is **at most $\alpha$ times the optimal cost**, where $\alpha$ is the **approximation factor (or ratio)**.

$$
\text{Cost(Algorithm)} \leq \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

We will analyze **two classical techniques**:

1. **Simple Rounding Technique** (LP-rounding) — solve the LP relaxation, then round.
2. **Primal-Dual Schema** — use LP duality to guide a greedy construction.

Both yield an **$f$-approximation**, where $f = \max_{e \in U} \vert \{S \in \mathcal{F} : e \in S\} \vert$ is the **maximum frequency** of any element.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LP Formulation (Integer Program and Its Relaxation)

### Integer Program (IP) for Set Cover

Define a binary variable $x_S \in \{0, 1\}$ for each set $S \in \mathcal{F}$, where $x_S = 1$ iff $S$ is chosen.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(IP)} \quad \min \quad & \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) \, x_S \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{S \,:\, e \in S} x_S \geq 1 \quad \forall \, e \in U \\
& x_S \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall \, S \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

### LP Relaxation

Relax the integrality constraint to $x_S \geq 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(LP)} \quad \min \quad & \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) \, x_S \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{S \,:\, e \in S} x_S \geq 1 \quad \forall \, e \in U \\
& x_S \geq 0 \quad \forall \, S \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $\text{LP-OPT}$ denote the optimal LP value. Since LP is a relaxation, $\text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}$.

## 2.2 LP Dual

By applying LP duality, the **dual LP** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)} \quad \max \quad & \sum_{e \in U} y_e \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{e \in S} y_e \leq c(S) \quad \forall \, S \in \mathcal{F} \\
& y_e \geq 0 \quad \forall \, e \in U
\end{aligned}
$$

The variables $y_e$ can be interpreted as **prices** assigned to elements. By **Weak LP Duality**:

$$
\sum_{e \in U} y_e \leq \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) x_S = \text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}
$$

So any **feasible dual solution** gives a **lower bound** on OPT.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the single most important revision resource for this topic.**

| Quantity | Formula / Expression | Role in Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Universe size | $\vert U \vert = n$ | Number of elements to cover |
| Family size | $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert = m$ | Number of available sets |
| Element frequency | $f_e = \vert \{S \in \mathcal{F} : e \in S\} \vert$ | Sets covering element $e$ |
| Max frequency | $f = \max_{e \in U} f_e$ | **Approximation factor for both algorithms** |
| LP objective | $\sum_{S} c(S) x_S$ | Lower bound on OPT |
| Dual objective | $\sum_{e} y_e$ | Lower bound on OPT (weak duality) |
| Covering constraint | $\sum_{S : e \in S} x_S \geq 1$ | Every element covered $\geq$ once |
| Tight set condition | $\sum_{e \in S} y_e = c(S)$ | Set $S$ is **tight** w.r.t. $y$ |
| Approx. guarantee (rounding) | $\text{Cost} \leq f \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Bound on LP-rounding algorithm |
| Approx. guarantee (Primal-Dual) | $\text{Cost} \leq f \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Bound on Primal-Dual algorithm |

## 2.4 Algorithm 1: Simple Rounding Technique

### High-Level Idea

1. Solve the LP relaxation to obtain an **optimal fractional solution** $x^*$.
2. **Threshold round**: include a set $S$ in the cover if $x^*_S \geq 1/f$.
3. Show that the resulting integral solution is feasible and costs at most $f \cdot \text{OPT}$.

### Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 — Solve LP.** Compute $x^* \in \arg\min \{ \sum_S c(S) x_S : x \text{ feasible to LP} \}$.

**Step 2 — Round.** Define the integral cover:
$$
\mathcal{C} = \left\{ S \in \mathcal{F} \;:\; x^*_S \geq \tfrac{1}{f} \right\}
$$

**Step 3 — Output** $\mathcal{C}$.

### Feasibility and Cost Analysis

- **Feasibility:** For every $e \in U$, the covering constraint gives $\sum_{S : e \in S} x^*_S \geq 1$. Since $e$ appears in at most $f$ sets, the **average** $x^*_S$ over sets containing $e$ is at least $1/f$. So **at least one** such set has $x^*_S \geq 1/f$, ensuring $e$ is covered.
- **Cost:** For each $S \in \mathcal{C}$, $x^*_S \geq 1/f$, so $c(S) \leq f \cdot c(S) x^*_S$. Summing:

$$
\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) \leq \sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} f \cdot c(S) x^*_S \leq f \cdot \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) x^*_S = f \cdot \text{LP-OPT} \leq f \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Simple Rounding yields a **$f$-approximation** for Set Cover.

## 2.5 Algorithm 2: Primal-Dual Schema

### High-Level Idea

Build the cover **iteratively**, dual-feasible throughout. At each step, pick an uncovered element, raise its dual price until some set becomes **tight**, then add that tight set to the cover.

### Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure

**Input:** Universe $U$, family $\mathcal{F}$, costs $c(S)$.

**Initialize:**
- $\mathcal{C} \leftarrow \emptyset$ (the cover being built)
- $y_e \leftarrow 0$ for all $e \in U$
- $\text{Uncovered} \leftarrow U$

**Main Loop:** While $\text{Uncovered} \neq \emptyset$:
1. Pick any $e \in \text{Uncovered}$.
2. **Raise** $y_e$ uniformly (i.e., for the chosen $e$ only, or simultaneously for any element of Uncovered contained in a set that has not yet become tight) until **some set $S$ becomes tight**: $\sum_{e' \in S} y_{e'} = c(S)$.
3. Add $S$ to $\mathcal{C}$.
4. Remove from $\text{Uncovered}$ all elements of $S$.

**Output:** $\mathcal{C}$.

### Key Properties

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Dual feasibility:** Throughout the algorithm, every tight set $S$ satisfies $\sum_{e \in S} y_e = c(S)$. Sets not yet tight satisfy $\sum_{e \in S} y_e \leq c(S)$. So $y$ is always a feasible dual solution.
> 2. **Packing LP:** This is essentially the primal-dual schema for the **packing LP** $\max \{ \sum y_e : \sum_{e \in S} y_e \leq c(S) \}$.
> 3. **Termination:** Each iteration covers at least the newly-tightened element $e$, so $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert \leq n$ iterations.

### Analysis: Why is it an $f$-approximation?

Consider the cost of the returned cover $\mathcal{C} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k\}$. Each $S_i$ was added because it became **tight**, so:

$$
c(S_i) = \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e \quad \text{(at the moment } S_i \text{ became tight)}
$$

Note that for any element $e$, its dual price $y_e$ may be raised **at most once** (the first time it gets covered and then removed from $\text{Uncovered}$). So $y_e$ is **frozen** after first being included in a tight set.

Now, for each $e \in U$, $e$ appears in at most $f$ sets of $\mathcal{F}$. Thus:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e = \sum_{e \in U} y_e \cdot (\text{number of tight sets containing } e) \leq \sum_{e \in U} y_e \cdot f = f \cdot \sum_{e \in U} y_e
$$

But $\sum_{e \in U} y_e$ is the dual objective, which by weak duality is at most $\text{OPT}$. Hence:

$$
\text{Cost}(\mathcal{C}) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) \leq f \cdot \sum_{e \in U} y_e \leq f \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Primal-Dual Schema yields a **$f$-approximation** for Set Cover.

## 2.6 Comparison of Both Techniques

| Aspect | LP Rounding | Primal-Dual |
|---|---|---|
| Solves LP? | **Yes** (uses LP solver) | **No** (combinatorial) |
| Running time | Polynomial (with LP solver) | Purely combinatorial, faster |
| Approximation factor | $f$ | $f$ |
| Mechanism | Threshold rounding of $x^*$ | Dual price raising |
| Best for | Theoretical analysis | Practical implementation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Set Cover models **facility location**, **network design**, **VLSI testing**, **machine learning (feature selection)**, **bioinformatics (probe set design)**, and **content distribution networks**. The $f$-approximation is the classical baseline; modern improvements use randomized rounding giving $O(\log n)$ approximation, but the deterministic $f$-approximation via these two techniques remains a cornerstone of KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the $f$-Approximation for LP Rounding

### Setup Recap

We are given:
- Universe $U = \{e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n\}$
- Family $\mathcal{F} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$
- Costs $c(S_j) > 0$
- Optimal fractional solution $x^*$ to LP with $\sum_S c(S) x^*_S = \text{LP-OPT}$.

Define the **element frequency** of any $e \in U$:
$$
f_e \;=\; \bigl\vert \{S \in \mathcal{F} \,:\, e \in S\} \bigr\vert
$$

Define the **maximum frequency**:
$$
f \;=\; \max_{e \in U} f_e
$$

### Derivation Step 1 — Feasibility

We claim that $\mathcal{C} = \{S : x^*_S \geq 1/f\}$ is a valid cover.

For any fixed element $e \in U$, the LP constraint requires:
$$
\sum_{S \,:\, e \in S} x^*_S \;\geq\; 1
$$

There are exactly $f_e \leq f$ sets containing $e$. Therefore the **arithmetic mean** of $x^*_S$ over sets containing $e$ is:
$$
\frac{1}{f_e} \sum_{S : e \in S} x^*_S \;\geq\; \frac{1}{f_e} \;\geq\; \frac{1}{f}
$$

By the **pigeonhole principle**, at least one set $S$ with $e \in S$ must have $x^*_S$ at least this average:
$$
\exists \, S \ni e \;\; \text{with} \;\; x^*_S \;\geq\; \frac{1}{f_e} \;\geq\; \frac{1}{f}
$$

Such an $S$ is included in $\mathcal{C}$, so $e$ is covered. Since $e$ was arbitrary, $\mathcal{C}$ covers all of $U$.

### Derivation Step 2 — Cost Bound

For each $S \in \mathcal{C}$, we have $x^*_S \geq 1/f$, which gives $c(S) \leq f \cdot c(S) x^*_S$ (multiply both sides of $x^*_S \geq 1/f$ by $c(S) > 0$).

Summing over all $S \in \mathcal{C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) &\leq \sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} f \cdot c(S) \, x^*_S \\
&\leq f \cdot \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) \, x^*_S \quad (\text{since } x^*_S \geq 0 \text{ for all } S, \text{ and } \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}) \\
&= f \cdot \text{LP-OPT} \\
&\leq f \cdot \text{OPT} \quad (\text{since LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT for any relaxation})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation Step 3 — Conclusion

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) \;\leq\; f \cdot \text{OPT}\;}
$$

Hence the algorithm is an **$f$-approximation**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the $f$-Approximation for the Primal-Dual Schema

### Setup Recap

The algorithm maintains a vector $y \in \mathbb{R}^n_{\geq 0}$. At each step, it increases dual variables and adds a tight set to the cover. Let $\mathcal{C} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k\}$ be the sets added in order of insertion.

### Derivation Step 1 — Tightness Implies Cost-Equality

When $S_i$ is added to $\mathcal{C}$, by construction:
$$
\sum_{e \in S_i} y_e \;=\; c(S_i)
$$

Therefore:

$$
c(S_i) \;=\; \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e
$$

### Derivation Step 2 — Summing Over All Added Sets

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e \\
&= \sum_{e \in U} y_e \cdot \bigl(\text{number of sets } S_i \in \mathcal{C} \text{ that contain } e\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

The interchange of summation is justified because $y_e$ is fixed once $e$ is covered (it never increases again).

### Derivation Step 3 — Bounding the Multiplicity

For each $e \in U$, the number of sets $S_i \in \mathcal{C}$ containing $e$ is **at most $f$**, since:
- Any $S_i$ containing $e$ is also a set of $\mathcal{F}$ containing $e$.
- There are at most $f$ sets in $\mathcal{F}$ containing $e$.

Hence:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) \;\leq\; \sum_{e \in U} y_e \cdot f \;=\; f \cdot \sum_{e \in U} y_e
$$

### Derivation Step 4 — Weak Duality

By construction, $y$ is feasible for the dual LP at every iteration. Therefore:

$$
\sum_{e \in U} y_e \;\leq\; \text{LP-OPT} \;\leq\; \text{OPT}
$$

### Derivation Step 5 — Final Bound

$$
\boxed{\;\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) \;\leq\; f \cdot \text{OPT}\;}
$$

This proves the Primal-Dual algorithm is also an **$f$-approximation**.

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Numerical Example

Consider the following instance:

- $U = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$
- $\mathcal{F} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$
- $S_1 = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $c(S_1) = 3$
- $S_2 = \{2, 4\}$, $c(S_2) = 2$
- $S_3 = \{3, 5, 6\}$, $c(S_3) = 3$
- $S_4 = \{4, 5, 6\}$, $c(S_4) = 3$

**Compute Frequencies:**
- $f_1 = 1$ (only $S_1$)
- $f_2 = 2$ ($S_1, S_2$)
- $f_3 = 2$ ($S_1, S_3$)
- $f_4 = 2$ ($S_2, S_4$)
- $f_5 = 2$ ($S_3, S_4$)
- $f_6 = 2$ ($S_3, S_4$)

Thus $f = 2$. The expected approximation ratio is $\leq 2$.

**Optimal Solution:** Pick $S_1$ and $S_3$ to cover $\{1,2,3,5,6\}$, then add $S_2$ or $S_4$ to cover $\{4\}$. Cost $= 3 + 3 + 2 = 8$. (Alternatively $S_1 + S_4 = 6$.) So $\text{OPT} = 6$.

**LP Rounding with $1/f = 1/2$:**

Solve LP — for brevity assume $x^*_{S_1} = 1, x^*_{S_4} = 1$ (others 0) is optimal: LP-OPT $= 6$.

Apply threshold $1/2$: all $x^*_S \geq 1/2$? Yes, $S_1$ and $S_4$. So $\mathcal{C} = \{S_1, S_4\}$, cost $= 6 = \text{OPT}$. Achieves ratio $1 \leq 2$.

**Primal-Dual Trace:**

- Initially $y = (0,0,0,0,0,0)$, $\text{Uncovered} = \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$.
- Pick $e = 1$. Raise $y_1$. $S_1$ becomes tight when $y_1 = c(S_1) = 3$. Add $S_1$.
- $\text{Uncovered} = \{4, 5, 6\}$.
- Pick $e = 4$. Raise $y_4$. $S_2$ tight at $y_4 = 2$. Add $S_2$.
- $\text{Uncovered} = \{5, 6\}$.
- Pick $e = 5$. Raise $y_5$. $S_4$ tight at $y_5 = 3$. Add $S_4$.
- $\text{Uncovered} = \emptyset$.

Cover $= \{S_1, S_2, S_4\}$, cost $= 3 + 2 + 3 = 8$. Ratio $= 8/6 \approx 1.33 \leq f = 2$. ✓

This example concretely demonstrates the $f$-approximation in action.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: LP Rounding Algorithm for Set Cover

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Universe U, Family F, Costs c] --> B[Formulate LP Relaxation]
    B --> C[Solve LP using Simplex or Interior Point]
    C --> D[Obtain Optimal Fractional Solution x-star]
    D --> E[Compute Maximum Frequency f]
    E --> F[For Each Set S in F]
    F --> G{Is x-star S greater or equal to 1 divided by f}
    G -->|Yes| H[Add S to Cover C]
    G -->|No| I[Skip Set S]
    H --> J[Move to Next Set]
    I --> J
    J --> F
    F --> K[End of Iteration]
    K --> L[Output Cover C]
    L --> M[Verify Feasibility]
    M --> N[Compute Cost of C]
    N --> O[Compare Cost with f times OPT]
    O --> P[Declare f-Approximation Achieved]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Primal-Dual Schema for Set Cover

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Universe U, Family F, Costs c] --> B[Initialize Cover C as Empty]
    B --> C[Set All Dual Variables y-e to Zero]
    C --> D[Set Uncovered to U]
    D --> E{Is Uncovered Empty}
    E -->|Yes| F[Output Cover C]
    E -->|No| G[Pick Any Uncovered Element e]
    G --> H[Raise y-e Uniformly]
    H --> I{Some Set S becomes Tight}
    I -->|Yes| J[Add S to Cover C]
    I -->|No| H
    J --> K[Remove All Elements of S from Uncovered]
    K --> E
    F --> L[Verify Dual Feasibility of y]
    L --> M[Compute Total Cost]
    M --> N[Bound Cost using Weak Duality]
    N --> O[Conclude f-Approximation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Relationship between Primal LP, Dual LP, and Both Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primal LP: min sum cS xS] -->|Duality Theorem| D[Dual LP: max sum y-e]
    P -->|Solve and Round| RA[LP Rounding Algorithm]
    D -->|Dual Prices Guide| PD[Primal-Dual Schema]
    RA -->|Threshold 1 over f| OUT1[f-Approximation]
    PD -->|Raise Prices| OUT2[f-Approximation]
    OUT1 --> CB[Cost less or equal to f times OPT]
    OUT2 --> CB
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Cost Flow Through the Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Instance] --> B[LP Solver or Dual Variable Loop]
    B --> C[Intermediate Fractional or Dual State]
    C --> D[Rounding or Set Selection Step]
    D --> E[Integral Cover C Output]
    E --> F[Cost Computation]
    F --> G{Is Cost at most f times OPT}
    G -->|Yes| H[Success: f-Approximation]
    G -->|No| I[Failure: Algorithm Needs Refinement]
```
