<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Define the Set Cover Problem. State the LP relaxation of Set Cover and explain why it provides a lower bound on the optimal integer solution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Set Cover Problem** is defined as: Given a universe $U$ of $n$ elements and a family $\mathcal{F} = \{S_1, \ldots, S_m\}$ of subsets with $U = \bigcup \mathcal{F}$, find a minimum-cost subfamily $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{F}$ such that $\bigcup_{S \in \mathcal{C}} S = U$.

The **LP relaxation** replaces the constraint $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_S \geq 0$:

$$
\min \sum_S c(S) x_S \quad \text{s.t.} \quad \sum_{S: e \in S} x_S \geq 1, \; x_S \geq 0
$$

Since the LP's feasible region strictly contains the IP's feasible region (all integer points are LP-feasible), the LP optimum is at most the IP optimum: $\text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}$. Hence it is a valid lower bound. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**What is meant by an "approximation algorithm" for an NP-hard optimization problem? Define approximation factor with respect to the Set Cover problem.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **approximation algorithm** is a polynomial-time algorithm that produces a solution whose cost is provably close to the optimum for every instance. For a minimization problem like Set Cover, an algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ has **approximation factor $\alpha \geq 1$** if for every instance:

$$
\text{Cost}(\mathcal{A}) \;\leq\; \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

For Set Cover with maximum frequency $f$, the algorithms studied (LP rounding and Primal-Dual) achieve $\alpha = f$. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Paper, Module 2, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A

**(a)** Write the Integer Programming formulation of the Set Cover problem. Derive its LP relaxation and the corresponding LP Dual. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **Simple Rounding Technique** for Set Cover. Prove that it achieves an $f$-approximation where $f$ is the maximum frequency of any element. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — LP Formulation and Duality

**Integer Program:** Define $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ for each $S \in \mathcal{F}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(IP)} \quad \min \quad & \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) x_S \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{S : e \in S} x_S \geq 1 \quad \forall e \in U \\
& x_S \in \{0,1\} \quad \forall S \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_S \geq 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(LP)} \quad \min \quad & \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) x_S \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{S : e \in S} x_S \geq 1 \quad \forall e \in U \\
& x_S \geq 0 \quad \forall S \in \mathcal{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**LP Dual:** Apply standard LP duality. The primal has $m$ variables (one per set) and $n$ constraints (one per element). The dual has $n$ variables $y_e \geq 0$ and $m$ constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(D)} \quad \max \quad & \sum_{e \in U} y_e \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{e \in S} y_e \leq c(S) \quad \forall S \in \mathcal{F} \\
& y_e \geq 0 \quad \forall e \in U
\end{aligned}
$$

**By weak LP duality:** Any feasible dual $y$ gives $\sum_e y_e \leq \text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Simple Rounding Technique

**Algorithm:**

1. Solve LP to obtain optimal fractional solution $x^*$.
2. Compute $f = \max_{e \in U} \vert \{S : e \in S\} \vert$.
3. Define $\mathcal{C} = \{S : x^*_S \geq 1/f\}$.
4. Output $\mathcal{C}$.

**Feasibility Proof:** Fix $e \in U$. The LP constraint gives $\sum_{S : e \in S} x^*_S \geq 1$. Since $e$ belongs to at most $f$ sets, by pigeonhole some $S \ni e$ has $x^*_S \geq 1/f$, hence $S \in \mathcal{C}$ covers $e$. As $e$ was arbitrary, $\mathcal{C}$ is a valid cover.

**Cost Bound Proof:**

$$
\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) \;\leq\; \sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} f \cdot c(S) x^*_S \;\leq\; f \cdot \sum_{S \in \mathcal{F}} c(S) x^*_S \;=\; f \cdot \text{LP-OPT} \;\leq\; f \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

**Conclusion:** Simple Rounding is an $f$-approximation. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Paper, Module 2, Dec 2024 Scheme]**

### Question B

**(a)** Explain the **Primal-Dual Schema** for the Set Cover problem with a clear algorithm statement. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Prove that the Primal-Dual algorithm achieves an $f$-approximation. Use the **tightness property** and **weak LP duality** in your proof. Illustrate with a small numerical example. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Primal-Dual Schema

**Algorithm Statement:**

```
Input: Universe U, Family F, Costs c(S)
Initialize C = empty, y_e = 0 for all e, Uncovered = U
While Uncovered is not empty:
    1. Pick any element e in Uncovered
    2. Raise y_e (and possibly y_e' for e' in Uncovered sharing a not-yet-tight set)
       until some set S becomes tight, i.e., sum_{e in S} y_e = c(S)
    3. Add S to C
    4. Remove all elements of S from Uncovered
Output C
```

**Conceptual Justification:** The algorithm maintains a **dual-feasible** solution $y$ throughout. Each picked set $S$ satisfies the dual constraint **tightly** at the moment of selection. This tight-set principle is the heart of the Primal-Dual paradigm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (b) — Approximation Factor Proof with Example

**Proof:** Let $\mathcal{C} = \{S_1, \ldots, S_k\}$ be the sets selected. By construction, each $S_i$ was added when it became tight:

$$
c(S_i) \;=\; \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e
$$

Summing over all selected sets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{e \in S_i} y_e = \sum_{e \in U} y_e \cdot d_e
\end{aligned}
$$

where $d_e$ is the number of times $e$ appears in some $S_i$. Since each $e$ is in at most $f$ sets of $\mathcal{F}$:

$$
d_e \;\leq\; f
$$

Hence:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} c(S_i) \;\leq\; \sum_{e \in U} f \cdot y_e \;=\; f \cdot \sum_{e \in U} y_e \;\leq\; f \cdot \text{LP-OPT} \;\leq\; f \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

- $U = \{1,2,3\}$, $\mathcal{F} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3\}$
- $S_1 = \{1,2\}$, $c = 2$
- $S_2 = \{2,3\}$, $c = 2$
- $S_3 = \{1,3\}$, $c = 2$

Here $f = 2$. Algorithm picks $e=1$, raises $y_1$, $S_1$ tight at $y_1 = 2$. Add $S_1$. Uncovered $=\{3\}$. Pick $e=3$, raise $y_3$, $S_2$ tight at $y_3 = 2$. Add $S_2$. Total cost $= 4$, OPT $= 4$ (e.g., $\{S_3, S_1\}$ or $\{S_1, S_2\}$). Ratio $= 1 \leq f$. ✓ **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting feasibility proof:** Many students prove only the cost bound and skip showing $\mathcal{C}$ is a valid cover (i.e., covers all elements). Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 2. **Mis-stating duality:** Writing "Dual is also minimization" or omitting $\leq c(S)$ constraint loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing LP-OPT and OPT:** Always distinguish: $\text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}$. Many students erroneously write $\text{LP-OPT} = \text{OPT}$.
> 4. **Forgetting to compute $f$:** In the cost bound, students must explicitly state $f = \max_e f_e$. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Primal-Dual trace errors:** When tracing the algorithm, students must show BOTH the dual variable values AND the cover set additions at each step. Showing only one loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Set Cover Approximation**

- **Set Cover Definition:** Minimize $\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S)$ such that $\bigcup \mathcal{C} = U$. NP-hard. Special case: vertex cover, dominating set.
- **LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_S \in \{0,1\}$ with $x_S \geq 0$. Provides lower bound $\text{LP-OPT} \leq \text{OPT}$.
- **LP Dual:** $\max \sum_e y_e$ subject to $\sum_{e \in S} y_e \leq c(S)$, $y_e \geq 0$.
- **Weak Duality:** Any feasible dual $y$ gives $\sum_e y_e \leq \text{OPT}$.
- **Maximum Frequency:** $f = \max_{e \in U} \vert \{S : e \in S\} \vert$ is the key parameter for both algorithms.
- **LP Rounding Algorithm:** Threshold at $1/f$; include $S$ if $x^*_S \geq 1/f$.
- **LP Rounding Bound:** Cost $\leq f \cdot \text{OPT}$ via $\sum_{S \in \mathcal{C}} c(S) \leq f \cdot \text{LP-OPT}$.
- **Primal-Dual Algorithm:** Iteratively raise dual prices until a set becomes tight; add tight set to cover.
- **Primal-Dual Bound:** Cost $\leq f \cdot \text{OPT}$ via tightness + multiplicity at most $f$ + weak duality.
- **Tight Set:** A set $S$ is **tight** w.r.t. dual $y$ if $\sum_{e \in S} y_e = c(S)$.
- **Comparison:** LP rounding needs an LP solver; Primal-Dual is purely combinatorial and faster.
- **Same Bound:** Both give an $f$-approximation, but neither is universally better — the choice depends on application context.
- **Engineering Applications:** Facility location, VLSI testing, network design, content delivery, machine learning feature selection.
- **Improvements Exist:** Randomized rounding gives $O(\log n)$ expected approximation, but the $f$-factor deterministic guarantee from these two classical methods is the **KTU Module 2 cornerstone**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized Algorithms: Concept of Randomization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Randomized Algorithms: Concept of Randomization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Randomized Algorithm** is an algorithm that incorporates one or more **random bits** (or random choices from a probability distribution) into its logical flow of computation, such that the execution path and/or the output may differ across different runs on the **same deterministic input**. The performance of such an algorithm is analyzed in terms of **expected running time** or **probability of correctness**, rather than deterministic worst-case bounds alone. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEITT751 - Module 2):** *Randomization* in the context of approximation algorithms refers to a **derandomization-friendly** technique where randomness is used to break ties, select random subsets, sample solutions, or guide the search, thereby achieving expected approximation ratios that are often tighter than their deterministic counterparts. ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Random Bit** | A bit whose value is chosen uniformly at random from {0, 1}. | | **Sample Space** ($\Omega$) | The set of all possible outcomes of the random choices. | | **Probability Distribution** | A function $P : \Omega \rightarrow [0,1]$ with $\sum P(\omega) = 1$. | | **Random Variable** | A measurable function $X : \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. | | **Expectation** $E[X]$ | The weighted average of $X$ over the sample space. | | **Indicator Variable** $I_A$ | A random variable that equals 1 if event $A$ occurs, else 0. | ## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Classes of Randomized Algorithms > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification used in KTU Board Examinations:** **1. Las Vegas Algorithm** — *Always correct, but running time is a random variable.* - Example: **Randomized Quicksort**, random pivot selection in the traveling salesman heuristic. - Guaranteed correct output; the *cost* varies. **2. Monte Carlo Algorithm** — *Fixed (bounded) running time, but output is correct only with some probability $p \geq 1/2$.* - Example: **Miller-Rabin Primality Test**, randomized rounding in MAX-CUT. - Bounded cost; the *correctness* varies. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Lucky Job Applicant" > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a student preparing for the KTU semester exam. Instead of solving every past paper (deterministic — guaranteed but slow), she randomly picks 5 past papers and solves them. Her final preparation time is *random*, but on average she'll be reasonably prepared. Sometimes she gets lucky (good questions), sometimes unlucky. The key is that **no single adversary (examiner) can consistently make her perform badly** — randomness neutralizes worst-case adversaries. This is precisely the philosophy behind randomized approximation algorithms. ## 1.5 Why Randomization Matters in Approximation | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Defeats worst-case adversaries** | Deterministic algorithms can be exploited by adversarial inputs; randomness breaks symmetry. | | **Simpler analysis** | Probabilistic arguments (Markov, Chernoff) are often cleaner than adversarial lower bounds. | | **Tighter expected ratios** | Many randomized approximation algorithms beat deterministic ones in expected cost. | | **Enables derandomization** | Random algorithms can often be converted to deterministic ones via the **Method of Conditional Expectations** or **Pairwise Independence**. | | **Models real-world uncertainty** | Inputs are often stochastic in practice (network delays, noisy data). | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a Randomized Algorithm A randomized algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ can be modeled as a pair $(\mathcal{D}, f)$ where: - $\mathcal{D}$ is a **randomness source** (e.g., a uniform distribution over $\{0,1\}^k$). - $f : \{0,1\}^* \times \{0,1\}^k \rightarrow \{0,1\}^*$ is a deterministic function taking the input $x$ and random string $r$ to produce output $y = f(x,r)$. The two standard views of the sample space are: 1. **Distributional View (Average-Case):** Randomness is in the *input distribution*. 2. **Randomized View (Probabilistic Algorithm):** Randomness is in the *algorithm's internal coin flips* (this is the KTU Module 2 focus). ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Flow A typical randomized approximation algorithm follows this structure: - **Step 1 — Input Reading:** Read the deterministic instance $I$ (graph, set, matrix). - **Step 2 — Randomization Step:** Flip $k$ random coins to obtain a random string $r \in \{0,1\}^k$. - **Step 3 — Randomized Construction:** Use $r$ to make a non-deterministic choice — e.g., pick a random vertex, random subset, random order, random rounding, or random hash. - **Step 4 — Deterministic Post-Processing:** Apply a deterministic rule (greedy, LP rounding, local search) given the random choices. - **Step 5 — Output & Repeat (if Monte Carlo):** Either return the solution (Las Vegas) or verify it (Monte Carlo) and possibly re-run for amplification. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these 7 formulas — they cover >80% of Module 2 KTU questions on randomization.** | # | Formula / Identity | Statement | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Expectation Definition** | $E[X] = \sum_{i} i \cdot P(X=i) = \int x \, dF(x)$ | Foundation of all randomized analysis. | | 2 | **Linearity of Expectation** | $E\left[\sum_i X_i\right] = \sum_i E[X_i]$ | Works for **any** random variables (even dependent). | | 3 | **Indicator Variable Trick** | $E[I_A] = P(A)$ | Converts probabilities to expectations. | | 4 | **Markov's Inequality** | $P(X \geq a) \leq \dfrac{E[X]}{a}$ for $X \geq 0$ | Tail bounds; used for amplification. | | 5 | **Chernoff Bound** | $P(\vert X - \mu \vert \geq \delta\mu) \leq 2e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3}$ for $X = \sum X_i$, $\mu = E[X]$ | Concentration for sums of independent 0-1 variables. | | 6 | **Union Bound** | $P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^n P(A_i)$ | Bounding probability of "any bad event". | | 7 | **Law of Total Expectation** | $E[X] = \sum_{i} E[X \mid B_i] \cdot P(B_i)$ | Conditioning on a partition of $\Omega$. | ## 2.4 Engineering Applications Across Domains - **Network Design:** Randomized routing protocols achieve expected $O(\log n)$ congestion vs. $\Omega(n)$ worst-case deterministic. - **Cryptography:** Every secure hash function (SHA family) and randomized encryption is built on this concept. - **Machine Learning:** Stochastic gradient descent is a randomized approximation to the true gradient. - **VLSI Layout:** Random sampling of wire delays yields expected polynomial-time solutions. - **Operations Research:** Randomized rounding of LP relaxations (Raghavan–Thomson theorem) yields integrality-gap-bounded approximations for packing/covering problems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Expected Number of Comparisons in Randomized Quicksort > [!NOTE] > **Canonical KTU Example** — illustrates the indicator-variable technique. **Problem Setup.** Randomized Quicksort picks a pivot uniformly at random from the current sub-array. Define the random variable $C$ = total number of comparisons performed. **Step 1 — Decompose $C$ using indicator variables.** Sort the $n$ elements. The elements, when sorted, occupy positions $z_1 < z_2 < \dots < z_n$ (these are fixed for a given input). Define: $$I_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } z_i \text{ and } z_j \text{ are ever compared} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Express total comparisons.** $$C = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} I_{i,j}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Linearity of Expectation.** $$E[C] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} E[I_{i,j}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} P(I_{i,j} = 1)$$ **Step 4 — Compute the probability of comparison.** Two elements $z_i, z_j$ are compared **iff** one of them is the first pivot chosen from the set $\{z_i, z_{i+1}, \dots, z_j\}$. This set has $j - i + 1$ elements, and each is equally likely to be the first pivot. $$P(I_{i,j} = 1) = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.** $$E[C] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$ **Step 6 — Re-index using $k = j - i$.** $$E[C] = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{k=1}^{n-i} \frac{2}{k+1}$$ **Step 7 — Swap summation order.** The sum of $\frac{2}{k+1}$ from $k=1$ to $k = n-i$ is bounded above by the harmonic number: $$E[C] \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \frac{2}{k+1} = (n-1) \cdot \sum_{k=2}^{n} \frac{2}{k} = 2(n-1) \cdot (H_n - 1)$$ **Step 8 — Asymptotic form.** Using $H_n = \ln n + \gamma + O(1/n)$ where $\gamma \approx 0.5772$: $$\boxed{E[C] = 2n \ln n - O(n) = O(n \log n)}$$ This is the celebrated result: **randomization eliminates the $O(n^2)$ worst-case** without needing the median-of-medians machinery. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chernoff Bound (Elementary Form) **Theorem Statement.** Let $X_1, \dots, X_n$ be independent Bernoulli trials with $P(X_i = 1) = p_i$. Let $X = \sum X_i$ and $\mu = E[X]$. Then for any $0 < \delta \leq 1$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq \left(\frac{e^\delta}{(1+\delta)^{(1+\delta)}}\right)^\mu \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3}$$ **Proof Outline (for KTU understanding):** **Step 1 — MGF method.** For any $t > 0$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) = P(e^{tX} \geq e^{t(1+\delta)\mu}) \leq \frac{E[e^{tX}]}{e^{t(1+\delta)\mu}}$$ where the inequality is Markov's. **Step 2 — Factor the MGF** (independence): $$E[e^{tX}] = \prod_{i=1}^n E[e^{tX_i}] = \prod_{i=1}^n \left(p_i e^t + (1-p_i)\right)$$ **Step 3 — Use $1 + x \leq e^x$ and the inequality $p e^t + (1-p) \leq \exp\left(p(e^t - 1)\right)$:** $$E[e^{tX}] \leq \exp\left(\mu(e^t - 1)\right)$$ **Step 4 — Combine and optimize over $t$.** Setting $t = \ln(1+\delta)$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq \frac{\exp\left(\mu(1+\delta - 1)\right)}{(1+\delta)^{(1+\delta)\mu}} = \left(\frac{e^\delta}{(1+\delta)^{(1+\delta)}}\right)^\mu$$ **Step 5 — Final bound** using $(1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta) \geq \delta + \delta^2/3$ for $\delta \in [0,1]$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Indicator Variable Toolkit | Random Process | Indicator $I$ | $E[I]$ | Used For | |---|---|---|---| | Pivot chosen from sub-array | $I = 1$ if first pivot in $\{z_i, \dots, z_j\}$ | $\frac{2}{j-i+1}$ | Quicksort analysis | | Hash collision | $I_h = 1$ if $h(x_i) = h(x_j)$ | $\frac{1}{m}$ for $m$ buckets | Universal hashing | | Edge selected in random graph | $I_e = 1$ if edge $e$ in $\mathcal{G}(n,p)$ | $p$ | MAX-CUT, sparse cuts | | Ball into bin | $I_b = 1$ if bin $b$ gets ball | $\frac{1}{m}$ | Load balancing | | Bit rounded up | $I_i = 1$ if $x_i \in [1/2, 1)$ rounded to 1 | $x_i$ | Randomized rounding of LP | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Classification Tree of Randomized Algorithms ```mermaid graph TD A["Randomized Algorithm A"]:::root --> B["By Output Correctness"] A --> C["By Running Time"] B --> D["Las Vegas
Always Correct"] B --> E["Monte Carlo
Correct w.p. p >= 1/2"] C --> F["Randomized Time
Expected T(n)"] C --> G["Deterministic Time
Worst-Case T(n)"] D --> H["Examples:
Randomized Quicksort
Random Pivot TSP"] E --> I["Examples:
Miller-Rabin Primality
Randomized MAX-CUT"] F --> J["Analyze via
Indicator Variables
+ Linearity of Expectation"] G --> K["Analyze via
Markov/Chernoff
Tail Bounds"] classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Randomized Approximation Algorithm ```mermaid graph LR IN["Input Instance I
e.g., Graph G = V,E"]:::input --> RD["Randomness Source
Uniform on 0,1^k"]:::random RD --> RS["Randomized Construction
Pick random subset S of V
Choose random pivot
Randomized rounding of LP"]:::process IN --> RS RS --> DP["Deterministic Post-Processing
Greedy extension
Local search
Verification"]:::process DP --> OUT["Output Solution y
Cost c and Approximation Ratio"]:::output DP --> Q{"Quality Check
c and lt or = rho times OPT"}:::decision Q -->|Pass| OUT Q -->|Fail| RE["Re-run with new
random string r"]:::retry RE --> RD classDef input fill:#2c5282,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef random fill:#b83227,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef process fill:#2f855a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef output fill:#744210,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#5a2d81,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef retry fill:#9b2c2c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Probability Flow Through a Las Vegas Algorithm ```mermaid graph TD S["Start: Input x"] --> F1["Flip Coin 1
Outcome in Omega"]:::coin F1 --> F2["Flip Coin 2"]:::coin F2 --> F3["Flip Coin k"]:::coin F3 --> P["Path pi in Computation Tree
Cost = T pi"]:::path P --> E["Correct Output f x"]:::end F1 -.Probability 1 to Omega 1.-> P F2 -.Probability 1 to Omega 2.-> P F3 -.Probability 1 to Omega k.-> P P --> ET["Expected Time = E T pi = sum over pi of T pi times Pr pi"]:::expect classDef coin fill:#b83227,color:#fff classDef path fill:#2f855a,color:#fff classDef end fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff classDef expect fill:#5a2d81,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Differentiate between Las Vegas and Monte Carlo randomized algorithms. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Las Vegas | Monte Carlo | |---|---|---| | Output | Always correct | Correct with probability $p \geq 1/2$ | | Running time | Random variable (analyzed in expectation) | Deterministic / bounded | | Worst-case time | Unbounded | Bounded | | Example | **Randomized Quicksort** | **Miller-Rabin Primality Test** | | Failure mode | Takes too long | Produces wrong answer | *(3 marks: 1 mark definition Las Vegas, 1 mark definition Monte Carlo, 1 mark examples)* ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State and prove Markov's inequality.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** For a non-negative random variable $X$ and any $a > 0$: $$P(X \geq a) \leq \frac{E[X]}{a}$$ **Proof:** For $a > 0$, the indicator function $\mathbf{1}_{\{X \geq a\}}$ satisfies $\mathbf{1}_{\{X \geq a\}} \leq \frac{X}{a}$. Taking expectation of both sides: $$E[\mathbf{1}_{\{X \geq a\}}] \leq \frac{E[X]}{a}$$ Since $E[\mathbf{1}_{\{X \geq a\}}] = P(X \geq a)$, the result follows. $\blacksquare$ *(3 marks: 1 mark statement, 2 marks proof)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a randomized approximation algorithm. Discuss the role of the random string $r$ and the deterministic post-processing step. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A randomized approximation algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ can be formally viewed as a function $f : \{0,1\}^* \times \{0,1\}^k \to \{0,1\}^*$ where the second argument is a uniformly random bit-string $r$ of length $k$. **Block Diagram (textual representation):** ``` [Input x] --+--> [Deterministic Core] --> [Output y = f(x, r)] | [Random r] -+ ``` **Components:** 1. **Input Layer:** Reads deterministic instance $x$ (graph, set system, metric). 2. **Randomness Source:** A *Random Bit Generator (RBG)* — physical or pseudo — providing $r \in \{0,1\}^k$ uniform. 3. **Randomized Construction Module:** Uses $r$ to make non-deterministic choices — e.g., sample a vertex, choose random order, random subset $S \subseteq V$, or random rounding of fractional LP solution $\tilde{x} \in [0,1]$. 4. **Deterministic Post-Processor:** Given the random choices, applies a deterministic rule (greedy extension, local search, feasibility repair). 5. **Output:** Returns solution $y$ with measured cost $c(y)$ satisfying $E[c(y)] \leq \rho \cdot \text{OPT}$ for some approximation ratio $\rho$. **Role of $r$:** Breaks the symmetry of the problem; defeats adversarial inputs; ensures that *no single input can be the worst case* across all possible $r$. The expected performance is over the uniform distribution of $r$. **Role of Post-Processing:** Guarantees feasibility (e.g., rounded LP solutions may need repair) and tightens the approximation ratio. *(7 marks: 2 architecture + 2 role of r + 2 role of post-processing + 1 neat explanation)* **(b)** Let $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ be independent Bernoulli random variables with $P(X_i = 1) = p_i$. Using the Chernoff bound, show that for $X = \sum X_i$ with $\mu = E[X]$ and any $0 < \delta < 1$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2 / 3}$$ **Outline the proof using the MGF method and Markov's inequality.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up the MGF bound:** For any $t > 0$, by Markov's inequality applied to the non-negative random variable $e^{tX}$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) = P(e^{tX} \geq e^{t(1+\delta)\mu}) \leq \frac{E[e^{tX}]}{e^{t(1+\delta)\mu}}$$ **Step 2 — Factor the MGF using independence:** $$E[e^{tX}] = E\left[\prod_{i=1}^n e^{tX_i}\right] = \prod_{i=1}^n E[e^{tX_i}] = \prod_{i=1}^n \left(p_i e^t + (1-p_i)\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the inequality $1 + u \leq e^u$:** For each $i$, $$p_i e^t + (1-p_i) = 1 + p_i(e^t - 1) \leq e^{p_i(e^t - 1)}$$ **Step 4 — Combine over all $i$:** $$E[e^{tX}] \leq \prod_{i=1}^n e^{p_i(e^t - 1)} = e^{\mu(e^t - 1)}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute back:** $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq e^{\mu(e^t - 1) - t(1+\delta)\mu} = e^{\mu(e^t - 1 - t(1+\delta))}$$ **Step 6 — Optimize over $t$:** Set $t^* = \ln(1+\delta)$, minimizing the exponent: $$e^t - 1 - t(1+\delta) = (1+\delta) - 1 - (1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta) = -\delta - (1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta)$$ Using the inequality $(1+\delta)\ln(1+\delta) \geq \delta + \delta^2/3$ for $0 < \delta < 1$: $$P(X \geq (1+\delta)\mu) \leq e^{-\mu \delta^2/3} \quad \blacksquare$$ *(7 marks: 1 setup + 2 factoring + 1 applying $e^u$ bound + 1 substitution + 1 optimization + 1 final form)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** Define a randomized algorithm. Explain with examples why a randomized algorithm can outperform a deterministic one. Discuss the concept of *derandomization*. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** A randomized algorithm is one whose behavior is determined not only by its input but also by values produced by a *random number generator*. Formally, for input $x$, the output $Y = \mathcal{A}(x, r)$ where $r$ is uniform on $\{0,1\}^k$. **Why Randomization Helps (with examples):** 1. **Quicksort vs. Deterministic Quicksort:** A deterministic Quicksort that always picks the first element as pivot takes $O(n^2)$ on a sorted input. Randomized Quicksort picks a random pivot, achieving $E[T] = O(n \log n)$ on *every* input. 2. **MAX-CUT:** The deterministic greedy gives a $\frac{1}{2}$-approximation. The randomized algorithm (place each vertex in $S$ or $\bar{S}$ with probability 1/2 each) achieves expected cut size $\frac{m}{2}$ which is at least $\frac{1}{2} \text{OPT}$ — and with a small modification achieves $0.878$ via the Goemans-Williamson SDP refinement. 3. **Hashing:** A deterministic hash function can be attacked by an adversary choosing inputs that all collide. Universal hashing (random from a family) gives expected $O(1)$ per lookup regardless of input. 4. **Primality Testing:** Deterministic polynomial-time algorithms exist (AKS, 2002) but are slow. Miller-Rabin is fast and practically correct with probability $\geq 1 - 4^{-k}$ after $k$ rounds. **Derandomization:** The process of converting a randomized algorithm into a deterministic one with comparable guarantees. The two main methods are: - **Method of Conditional Probabilities:** Maintains a partial assignment of random bits that keeps the conditional expectation bounded; deterministically chooses each bit to keep the expectation below the threshold. - **Pairwise Independence:** Replace fully independent samples with pairwise-independent samples (which can be generated deterministically using $O(\log n)$ truly random bits). *(7 marks: 1 definition + 3 examples + 2 derandomization methods + 1 conclusion)* **(b)** Consider the **randomized MAX-CUT algorithm**: given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $m$ edges and $n$ vertices, place each vertex $v \in V$ independently into set $S$ or $\bar{S}$ with probability $1/2$ each. Show that the expected size of the cut is at least $m/2$. Use the indicator-variable technique. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Setup:** Let $(S, \bar{S})$ be the random partition. For each edge $e = (u,v) \in E$, define the indicator random variable: $$I_e = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if edge } e \text{ is cut, i.e., } u \text{ and } v \text{ in different sets} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The size of the cut is $C = \sum_{e \in E} I_e$. **Step 1 — Apply Linearity of Expectation:** $$E[C] = \sum_{e \in E} E[I_e]$$ **Step 2 — Compute $E[I_e] = P(\text{edge } e \text{ is cut})$.** Since $u$ and $v$ are placed independently, the four possible placements $(S, \bar{S})$, $(\bar{S}, S)$, $(S, S)$, $(\bar{S}, \bar{S})$ each occur with probability $1/4$. The edge is cut in exactly 2 of these 4 cases. $$P(I_e = 1) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Sum over all $m$ edges:** $$E[C] = \sum_{e \in E} \frac{1}{2} = \frac{m}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Conclude.** Since the optimal cut $\text{OPT} \leq m$ (an edge can be in a cut at most once): $$E[C] = \frac{m}{2} \geq \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}$$ Thus the randomized algorithm achieves an *expected* $\frac{1}{2}$-approximation for MAX-CUT. $\blacksquare$ *(7 marks: 1 setup + 1 linearity + 1 probability + 1 sum + 2 conclusion + 1 final bound)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones in KTU 2024 Board Exams for this topic:** > 1. **Confusing Las Vegas vs Monte Carlo** — many students swap the examples. *Tip:* Las Vegas = variable **T**ime; Monte Carlo = variable **T**ruth (output). > 2. **Forgetting to apply Linearity of Expectation when variables are DEPENDENT** — actually LOE works for *any* dependence, but students wrongly assume independence. Always cite it. > 3. **Misapplying the Chernoff bound** — it requires **independent** $X_i$ (or negatively associated). Don't use it on positively-correlated sums. > 4. **In indicator-variable proofs:** forgetting to define the indicator *clearly* and *first*. Examiners explicitly check this. > 5. **Confusing $E[X]$ with $\max X$** — expected value is *not* a worst-case bound; do not write "the algorithm always finishes in $E[T]$ time." > 6. **Skipping the random string $r$ in the formal definition** — every full-mark answer must mention $r \in \{0,1\}^k$ uniform. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Randomization Concept):** > - Randomized algorithm = deterministic algorithm + uniform random bit-string $r \in \{0,1\}^k$. > - **Las Vegas** = correct output, random time. **Monte Carlo** = bounded time, probabilistic correctness. > - **Five core tools:** Indicator variables, Linearity of Expectation, Markov's Inequality, Chernoff Bound, Union Bound. > - **Indicator trick:** $E[I_A] = P(A)$ — bridge between probability and expectation. > - **LOE works always** — even for dependent random variables. > - **Chernoff bound** requires independence; gives *exponential* concentration around the mean $\mu$. > - **Markov's inequality** is universal (no independence) but gives a *weaker* polynomial bound. > - Randomization defeats adversarial inputs — no input is worst-case across all $r$. > - **Derandomization methods:** Conditional Expectations and Pairwise Independence. > - **Canonical examples:** Randomized Quicksort ($O(n \log n)$ expected), MAX-CUT (1/2-approx), Miller-Rabin primality, Universal Hashing, Randomized Rounding of LP (Raghavan-Thomson). > - The expected cost of randomized Quicksort is exactly $2n \ln n + O(n)$ using $H_n = \ln n + \gamma$. > - The randomized MAX-CUT bound is $E[\text{cut}] = m/2 \geq \text{OPT}/2$. > - Randomization is a **bridge** to approximation — many NP-hard problems admit *better* expected ratios than deterministic ones.

topics.title: Randomized Rounding - Set Cover Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Randomized Rounding - Set Cover Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Set Cover Problem (SCP)

The **Set Cover Problem (SCP)** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem formally defined as follows.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Set Cover):**
> Given a finite universe $\mathcal{U} = \{e_1, e_2, \dots, e_n\}$ and a collection of $m$ subsets $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \dots, S_m\}$ such that $S_i \subseteq \mathcal{U}$, find a sub-collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ of **minimum cardinality** such that the union of the chosen sets equals the universe, i.e.,
> $$\bigcup_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}} S_i = \mathcal{U}.$$

The decision version of SCP is **NP-Complete**, and the optimization version is **NP-Hard**. Therefore, we resort to approximation algorithms.

## 1.2 Randomized Rounding — The Concept

**Randomized Rounding** is a general technique used in approximation algorithms where:

1. We first **relax** the discrete (integral) optimization problem into a continuous one (a Linear Program, LP).
2. We solve the LP to obtain an **optimal fractional solution** $x^* \in [0, 1]^m$.
3. We then **round** each fractional variable $x_i^*$ to an integer $\{0, 1\}$ using a **randomized** rule — typically by setting $x_i = 1$ with probability $x_i^*$ and $x_i = 0$ with probability $1 - x_i^*$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The probability of selecting each set $S_i$ is **directly proportional to its LP value** $x_i^*$. Higher fractional values $\Rightarrow$ higher selection chance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are the **manager of a fire station network** and you need to cover all $n$ neighborhoods in a city. You have $m$ possible stations to build, each at a certain cost. The LP solver tells you: *"Build station 1 at 70% scale, station 2 at 50% scale, station 3 at 20% scale..."* — but you cannot build a station at fractional scale.

**Randomized Rounding** flips a biased coin for each station: station $i$ is built with probability $x_i^*$. So station 1 (70%) gets built in about 70 out of 100 trials. This translates the "soft" LP recommendation into a **hard yes/no decision**, while preserving the **expected size** of the solution.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The expected number of selected sets in one trial equals the LP value. We repeat the process to guarantee full coverage of the universe.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Randomized Rounding for Set Cover

- **Approximation Ratio:** $O(\log n)$ where $n = \vert \mathcal{U} \vert$.
- **Coverage Probability per trial:** $\geq 1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.6321$.
- **Repetition factor:** $t = \lceil \ln n \rceil$ trials ensure coverage with high probability.
- **Chernoff Bound parameter** $\delta = 1$ typically used for amplification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Integer Program (IP) Formulation

The exact SCP can be expressed as an Integer Linear Program. For each set $S_i$, define a binary indicator variable:

$$x_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if set } S_i \text{ is selected in the cover} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The **IP** is:

$$\text{(IP)} \quad \min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \; \forall e_j \in \mathcal{U}, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}.$$

## 2.2 The LP Relaxation

By relaxing $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ to $x_i \in [0, 1]$, we obtain the LP relaxation:

$$\text{(LP)} \quad \min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \; \forall e_j \in \mathcal{U}, \quad 0 \leq x_i \leq 1.$$

Let $x^* = (x_1^*, x_2^*, \dots, x_m^*)$ be the optimal LP solution with value $\text{LP}^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Property:** $\text{LP}^* \leq \text{OPT}$, where $\text{OPT}$ is the optimal integer solution. This is because the IP's feasible region is a subset of the LP's feasible region.

## 2.3 The Randomized Rounding Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Solve LP**: Compute $x^*$ optimally. Record $\text{LP}^* = \sum_i x_i^*$.
2. **Round**: For each set $S_i$ independently, set $\hat{x}_i = 1$ with probability $x_i^*$, else $\hat{x}_i = 0$.
3. **Collect**: Let $\hat{\mathcal{C}} = \{S_i \mid \hat{x}_i = 1\}$.
4. **Cleanup**: For every element $e \in \mathcal{U}$ not covered by $\hat{\mathcal{C}}$, add all sets containing $e$ to $\hat{\mathcal{C}}$.
5. **Repeat** (Amplification): Run steps 2–4 for $t = c \ln n$ independent rounds; output the **smallest** cover found.

## 2.4 Why Each Step Works — The "Why & How"

- **Why Solve LP First?** The LP gives a globally optimized lower bound, encoding fractional "sharing" of coverage responsibility.
- **Why Use Probability $x_i^*$?** It preserves the **expected** size of the solution: $\mathbb{E}[\sum \hat{x}_i] = \sum x_i^* = \text{LP}^*$.
- **Why the Cleanup Step?** A single round may miss elements. Adding missing sets ensures feasibility (full coverage).
- **Why Repeat $O(\log n)$ Times?** Repetition allows us to apply probabilistic amplification (union bound + Chernoff), shrinking the failure probability to exponentially small values.

## 2.5 The Key Inequality — Coverage Probability

For any element $e \in \mathcal{U}$, the LP constraint guarantees:

$$\sum_{i \,:\, e \in S_i} x_i^* \geq 1.$$

Since each $x_i^* \leq 1$, the probability that **none** of the sets containing $e$ is selected in one trial is:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) = \prod_{i \,:\, e \in S_i} (1 - x_i^*).$$

By the **AM-GM inequality**, this is maximized (worst case) when all $x_i^* = \frac{1}{k}$ for $k = \vert \{i : e \in S_i\} \vert$:

$$\prod_{i \,:\, e \in S_i} (1 - x_i^*) \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^k \leq \frac{1}{e}.$$

Hence $P(e \text{ covered}) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{e}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important inequality for the Set Cover randomized rounding analysis at KTU exams.**

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| $\min \sum_i x_i$ | IP/LP objective | Direct question worth 7 marks |
| $\sum_{i: e \in S_i} x_i \geq 1$ | Coverage constraint | Central constraint |
| $x_i^* \in [0,1]$ | LP fractional variable | Distinguishes LP from IP |
| $P(\hat{x}_i = 1) = x_i^*$ | Rounding rule | Core rounding step |
| $\mathbb{E}[\sum_i \hat{x}_i] = \text{LP}^*$ | Expected cost preservation | Key proof idea |
| $\prod (1 - x_i^*) \leq \frac{1}{e}$ | AM-GM coverage bound | Most cited inequality |
| $P(e \text{ cov}) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{e}$ | Per-element coverage | Used with union bound |
| $t = \lceil c \ln n \rceil$ | Number of repetitions | Amplification rounds |
| $\text{Approx Ratio} = O(\log n)$ | Final guarantee | KTU often asks this |
| $1 - (1 - p)^t \geq 1 - \frac{1}{n^c}$ | Amplified coverage | After $t = c \ln n$ rounds |
| $P(\hat{x}_i = 0) = 1 - x_i^*$ | Rounding complement | Used in product |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Set Cover formulation and its randomized rounding technique are used in:

- **Telecommunications**: Minimum number of cell towers to cover all users.
- **VLSI Design**: Minimum set of test patterns to cover all circuit faults.
- **Bioinformatics**: Selecting minimum probe sets for DNA microarrays.
- **Network Security**: Minimum number of sensors/monitors to cover all assets.
- **Cloud Computing**: Minimum VM instances to satisfy diverse SLA requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Size of the Rounded Solution

We want to compute $\mathbb{E}[\vert \hat{\mathcal{C}} \vert] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{x}_i\right]$.

**Linearity of Expectation** allows us to swap sum and expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{E}[\hat{x}_i].$$

Since $\hat{x}_i$ is a Bernoulli random variable with success probability $x_i^*$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{x}_i] = 1 \cdot P(\hat{x}_i = 1) + 0 \cdot P(\hat{x}_i = 0) = x_i^*.$$

Substituting back:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i^* = \text{LP}^*.$$

> **Conclusion:** The expected number of selected sets equals the LP optimum, which is $\leq$ OPT.

## 3.2 Derivation: AM-GM Bound on Uncovered Probability

For element $e$, let $\mathcal{I}(e) = \{i : e \in S_i\}$ be the index set of all sets containing $e$. The LP constraint states:

$$\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} x_i^* \geq 1, \quad \text{with} \quad 0 \leq x_i^* \leq 1.$$

We want to upper-bound:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) = \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*).$$

Let $k = \vert \mathcal{I}(e) \vert$. Apply the **AM-GM inequality** to the terms $(1 - x_i^*)$:

$$\left(\prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*)\right)^{1/k} \leq \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*) = 1 - \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} x_i^*.$$

Since $\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} x_i^* \geq 1$:

$$\prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*) \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^k.$$

Finally, by the standard limit $\left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^k \leq \frac{1}{e}$ for all $k \geq 1$:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) \leq \frac{1}{e} \implies P(e \text{ covered}) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{e}.$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Amplification via Repetition

After one trial, $P(e \text{ uncovered}) \leq \frac{1}{e}$. After $t$ independent trials, since trials are independent:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered in all } t \text{ trials}) \leq \left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^t = e^{-t}.$$

Choose $t = \lceil c \ln n \rceil$ for some constant $c$:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered in all } t \text{ trials}) \leq e^{-c \ln n} = n^{-c}.$$

By the **Union Bound** over all $n$ elements:

$$P(\text{at least one element uncovered}) \leq \sum_{e \in \mathcal{U}} n^{-c} = n \cdot n^{-c} = n^{1-c}.$$

> For $c \geq 2$, this failure probability is $\leq \frac{1}{n}$, i.e., exponentially small.

## 3.4 Derivation: Final Approximation Ratio

After $t = c \ln n$ independent rounds, take the **smallest** cover found. With high probability, this cover has size:

$$\text{ALG} \leq t \cdot \text{LP}^* = O(\log n) \cdot \text{LP}^* \leq O(\log n) \cdot \text{OPT}.$$

Therefore, the **Randomized Rounding Algorithm achieves an $O(\log n)$ approximation ratio** for the Set Cover problem.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Instance:** $\mathcal{U} = \{a, b, c, d\}$, $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3\}$ where:
- $S_1 = \{a, b\}$, $S_2 = \{b, c, d\}$, $S_3 = \{a, d\}$.

**Step 1: Solve LP.** Assume the LP solver returns $x^* = (0.5, 0.5, 0.5)$ with $\text{LP}^* = 1.5$.

**Step 2: Single trial rounding.** Flip three independent coins with bias $0.5$:

| Trial | $\hat{x}_1$ | $\hat{x}_2$ | $\hat{x}_3$ | Selected Sets | Covered Elements |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | $S_1, S_3$ | $\{a, b, d\}$ — **misses $c$** |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | $S_2, S_3$ | $\{a, b, c, d\}$ — **full cover, size 2** |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | $S_1, S_2$ | $\{a, b, c, d\}$ — **full cover, size 2** |

> [!TIP]
> Notice that size = 2 satisfies the $O(\log 4) \approx 2$ approximation bound, since $\text{OPT} = 2$ (e.g., $\{S_1, S_2\}$).

**Step 3: Cleanup rule.** In Trial 1, element $c$ is uncovered — algorithmically, we add the cheapest single set containing $c$ (here $S_2$), increasing the size by 1.

**Step 4: Final Answer.** The minimum cover among $t$ trials is selected; with high probability over $O(\log n)$ trials, we obtain a cover of size $\leq 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Trade-offs

| Property | LP Solution | Randomized Rounded Solution | Greedy (Chvátal) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Fractional | Integral | Integral |
| Approx. Ratio | $1$ (lower bound) | $O(\log n)$ | $H_n \approx \ln n$ |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Randomized | Deterministic |
| Running Time | Polynomial | Polynomial | Polynomial |
| Coverage Guarantee | Fractional | Probabilistic | Deterministic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input Universe U and Sets S]) --> lpStep[Solve LP Relaxation]
    lpStep --> lpSol[Obtain Fractional Solution x-star]
    lpSol --> lpVal[Compute LP-star = sum of x_i-star]
    lpVal --> initLoop[Initialize Round r = 1]
    initLoop --> coinFlip{For each set S_i, flip biased coin with P=1 equals x_i-star}
    coinFlip --> select[Mark S_i as selected with probability x_i-star]
    select --> buildCover[Build Trial Cover C-hat in this round]
    buildCover --> checkCov{All elements of U covered?}
    checkCov -- No --> cleanup[Add missing elements via deterministic sets]
    cleanup --> nextRound
    checkCov -- Yes --> nextRound[Increment round r = r + 1]
    nextRound --> testCond{r less than c times ln n?}
    testCond -- Yes --> coinFlip
    testCond -- No --> pickMin[Pick smallest C-hat over all rounds]
    pickMin --> output([Output: Final Set Cover C with size O log n times OPT])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputLayer[Input Layer]
        U_Node[Universe U with n elements]
        S_Node[Collection of m sets S_1 to S_m]
    end

    subgraph solverLayer[Solver Layer]
        LP_Node[LP Relaxation Solver]
        Frac_Node[Optimal Fractional x-star]
    end

    subgraph roundingLayer[Rounding Layer]
        RNG_Node[Random Number Generator]
        Prob_Node[Probability Mapper P-hat-x-i = x-i-star]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer[Output Layer]
        C_Node[Final Set Cover C]
        Cost_Node[Cost Estimator O log n times OPT]
    end

    U_Node --> LP_Node
    S_Node --> LP_Node
    LP_Node --> Frac_Node
    Frac_Node --> Prob_Node
    RNG_Node --> Prob_Node
    Prob_Node --> C_Node
    C_Node --> Cost_Node
```

## 4.3 Probability Coverage Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Elem[Element e in U] --> LPCon[LP Constraint: sum x-i-star greater than or equal 1]
    LPCon --> AMGM[Apply AM-GM Inequality]
    AMGM --> Bound[Upper bound: 1 over e approximately 0.3679]
    Bound --> PerTrial[Per-trial coverage: at least 0.6321]
    PerTrial --> Repeats[Repeat t = c ln n times]
    Repeats --> Amplified[Amplified coverage: at least 1 minus 1 over n to the c]
    Amplified --> WHP[C over e uncovered with high probability]
```

## 4.4 IP-LP-Rounding Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IP[Integer Program IP] -->|Relax x_i in 0 1 to x_i in 0 1| LP[Linear Program LP]
    LP -->|Solve using simplex or interior point| OptLP[Optimal LP Solution x-star with LP-star]
    OptLP -->|Apply randomized rounding| Frac[Fractional to Binary]
    Frac -->|Repeat t = c ln n times| Trial[Independent Trials]
    Trial -->|Take minimum| Final[Final Approximate Cover]
    IP -.->|OPT greater than or equal to LP-star| OptLP
    Final -.->|size O log n times OPT| Ratio[Approximation Ratio O log n]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the Set Cover Problem and write its Integer Linear Program formulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Set Cover Problem (SCP)** is: given a universe $\mathcal{U} = \{e_1, \dots, e_n\}$ and a family of subsets $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, \dots, S_m\}$ with $S_i \subseteq \mathcal{U}$, find the minimum cardinality subfamily $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$ such that $\bigcup_{S_i \in \mathcal{C}} S_i = \mathcal{U}$.

**ILP Formulation (2 marks):**

$$\min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \; \forall j = 1, \dots, n, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}.$$

Here $x_i = 1$ indicates set $S_i$ is chosen, and the constraint ensures every element $e_j$ is in at least one chosen set.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**What is randomized rounding? Why is it used in approximation algorithms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Randomized rounding is a technique where a fractional solution $x_i^* \in [0,1]$ of an LP relaxation is converted into an integral solution $\hat{x}_i \in \{0, 1\}$ by setting $\hat{x}_i = 1$ with probability $x_i^*$ (and $0$ otherwise) independently for each variable.

**Why used (1 mark):** NP-hard problems cannot be solved exactly in polynomial time. Randomized rounding yields **probabilistically feasible** integral solutions whose **expected cost equals the LP optimum**, giving provable approximation guarantees (e.g., $O(\log n)$ for Set Cover).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students commonly **forget to mention the AM-GM inequality** when bounding the uncovered probability — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> - Always state **$\text{LP}^* \leq \text{OPT}$** explicitly before using it in the approximation ratio.
> - The **cleanup/amplification step** is mandatory; omitting it forfeits the coverage feasibility argument (loses 2–3 marks).
> - The factor $O(\log n)$ arises from $t = c \ln n$ **repetitions**, not from a single trial — examiners test this distinction strictly.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Formulate the **Set Cover Problem** as an Integer Linear Program and derive its **LP Relaxation**. Show that $\text{LP}^* \leq \text{OPT}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Randomized Rounding algorithm** for Set Cover in detail. Prove that the expected number of sets selected in one trial equals $\text{LP}^*$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IP and LP Relaxation (7 marks):**

For each set $S_i$, define $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ as the selection indicator. The IP is:

$$\min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \; \forall e_j \in \mathcal{U}, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}.$$

**LP Relaxation:** Replace $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$:

$$\min \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i \,:\, e_j \in S_i} x_i \geq 1 \; \forall e_j \in \mathcal{U}, \quad 0 \leq x_i \leq 1.$$

**Proof of $\text{LP}^* \leq \text{OPT}$ (4 marks):**

Let $x^{\text{IP}}$ be the optimal integral solution with value $\text{OPT}$. Since $x^{\text{IP}}$ has $x_i \in \{0,1\} \subset [0,1]$, it is a **feasible solution** of the LP. The LP's optimum $\text{LP}^*$ is the minimum over a **larger feasible region** (including fractional points), hence:

$$\text{LP}^* \leq \text{value}(x^{\text{IP}}) = \text{OPT}.$$

---

**(b) Randomized Rounding Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm steps (3 marks):**
1. Solve LP, get $x^* = (x_1^*, \dots, x_m^*)$ with value $\text{LP}^*$.
2. For each $i \in \{1, \dots, m\}$ independently, set $\hat{x}_i = 1$ with probability $x_i^*$.
3. Output $\hat{\mathcal{C}} = \{S_i : \hat{x}_i = 1\}$.
4. (Cleanup) For uncovered elements, add covering sets.
5. (Amplification) Repeat for $t = c \ln n$ rounds and return the smallest cover.

**Proof of $\mathbb{E}[\vert \hat{\mathcal{C}} \vert] = \text{LP}^*$ (4 marks):**

By linearity of expectation:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{E}[\hat{x}_i].$$

Since $\hat{x}_i$ is Bernoulli with parameter $x_i^*$:

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{x}_i] = 1 \cdot x_i^* + 0 \cdot (1 - x_i^*) = x_i^*.$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{m} \hat{x}_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{m} x_i^* = \text{LP}^*.$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** For the Set Cover problem, show that the probability an element $e \in \mathcal{U}$ remains uncovered after **one trial** of randomized rounding is at most $\frac{1}{e}$. Use this to bound the per-trial coverage probability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result of (a), prove that by repeating the rounding $t = c \ln n$ times, the algorithm achieves an $O(\log n)$ approximation ratio with high probability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coverage Probability Bound (7 marks):**

Let $\mathcal{I}(e) = \{i : e \in S_i\}$ be the set of indices of sets containing $e$, with $\vert \mathcal{I}(e) \vert = k$. The LP constraint gives:

$$\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} x_i^* \geq 1, \quad 0 \leq x_i^* \leq 1.$$

The probability that **none** of these sets is selected:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) = \prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*).$$

Apply the **AM-GM inequality**:

$$\left(\prod_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*)\right)^{1/k} \leq \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} (1 - x_i^*) = 1 - \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{I}(e)} x_i^* \leq 1 - \frac{1}{k}.$$

Raising to the $k$-th power:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) \leq \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)^k \leq \frac{1}{e}.$$

Hence:

$$P(e \text{ covered}) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.6321.$$

---

**(b) Amplification and $O(\log n)$ Approximation (7 marks):**

After $t$ independent trials, by independence:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered in all } t \text{ trials}) \leq \left(\frac{1}{e}\right)^t = e^{-t}.$$

Set $t = c \ln n$ for constant $c \geq 2$:

$$P(e \text{ uncovered}) \leq e^{-c \ln n} = n^{-c}.$$

By the **union bound** over all $n$ elements:

$$P(\exists e \text{ uncovered}) \leq n \cdot n^{-c} = n^{1-c} \leq \frac{1}{n}.$$

The expected size of each trial's cover is $\text{LP}^*$. The total size over $t$ trials is at most $t \cdot \text{LP}^*$. Taking the minimum over rounds:

$$\text{ALG} \leq c \ln n \cdot \text{LP}^* \leq c \ln n \cdot \text{OPT} = O(\log n) \cdot \text{OPT}.$$

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** With probability $\geq 1 - \frac{1}{n}$, the algorithm returns a set cover of size $O(\log n) \cdot \text{OPT}$, achieving an $O(\log n)$ randomized approximation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **SCP Definition**: Minimize number of sets covering all elements — **NP-Hard**.
- ✅ **IP Formulation**: $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with coverage constraints $\sum_{i: e \in S_i} x_i \geq 1$.
- ✅ **LP Relaxation**: Replace $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ with $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Key Inequality**: $\text{LP}^* \leq \text{OPT}$ (LP feasible region superset).
- ✅ **Rounding Rule**: $\hat{x}_i = 1$ w.p. $x_i^*$, else $0$.
- ✅ **Expected Size**: $\mathbb{E}[\vert \hat{\mathcal{C}} \vert] = \text{LP}^*$ (linearity of expectation).
- ✅ **AM-GM Bound**: $P(\text{uncovered}) \leq \frac{1}{e}$; coverage $\geq 1 - \frac{1}{e}$.
- ✅ **Repetitions**: $t = c \ln n$ rounds give $P(\text{success}) \geq 1 - \frac{1}{n}$.
- ✅ **Final Approximation**: $O(\log n)$ — log factor arises from **repetitions**, not single trial.
- ✅ **Cleanup Step**: Always required for full coverage in worst case.
- ✅ **Union Bound**: Used to aggregate per-element failures to overall success probability.
- ✅ **Real-World Apps**: Telecom tower placement, VLSI testing, bioinformatics.
- ✅ **Compare with Greedy**: Both give $O(\log n)$ — but randomized is parallelizable; greedy is deterministic.

topics.title: Randomized Algorithm for MAX CUT Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Randomized Algorithm for MAX CUT Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **MAX CUT Problem (Optimization Form)**  
> Given an undirected, unweighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n = \vert V \vert$ vertices and $m = \vert E \vert$ edges, the **MAX CUT** problem asks for a partition of the vertex set $V$ into two disjoint subsets $S$ and $\bar{S} = V \setminus S$ such that the number of edges with one endpoint in $S$ and the other in $\bar{S}$ is **maximized**.

Formally, a **cut** $\delta(S)$ is defined as:

$$\delta(S) = \left\{ (u, v) \in E : u \in S \text{ and } v \in \bar{S} \right\}$$

The objective is to compute:

$$\text{OPT}(G) = \max_{S \subseteq V} \vert \delta(S) \vert$$

The **decision version** of MAX CUT (Is $\vert \delta(S) \vert \geq k$?) is **NP-Complete**, hence a polynomial-time exact solution is not expected unless $P = NP$. This places MAX CUT in the class of canonical NP-hard optimization problems that *require* approximation or randomized strategies.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Distinction from MIN CUT:** MAX CUT seeks the *largest* crossing edge set (NP-hard), whereas MIN CUT (source–sink connectivity) is solvable in polynomial time via max-flow/min-cut duality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **social network** of $n$ people (vertices) where friendships (edges) need to be divided into two opposing teams. The goal is to make as many friendships as possible **cross-team** friendships (cut edges), because friendly ties across rival groups cause the most "drama" or social tension.

A **randomized algorithm** for this is equivalent to flipping an unbiased coin for each person — *Heads* → put them in Team A, *Tails* → put them in Team B. Randomly dividing people already creates, **on average**, half of all friendships as cross-team ones. The "trick" of the algorithm is that even though *some* random partitions may be terrible, the **expected** cut size is provably large, giving a guaranteed quality ratio.

> [!TIP]  
> The expected value being a **lower bound** on the optimum is the golden key here. Since $\text{OPT} \leq m$ trivially, the expected value $m/2$ already implies a 2-approximation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Approximation Ratio** of the algorithm: $\rho = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[\vert \delta(S) \vert]}{\text{OPT}(G)} \geq \dfrac{1}{2}$.
- **Expected Cut Size**: $\mathbb{E}[\vert \delta(S) \vert] = \dfrac{\vert E \vert}{2} = \dfrac{m}{2}$.
- **Trivial Upper Bound**: $\text{OPT}(G) \leq m$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

The randomized MAX CUT algorithm is the simplest nontrivial randomized algorithm in approximation theory. Its operation can be broken into the following logical steps:

1. **Input Phase**: Receive the graph $G = (V, E)$ in adjacency list or adjacency matrix form.
2. **Independent Random Assignment**: For each vertex $v_i \in V$, independently and uniformly at random assign a label:
   $$\sigma(v_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{with probability } p = \tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \text{with probability } q = \tfrac{1}{2} \end{cases}$$
3. **Partition Construction**: Define the cut as $S = \{ v \in V : \sigma(v) = 1 \}$ and $\bar{S} = \{ v \in V : \sigma(v) = 0 \}$.
4. **Cut Size Computation**: Count the edges $(u, v) \in E$ such that $\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)$.
5. **Output**: The partition $(S, \bar{S})$ along with $\vert \delta(S) \vert$.

### 2.1.1 The 'Why' Behind the Design

- The **Why**: Why probability $1/2$? It maximizes entropy and ensures the expected contribution of every edge is exactly $1/2$ — there is no bias that could systematically favor one side.
- The **How**: How do we analyze it? Using the **linearity of expectation** on indicator random variables for each edge. This bypasses the need to know the joint distribution of the cut size.

## 2.2 Indicator Variable Setup (Foundation of the Analysis)

For each edge $e = (u, v) \in E$, define the **Bernoulli indicator variable**:

$$X_e = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } e \text{ is a cut edge (i.e., } \sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The total number of cut edges is the sum of these independent (in expectation, but not strictly) random variables:

$$\vert \delta(S) \vert = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why Linearity of Expectation is the Central Tool:** The $X_e$ variables are *not mutually independent* because the labeling of a shared vertex influences two edges. However, **linearity of expectation holds unconditionally**, regardless of independence. This is the mathematical heart of the proof.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Cut Definition | $\delta(S) = \{(u,v) \in E : u \in S, v \in \bar{S}\}$ | Set of edges crossing the partition |
| 2 | Cut Size | $\vert \delta(S) \vert = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$ | Cardinality of the cut |
| 3 | Indicator Probability | $\Pr[X_e = 1] = \Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)]$ | For independent fair coin: equals $1/2$ |
| 4 | Edge Crossing Probability | $\Pr[X_e = 1] = 2 \cdot p \cdot q$ | For arbitrary $p$ assignment |
| 5 | Expected Cut Size | $\mathbb{E}[\vert \delta(S) \vert] = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e] = \dfrac{m}{2}$ | Linearity of expectation |
| 6 | Approximation Ratio | $\rho = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[\vert \delta(S) \vert]}{\text{OPT}(G)} \geq \dfrac{1}{2}$ | Lower bound on quality |
| 7 | Trivial Upper Bound | $\text{OPT}(G) \leq m$ | Every edge is at most one cut edge |
| 8 | Derandomization via Conditional Expectation | $\mathbb{E}[Z \mid \sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_i] \geq \mathbb{E}[Z]$ | Greedy deterministic assignment |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Circuit Design**: Partitioning circuits into two regions to minimize inter-region wire crossings (dual problem in minimization form).
- **Graph Partitioning in Parallel Computing**: Load balancing for distributed processing.
- **Statistical Physics (Ising Model)**: MAX CUT corresponds to finding the **ground state energy** of an antiferromagnetic Ising spin system — a deep analogy used in physics-based computing (e.g., quantum annealing with D-Wave).
- **Social Network Analysis**: Detecting polarized community structures.
- **Data Clustering**: Spectral clustering and bipartition heuristics in unsupervised learning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Expected Cut Size

> [!NOTE]  
> **Theorem (Randomized MAX CUT Guarantee).** For any graph $G = (V, E)$, the randomized algorithm that places each vertex independently into $S$ or $\bar{S}$ with probability $1/2$ produces a cut with expected size at least $\dfrac{m}{2}$. Hence it is a $\dfrac{1}{2}$-approximation algorithm.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Define Indicator Random Variable for Each Edge**

For each edge $e = (u, v) \in E$, let $X_e$ be a random variable defined as:

$$X_e = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if exactly one of } u, v \text{ lies in } S \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Express Total Cut Size as a Sum of Indicators**

The total number of cut edges in the random partition equals the sum of these indicators over all edges:

$$Z = \vert \delta(S) \vert = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$$

**Step 3: Apply Linearity of Expectation**

The expectation of the total cut size is:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{e \in E} X_e\right]$$

Using **linearity of expectation** (which holds for any collection of random variables, independent or not):

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e]$$

**Step 4: Compute the Per-Edge Expectation**

For a single edge $e = (u, v)$, the variable $X_e = 1$ precisely when the labels of $u$ and $v$ differ. Since each label is chosen independently and uniformly:

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) = 1] = \Pr[\sigma(u) = 0] = \dfrac{1}{2}$$
$$\Pr[\sigma(v) = 1] = \Pr[\sigma(v) = 0] = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

The probability that the labels differ is computed by enumerating the four equally likely outcomes $(1,1), (1,0), (0,1), (0,0)$:

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)] = \Pr[(\sigma(u), \sigma(v)) \in \{(1,0), (0,1)\}]$$

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)] = \Pr[\sigma(u)=1] \cdot \Pr[\sigma(v)=0] + \Pr[\sigma(u)=0] \cdot \Pr[\sigma(v)=1]$$

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)] = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the Per-Edge Expectation Back**

Since $X_e$ is a Bernoulli random variable:

$$\mathbb{E}[X_e] = 1 \cdot \Pr[X_e = 1] + 0 \cdot \Pr[X_e = 0] = \Pr[X_e = 1] = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e] = \sum_{e \in E} \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{e \in E} 1 = \dfrac{m}{2}$$

**Step 6: Establish the Approximation Ratio**

Since the optimum is trivially bounded above:

$$\text{OPT}(G) \leq m \quad \text{(no more than all edges can be cut)}$$

Therefore the expected cut size gives a lower bound on OPT:

$$\text{OPT}(G) \geq \mathbb{E}[Z] = \dfrac{m}{2}$$

This implies the algorithm achieves an **expected approximation ratio**:

$$\rho = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[Z]}{\text{OPT}(G)} \geq \dfrac{m/2}{m} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Hence the algorithm is a **2-approximation** in expectation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Extension: General Probability Assignment

If we generalize to a vertex-specific probability $p_v$ for placing $v \in S$:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \left[ p_u(1 - p_v) + (1 - p_u) p_v \right] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \left[ p_u + p_v - 2p_u p_v \right]$$

This formula is used in **semidefinite programming (SDP) based MAX CUT algorithms** (Goemans–Williamson), where $p_v$ is replaced by $\frac{1 - \cos \theta_v}{2}$ for angles on a unit circle.

## 3.3 Derandomization via the Method of Conditional Expectations

> [!NOTE]  
> **Derandomization Principle:** A randomized algorithm with guaranteed expectation can be converted into a deterministic one by fixing the random bits *one at a time* to the value that preserves (or increases) the conditional expectation.

The conditional expectation after fixing labels $\sigma(v_1), \sigma(v_2), \ldots, \sigma(v_k)$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[Z \,\Big|\, \sigma(v_1), \ldots, \sigma(v_k)\right]$$

This expectation can be computed by maintaining a running count:

$$C_v = \text{net contribution of vertex } v = \sum_{u \in N(v)} \mathbb{1}_{\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)} - \mathbb{1}_{\sigma(u) = \sigma(v)}$$

At each step we choose $\sigma(v_i) \in \{0, 1\}$ to keep the conditional expectation non-decreasing, ending with a deterministic cut of size $\geq m/2$.

## 3.4 Comparison Table: Randomization vs. Determinism

| Property | Randomized MAX CUT | Derandomized (Conditional Expectation) | Greedy 0.5-Approx. |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------------------------|---------------------|
| Running Time | $O(m)$ expected | $O(n + m)$ deterministic | $O(n + m)$ |
| Guarantee | $\mathbb{E}[Z] \geq m/2$ | $Z \geq m/2$ deterministic | $Z \geq m/2$ |
| Variance | Yes (Markov / Chernoff bounds apply) | None (fixed output) | None |
| Typical Cut Size | $\sim m/2 + O(\sqrt{m \log n})$ w.h.p. | $\geq m/2$ | $\geq m/2$ |
| Implementation Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate |

## 3.5 Concentration: Chernoff Bound Application

> [!TIP]  
> The random variables $X_e$ are **negatively associated** when conditioned on the vertex assignments, so a Chernoff-style concentration bound holds.

For any $\epsilon > 0$:

$$\Pr\left[Z \leq (1 - \epsilon) \cdot \dfrac{m}{2}\right] \leq \exp\left(-\dfrac{\epsilon^2 m}{4}\right)$$

This means for sufficiently large $m$, the algorithm almost always outputs a cut of size within a small relative gap of $m/2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Graph G = V, E]:::inputNode
    B[Initialize Empty Set S]:::initNode
    C[For each vertex v in V]:::loopStart
    D[Generate random sigma_v uniform 0 or 1 with prob 0.5]:::randomStep
    E{Is sigma_v equal to 1?}:::decisionNode
    F[Add v to S]:::assignNode
    G[Add v to V minus S]:::assignNode
    H[Increment edge counter for cut edges]:::countNode
    I[Output partition S, V minus S]:::outputNode
    J[Output cut size delta S]:::outputNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -- Yes --> F
    E -- No --> G
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> C
    C -- All vertices processed --> I
    I --> J

    classDef initNode fill:#0369a1,stroke:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    classDef loopStart fill:#0e7490,stroke:#164e63,color:#ffffff
    classDef randomStep fill:#a16207,stroke:#713f12,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionNode fill:#9d174d,stroke:#831843,color:#ffffff
    classDef assignNode fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef countNode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff
    classDef outputNode fill:#581c87,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Expectation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Graph G with m edges]:::inputNode
    B[Define Indicator X_e per edge]:::defNode
    C[Independence of vertex labels]:::probNode
    D[Linearity of Expectation]:::analysisNode
    E[Per edge expectation equals 0.5]:::calcNode
    F[Total expected cut equals m over 2]:::resultNode
    G[Approximation ratio at least 0.5]:::guaranteeNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
```
