# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the MAX CUT problem. Why is it NP-hard, and what type of approximation algorithm can guarantee a constant factor approximation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **MAX CUT problem** is defined as follows: Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, find a partition $(S, \bar{S})$ of $V$ such that the number of edges crossing the cut (i.e., with one endpoint in $S$ and the other in $\bar{S}$) is maximized.

The **decision version** of MAX CUT (whether there exists a cut of size $\geq k$) is known to be **NP-Complete**. NP-hardness arises because MAX CUT can encode 3-SAT-like structures and has been shown NP-hard via reductions from independent set and other classical NP-complete problems.

A **randomized algorithm** that assigns each vertex to $S$ or $\bar{S}$ independently with probability $1/2$ achieves an expected cut size of $m/2$, which is a **1/2-approximation** (or equivalently a factor-2 approximation in minimization form). It can also be derandomized using the **method of conditional expectations** to yield a deterministic 1/2-approximation.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Q: State and explain the linearity of expectation. Why is it central to the analysis of randomized MAX CUT?**

**Model Answer:**

**Linearity of Expectation** states that for any finite collection of random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (not necessarily independent):

$$\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}[X_i]$$

In the analysis of randomized MAX CUT, indicator variables $X_e$ are defined for each edge $e \in E$. While these indicators are *not independent* (they share vertices), linearity of expectation still applies, allowing us to compute:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e] = m \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{m}{2}$$

without needing to know the joint distribution. This makes linearity of expectation the **central analytical tool** of randomized algorithms in general.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Students often confuse the *approximation ratio* with the *approximation factor*. The randomized algorithm is a **1/2-approximation** (i.e., it produces a solution that is at least 1/2 of the optimum in expectation). It is a **factor-2** algorithm in the sense that OPT $\leq 2 \times \text{ALG}$.  
> - Do **not** state that the algorithm always produces a cut of size $m/2$. It is an **expected** value, not a deterministic one.  
> - Failure to mention **derandomization** (conditional expectations) loses up to 2 marks.  
> - Always show the **indicator variable setup** explicitly — examiners award marks for the formal construction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Design a randomized algorithm for the MAX CUT problem. Define all random variables used.  
**Q: (b)** Prove rigorously that the expected size of the cut produced by your algorithm is at least $m/2$, and conclude the approximation ratio.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Algorithm Design [7 marks]**

**Algorithm: Randomized MAX CUT**

```
Input:  An undirected graph G = (V, E) with n = |V| and m = |E|
Output: A cut (S, V \ S) and its size

1. Initialize S = empty set
2. For each vertex v in V:
       Flip an unbiased coin (probability 1/2 for each outcome)
       If coin shows HEADS:
            Add v to S
       Else:
            Add v to V \ S
3. For each edge e = (u, v) in E:
       If (u in S and v not in S) or (u not in S and v in S):
            Increment cut counter by 1
4. Return partition (S, V \ S) and the cut size
```

**Random Variables Defined:**

- For each vertex $v \in V$: $\sigma(v) \in \{0, 1\}$ assigned independently and uniformly at random.
- For each edge $e = (u, v) \in E$:

$$X_e = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- The total cut size:

$$Z = \sum_{e \in E} X_e$$

**Part (b) — Proof of Guarantee and Approximation Ratio [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Compute the per-edge probability**

For any edge $e = (u, v)$ where $\sigma(u), \sigma(v)$ are independent fair coin flips:

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)] = \Pr[\sigma(u)=1, \sigma(v)=0] + \Pr[\sigma(u)=0, \sigma(v)=1]$$

$$\Pr[\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)] = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute expected value of $X_e$**

Since $X_e$ is a Bernoulli indicator:

$$\mathbb{E}[X_e] = 1 \cdot \Pr[X_e = 1] + 0 \cdot \Pr[X_e = 0] = \Pr[X_e = 1] = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3: Apply linearity of expectation**

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{e \in E} X_e\right] = \sum_{e \in E} \mathbb{E}[X_e] = \sum_{e \in E} \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{m}{2}$$

**Step 4: Conclude the approximation ratio**

Since trivially $\text{OPT}(G) \leq m$, we have:

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \dfrac{m}{2} \geq \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \text{OPT}(G)$$

Therefore the algorithm is a **1/2-approximation algorithm** in expectation, and by derandomization via the **method of conditional expectations**, it becomes a deterministic **factor-1/2 approximation**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the method of conditional expectations and show how the randomized MAX CUT algorithm can be derandomized using it.  
**Q: (b)** For the graph $G$ shown below (a triangle $K_3$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$), enumerate all $2^3 = 8$ possible partitions and verify the expected cut size theorem. Compute the approximation ratio attained.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Method of Conditional Expectations [7 marks]**

The **method of conditional expectations** is a generic derandomization technique. The principle states:

> If a randomized algorithm achieves expected value $\mathbb{E}[Z] = T$ for a quantity $Z$, then there exists a deterministic assignment of random bits that achieves a value of at least $T$.

**Derandomization for MAX CUT:**

1. Start with all vertices unlabeled.
2. At each step, pick an unfixed vertex $v$. Compute the conditional expectation of the cut size under both choices $\sigma(v) = 0$ and $\sigma(v) = 1$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[Z \,\Big|\, \sigma(v) = 0, \text{other labels random}\right]$$
$$\mathbb{E}\left[Z \,\Big|\, \sigma(v) = 1, \text{other labels random}\right]$$

3. Choose the value of $\sigma(v)$ that **does not decrease** the conditional expectation.
4. Update and continue until all vertices are fixed.

**Efficient Implementation:** Maintain, for each unfixed vertex $v$, the value:

$$C_v = \sum_{u \in N(v)} \left[ \mathbb{1}[\sigma(u) \text{ fixed and differs from fixed-label of } v] - \mathbb{1}[\sigma(u) \text{ fixed and matches}] \right]$$

Choose the label for $v$ that keeps the conditional expectation $\geq m/2$. This runs in $O(n + m)$ time and produces a deterministic cut of size $\geq m/2$.

**Part (b) — Verification on $K_3$ (Triangle Graph) [7 marks]**

For $K_3$ on vertices $\{1, 2, 3\}$, all $2^3 = 8$ partitions (labelings) are:

| # | $\sigma(1)$ | $\sigma(2)$ | $\sigma(3)$ | Cut Edges | $\vert \delta(S) \vert$ |
|---|-------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | none | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | (2,3) | 1 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | (1,3) | 1 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | (1,2) | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | (1,2) | 1 |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | (1,3) | 1 |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 0 | (2,3) | 1 |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | none | 0 |

**Total cut edges over all 8 partitions:** $0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 6$

**Expected cut size:**

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \dfrac{6}{8} = \dfrac{3}{4}$$

**Verification against formula:** $m/2 = 3/2 = 1.5$. Wait — for $K_3$, $m = 3$, so $m/2 = 1.5$. But our computed expectation is $0.75$.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Correction Note (Mandatory for Examiner Clarity):** Re-checking: For $K_3$, $m = 3$, so $m/2 = 1.5$. But the enumeration gives $6/8 = 0.75 = m/4$, not $m/2$. This is because for **any single edge**, $\Pr[X_e = 1] = 1/2$, but in a 3-vertex graph, the cut size $\in \{0, 1, 2\}$ only. The sum of expectations is still $3 \times 1/2 = 1.5$. The enumeration above *incorrectly counted* the cut size.

**Re-verification of the enumeration** (correct): For labeling 2 (0,0,1): $\sigma(1)=0, \sigma(2)=0, \sigma(3)=1$. Edges: $(1,2)$ both 0 — internal. $(1,3)$: 0 vs 1 — cut. $(2,3)$: 0 vs 1 — cut. So **2 cut edges**, not 1. The corrected table:

| # | $\sigma(1)$ | $\sigma(2)$ | $\sigma(3)$ | Cut Edges | $\vert \delta(S) \vert$ |
|---|-------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | none | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | (1,3), (2,3) | 2 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | (1,2), (2,3) | 2 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | (1,2) | 1 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | (1,2) | 1 |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | (1,3) | 1 |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 0 | (2,3) | 1 |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | none | 0 |

**Sum** $= 0 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 8$

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \dfrac{8}{8} = 1 = \dfrac{m}{3} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{3}{2}$$

Wait, this gives $1$, not $1.5$. Let me recount the cut edges carefully:

For labeling 4 (0,1,1): $\sigma(1)=0, \sigma(2)=1, \sigma(3)=1$. Edges: $(1,2)$: 0 vs 1 → cut. $(1,3)$: 0 vs 1 → cut. $(2,3)$: 1 vs 1 → internal. So **2 cut edges**, not 1.

Re-corrected enumeration:

| # | $\sigma(1)$ | $\sigma(2)$ | $\sigma(3)$ | $\vert \delta(S) \vert$ |
|---|-------------|-------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| 6 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 7 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

**Sum** $= 0 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 0 = 12$

$$\mathbb{E}[Z] = \dfrac{12}{8} = \dfrac{3}{2} = \dfrac{m}{2} \checkmark$$

This **confirms the theorem**: $\mathbb{E}[Z] = m/2 = 3/2$.

**Approximation ratio for $K_3$:** The optimum is $2$ (any labeling with one side of size 1 and the other of size 2 cuts both edges from the singleton). Thus:

$$\rho = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[Z]}{\text{OPT}(G)} = \dfrac{3/2}{2} = \dfrac{3}{4} \geq \dfrac{1}{2} \checkmark$$

The bound is not tight for $K_3$ because $\text{OPT}(K_3) = 2 < m = 3$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for MAX CUT Randomized Algorithm:**

- **Problem**: Partition $V$ into $(S, \bar{S})$ to maximize crossing edges.
- **Algorithm**: Independent uniform random labeling of vertices.
- **Indicator Setup**: $X_e = 1$ iff $\sigma(u) \neq \sigma(v)$ for $e = (u,v)$.
- **Key Probability**: $\Pr[X_e = 1] = 1/2$ for any edge (independent fair coin).
- **Expected Cut**: $\mathbb{E}[Z] = m/2$ via **linearity of expectation**.
- **Approximation Ratio**: $1/2$ in expectation (factor-2 algorithm).
- **Independent Variables Caveat**: $X_e$'s are *not* independent, but linearity of expectation bypasses this.
- **Derandomization Tool**: **Method of conditional expectations** — greedy deterministic assignment.
- **Generalization**: $\mathbb{E}[Z] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} [p_u(1-p_v) + (1-p_u)p_v]$ for biased assignments.
- **Concentration**: Chernoff bound $\Pr[Z \leq (1-\epsilon)m/2] \leq \exp(-\epsilon^2 m/4)$.
- **Tightness**: Bound is tight for triangle-free bipartite-like graphs; for general graphs, SDP-based 0.878-approximation (Goemans–Williamson) improves on the 1/2-approximation.
- **NP-Hardness**: Decision version is NP-Complete; optimization is NP-Hard.
- **Time Complexity**: Randomized $O(m)$; Derandomized $O(n+m)$.
- **Common Examiner Traps**: Confusing 1/2-approximation with 2-approximation language; forgetting derandomization; misusing independence in expectation proof.
- **Real-World Link**: VLSI design, Ising model in statistical physics, spectral clustering, social polarization.

topics.title: Derandomization of MAX CUT Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Derandomization of MAX CUT Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The MAX CUT Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAX CUT (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices and $\vert E \vert = m$ edges, the **MAX CUT** problem asks for a partition of the vertex set $V$ into two disjoint sets $(S, \bar{S})$ such that $S \cup \bar{S} = V$, that **maximizes the number of edges** with one endpoint in $S$ and the other in $\bar{S}$.

The objective is to maximize:
$$C(S) = \vert \{\, (u,v) \in E \;\mid\; u \in S,\; v \in \bar{S} \,\} \vert$$

MAX CUT is **NP-hard**, so a polynomial-time exact solution is not known (unless $P = NP$). The study of this module is on **approximation algorithms** that guarantee a provable ratio against the optimal cut.

## 1.2 Randomized vs. Deterministic Algorithms

A **randomized algorithm** uses random bits during its execution and may produce different outputs on the same input across runs. Its performance is judged in **expectation** or with high probability.

A **deterministic algorithm** always produces the same output for the same input, and its guarantee must hold on **every** instance.

**Derandomization** is the process of converting a randomized algorithm into a deterministic one of comparable (often identical) performance, typically by removing the randomness while preserving the expected-value guarantee on the worst-case instance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Split" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine a classroom of $n$ students (vertices) where some pairs of students are friends (edges). You want to split the class into two rooms so as to **maximize the number of friendships that are broken** (i.e., friends placed in different rooms). A **randomized strategy** flips an unbiased coin for each student. The **expected number of broken friendships** is exactly half the total number of friendships. **Derandomization** is the discipline of walking through the students one by one and *deliberately* placing each student in the room that, given the choices already made, is provably no worse than a random coin flip on average — guaranteeing the same 50% lower bound **deterministically** on every classroom arrangement.

## 1.4 Key Metric

The standard guarantee for the (de)randomized MAX CUT algorithm is a **0.5-approximation ratio**, meaning the algorithm always returns a cut of size at least $\mathbf{0.5 \cdot \vert E \vert}$, which is at least half of the optimal (since the optimum is at most $\vert E \vert$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Randomized MAX CUT Algorithm

**Algorithm (Randomized MAX CUT):**
1. For each vertex $v \in V$, independently assign $v$ to $S$ with probability $1/2$ and to $\bar{S}$ with probability $1/2$.
2. Output the resulting cut $(S, \bar{S})$.

**Why it gives a 0.5-approximation in expectation:**

For each edge $(u,v) \in E$, define the indicator random variable:
$$Y_{uv} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } (u,v) \text{ is cut} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Since $u$ and $v$ are placed independently and uniformly at random:
$$P(Y_{uv} = 1) = P(u \in S) \cdot P(v \in \bar{S}) + P(u \in \bar{S}) \cdot P(v \in S) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

By **linearity of expectation**, the expected number of cut edges is:
$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[ \sum_{(u,v) \in E} Y_{uv} \right] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \mathbb{E}[Y_{uv}] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{\vert E \vert}{2}$$

Since the optimal cut is at most $\vert E \vert$, the algorithm achieves an expected ratio of $1/2$.

## 2.2 The Method of Conditional Expectations (MCE)

The **Method of Conditional Expectations** is the principal derandomization tool. It works by *greedily* fixing random variables one at a time to values that do not increase the conditional expectation.

**Key Invariant (Pessimistic Estimator Property):**
At every step of the algorithm, the conditional expectation **never increases**. Therefore, the final deterministic outcome is at least the initial expected value:
$$\mathbb{E}[C] \;\geq\; \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1] \;\geq\; \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, X_2 = a_2] \;\geq\; \cdots \;\geq\; C(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)$$

This chain guarantees a **deterministic lower bound** of $\vert E \vert / 2$ on the cut produced.

## 2.3 Choosing the Optimal Value at Each Step

For vertex $v_i$ (after fixing $v_1, \ldots, v_{i-1}$), we compute two conditional expectations:
$$\alpha_i^{(0)} = \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}, X_i = 0]$$
$$\alpha_i^{(1)} = \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}, X_i = 1]$$

We then set:
$$a_i = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \alpha_i^{(0)} \leq \alpha_i^{(1)} \\ 1 & \text{if } \alpha_i^{(1)} < \alpha_i^{(0)} \end{cases}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formulas for Derandomization of MAX CUT**

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|
| $n = \vert V \vert$ | Number of vertices | Input parameter |
| $m = \vert E \vert$ | Number of edges | Input parameter |
| $X_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Indicator: $v_i \in S$ if $X_i=1$ | Binary |
| $Y_{uv}$ | Cut-indicator for edge $(u,v)$ | $0$ or $1$ |
| $P(Y_{uv}=1)$ | Probability edge $(u,v)$ is cut | $1/2$ (uniform) |
| $\mathbb{E}[C]$ | Expected number of cut edges | $m/2$ |
| Approx. ratio | $\mathbb{E}[C] / \text{OPT}$ | $\geq 1/2$ |
| $C(X) = \sum_{(u,v)\in E}(X_u \oplus X_v)$ | Cut-size as a function of assignments | Integer $\in [0,m]$ |
| $\alpha_i^{(0)}, \alpha_i^{(1)}$ | Conditional expectations for $X_i$ | Reals |
| Time complexity | Greedy scan over $n$ vertices | $O(n + m)$ |
| Space complexity | Storage of graph and assignments | $O(n + m)$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The derandomized MAX CUT algorithm (and conditional-expectation techniques broadly) is foundational in:
- **VLSI Circuit Design**: Partitioning a circuit graph across two chip layers to minimize inter-layer wiring.
- **Statistical Physics (Ising Model)**: Ground-state energy corresponds to a maximum cut.
- **Clustering & Data Mining**: Splitting large datasets into two coherent groups.
- **Network Design**: Balancing traffic between two sub-networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Cut Size from First Principles

**Step 1**: Represent each edge-cut event by an indicator.
For each edge $(u,v) \in E$, define $Y_{uv} = X_u \oplus X_v$, where $X_u, X_v \in \{0,1\}$ are independent Bernoulli($1/2$) and $\oplus$ denotes XOR (addition mod 2).

**Step 2**: Compute the expectation of a single indicator.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[Y_{uv}] &= \mathbb{E}[X_u \oplus X_v] \\
&= P(X_u = 0, X_v = 1) + P(X_u = 1, X_v = 0) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Sum over all edges using linearity of expectation.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[C] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[ \sum_{(u,v) \in E} Y_{uv} \right] \\
&= \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \mathbb{E}[Y_{uv}] \\
&= \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \tfrac{1}{2} \\
&= \tfrac{m}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the expected cut size is exactly half the number of edges, regardless of graph structure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed Form for the Conditional Expectation

**Step 1**: Decompose the cut sum by the status of each endpoint relative to step $i$.
Let $V_{<i} = \{v_1, \ldots, v_{i-1}\}$ (already fixed), $V_{=i} = \{v_i\}$, and $V_{>i} = \{v_{i+1}, \ldots, v_n\}$ (still random).

Each edge $(u,v) \in E$ falls into one of three categories:

| Category | Description | Contribution to $\mathbb{E}[Y_{uv} \mid \cdot]$ |
|---|---|---|
| Both endpoints in $V_{<i}$ | Fully determined | $a_u \oplus a_v$ (a constant) |
| Exactly one endpoint equals $v_i$ | Depends on $X_i$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (the other is uniform in $\{0,1\}$) |
| Both endpoints in $V_{>i}$ | Both still random | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (independent uniform) |

**Step 2**: Define $N_i^{\text{fixed}} = \{\, j < i \mid (v_i, v_j) \in E \,\}$ (already-fixed neighbors of $v_i$) and $N_i^{\text{free}} = \{\, j > i \mid (v_i, v_j) \in E \,\}$ (unfixed neighbors).

**Step 3**: Write the conditional expectation given $X_i = b$, where $b \in \{0,1\}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_i^{(b)} &= \sum_{(u,v) \in E,\; u,v < i} (a_u \oplus a_v) \;+\; \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ b \oplus X_j \right] \;+\; \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ X_j \oplus b \right] \;+\; \sum_{(u,v) \in E,\; u,v > i} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ X_u \oplus X_v \right] \\
&= \text{const}_1 + \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ b \oplus X_j \right] + \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ X_j \oplus b \right] + \text{const}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: For an unfixed $X_j$ (uniform in $\{0,1\}$), compute $\mathbb{E}[b \oplus X_j]$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[b \oplus X_j] &= P(X_j = 0)\cdot(b \oplus 0) + P(X_j = 1)\cdot(b \oplus 1) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot b + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot(1-b) \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Substituting back, the **difference** between the two conditional expectations simplifies dramatically:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_i^{(0)} - \alpha_i^{(1)} &= \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \Big[ (0 \oplus a_j) - (1 \oplus a_j) \Big] \\
&= \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} \Big[ a_j - (1 - a_j) \Big] \\
&= \sum_{j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}}} (2a_j - 1) \\
&= \vert \{\, j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}} \mid a_j = 1 \,\} \vert \;-\; \vert \{\, j \in N_i^{\text{fixed}} \mid a_j = 0 \,\} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6**: Greedy interpretation.
- Let $p_i$ = number of **already-fixed neighbors of $v_i$ that are in $S$** (i.e., $a_j = 1$).
- Let $q_i = \deg_{<i}(v_i) - p_i$ = number of already-fixed neighbors in $\bar{S}$.

Then:
- Choosing $X_i = 1$ yields $p_i$ cut edges with already-fixed neighbors.
- Choosing $X_i = 0$ yields $q_i$ cut edges with already-fixed neighbors.

We pick the side that gives the **larger contribution** (or either if tied). This is the famous **greedy MAX CUT** algorithm, recovered exactly from derandomization.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Final Deterministic Lower Bound

After fixing all $n$ vertices, we have the inequality chain:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tfrac{m}{2} = \mathbb{E}[C] &\geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1] \\
&\geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, X_2 = a_2] \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&\geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_n = a_n] \\
&= C(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

The last equality holds because once all $X_i$ are fixed, the conditional expectation is just the actual value. Therefore:
$$C(a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n) \geq \tfrac{m}{2}$$

This establishes a **deterministic 0.5-approximation**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Complexity Analysis

- **Initialization**: Read the graph — $O(n + m)$.
- **Main loop**: For each of $n$ vertices, scan the neighbor list and count $p_i$ vs $q_i$ — total $O(m)$ across all iterations.
- **Total time**: $O(n + m)$.
- **Space**: $O(n + m)$ for the graph and the assignment array.

This matches the randomized algorithm's complexity, demonstrating that derandomization comes at **no asymptotic cost**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Method of Conditional Expectations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Graph G = V, E] --> B[Initialize: All vertices unassigned, i = 1]
    B --> C{v_i has unassigned neighbors?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute p_i = fixed neighbors in S]
    D --> E[Compute q_i = fixed neighbors in complement S]
    E --> F{p_i greater than or equal q_i?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Assign X_i = 1, place v_i in S]
    F -- No --> H[Assign X_i = 0, place v_i in complement S]
    G --> I[Increment i, update fixed set]
    H --> I
    I --> C
    C -- No --> J[All vertices assigned, output cut S, complement S]
    J --> K[End: Return cut size greater than or equal m by 2]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        I1[Graph Adjacency List]
        I2[Vertex Count n]
        I3[Edge Count m]
    end
    subgraph State
        S1[Assignment Array a of size n]
        S2[Fixed-Set Counter i]
        S3[Running Cut Counter C]
    end
    subgraph Engine
        E1[Conditional Expectation Module: Computes alpha_i^0 and alpha_i^1]
        E2[Decision Module: Picks argmin alpha_i]
    end
    subgraph Output
        O1[Deterministic Cut S]
        O2[Cut Size lower bound m by 2]
    end
    I1 --> E1
    I2 --> S2
    I3 --> O2
    S1 --> E1
    S2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> S1
    E2 --> S3
    S3 --> O1
    S3 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — From Randomness to Determinism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Randomized Algorithm: Coin flip for each vertex] --> R2[Expected Cut Size equals m by 2]
    R2 --> R3[Existence of good cut, but not constructive]
    R3 --> D1[Apply Method of Conditional Expectations]
    D1 --> D2[Fix one variable at a time, keep conditional expectation monotone non-increasing]
    D2 --> D3[Greedy local choice recovers 0.5-approximation]
    D3 --> D4[Deterministic Cut of size at least m by 2]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** State the MAX CUT problem. Give the expected size of the cut returned by the randomized algorithm that places each vertex in $S$ or $\bar{S}$ with equal probability.

**Model Answer:**
MAX CUT: Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$, find a partition $(S, \bar{S})$ of $V$ that maximizes the number of edges with one endpoint in $S$ and the other in $\bar{S}$.

For the randomized algorithm, each edge is cut with probability $1/2$ (by independence of the two endpoint choices). By linearity of expectation, the expected cut size is:
$$\mathbb{E}[C] = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{\vert E \vert}{2}$$

Hence the randomized algorithm returns a cut of expected size $\vert E \vert / 2$, giving a 0.5-approximation ratio. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** What is the *pessimistic estimator* property in the method of conditional expectations? Why is it crucial for derandomization?

**Model Answer:**
The pessimistic estimator property states that the **conditional expectation is monotonically non-increasing** as more random variables are fixed:
$$\mathbb{E}[C] \;\geq\; \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1] \;\geq\; \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1, X_2] \;\geq\; \cdots$$

This is crucial because, when all variables are fixed, the conditional expectation equals the actual cut value, so the final deterministic cut is guaranteed to be at least the **initial expected value** $\vert E \vert / 2$. This converts a probabilistic existence statement into a constructive deterministic guarantee. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Evaluation – Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Consider the following graph $G$ with vertices $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ and edges $\{(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5), (2,5)\}$. Apply the derandomized MAX CUT algorithm (method of conditional expectations) in the vertex ordering $v_1 = 1, v_2 = 2, v_3 = 3, v_4 = 4, v_5 = 5$. Show each step and the final cut produced. Verify the cut size satisfies the 0.5-approximation bound.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (Vertex 1):** No fixed neighbors yet. $p_1 = q_1 = 0$. Tie — assign $a_1 = 1$ (place $v_1$ in $S$). Fixed set: $\{v_1\}$ with $S = \{1\}$.

**Step 2 (Vertex 2):** Fixed neighbors of $v_2$: $\{v_1\}$ (in $S$, so $p_2 = 1$, $q_2 = 0$). Choose $a_2 = 0$ to maximize cut contribution. $S = \{1\}, \bar{S} = \{2\}$.

**Step 3 (Vertex 3):** Fixed neighbors of $v_3$: $\{v_1 \in S, v_2 \in \bar{S}\}$. So $p_3 = 1, q_3 = 1$. Tie — assign $a_3 = 1$ (place $v_3$ in $S$). $S = \{1, 3\}, \bar{S} = \{2\}$.

**Step 4 (Vertex 4):** Fixed neighbors of $v_4$: $\{v_3 \in S\}$. So $p_4 = 1, q_4 = 0$. Choose $a_4 = 0$. $S = \{1, 3\}, \bar{S} = \{2, 4\}$.

**Step 5 (Vertex 5):** Fixed neighbors of $v_5$: $\{v_2 \in \bar{S}, v_4 \in \bar{S}\}$. So $p_5 = 0, q_5 = 2$. Choose $a_5 = 1$. $S = \{1, 3, 5\}, \bar{S} = \{2, 4\}$.

**Final cut:** $S = \{1, 3, 5\}, \bar{S} = \{2, 4\}$.

**Count cut edges:**
- $(1,2)$: $1 \in S, 2 \in \bar{S}$ ✓
- $(1,3)$: both in $S$ ✗
- $(2,3)$: $2 \in \bar{S}, 3 \in S$ ✓
- $(3,4)$: $3 \in S, 4 \in \bar{S}$ ✓
- $(4,5)$: $4 \in \bar{S}, 5 \in S$ ✓
- $(2,5)$: $2 \in \bar{S}, 5 \in S$ ✓

Cut size $= 5$. With $m = 6$, we have $C = 5 \geq m/2 = 3$. ✓ Approximation ratio $5/6 \geq 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** [7 Marks] Prove rigorously that the derandomized algorithm always produces a cut of size at least $\vert E \vert / 2$.

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** Let $X_i \in \{0,1\}$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$ be independent Bernoulli($1/2$) random variables. The cut size is:
$$C(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} (X_u \oplus X_v)$$

**Step 1 (Initial expectation):** For each edge, $\mathbb{E}[X_u \oplus X_v] = 1/2$, so:
$$\mathbb{E}[C] = \tfrac{\vert E \vert}{2}$$

**Step 2 (Conditional-expectation chain):** Define $a_i \in \{0,1\}$ as the value chosen at step $i$. By the algorithm's rule:
$$a_i = \arg\min_{b \in \{0,1\}} \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}, X_i = b]$$

Hence:
$$\mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}] = \min_{b} \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}, X_i = b] \leq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}]$$

**Step 3 (Monotonicity):** By induction:
$$\mathbb{E}[C] \geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1] \geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1, X_2] \geq \cdots \geq \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1, \ldots, X_n]$$

**Step 4 (Conclusion):** When all $X_i$ are fixed, the conditional expectation equals the actual value:
$$C(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_n = a_n] \geq \mathbb{E}[C] = \tfrac{\vert E \vert}{2}$$

Thus the algorithm always returns a cut of size $\geq \vert E \vert / 2$, which is at least $0.5 \cdot \text{OPT}$ since $\text{OPT} \leq \vert E \vert$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the method of conditional expectations. How does it derandomize the MAX CUT algorithm? Show that the deterministic algorithm obtained is equivalent to a simple greedy procedure.

**Model Solution:**

**Method of Conditional Expectations (MCE):** A general derandomization technique that converts any randomized algorithm with a non-negative objective function $f(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ whose expected value is known, into a deterministic algorithm achieving at least the expected value.

**Step 1 — Setup for MAX CUT:**
Let $X_i \in \{0,1\}$ be independent Bernoulli($1/2$) for each vertex $v_i$. The cut size is:
$$C(X) = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} (X_u \oplus X_v)$$
with $\mathbb{E}[C] = \vert E \vert / 2$.

**Step 2 — Greedy step:** For each $v_i$ in turn, compute:
$$\alpha_i^{(b)} = \mathbb{E}[C \mid X_1 = a_1, \ldots, X_{i-1} = a_{i-1}, X_i = b]$$

For an unfixed $X_j$, the term $(X_i \oplus X_j)$ contributes $1/2$ to the expectation. Only edges between $v_i$ and **already-fixed** vertices depend on $b$ directly.

**Step 3 — Greedy equivalence:** From Derivation 2 in Section 3, the decision reduces to:
- Count $p_i$ = number of fixed neighbors of $v_i$ in $S$.
- Count $q_i$ = number of fixed neighbors of $v_i$ in $\bar{S}$.
- Place $v_i$ on the side with the **larger** count, breaking ties arbitrarily.

This is the classical **greedy MAX CUT** algorithm — at each step, place the current vertex on the side that creates the most cut edges with already-placed vertices. Hence MCE exactly recovers greedy MAX CUT, while explaining *why* it achieves the $0.5$ guarantee. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the time and space complexity of the derandomized algorithm. Compare it with the randomized algorithm and the optimal exponential-time MAX CUT via brute force.

**Model Solution:**

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|
| Randomized MAX CUT | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ | $\mathbb{E}[C] = m/2$ (random) |
| Derandomized MAX CUT (MCE) | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ | $C \geq m/2$ (deterministic) |
| Brute Force MAX CUT | $O(2^n \cdot m)$ | $O(n + m)$ | Optimal $C = \text{OPT}$ |

**Derandomized algorithm analysis:**
- **Time:** $O(n + m)$. We process each of $n$ vertices once, and for each, scan the adjacency list of its already-fixed neighbors. Summed over all vertices, the total neighbor scans equal $\sum_v \deg(v) = 2m$, plus $O(n)$ overhead. So total is $O(n + m)$.
- **Space:** $O(n + m)$ to store the graph (adjacency list) and the assignment array $a[1 \ldots n]$.

**Comparison:**
- Derandomization adds **no asymptotic overhead** vs. the randomized version.
- The deterministic algorithm is **strongly preferable in practice** for VLSI and embedded systems where reproducibility is mandatory.
- Brute force is exponential and infeasible for $n > 30$ or so; the derandomized algorithm is the practical choice. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing expectation with guarantee**: Students often state the randomized algorithm "always" gives $\vert E \vert / 2$. In reality, the bound holds only in **expectation**; some runs are worse. The derandomized version is what guarantees the bound **every time**.
> 2. **Forgetting the pessimistic-estimator monotonicity**: Skipping the proof that the conditional expectation does not increase is a common 2–3 mark loss. The chain of inequalities is the heart of the argument.
> 3. **Mistaking OPT bound**: The optimal cut can be at most $\vert E \vert$, so the approximation ratio is $\geq 1/2$, not strictly $= 1/2$. Some instances achieve ratio exactly $1/2$.
> 4. **Greedy equivalence derivation**: Many students state the greedy algorithm matches MCE but skip the algebraic derivation showing $p_i - q_i$ is the deciding quantity. This costs full marks on the "show that" sub-part.
> 5. **Time complexity omission**: Forgetting to sum degrees to get $2m$ in the total scan is a frequent mistake. Always write $\sum_v \deg(v) = 2m$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Derandomization of MAX CUT**
> - **MAX CUT** = partition of $V$ into $(S, \bar{S})$ maximizing edges with endpoints in different sets; **NP-hard**.
> - **Randomized algorithm**: place each vertex in $S$ or $\bar{S}$ with probability $1/2$. Expected cut size $= \vert E \vert / 2$.
> - **Per-edge cut probability** $= 1/2$ by independence of uniform coin flips.
> - **Derandomization** = conversion of a randomized algorithm into a deterministic one with the same (or comparable) worst-case guarantee.
> - **Method of Conditional Expectations (MCE)**: fix $X_i$ one at a time, choosing the value that minimizes the conditional expectation.
> - **Pessimistic estimator property**: conditional expectation is monotonically non-increasing — this is the *key invariant*.
> - **Final deterministic cut** satisfies $C \geq \mathbb{E}[C] = \vert E \vert / 2$, giving a **0.5-approximation**.
> - **Greedy equivalence**: the deterministic algorithm is identical to the greedy MAX CUT that, at each vertex, picks the side with more fixed neighbors.
> - **Time complexity**: $O(n + m)$. **Space**: $O(n + m)$. No asymptotic penalty vs. randomized version.
> - **Approximation ratio** $1/2$ is tight: for odd cycles and complete bipartite-like graphs, the optimal cut is close to $\vert E \vert$, and the algorithm can be forced to give exactly $\vert E \vert / 2$ on some instances.
> - **Higher ratios** are achievable with **semidefinite programming** (Goemans–Williamson $0.878$-approximation), covered in higher-level modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semidefinite Programming (SDP): Basics of SDP and its Relation to LP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semidefinite Programming (SDP): Basics of SDP and its Relation to LP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Semidefinite Programming (SDP)

A **Semidefinite Program (SDP)** is a convex optimization problem that generalizes a Linear Program (LP). The decision variable in an SDP is not just a vector, but a **symmetric matrix** $X \in \mathbb{S}^{n}$, where the constraint requires this matrix to be **Positive Semidefinite** (denoted as $X \succeq 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Primal Form):**
> 
> A primal SDP in standard form is given as:
> 
> $$\text{(P)} \quad \min \; \langle C, X \rangle$$
> 
> $$\text{subject to: } \langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$
> 
> $$X \succeq 0$$
> 
> where $C, A_i \in \mathbb{S}^{n}$ are given symmetric matrices, $b \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is a known vector, and the inner product $\langle A, B \rangle = \text{trace}(A^T B)$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Elastic Sheet" Model

Imagine you are holding a **square rubber sheet** stretched over a frame. You want to minimize the "tension energy" of the sheet (the objective) subject to some nails (constraints) pinning the sheet at specific points.

- In a **Linear Program**, the variables are like beads sliding on a one-dimensional **straight wire** — restricted to non-negative positions.
- In an **Semidefinite Program**, the variables form an entire **2D rubber sheet (matrix)** that can stretch and flex — but the rule is that the sheet must never "fold inside out" in a mathematically unstable way. This stability condition is precisely the **Positive Semidefiniteness** constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of $X \succeq 0$:**
> A symmetric matrix $X$ is Positive Semidefinite if and only if for **every** direction vector $v$, the quadratic form $v^T X v \geq 0$. Geometrically, this means the matrix defines a "bowl-shaped" or "flat" quadratic surface — never an inverted bowl (saddle point).

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU 2024 Scheme

| Term | Mathematical Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Symmetric Matrix Space | $\mathbb{S}^{n}$ | Set of all $n \times n$ real symmetric matrices |
| Positive Semidefinite | $X \succeq 0$ | All eigenvalues $\lambda_i \geq 0$ |
| Matrix Inner Product | $\langle A, B \rangle$ | Equals $\sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij} = \text{trace}(A^T B)$ |
| Convex Cone | $\mathbb{S}_{+}^{n}$ | The set of all PSD matrices forms a convex cone |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Primal-Dual SDP Pair

SDPs, much like LPs, have beautifully symmetric **Primal-Dual** formulations. The Ellipsoid method and Interior Point methods solve them in polynomial time.

**Primal SDP (P):**

$$\min \; \langle C, X \rangle \quad \text{s.t. } \mathcal{A}(X) = b, \; X \succeq 0$$

**Dual SDP (D):**

$$\max \; b^T y \quad \text{s.t. } \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i + S = C, \; S \succeq 0$$

where $\mathcal{A}(X) = (\langle A_1, X \rangle, \ldots, \langle A_m, X \rangle)^T$ is the constraint operator, $y \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the dual variable, and $S \in \mathbb{S}_{+}^{n}$ is the **dual slack matrix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Weak Duality:** For any feasible $X \succeq 0$ and $(y, S)$:
> $$b^T y \leq \langle C, X \rangle$$
> **Strong Duality:** Under Slater's condition (strict feasibility exists), optimal values coincide and there exist optimal $X^*, y^*, S^*$ satisfying **complementary slackness**: $X^* S^* = 0$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind SDPs in Approximation Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Approximation Power of SDP:**
> Many NP-hard combinatorial problems (e.g., **MAX-CUT**, **Graph Coloring**, **MAX-3SAT**) can be *relaxed* to an SDP. The SDP relaxation is often *strictly tighter* than the LP relaxation, yielding better approximation ratios. For example, Goemans-Williamson gives a **0.878-approximation** for MAX-CUT using SDP, beating the trivial 0.5 LP-based ratio.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primal Standard Form** | $\min \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$, $X \succeq 0$ | Standard matrix form |
| **Dual Standard Form** | $\max b^T y$ s.t. $C - \sum y_i A_i \succeq 0$ | Dual slack $S \succeq 0$ |
| **Matrix Inner Product** | $\langle A, B \rangle = \text{trace}(A^T B) = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij}$ | Defines the objective |
| **PSD Definition** | $X \succeq 0 \iff v^T X v \geq 0, \; \forall v \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Quadratic form test |
| **PSD Eigenvalue Test** | $X \succeq 0 \iff \lambda_i \geq 0, \; \forall i$ | All eigenvalues non-negative |
| **Cholesky Factorization** | $X = V^T V$ for some $V \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$ | Used in Goemans-Williamson rounding |
| **LP-as-SDP Equivalence** | Diagonal matrices $X = \text{diag}(x)$ | LP is a special case |
| **Schur Complement Lemma** | $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B^T & D \end{bmatrix} \succeq 0 \iff A \succeq 0, \; D - B^T A^{-1} B \succeq 0$ | Useful for conic reformulation |
| **Weak Duality Gap** | $\langle C, X \rangle - b^T y = \langle X, S \rangle \geq 0$ | Proof via trace properties |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SDPs are the workhorse of:
- **Control Theory:** Lyapunov stability analysis (finding a matrix $P \succ 0$ such that $A^T P + P A \prec 0$).
- **Structural Engineering:** Truss topology optimization.
- **Machine Learning:** Kernel methods, matrix completion, sensor network localization.
- **Combinatorial Optimization:** Approximation algorithms for quadratic optimization problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: LP as a Special Case of SDP

A Linear Program in standard form is:
$$\min \; c^T x \quad \text{s.t. } a_i^T x = b_i, \; x \geq 0$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that the non-negativity constraint $x \geq 0$ means the vector $x$ has all non-negative components.

**Step 2:** Construct a **diagonal matrix** $X = \text{diag}(x)$, i.e.,
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & x_2 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & x_n \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Since $X$ is diagonal, $X \succeq 0$ (Positive Semidefinite) if and only if all diagonal entries $x_i \geq 0$.

**Step 4:** Compute the matrix inner product. Take $C = \text{diag}(c)$ and $A_i = \text{diag}(a_i)$ (where $a_i$ is the $i$-th constraint row). Then:

$$\langle C, X \rangle = \text{trace}(C^T X) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j = c^T x$$

$$\langle A_i, X \rangle = \text{trace}(A_i^T X) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (a_i)_j x_j = a_i^T x$$

**Step 5:** Thus the LP is **exactly recovered** as the SDP with diagonal matrix variables:

$$\min \; \langle \text{diag}(c), X \rangle \quad \text{s.t. } \langle \text{diag}(a_i), X \rangle = b_i, \; X \succeq 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SDPs strictly generalize LPs because the matrix variable $X$ can be **non-diagonal**, capturing quadratic correlations between variables that LPs cannot model.

### 3.2 Derivation: Weak Duality for SDP

We prove that the dual objective $b^T y$ is always a lower bound on the primal objective $\langle C, X \rangle$.

**Step 1:** Let $X \succeq 0$ be primal feasible and $(y, S)$ with $S \succeq 0$ be dual feasible.

**Step 2:** From the dual feasibility constraint: $\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i + S = C$.

**Step 3:** Compute the difference of objectives using the inner product:

$$\langle C, X \rangle - b^T y = \left\langle \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i + S, \, X \right\rangle - \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i$$

**Step 4:** Use the bilinearity of the trace inner product:

$$= \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i \langle A_i, X \rangle + \langle S, X \rangle - \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i$$

**Step 5:** Apply primal feasibility $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$:

$$= \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i - \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i + \langle S, X \rangle = \langle S, X \rangle$$

**Step 6:** Use the identity $\langle S, X \rangle = \text{trace}(S X) = \text{trace}(X S) \geq 0$ since both $S \succeq 0$ and $X \succeq 0$ (the trace of a product of two PSD matrices is non-negative).

**Conclusion:**

$$b^T y \leq \langle C, X \rangle \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Converting a Quadratic Program to SDP

Consider a **Quadratically Constrained Quadratic Program (QCQP):**

$$\min \; x^T C_0 x \quad \text{s.t. } x^T A_i x = b_i, \quad x^T A_i x \geq b_i$$

**Step 1:** Introduce the substitution $X = x x^T$. Note that $X$ is rank-1 and PSD by construction.

**Step 2:** Rewrite the objective using the inner product. Since $\text{trace}(x^T C_0 x) = x^T C_0 x$, we have $x^T C_0 x = \langle C_0, \, x x^T \rangle = \langle C_0, X \rangle$.

**Step 3:** Similarly, $x^T A_i x = \langle A_i, X \rangle$.

**Step 4:** The QCQP becomes:

$$\min \; \langle C_0, X \rangle \quad \text{s.t. } \langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i, \quad \langle A_i, X \rangle \geq b_i, \quad X \succeq 0$$

**Step 5:** **Relaxation:** Drop the rank-1 constraint. Now $X$ is a general PSD matrix — this is the **SDP relaxation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Approximation Algorithms:** Dropping the rank-1 constraint makes the problem convex (hence solvable in polynomial time), and the optimal SDP value gives a tractable lower bound on the NP-hard QCQP. This is the foundation of the **Goemans-Williamson MAX-CUT algorithm**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
