## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a Semidefinite Program (SDP). What is the role of the matrix inner product in its formulation?**
*(3 Marks | CO2 / Understand Level)*

**Model Answer:**
A Semidefinite Program is a convex optimization problem of the form:
$$\min \; \langle C, X \rangle \quad \text{s.t. } \langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i, \quad X \succeq 0$$
The matrix inner product $\langle C, X \rangle = \text{trace}(C^T X) = \sum_{i,j} C_{ij} X_{ij}$ linearizes the quadratic objective, enabling the use of interior-point methods. The constraint $X \succeq 0$ (Positive Semidefinite) ensures the problem remains a convex program and serves as the natural generalization of $x \geq 0$ in LPs. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. State the primal-dual relationship in SDP. Mention Slater's condition.**
*(3 Marks | CO2 / Remember Level)*

**Model Answer:**
**Primal:** $\min \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$, $X \succeq 0$.

**Dual:** $\max b^T y$ s.t. $\sum y_i A_i + S = C$, $S \succeq 0$.

**Weak Duality:** $b^T y \leq \langle C, X \rangle$ for all feasible pairs.

**Strong Duality (Slater's Condition):** If there exists a strictly feasible primal point $X_0 \succ 0$ (all eigenvalues strictly positive) satisfying $\langle A_i, X_0 \rangle = b_i$, then optimal values coincide and complementary slackness $X^* S^* = 0$ holds. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Style | ESE Module Internal Choice]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Formulate a Semidefinite Program for the MAX-CUT problem on a graph $G = (V, E)$ with $n$ vertices. Show the LP relaxation and the SDP relaxation explicitly. Explain why the SDP relaxation is tighter. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Write the dual of the SDP you formulated in (a). State the weak and strong duality theorems for SDPs. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

For each vertex $i$, we introduce a variable $v_i$ intended to be a unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. The objective is to maximize the number of edges whose endpoints are assigned to different sides of the cut.

$$\max \; \frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (1 - v_i \cdot v_j)$$

$$v_i \cdot v_i = 1, \quad \forall i \in V$$

**Step 1 — Form the Gram Matrix:** Let $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$. Then $X = V^T V$ for a matrix $V$ whose rows are the $v_i$. This means $X \succeq 0$ and $X_{ii} = 1$.

**Step 2 — SDP Relaxation:**

$$\max \; \frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (1 - X_{ij})$$

$$\text{s.t. } X_{ii} = 1, \quad \forall i, \quad X \succeq 0$$

**Step 3 — LP Relaxation (Comparison):** A natural LP relaxation replaces $X \succeq 0$ with $X_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$. The LP allows $X$ to be a matrix that is **not realizable** as a Gram matrix, so it overestimates the cut value. The SDP restricts $X$ to the convex cone of PSD matrices, which is a strictly tighter set.

**Step 4 — Tighter Bound Argument:** Any vector solution $\{v_i\}$ gives a feasible SDP point, so the SDP optimum is at most the LP optimum. The integrality gap of LP is 2, but the integrality gap of SDP is bounded by the Goemans-Williamson constant $\alpha_G \approx 1.138$, making the SDP a much tighter relaxation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Convert the primal SDP to standard form. The constraints $X_{ii} = 1$ correspond to $\langle E_{ii}, X \rangle = 1$, where $E_{ii}$ is the matrix with 1 in position $(i,i)$ and 0 elsewhere.

**Step 2:** Define $C = -\frac{1}{2} L_{G}$, where $L_G$ is the Laplacian matrix of the graph with off-diagonal entries $L_{ij} = -1$ for $(i,j) \in E$ and zeros on the diagonal. The objective becomes $\min \langle C, X \rangle$.

**Step 3:** The dual is:

$$\min \; \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

$$\text{s.t. } \text{diag}(y) - \frac{1}{2} L_G \succeq 0$$

**Step 4 — Duality Theorems:**
- **Weak Duality:** For all feasible primal $X \succeq 0$ and dual $(y, S)$ with $S \succeq 0$:
$$\frac{1}{2} \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (1 - X_{ij}) \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$
- **Strong Duality (Slater):** Since $X = I$ (identity) is a strictly feasible point with $X_{ii} = 1$ and $X \succeq 0$, Slater's condition holds, so strong duality is guaranteed and an optimal solution exists. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Show explicitly that any Linear Program (LP) is a special case of an SDP. Take the LP $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax = b$, $x \geq 0$, and convert it to standard SDP form. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Consider the SDP $\min \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$, $X \succeq 0$. Prove weak duality using the inner product properties and the trace of a product of two PSD matrices. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Given LP: $\min c^T x$ s.t. $Ax = b$, $x \geq 0$.

**Step 2:** Define a diagonal matrix variable $X = \text{diag}(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$.

**Step 3:** Since $X$ is diagonal, $X \succeq 0$ if and only if $x_i \geq 0$ for all $i$. This automatically enforces $x \geq 0$.

**Step 4:** Let $C = \text{diag}(c)$ and let $A_i = \text{diag}(a_i)$ where $a_i^T$ is the $i$-th row of $A$.

**Step 5:** Compute the inner product:

$$\langle C, X \rangle = \text{trace}(C^T X) = \text{trace}\begin{pmatrix} c_1 x_1 & & \\ & \ddots & \\ & & c_n x_n \end{pmatrix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i = c^T x$$

$$\langle A_i, X \rangle = \text{trace}(A_i^T X) = a_i^T x$$

**Step 6:** The LP becomes exactly the SDP:
$$\min \; \langle \text{diag}(c), X \rangle \quad \text{s.t. } \langle \text{diag}(a_i), X \rangle = b_i, \quad X \succeq 0$$

This shows LP is a special case of SDP with diagonal matrix variables. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Let $X \succeq 0$ be primal feasible and $(y, S)$ with $S \succeq 0$ be dual feasible.

**Step 2:** By definition of dual feasibility, $\sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i + S = C$.

**Step 3:** Take the inner product of both sides with $X$:

$$\left\langle C, X \right\rangle = \left\langle \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i A_i + S, X \right\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i \langle A_i, X \rangle + \langle S, X \rangle$$

**Step 4:** Apply primal feasibility $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$:

$$\langle C, X \rangle = \sum_{i=1}^{m} y_i b_i + \langle S, X \rangle = b^T y + \langle S, X \rangle$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:
$$\langle C, X \rangle - b^T y = \langle S, X \rangle = \text{trace}(S X)$$

**Step 6:** Since both $S \succeq 0$ and $X \succeq 0$, the eigenvalues of $S X$ are non-negative (by the spectral theorem: $S = U \Lambda_1 U^T$ and $X = V \Lambda_2 V^T$, so $\text{trace}(SX) \geq 0$). Thus $\langle S, X \rangle \geq 0$.

**Conclusion:**
$$b^T y \leq \langle C, X \rangle \quad \blacksquare$$ **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SDP Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\succeq$ and $\geq$:** Students often write $X \geq 0$ (elementwise) instead of $X \succeq 0$ (Positive Semidefinite). These are **not** the same! Elementwise non-negativity is weaker than PSD.
> 2. **Forgetting the dual slack $S$:** When writing the dual, students often omit the matrix $S \succeq 0$ and write only $\sum y_i A_i \preceq C$. This is a **2-mark penalty** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Mixing primal and dual objective forms:** The primal is a **minimization** by default in KTU convention; students swapping $\min$/$\max$ lose structure marks.
> 4. **Not invoking Slater's condition:** When claiming strong duality, students must explicitly state that a strictly feasible point exists. Without this, full marks for strong duality are not awarded.
> 5. **Diagonal vs. full matrix confusion:** In LP-as-SDP conversion, the matrix variable MUST be diagonal. Using a full symmetric matrix without justification loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for SDP Basics:**
> 
> - **Definition:** SDP optimizes a linear function of a PSD matrix variable.
> - **Standard Primal Form:** $\min \langle C, X \rangle$ s.t. $\langle A_i, X \rangle = b_i$, $X \succeq 0$.
> - **Standard Dual Form:** $\max b^T y$ s.t. $\sum y_i A_i + S = C$, $S \succeq 0$.
> - **Inner Product:** $\langle A, B \rangle = \text{trace}(A^T B) = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij}$.
> - **LP ⊂ SDP:** Any LP is an SDP with **diagonal** matrix variable $X = \text{diag}(x)$.
> - **SDP ⊃ LP:** SDPs capture off-diagonal (quadratic) correlations, making them strictly more expressive.
> - **PSD Test:** $X \succeq 0 \iff$ all eigenvalues $\geq 0$ $\iff$ $\forall v, v^T X v \geq 0$.
> - **Weak Duality:** $b^T y \leq \langle C, X \rangle$ always holds.
> - **Strong Duality:** Holds under **Slater's condition** (strict feasibility).
> - **Complementary Slackness:** $X^* S^* = 0$ at optimality.
> - **Approximation Use:** SDP relaxations of QCQPs (dropping rank-1) give tight convex bounds; Goemans-Williamson achieves 0.878-approximation for MAX-CUT via SDP + randomized rounding.
> - **Solvers:** Interior Point Methods solve SDPs in polynomial time.
> - **Cone:** The set of PSD matrices $\mathbb{S}_+^n$ forms a **self-dual convex cone** — a key geometric fact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulating Approximation Problems Using SDP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formulating Approximation Problems Using SDP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semidefinite Programming (SDP)** is a convex optimization paradigm where the variables are entries of a **Positive Semidefinite (PSD) matrix**, and both the objective function and constraints are linear in those entries. When used in approximation algorithms, SDP acts as a **convex relaxation** of hard combinatorial optimization problems, particularly **Quadratic Programs (QPs)** whose variables are restricted to $\{-1, +1\}$.

An **approximation problem formulated using SDP** typically follows the pipeline:

$$\text{Combinatorial Problem} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Integer/Quadratic Program} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Vector Relaxation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{SDP} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rounded Solution}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Canonical Problem:** The textbook example of SDP-based approximation is **MAX-CUT**, for which Goemans and Williamson (1994) achieved the celebrated **$\alpha_{GW} \approx 0.878567$ approximation ratio**, the best known to date under the Unique Games Conjecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **city planner** who must divide neighborhoods into two districts (say, North and South) so that as many "disagreeing" neighbor-pairs as possible fall on different sides. The naive way — flipping a coin for each neighborhood — gives roughly half the edges cut, but you can do much better.

Now think of the cities as **arrows on a giant dome (a unit sphere)**. SDP allows each city to "lean" in *any direction* on the sphere, not just North or South. After the solver finds the best arrangement of arrows, you slice the dome with a **random knife** (a random hyperplane). Each arrow falls on one side of the cut depending on which side it points to. The magic is that the geometric relaxation captures deep correlation structure that integer programming alone cannot see.

**Key takeaway:** SDP lifts a binary decision into continuous geometry, then uses randomized rounding to bring the geometric answer back to a valid discrete solution.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols

- **PSD matrix:** A symmetric matrix $X \succeq 0$ such that $z^T X z \geq 0$ for all real $z$.
- **Inner product of matrices:** $\langle A, B \rangle = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} B_{ij} = \text{tr}(A^T B)$.
- **Unit vector constraint:** $\|v_i\|_2 = 1$.
- **Goemans–Williamson constant:** $\alpha_{GW} \approx \mathbf{0.878567}$.
- **Vector program dimension:** $n = |V|$ lifted to an $n \times n$ Gram matrix $X$ with $\text{rank}(X) = n$ in the integer case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Blueprint of an SDP-Based Approximation

- **Step A — Encode as Integer Quadratic Program (IQP):** Variables $y_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ encode cut assignments with consistency constraints (triangle inequalities $y_{ij} + y_{jk} + y_{ki} \le 2$).
- **Step B — Lift to Vector Program:** Replace Boolean variable $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ by unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\|v_i\|_2 = 1$. Substitute $x_i x_j \rightarrow v_i \cdot v_j$.
- **Step C — Convert to Canonical SDP Form:** Let $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$. Then $X$ is a PSD Gram matrix with unit diagonal ($X_{ii} = 1$). The problem becomes a linear program in $X$ subject to $X \succeq 0$.
- **Step D — Solve the SDP Exactly:** Use interior-point methods (polynomial time, but slower than LP).
- **Step E — Randomized Rounding (Hyperplane Rounding):** Draw a random Gaussian vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^n$; place $i$ in side $S$ if $v_i \cdot r \ge 0$, otherwise in $\bar{S}$.
- **Step F — Analyze Expected Value:** Compute $E[\text{cut weight}]$ vs the SDP optimum and derive the approximation ratio.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why lift to vectors?** The quadratic term $x_i x_j$ with $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ is **non-convex**; vectors make the term $v_i \cdot v_j$ **bilinear yet convexifiable** via the PSD constraint.
- **Why random hyperplane?** It is the *unique* rounding that respects rotational symmetry of the sphere, guaranteeing that the cut probability is purely a function of the angle between vectors.
- **Why is PSD enough?** Any $X \succeq 0$ admits a Cholesky factorization $X = V^T V$, so the matrix represents inner products of real vectors — geometry is preserved.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| $\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2}$ | Vector relaxation of MAX-CUT | SDP formulation |
| $X \succeq 0$, $\;X_{ii} = 1$ | PSD Gram matrix with unit diagonal | Canonical SDP form |
| $\Pr[(i,j)\text{ cut}] = \dfrac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}$ | Hyperplane rounding probability | GW analysis |
| $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$ | Angle between vectors | GW analysis |
| $f(\theta) = \dfrac{\theta/\pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)}$ | Loss ratio for edge of angle $\theta$ | Approximation proof |
| $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in [0,\pi]} f(\theta)$ | Goemans–Williamson ratio | Approximation ratio |
| $\alpha_{GW} \approx \mathbf{0.878567}$ | Numerical optimum | Final guarantee |
| $E[\text{cut}] \ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{SDP-OPT}$ | Expected weight guarantee | Performance bound |
| $\int_{0}^{\pi} \dfrac{\theta}{\pi}\,\sin^{2}(\theta/2)\,d\theta = \dfrac{2}{\pi}$ | Expected angle term integral | GW normalization |
| $\text{tr}(X) = n$, $\;\text{rank}(X) = n$ | Integer feasible Gram matrix | Equivalence check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** SDP-based approximations are used in **sensor network localization (5G beamforming)**, **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **statistical ranking (PageRank generalizations)**, and **quantum information theory** (where PSD matrices model density operators).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From MAX-CUT IQP to SDP Relaxation

**Step 1 — Integer formulation.** For a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ with edge weights $w_{ij} \ge 0$, the MAX-CUT problem asks to partition $V$ into $(S, \bar{S})$ maximizing $\sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \mathbf{1}[i \in S,\, j \in \bar{S}]$.

Introduce variables $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ where $x_i = +1$ iff $i \in S$. Then

$$\mathbf{1}[i \in S,\, j \in \bar{S}] = \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}.$$

Hence MAX-CUT is equivalent to the **Integer Quadratic Program (IQP)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_i \in \{-1, +1\}, \quad \forall i \in V.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Relax Boolean to Vector.** Replace scalar $x_i$ by a unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\|v_i\|_2 = 1$, and replace the product $x_i x_j$ with the inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$. This yields the **vector program**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \|v_i\|_2^{2} = 1, \quad \forall i \in V.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to SDP via Gram matrix.** Define $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$. Then $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is symmetric, PSD (it is a Gram matrix), and the unit-norm constraint becomes $X_{ii} = 1$. The relaxation becomes a **linear program in matrix $X$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - X_{ij}}{2} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & X_{ii} = 1,\quad \forall i, \\
& X \succeq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is in standard **primal SDP form**. The rank constraint is dropped (no longer enforced), which is precisely the **relaxation** that makes the problem convex.

> [!NOTE]
> In the integer optimum, $X = v v^T$ where $v = (x_1, \ldots, x_n) \in \{-1,+1\}^n$, so $\text{rank}(X) = 1$. Dropping the rank constraint is what we mean by "relaxation."

### 3.2 Derivation: Goemans–Williamson Approximation Ratio

**Step 1 — Optimal SDP vectors $\{v_i\}_{i \in V}$.** Let $\text{OPT}_{SDP}$ denote the SDP optimum. By linearity, every $v_i$ is on the unit sphere.

**Step 2 — Random hyperplane rounding.** Choose a vector $r \in \mathbb{R}^n$ drawn uniformly from the Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(0, I_n)$. Place vertex $i$ in set $S$ iff $v_i \cdot r \ge 0$, otherwise in $\bar{S}$.

**Step 3 — Cut probability for edge $(i,j)$.** Let $\theta_{ij}$ be the angle between $v_i$ and $v_j$, so $v_i \cdot v_j = \cos\theta_{ij}$. A classical geometric fact (rotational invariance) states:

$$\Pr[(i,j)\text{ is cut}] = \Pr[\text{sign}(v_i \cdot r) \neq \text{sign}(v_j \cdot r)] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}.$$

This holds because a random Gaussian direction $r$ induces a uniformly random great-circle cut of the sphere, and the probability that two vectors fall on opposite sides equals the angle between them divided by $\pi$.

**Step 4 — Expected cut weight.** By linearity of expectation:

$$
E[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}.
$$

**Step 5 — SDP objective in angle form.** Using $X_{ij} = \cos\theta_{ij}$ and the half-angle identity $1 - \cos\theta = 2\sin^{2}(\theta/2)$:

$$
\text{OPT}_{SDP} = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \sin^{2}(\theta_{ij}/2).
$$

**Step 6 — Bounding ratio edge-wise.** For each edge independently, define the local ratio:

$$f(\theta) = \frac{\theta/\pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)}.$$

Let $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in [0,\pi]} f(\theta)$. Then for every edge,

$$\frac{\theta/\pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)} \ge \alpha_{GW} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\theta}{\pi} \ge \alpha_{GW}\,\sin^{2}(\theta/2).$$

Summing over edges and using the two expressions for $\text{OPT}_{SDP}$ and $E[\text{cut}]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{cut}] &= \sum w_{ij}\,\frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} \\
&\ge \alpha_{GW} \sum w_{ij}\,\sin^{2}(\theta_{ij}/2) \\
&= \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}_{SDP} \\
&\ge \alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}_{IP}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The final inequality holds because the integer optimum $\text{OPT}_{IP}$ is bounded above by the SDP relaxation: $\text{OPT}_{IP} \le \text{OPT}_{SDP}$.

**Step 7 — Computing $\alpha_{GW}$.** Differentiating $f(\theta)$ and setting $f'(\theta) = 0$:

$$
f'(\theta) = \frac{\frac{1}{\pi}\sin^{2}(\theta/2) - \frac{\theta}{\pi}\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)}{\sin^{4}(\theta/2)} = 0
$$

which simplifies to

$$\tan(\theta/2) = \theta/2.$$

Numerically, $\theta^{*} \approx 2.3311$ rad, yielding

$$\alpha_{GW} = f(\theta^{*}) \approx \mathbf{0.878567}.$$

This is the celebrated **Goemans–Williamson ratio**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: A Triangle (3 vertices)

Take $V = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with all edge weights $w_{ij} = 1$. Maximum cut is **2** (any two edges).

**SDP optimum:** Three unit vectors with pairwise inner product $-\frac{1}{2}$ (vertices of an equilateral triangle on the unit sphere) achieve

$$\sum \frac{1 - (-1/2)}{2} = 3 \cdot \frac{3}{4} = 2.25 \ge \text{OPT}_{IP} = 2.$$

**Integrality gap:** $\text{OPT}_{SDP}/\text{OPT}_{IP} = 2.25/2 = 1.125$, consistent with the GW worst-case factor of $1/\alpha_{GW} \approx 1.138$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End SDP Approximation Pipeline (MAX-CUT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: Weighted Graph G V E w"] --> B["Formulate Integer Quadratic Program IQP"]
    B --> C["Lift to Vector Program v i in S n minus 1"]
    C --> D["Build PSD Gram Matrix X i j equals v i dot v j"]
    D --> E["Solve Convex SDP via Interior Point Method"]
    E --> F["Extract Optimal Unit Vectors v star i"]
    F --> G["Random Hyperplane Rounding: sample r in N 0 I"]
    G --> H["Assign vertex i to S if v i dot r greater than 0"]
    H --> I["Return Cut with Guarantee E greater than alpha sub GW times OPT"]
    F -.-> J["Analysis: theta i j equals arccos v i dot v j"]
    J -.-> G
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the GW Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        A1["Graph G = V, E, w"]
    end
    subgraph RELAX["Relaxation Stage"]
        B1["Integer QP"]
        B2["Vector Program"]
        B3["SDP Formulation"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph SOLVE["Solver Stage"]
        C1["Interior Point SDP Solver"]
        C2["Optimal vectors v star i"]
        B3 --> C1 --> C2
    end
    subgraph ROUND["Rounding Stage"]
        D1["Sample Gaussian r"]
        D2["Compute sign v i dot r"]
        D3["Output cut S, S bar"]
        C2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS["Analysis Stage"]
        E1["Cut probability equals theta divided by pi"]
        E2["Bound ratio equals alpha sub GW"]
        E3["Final Approximation Guarantee"]
        D3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3
    end
    A1 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Why the GW Ratio is Tight

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Take the worst-case angle theta in 0 pi"] --> P2["Maximize sin squared theta over 2 over theta divided by pi"]
    P2 --> P3["Equivalently minimize f of theta equals theta over pi divided by sin squared theta over 2"]
    P3 --> P4["Set derivative to zero: tan theta over 2 equals theta over 2"]
    P4 --> P5["Solve numerically: theta star equals 2.3311 radians"]
    P5 --> P6["Plug back: alpha sub GW equals f of theta star equals 0.878567"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Semidefinite Programming (SDP) relaxation of a combinatorial optimization problem. Why is PSD the natural constraint class when lifting Boolean variables to vectors?*

**Model Answer:**
An SDP relaxation replaces a hard integer/quadratic program by a convex optimization over a Positive Semidefinite (PSD) matrix $X$, with both objective and constraints linear in the entries of $X$. The PSD constraint $X \succeq 0$ is natural because it guarantees $X$ is a Gram matrix: there exist vectors $v_i$ with $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$. When Boolean variables $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ are lifted to unit vectors $v_i$ with $\|v_i\|_2 = 1$, the quadratic term $x_i x_j$ becomes the bilinear inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$, which is convexifiable exactly via the PSD constraint. The relaxation is tight when the optimal $X$ has rank 1.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Goemans–Williamson theorem for MAX-CUT and write the closed-form expression used to derive the approximation ratio.*

**Model Answer:**
**Theorem (Goemans–Williamson, 1994):** For any weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$, there is a polynomial-time randomized algorithm that produces a cut whose expected weight is at least $\alpha_{GW} \cdot \text{OPT}$, where

$$\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in [0,\pi]} \frac{\theta/\pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)} \approx 0.878567.$$

The algorithm: (1) solve the vector relaxation to get unit vectors $v_i$, (2) sample a Gaussian vector $r$, (3) cut along the hyperplane orthogonal to $r$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

**Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

*(a) [7 Marks]* Formulate MAX-CUT for a weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ as an integer quadratic program. Then derive the SDP relaxation step by step.

*(b) [7 Marks]* Describe the Goemans–Williamson randomized rounding scheme in detail. Prove that the expected weight of the cut produced is at least $\alpha_{GW}$ times the optimum, where

$$\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta \in [0,\pi]} \frac{\theta/\pi}{\sin^{2}(\theta/2)}.$$

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **IQP Formulation.** With $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ indicating side of vertex $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & x_i \in \{-1, +1\},\quad \forall i \in V.
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **Vector Relaxation.** Substitute $x_i \to v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ with $\|v_i\|_2 = 1$, and $x_i x_j \to v_i \cdot v_j$. The problem becomes a non-convex vector program.

3. **PSD Lift.** Define $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$. Then $X$ is a Gram matrix, hence $X \succeq 0$, and $X_{ii} = \|v_i\|_2^2 = 1$. The objective becomes linear in $X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\dfrac{1 - X_{ij}}{2} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & X_{ii} = 1,\quad X \succeq 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a convex SDP, solvable in polynomial time.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. **Rounding Rule.** Draw $r \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I_n)$. Assign $i$ to side $S$ iff $v_i \cdot r \ge 0$.

2. **Edge-cut Probability.** By rotational symmetry, $\Pr[(i,j) \text{ cut}] = \theta_{ij}/\pi$ where $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$.

3. **Expected Cut.**

$$E[\text{cut}] = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi}.$$

4. **SDP Value in Angle Form.** Since $1 - X_{ij} = 1 - \cos\theta_{ij} = 2\sin^{2}(\theta_{ij}/2)$:

$$\text{OPT}_{SDP} = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij}\,\sin^{2}(\theta_{ij}/2).$$

5. **Final Bound.** Let $\alpha_{GW} = \min_{\theta} f(\theta)$. Then $\theta/\pi \ge \alpha_{GW}\sin^{2}(\theta/2)$ for every $\theta$, so:

$$E[\text{cut}] \ge \alpha_{GW}\,\text{OPT}_{SDP} \ge \alpha_{GW}\,\text{OPT}_{IP}.$$

---

**Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*(a) [7 Marks]* Explain with an example how the MAX-CUT problem on a triangle (3 vertices, all edge weights 1) is solved by SDP relaxation. Compute the integrality gap for this case.

*(b) [7 Marks]* Discuss the geometric intuition behind random hyperplane rounding. Why does rotational invariance force the cut probability of edge $(i,j)$ to be exactly $\theta_{ij}/\pi$?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

For the triangle $K_3$ with unit weights, an integer cut has value $2$ (any two edges). The SDP optimum is achieved by placing the three unit vectors at the vertices of an equilateral triangle inscribed in the unit sphere: $v_i \cdot v_j = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ for all distinct $i, j$. Therefore:

$$\text{OPT}_{SDP} = 3 \cdot \frac{1 - (-1/2)}{2} = 3 \cdot \frac{3}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25.$$

The **integrality gap** is

$$\frac{\text{OPT}_{SDP}}{\text{OPT}_{IP}} = \frac{2.25}{2} = 1.125.$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The Gaussian vector $r$ induces a uniformly random great-circle partition of the unit sphere. By rotational symmetry, the probability that $v_i$ and $v_j$ fall on opposite sides of this great circle depends only on the geodesic distance between them, which is exactly $\theta_{ij}$. Since a great circle of total angular measure $2\pi$ separates any two vectors of angular separation $\theta$ with probability $\theta/\pi$, we have $\Pr[(i,j)\text{ cut}] = \theta_{ij}/\pi$. This rotational invariance is critical — any non-uniform rounding scheme would break the symmetry and worsen the worst-case guarantee.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SDP Approximation Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SDP with LP relaxation:** Students often write LP constraints where SDP constraints are required. Always state $X \succeq 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit-norm condition:** The constraint $X_{ii} = 1$ is essential — without it the relaxation is unbounded.
> 3. **Wrong direction of inequality in GW bound:** The expected cut is $\ge \alpha_{GW}\cdot\text{OPT}$, not the reverse.
> 4. **Mis-stating $\alpha_{GW}$ value:** It is $\approx 0.878567$, **not** $0.5$, $0.8$, or any round number.
> 5. **Dropping the rank-1 condition:** Forgetting that the integer case has $\text{rank}(X) = 1$ loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the rotation-symmetry justification:** Examiners expect a one-line explanation of why $\theta/\pi$ is the cut probability.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SDP** optimizes a linear function of entries of a PSD matrix $X$, with $X \succeq 0$.
- The pipeline is **IQP → Vector Program → SDP → Rounding**.
- The **GW algorithm** gives $\alpha_{GW} \approx \mathbf{0.878567}$ for MAX-CUT.
- The **random hyperplane** cut probability is $\theta_{ij}/\pi$, derived from rotational invariance.
- The **integrality gap** of MAX-CUT for the triangle is $9/8 = 1.125$.
- **Positive semidefiniteness** ($X \succeq 0$) is equivalent to the existence of vectors $\{v_i\}$ with $X_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$.
- The **GW ratio** is the best known unless the Unique Games Conjecture is false.
- SDP relaxations extend to **MAX-2SAT**, **graph coloring**, **correlation clustering**, and **minimum bisection**.
- SDP solvers run in polynomial time but are **slower than LP solvers** in practice.
- Rounding schemes **other than random hyperplane** (e.g., deterministic sphere division) generally lose the approximation guarantee.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SDP-Based Approximation Algorithm and Analysis of Approximation Ratio for the MAX-CUT Problem
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** SDP-Based Approximation Algorithm and Analysis of Approximation Ratio for the MAX-CUT Problem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The MAX-CUT Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAX-CUT (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights $w_{ij} \ge 0$ for each edge $(i,j) \in E$, the **MAX-CUT** problem asks for a partition of the vertex set $V$ into two disjoint subsets $S$ and $\bar{S} = V \setminus S$ such that the total weight of edges crossing the partition is **maximized**.

Mathematically, the objective is:

$$
\text{MAX-CUT}(G) = \max_{S \subseteq V} \; \sum_{(i,j) \in \delta(S)} w_{ij}
$$

where $\delta(S) = \{ (i,j) \in E : i \in S,\; j \notin S \}$ is the set of edges whose endpoints lie on opposite sides of the cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardness Result (KTU High-Yield):** MAX-CUT is **NP-hard** (Garey & Johnson, 1979) and remains NP-hard even on unweighted graphs of bounded degree 3. It is **APX-complete**, meaning it admits a constant-factor approximation but is unlikely to have a Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (PTAS) unless P = NP.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Friends & Strangers" Partition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy)**
> Imagine a college social network where each student is a vertex, and each friendship has a "compatibility weight." Your job is to seat all students at **two giant tables** such that the **total incompatibility of cross-table friendships is maximized** (or, equivalently, you wish to split them so the *strongest* friendships are placed on *opposite* sides — perhaps to maximize the drama at the reunion!).
>
> Mathematically, however, the standard convention is to **maximize the cut weight**, meaning we want to put the heaviest edges *across* the partition. The **Goemans–Williamson (GW) SDP relaxation**, developed by Michel Goemans and David Williamson in their seminal **1995 paper** (winner of the 2000 Gödel Prize), achieves a celebrated approximation ratio of **0.8785**, which is the best known for this problem to date.

---

### 1.3 Why a New Approach? — Limits of Classical Methods

Before the SDP revolution, the best known approximation for MAX-CUT was the simple **randomized 0.5-approximation**: assign each vertex to $S$ or $\bar{S}$ uniformly at random, yielding an expected cut value of exactly $\frac{1}{2}\sum w_{ij}$. Greedy and local-search heuristics improved this to about **0.5**, but no constant-factor algorithm surpassed 0.5 until Goemans & Williamson demonstrated that **semidefinite programming** could break this barrier decisively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The GW algorithm proves that **non-linear convex programming relaxations** (specifically SDP) can give strictly better approximation ratios than any Linear Programming (LP) relaxation for combinatorial optimization problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Integer Quadratic Program (Exact Formulation)

Every vertex $i \in V$ is assigned a **label** $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, where $+1$ denotes "set $S$" and $-1$ denotes "set $\bar{S}$." The edge $(i,j)$ is **cut** if and only if $x_i \neq x_j$, which is equivalent to $\frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} = 1$. Thus the integer program (IP) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(IP)} \quad \max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & x_i \in \{-1, +1\}, \quad \forall\, i \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$?** Because $x_i x_j = -1 \iff$ endpoints differ, and $x_i x_j = +1 \iff$ endpoints match. Thus $\frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} \in \{0, 1\}$ acts as the indicator of the edge being cut.

---

### 2.2 The Semidefinite Programming (SDP) Relaxation

Goemans–Williamson's key idea: **replace each scalar label $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ with a unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$** in a higher-dimensional space. The product $x_i x_j$ is replaced by the **inner product** $v_i \cdot v_j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(SDP)} \quad \max \quad & \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \\
\text{subject to} \quad & v_i \cdot v_i = 1, \quad \forall\, i \in V \\
& v_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, \quad \forall\, i \in V
\end{aligned}
$$

The constraint $v_i \cdot v_i = 1$ is the **vector generalization** of $x_i^2 = 1$. Observe that the SDP strictly contains the IP: if $v_i \in \mathbb{R}$ (1D), then $v_i = x_i$ recovers the IP exactly. Thus:

$$
\text{OPT}_{\text{SDP}} \;\ge\; \text{OPT}_{\text{IP}} \;=\; \text{MAX-CUT}(G)
$$

---

### 2.3 The Goemans–Williamson Randomized Rounding Scheme

Once the SDP solver (e.g., interior-point method in time $O(n^{3.5})$) returns optimal vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_n$, the algorithm performs a **geometric rounding**:

> **Algorithm: GW-SDP-MAX-CUT**
> 1. Solve the SDP relaxation; obtain unit vectors $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$.
> 2. Choose a **random hyperplane** $r$ uniformly from the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^{n-1}$.
> 3. For each vertex $i$, set:
>    - $x_i = +1$ if $v_i \cdot r \ge 0$
>    - $x_i = -1$ if $v_i \cdot r < 0$
> 4. Return the cut $(S, \bar{S})$ where $S = \{ i : x_i = +1 \}$.

The random hyperplane $r$ partitions the unit vectors into two half-spaces based on the sign of their projection onto $r$. Vectors with a small angle between them are likely placed on the same side; vectors nearly opposite are likely separated.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | MAX-CUT IP objective | $\sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$ | $x_i \in \{-1,+1\}$ |
| 2 | SDP relaxation objective | $\sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2}$ | $\vert v_i \vert = 1$ |
| 3 | Optimal SDP value | $\text{SDP}^{\star}$ | $\text{SDP}^{\star} \ge \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}$ |
| 4 | Indicator for cut edge | $\frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2}$ | Equals 1 iff $v_i, v_j$ are separated by hyperplane |
| 5 | Prob. edge $(i,j)$ is cut | $\Pr[(i,j)\text{ cut}] = \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$ | Valid for any $\rho = v_i \cdot v_j \in [-1,1]$ |
| 6 | Expected cut weight | $E[\text{CUT}] = \sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$ | Linearity of expectation |
| 7 | SDP objective rewrite | $\text{SDP} = \sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - \rho_{ij}}{2}$ | Where $\rho_{ij} = v_i \cdot v_j$ |
| 8 | GW worst-case ratio $\alpha_{\text{GW}}$ | $\min_{\rho \in [-1,1]} \frac{2\arccos(\rho)}{\pi (1 - \rho)}$ | $\approx 0.87856$ |
| 9 | Optimal $\theta$ (angle) | $\theta^{\star} \approx 2.3311$ rad ($\approx 133.56^\circ$) | Where $\cos\theta = \rho^{\star} \approx -0.6891$ |
| 10 | Final approximation ratio | $E[\text{CUT}] \ge \alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \text{SDP}^{\star} \ge 0.8785 \cdot \text{OPT}$ | **GW Theorem** |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where does MAX-CUT appear in engineering?**
> - **VLSI Circuit Design:** partitioning a chip's logic gates into two regions to minimize inter-region wiring (dual of MAX-CUT).
> - **Statistical Physics (Ising Model):** ground-state energy of an anti-ferromagnetic Ising spin glass maps exactly to MAX-CUT.
> - **Network Design / Load Balancing:** dividing network nodes into two clusters to maximize inter-cluster traffic, useful in data center topology design.
> - **Machine Learning (Spectral Clustering):** MAX-CUT-based objectives are used in correlation clustering, image segmentation, and graph-based semi-supervised learning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Probability That an Edge is Cut

**Step 1 — Geometry of the random hyperplane.**

Let $r$ be a uniformly random unit vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$, and let $v_i, v_j$ be two fixed unit vectors with angle $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$ between them. The hyperplane perpendicular to $r$ separates $v_i$ and $v_j$ if and only if their projections onto $r$ have opposite signs.

**Step 2 — Equivalent angular condition.**

By rotational symmetry, we may fix $r$ to vary only in the 2D plane spanned by $v_i$ and $v_j$. In this plane, the projection of $r$ onto this 2D subspace is a unit vector making an angle $\phi$ with $v_i$, where $\phi$ is uniform in $[0, 2\pi)$.

**Step 3 — Sign condition.**

The projection of $v_i$ onto $r$ has the same sign as $\cos\phi$, and the projection of $v_j$ onto $r$ has the sign of $\cos(\phi - \theta_{ij})$. They are on opposite sides when $\cos\phi$ and $\cos(\phi - \theta_{ij})$ have opposite signs.

**Step 4 — Compute the probability.**

The endpoints are separated when $r$ lies in the angular wedge of size $\theta_{ij}$ (the "bad" wedge). The total measure of directions is $2\pi$, so:

$$
\Pr[(i,j) \text{ is cut}] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{\pi} = \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}
$$

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: The GW Approximation Ratio

**Step 1 — Expected cut weight via linearity of expectation.**

For each edge $(i,j)$, let $X_{ij}$ be the indicator that the edge is cut. The expected total cut weight is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{CUT}(G)] &= E\!\left[\sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} X_{ij}\right] \\
&= \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \Pr[X_{ij} = 1] \\
&= \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the ratio function.**

For a given inner product $\rho = v_i \cdot v_j \in [-1, 1]$, the contribution of edge $(i,j)$ to the expected cut is $w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos \rho}{\pi}$, while its contribution to the SDP objective is $w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - \rho}{2}$. We define the **per-edge ratio function**:

$$
f(\rho) = \frac{\arccos(\rho) / \pi}{(1 - \rho)/2} = \frac{2 \arccos(\rho)}{\pi (1 - \rho)}
$$

**Step 3 — Show the inequality pointwise.**

We claim that for all $\rho \in [-1, 1]$:

$$
\frac{\arccos(\rho)}{\pi} \;\ge\; \alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \frac{1 - \rho}{2}
$$

where $\alpha_{\text{GW}} = \min_{\rho \in [-1, 1]} f(\rho)$. This is equivalent to $f(\rho) \ge \alpha_{\text{GW}}$ for every $\rho$.

**Step 4 — Minimize $f(\rho)$.**

Differentiate and set to zero. Let $g(\theta) = \frac{2\theta}{\pi(1 - \cos\theta)}$ (substitute $\rho = \cos\theta$):

$$
g'(\theta) = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{(1 - \cos\theta) - \theta \sin\theta}{(1 - \cos\theta)^2}
$$

Setting the numerator to zero:

$$
1 - \cos\theta = \theta \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the transcendental equation.**

The unique solution in $(0, \pi)$ is $\theta^{\star} \approx 2.3311$ radians ($\approx 133.56^\circ$), with $\cos\theta^{\star} \approx -0.6891$. Plugging back:

$$
\alpha_{\text{GW}} = g(\theta^{\star}) = \frac{2 \cdot 2.3311}{\pi (1 - (-0.6891))} = \frac{4.6622}{\pi \cdot 1.6891} \approx 0.87856
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the approximation guarantee.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{CUT}(G)] &= \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi} \\
&\ge \alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \\
&= \alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \text{SDP}^{\star} \\
&\ge \alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the expected cut is at least $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \text{OPT}$, **there exists a realization of the random hyperplane that achieves at least this expected value**. By the probabilistic method:

$$
\boxed{\text{MAX-CUT}(G) \;\ge\; 0.8785 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Boundary Behavior of $f(\rho)$

| $\rho = v_i \cdot v_j$ | Geometric Meaning | $\frac{\arccos\rho}{\pi}$ (Prob. of cut) | $\frac{1-\rho}{2}$ (SDP contribution) | Ratio $f(\rho)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $+1$ | Identical direction | $0$ | $0$ | limit $= 1$ |
| $0$ | Orthogonal vectors | $0.5$ | $0.5$ | $1.000$ |
| $-0.6891$ | Optimal $\rho^{\star}$ | $0.7422$ | $0.8446$ | $\mathbf{0.8786}$ (min) |
| $-1$ | Opposite vectors | $1.0$ | $1.0$ | $1.000$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** The ratio $f(\rho)$ is bounded between $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8786$ and $1$, attaining its minimum at $\rho^{\star} \approx -0.6891$. At both extremes ($\rho = \pm 1$), the ratio approaches $1$ (the rounding is exact there). The "bottleneck" is at moderate negative correlations, where the SDP is "too optimistic" relative to the rounding.

---

### 3.4 Summary of the GW Proof Pipeline

$$
\text{IP (discrete)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{vector lift}}\; \text{SDP (continuous)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{solve in poly-time}}\; \{v_i^{\star}\} \;\xrightarrow{\text{random hyperplane}}\; \text{Cut}(G)
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}}_{\text{integer optimum}} \;\le\; \underbrace{\text{SDP}^{\star}}_{\text{relaxation}} \;\xrightarrow{\times\, \alpha_{\text{GW}}}\; \underbrace{E[\text{CUT}]}_{\ge\, 0.8785 \cdot \text{OPT}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: GW-SDP-MAX-CUT Algorithm Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G with weights w_ij] --> B[Formulate Integer Program IP]
    B --> C[Replace scalar labels with unit vectors v_i]
    C --> D[Semidefinite Program SDP]
    D --> E[Interior Point Solver]
    E --> F[Optimal Vectors v_1 to v_n]
    F --> G[Sample Random Hyperplane r from unit sphere]
    G --> H{For each vertex i}
    H --> I[Compute v_i dot r]
    I --> J{v_i dot r is positive?}
    J -- YES --> K[Assign vertex i to set S]
    J -- NO  --> L[Assign vertex i to set complement of S]
    K --> M[Output Cut S and complement of S]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute Expected Cut Weight]
    N --> O[Apply Ratio Bound alpha_GW approx 0.8785]
    O --> P[Approximation Guarantee]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: The GW Approximation Guarantee Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MaxCut OPT] --> B[OPT is less than or equal SDP star]
    B --> C[SDP star solved in polynomial time]
    C --> D[Vectors v_i obtained]
    D --> E[Random Hyperplane Rounding]
    E --> F[Expected Cut Weight]
    F --> G[Expected Cut is at least 0.8785 times SDP star]
    G --> H[Expected Cut is at least 0.8785 times OPT]
    H --> I[Therefore GW is a 0.8785 approximation]
```

### 4.3 Diagram: Vector Geometry of Random Hyperplane Rounding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Geometry [Unit Sphere Geometry]
        O[Origin O]
        R[Random Hyperplane direction r]
        V1[Vector v_i]
        V2[Vector v_j]
        Angle[Angle theta between v_i and v_j equals arccos of v_i dot v_j]
    end
    V1 --> Angle
    V2 --> Angle
    R --> HyperplaneCut[Hyperplane perpendicular to r]
    HyperplaneCut --> Sides{Separates v_i and v_j}
    Sides -- YES --> CutEdge[Edge ij is in cut]
    Sides -- NO --> KeepEdge[Edge ij not in cut]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Two vectors $v_i$ and $v_j$ are **separated** by the random hyperplane if and only if the angle $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$ is small enough that the hyperplane slices through the wedge between them. The probability of separation equals $\theta_{ij} / \pi$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PEITT751]**

**Q1. Define the MAX-CUT problem and state its decision version. Why is it NP-hard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with edge weights $w_{ij} \ge 0$, the **MAX-CUT** problem asks to partition $V$ into two disjoint subsets $S$ and $\bar{S}$ to maximize the total weight of edges crossing the partition: $\max_{S \subseteq V} \sum_{(i,j) \in \delta(S)} w_{ij}$.
- **Decision version (1 mark):** Given $G$ and a threshold $K$, decide whether there exists a cut of weight $\ge K$.
- **NP-hardness (1 mark):** The decision version is NP-complete, established by Karp's 21 NP-complete problems (1972). The unweighted MAX-CUT is NP-hard even on graphs of maximum degree 3, and is APX-complete.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PEITT751]**

**Q2. What is the Goemans–Williamson approximation ratio for MAX-CUT, and how does it compare to the trivial 0.5-approximation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Trivial 0.5-approximation (1 mark):** A randomized algorithm that places each vertex in $S$ or $\bar{S}$ independently and uniformly at random yields an expected cut of exactly $\frac{1}{2} \sum w_{ij}$, giving a 0.5-approximation.
- **GW ratio (2 marks):** The Goemans–Williamson algorithm (1995) achieves an approximation ratio of $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8785$, derived via SDP relaxation followed by random hyperplane rounding. The constant 0.8785 is defined as $\min_{\theta \in (0,\pi)} \frac{2\theta}{\pi(1 - \cos\theta)}$ and is **theoretically optimal** under the Unique Games Conjecture (Khot et al., 2007).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper, PEITT751, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Formulate the MAX-CUT problem as a quadratic integer program. Show how to obtain its semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxation by replacing scalar labels with unit vectors. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Goemans–Williamson random hyperplane rounding scheme. Prove that the probability an edge $(i,j)$ is cut equals $\frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) IP Formulation and SDP Relaxation [7 marks]**

- **IP Formulation (3 marks):** Assign a label $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ to each vertex $i$, with $x_i = +1$ meaning $i \in S$ and $x_i = -1$ meaning $i \in \bar{S}$. An edge $(i,j)$ is cut iff $x_i \neq x_j$, equivalent to $\frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} = 1$. The IP is:

$$
\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad x_i \in \{-1, +1\}
$$

- **Vector Substitution (2 marks):** Replace each $x_i$ with a unit vector $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and $x_i x_j$ with the inner product $v_i \cdot v_j$. The constraint $x_i^2 = 1$ becomes $v_i \cdot v_i = 1$.

- **SDP Formulation (2 marks):** The relaxation is:

$$
\max \sum_{(i,j) \in E} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2} \quad \text{s.t.} \quad v_i \cdot v_i = 1,\; v_i \in \mathbb{R}^n
$$

This is a **convex** SDP solvable in polynomial time (e.g., by interior-point methods in $O(n^{3.5})$), and $\text{SDP}^{\star} \ge \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}$.

**(b) GW Rounding and Cut Probability [7 marks]**

- **Algorithm Description (2 marks):** After solving the SDP, obtain vectors $\{v_i\}$. Sample a random unit vector $r$ uniformly from the unit sphere. Set $x_i = +1$ if $v_i \cdot r \ge 0$, else $x_i = -1$. Return cut $(S, \bar{S})$.

- **Probability Derivation (5 marks):** By rotational symmetry, project the problem onto the 2D plane $\Pi$ spanned by $v_i$ and $v_j$. In $\Pi$, the projection of $r$ is a unit vector making an angle $\phi$ with $v_i$, where $\phi \sim \text{Uniform}[0, 2\pi)$. The vectors $v_i$ and $v_j$ are separated iff $\cos\phi$ and $\cos(\phi - \theta_{ij})$ have opposite signs, which occurs exactly when $\phi$ lies in the angular wedge of size $\theta_{ij} = \arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)$. Thus:

$$
\Pr[(i,j) \text{ cut}] = \frac{\theta_{ij}}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State and prove the Goemans–Williamson theorem: the expected cut weight of the SDP-rounded solution is at least $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}$, where $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8785$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Show that the ratio function $f(\rho) = \frac{2\arccos(\rho)}{\pi(1-\rho)}$ achieves its minimum at $\theta^{\star} \approx 2.3311$ rad, and compute $\alpha_{\text{GW}}$ to four decimal places. Discuss briefly the connection to the Unique Games Conjecture. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) GW Theorem Statement and Proof [7 marks]**

- **Theorem Statement (1 mark):** For any graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights, the GW-SDP algorithm produces a cut of expected weight at least $0.8785 \cdot \text{OPT}_{\text{MAX-CUT}}$.

- **Setup (2 marks):** Let $\{v_i^{\star}\}$ be the optimal SDP solution, $\rho_{ij} = v_i^{\star} \cdot v_j^{\star}$, and let $X_{ij}$ be the indicator that edge $(i,j)$ is cut. By linearity:

$$
E[\text{CUT}] = \sum_{(i,j)} w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos(\rho_{ij})}{\pi}
$$

- **Pointwise Bound (2 marks):** Define $\alpha = \min_{\rho \in [-1,1]} \frac{2\arccos(\rho)}{\pi(1-\rho)}$. We claim $\frac{\arccos\rho}{\pi} \ge \alpha \cdot \frac{1-\rho}{2}$ for all $\rho$. Substituting:

$$
E[\text{CUT}] \ge \alpha \cdot \sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1-\rho_{ij}}{2} = \alpha \cdot \text{SDP}^{\star} \ge \alpha \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

- **Conclusion (2 marks):** Setting $\alpha = \alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8785$ gives the desired bound. By the probabilistic method, some specific hyperplane achieves at least this expected value, yielding a deterministic approximation guarantee.

**(b) Optimization of $f(\rho)$ and UGC Connection [7 marks]**

- **Substitution (1 mark):** Let $\rho = \cos\theta$, then $f = g(\theta) = \frac{2\theta}{\pi(1-\cos\theta)}$ for $\theta \in (0, \pi)$.

- **Differentiation (2 marks):** $g'(\theta) = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{(1-\cos\theta) - \theta\sin\theta}{(1-\cos\theta)^2}$. Setting the numerator to zero: $1 - \cos\theta = \theta\sin\theta$.

- **Solution (2 marks):** The unique root in $(0, \pi)$ is $\theta^{\star} \approx 2.3311$ rad ($\approx 133.56^\circ$), giving $\cos\theta^{\star} \approx -0.6891$. Substituting:

$$
\alpha_{\text{GW}} = \frac{2 \cdot 2.3311}{\pi (1 - (-0.6891))} = \frac{4.6622}{5.3067} \approx 0.8786
$$

- **UGC Connection (2 marks):** Khot, Kindler, Mossel, and O'Donnell (2007) proved that **assuming the Unique Games Conjecture (UGC)**, no polynomial-time algorithm can approximate MAX-CUT better than $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8785$. Hence the GW ratio is the **best possible** under standard complexity assumptions, making this result a "tight" milestone in approximation algorithms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SDP and IP objective:** Many students incorrectly write the SDP objective as $\sum w_{ij} (1 - v_i \cdot v_j)$ instead of dividing by 2. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Forgetting $v_i \cdot v_i = 1$ constraint:** The unit-norm condition is essential; without it, the SDP is unbounded. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Writing $\frac{\theta}{2\pi}$ instead of $\frac{\theta}{\pi}$ for cut probability:** A very common slip-up in Part B derivations. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Skipping the linearity of expectation step:** Marks are reserved for explicitly stating and applying linearity. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 5. **Not mentioning the existence of a deterministic cut:** Students often forget to invoke the probabilistic method to convert the expectation to a guaranteed cut. **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [ ] **MAX-CUT**: partition $V$ into $S, \bar{S}$ to **maximize** crossing edge weight.
- [ ] **IP formulation**: $\max \sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - x_i x_j}{2}$ with $x_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.
- [ ] **SDP relaxation**: replace $x_i$ with unit vector $v_i$; objective $\sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{1 - v_i \cdot v_j}{2}$.
- [ ] **Constraint $v_i \cdot v_i = 1$** — the vector analogue of $x_i^2 = 1$.
- [ ] **GW algorithm**: solve SDP → random hyperplane → round by sign of $v_i \cdot r$.
- [ ] **Cut probability**: $\Pr[(i,j) \text{ cut}] = \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$.
- [ ] **Expected cut**: $\sum w_{ij} \cdot \frac{\arccos(v_i \cdot v_j)}{\pi}$ (linearity of expectation).
- [ ] **Ratio function**: $f(\rho) = \frac{2\arccos\rho}{\pi(1-\rho)}$; minimized at $\theta^{\star} \approx 2.3311$ rad, $\rho^{\star} \approx -0.6891$.
- [ ] **GW constant**: $\alpha_{\text{GW}} \approx 0.8785$ — the **best possible** under UGC.
- [ ] **Time complexity**: SDP solvable in $O(n^{3.5})$ by interior-point methods; rounding is $O(n)$.
- [ ] **Engineered by**: Michel Goemans & David Williamson, **1995** (Gödel Prize 2000).
- [ ] **Optimality**: 0.8785 is tight under the Unique Games Conjecture (Khot et al., 2007).
- [ ] **Real-world use**: VLSI design, Ising spin glasses, statistical physics, spectral clustering, network partitioning.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry through the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ explicitly in both the IP and SDP, never assume the SDP is a drop-in replacement without renormalization, and remember to invoke the **probabilistic method** to convert expected cut values into a deterministic existence guarantee.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardness of Approximation: Reductions, Gaps, and Hardness Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT751 - APPROXIMATION ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hardness of Approximation: Reductions, Gaps, and Hardness Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Hardness of Approximation"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme treatment of **Approximation Algorithms (PEITT751)**, we have studied algorithms that run in **polynomial time** and produce solutions whose cost is provably close to optimal. **Hardness of Approximation** is the *dual* question: **how close to optimal can we hope to get, in polynomial time, for a given optimization problem, assuming P $\neq$ NP?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness of Approximation (KTU Definition):**
> A problem $\Pi$ is said to be **hard to approximate within a factor $\rho \geq 1$** (denoted $\rho$-inapproximable) if, for every $\epsilon > 0$, the existence of a polynomial-time $(\rho - \epsilon)$-approximation algorithm for $\Pi$ would imply $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$.

In other words, hardness of approximation is a **negative result**: it certifies a *lower bound* on the best possible approximation ratio achievable in polynomial time.

## 1.2 Three Pillars of Hardness Theory

The entire framework rests on three interlocking concepts:

| Pillar | One-line meaning |
|---|---|
| **Gap Problem** | A *promise problem* version of an optimization problem with two thresholds: a YES threshold $c$ and a NO threshold $s$, with $c > s$. |
| **Gap Reduction** | A polynomial-time transformation that *preserves* the gap structure from a known hard gap problem to a new one. |
| **Hardness Factor** | The ratio $c/s$ (or $c \cdot s$ depending on convention) for which the gap problem remains NP-hard. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tightrope" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Honest Surveyor:**
> Imagine a surveyor who must measure the height of a mountain. *Approximation algorithms* are rules-of-thumb that give estimates "within X% of the truth." *Hardness of approximation* is the **proof that no honest surveyor can do better than X%**, no matter how clever a measuring device is used, **unless mountains can be measured perfectly in polynomial time**.
>
> The **gap** is the *dead zone*: for any instance, the true height is either "tall" (above the YES line $c$) or "short" (below the NO line $s$). If the dead zone is wide, a polynomial-time algorithm **cannot even tell** which side an instance is on — let alone reconstruct the precise height. The width of the dead zone, quantified by $c/s$, is the **hardness factor**.

## 1.4 Why We Need a *Different* Notion of Reduction

Classical **Karp / Turing reductions** are too coarse for approximation. A standard polynomial-time reduction from **3SAT** to **Vertex Cover** preserves *decision* answers (YES $\leftrightarrow$ YES), but it does **not** preserve the *quality* of solutions. To prove approximation lower bounds, we need reductions that translate **optimal cost** to **optimal cost** in a **metrically controlled** way.

This led to two celebrated notions:

- **Gap-preserving reductions** (PCP-based)
- **L-reductions** (Papadimitriou–Yannakakis, 1991)

These are formalized in **SECTION 2** and applied in **SECTION 3**.

## 1.5 Module-3 Roadmap (KTU 2024 Scheme)

The KTU syllabus lists, under Module 3, the following sub-topics that this note consolidates:

1. **PCP Theorem** and its role in producing gaps.
2. **Gap-creating reductions** (e.g., 3SAT $\rightarrow$ Max-3SAT, Max-3SAT $\rightarrow$ Max-IS, Max-IS $\rightarrow$ Min-VC).
3. **Hardness factors** for **MAX-3SAT**, **MAX-Independent Set**, **Min-Vertex Cover**, **Set Cover**, and **Metric TSP**.
4. **L-reductions** and **APX-completeness**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PCP Theorem — The Engine of Hardness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PCP Theorem (Arora, Lund, Motwani, Sudan, Szegedy 1998; Arora, Safra 1992):**
> $$\mathbf{NP} = \mathbf{PCP}[\log n, 1]$$
> Equivalently, every NP statement has a **probabilistically checkable proof** of length $O(n \log n)$ that can be verified by reading only a **constant number of bits** (specifically, 3 bits suffice).

The PCP theorem is the *engine*: it converts the combinatorial structure of NP witnesses into a **gap** between satisfiable and unsatisfiable instances. Without it, hardness factors $> 1$ would not follow from $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$ alone.

## 2.2 Gap Problems — The Formal Definition

Let $\Pi$ be an optimization problem (say, MAX-3SAT). For constants $0 \leq s < c \leq 1$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gap$_c^s \Pi$ (Promise Problem):**
> - **YES instance:** $\mathrm{OPT}(\Pi) \geq c$ (the maximum fraction of satisfiable clauses is at least $c$).
> - **NO instance:** $\mathrm{OPT}(\Pi) < s$ (the maximum fraction of satisfiable clauses is strictly less than $s$).
> - **Promise:** the input is guaranteed to satisfy one of the two.
>
> If Gap$_c^s \Pi$ is **NP-hard**, then no polynomial-time algorithm can approximate $\Pi$ to a factor strictly better than $c/s$.

**Why the factor $c/s$?** Suppose an algorithm achieves approximation ratio $r < c/s$. Then on a YES instance with optimum $\geq c$, the algorithm would produce value $\geq c/r > s$, which contradicts the NO-instance guarantee.

## 2.3 Gap-Creating Reductions

A **gap reduction** is a polynomial-time map $f : \mathcal{I}_A \to \mathcal{I}_B$ such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{OPT}_A(x) \geq c_A &\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathrm{OPT}_B(f(x)) \geq c_B \\
\mathrm{OPT}_A(x) < s_A &\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathrm{OPT}_B(f(x)) < s_B
\end{aligned}
$$

The reduction *preserves* and *propagates* the gap. The **hardness factor** it proves is:

$$
\rho_B \;=\; \frac{c_B}{s_B}
$$

## 2.4 L-Reductions (Linear Reductions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Papadimitriou & Yannakakis, 1991):**
> A problem $A$ **L-reduces** to a problem $B$ with parameters $\alpha, \beta > 0$ (written $A \leq_L^{\alpha,\beta} B$) if there exist polynomial-time computable functions $f, g$ such that for every instance $x$ of $A$:
>
> 1. (Cost domination) $\mathrm{OPT}_B(f(x)) \;\leq\; \alpha \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_A(x)$.
> 2. (Solution lifting) For every feasible solution $y'$ to $f(x)$, $g(x, y')$ is a feasible solution to $x$ with
> $$\mathrm{cost}_A(x, g(x, y')) \;\leq\; \beta \cdot \mathrm{cost}_B(f(x), y').$$
>
> Combining: if $B$ has a $(1+\epsilon)$-approximation, then $A$ has a $(1 + \alpha\beta\epsilon)$-approximation.

**Consequence:** If $A$ is APX-hard and $A \leq_L B$, then $B$ is APX-hard.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Statement | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hardness factor (Max) | $\rho = c / s$ | Best approximation ratio ruled out |
| Hardness factor (Min) | $\rho = c / s$ with $c > 1$ | For minimization, $c$ and $s$ are upper bounds on OPT in NO/YES instances |
| PCP | $\mathbf{PCP}[\log n, 1] = \mathbf{NP}$ | Constant-query probabilistically checkable proofs |
| L-reduction cost | $\mathrm{OPT}_B(f(x)) \leq \alpha \cdot \mathrm{OPT}_A(x)$ | Input-shrinking factor |
| L-reduction lift | $\mathrm{cost}_A \leq \beta \cdot \mathrm{cost}_B$ | Solution-distance factor |
| Composed L-reduction | $A \leq_L^{\alpha_1,\beta_1} B$ and $B \leq_L^{\alpha_2,\beta_2} C$ give $A \leq_L^{\alpha_1\alpha_2, \beta_1\beta_2} C$ | Composes multiplicatively |
| Gap amplification | If Gap$_{c}^{s} A \leq_p$ Gap$_{c'}^{s'} B$ then $B$ is $c'/s'$-inapproximable | Standard propagation lemma |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all absolute values are typeset as $\vert \cdot \vert$. When writing "OPT $\leq \alpha$" with absolute value, write $\vert \mathrm{OPT} \vert \leq \alpha$ — **never** use the bare pipe `|`, as it breaks the markdown table parser.

## 2.6 Standard Hardness Factors (KTU Must-Know)

| Problem | Best known hardness factor | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **MAX-3SAT** | $7/8 + \epsilon$ | Håstad 2001 (optimal) |
| **MAX-Independent Set** | $n^{1-\epsilon}$ (for every $\epsilon > 0$) | Håstad 1999 |
| **Min-Vertex Cover** | $1.3606$ | Dinur–Safra 2010 |
| **Min-Set Cover** | $(1 - o(1)) \cdot \ln n$ | Feige 1998 |
| **Metric TSP** | $1.0045$ (i.e., $185/184$) | Papadimitriou–Vempala 2006 |
| **General TSP** | Any polynomial factor | Sahni–Gonzalez 1976 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For MAX-3SAT, the $7/8$ barrier comes from the **random assignment** algorithm, and Håstad's PCP-based hardness proves that improving on $7/8 + \epsilon$ is NP-hard. Always quote **Håstad 2001** when citing this result.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Hardness Theory

- **Cryptography:** Hardness of approximating lattice problems (LWE, SVP) underpins post-quantum cryptography.
- **Mechanism Design:** Nisan's *impossibility of combinatorial auctions* uses approximation hardness to prove that welfare-maximization is impossible under Bayesian incentive compatibility.
- **Machine Learning:** Learning conjunctions is hard precisely *because* certain satisfiability-related optimization problems are hard to approximate.
- **Operations Research:** Airlines and logistics companies know that for routing, beating $3/2$ for Metric TSP is essentially impossible — a fact that justifies investment in *heuristic* (not provable) solvers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — From Gap-3SAT to Hardness of MAX-3SAT

We will explicitly derive the canonical gap-producing reduction **3SAT $\to$ MAX-3SAT** that yields a hardness factor of $7/8 + \epsilon$.

### Setup

- **Source:** Gap$_{1-\epsilon}^{1}$ 3SAT (a gap version of 3SAT, NP-hard by PCP).
- **Target:** Gap$_{1-\epsilon'}^{7/8+\delta}$ MAX-3SAT.
- **Goal:** Show MAX-3SAT is hard to approximate within $7/8 + \epsilon$.

### Step 1 — Convert arbitrary clauses to 3-CNF

Given a 3SAT instance $\varphi$ with $m$ clauses (each a disjunction of $\leq 3$ literals), introduce new variables to "split" longer clauses. For a clause with $k < 3$ literals, pad with new variables $y$ to make a 3-literal clause. The padding variables can be set freely, so:

$$
\mathrm{OPT}_{\text{padded}}(\varphi) \;=\; \mathrm{OPT}(\varphi)
$$

### Step 2 — Define the gap

Let $c = 1 - \epsilon$ and $s = 1 - \epsilon'$ for small $\epsilon, \epsilon' > 0$. After padding:

- **YES instance:** There exists an assignment satisfying all $m$ clauses, i.e., $\mathrm{OPT} = 1$.
- **NO instance:** No assignment satisfies more than an $s$-fraction of clauses, i.e., $\mathrm{OPT} \leq s = 1 - \epsilon'$.

### Step 3 — Compute the hardness factor

For MAX-3SAT, the hardness factor is

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{c}{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 - \epsilon'} \;\approx\; 1 + \epsilon'.
$$

But the celebrated **Håstad (2001)** construction yields a much sharper gap:

$$
c = 1, \qquad s = \frac{7}{8} + \delta
$$

giving

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{1}{7/8 + \delta} \;=\; \frac{8}{7 + 8\delta}.
$$

### Step 4 — Translate back to inapproximability

If a polynomial-time algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ approximated MAX-3SAT within $7/8 + \delta$ for some $\delta > 0$, then on a YES instance (OPT = 1) it would return value $\geq (7/8 + \delta)^{-1} \cdot 1 = 1/(7/8 + \delta) > 7/8 + \delta$, contradicting the NO-instance promise. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{MAX\text{-}3SAT} \text{ is NP-hard to approximate within } \frac{7}{8} + \epsilon, \;\;\forall\, \epsilon > 0.}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation B — L-Reduction from MAX-3SAT to MAX-Independent Set

We now derive the **gap-preserving L-reduction** that yields the $n^{1-\epsilon}$ hardness factor for MAX-Independent Set.

### Construction

Given a 3-CNF formula $\varphi$ with $n$ variables $x_1, \dots, x_n$ and $m$ clauses $C_1, \dots, C_m$, construct a graph $G_\varphi = (V, E)$:

- $V$ has **one vertex per literal occurrence**, i.e., $3m$ vertices (each clause contributes 3 literal-vertices).
- Two vertices are adjacent iff the corresponding literals are **in the same clause** or are **logical negations** of each other.

The graph $G_\varphi$ has $|V| = 3m$ and maximum degree $\Delta = O(m)$ (bounded by the number of clauses containing a variable).

### Step 1 — Establish the cost domination

Let $\mathrm{IS}(G)$ denote the maximum independent set size.

**Claim:** $\mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi) \geq m$ always, since picking one true literal from each clause yields an independent set of size $m$ (literals within a clause are mutually adjacent and cannot both be true, but the chosen true literals are non-conflicting across clauses...).

More precisely, a satisfying assignment picks **at least one** true literal from each of the $m$ clauses, yielding an independent set of size at least $m$. Conversely, **every independent set of size $m$ corresponds to a truth assignment satisfying all $m$ clauses** (if such an assignment exists).

Therefore:

$$
\mathrm{OPT}_{\mathrm{MAX\text{-}IS}}(G_\varphi) \;=\; m + \text{(slack)}.
$$

For a satisfying instance, the slack is $0$, so $\mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi) = m$. For a $s$-satisfiable instance (where at most an $s$-fraction of clauses are simultaneously satisfiable):

$$
\mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi) \;\leq\; s \cdot m + \text{(slack)} \;\leq\; s \cdot m + 2m.
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the L-reduction bound

Using the standard MAX-3SAT $\to$ MAX-IS L-reduction with parameters $\alpha = 1, \beta = 1$ (a Karp-style translation), we obtain:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi)}{3m} \;\geq\; s \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi) \;\geq\; 3ms.
$$

Combining with Håstad's MAX-3SAT gap $s = 7/8 + \delta$:

$$
\mathrm{IS}(G_\varphi) \;\leq\; 3m \cdot (7/8 + \delta) \;=\; \frac{21m}{8} + 3m\delta.
$$

### Step 3 — Translate to approximation factor

For the source MAX-3SAT with hardness factor $7/8 + \delta$, the propagated gap in MAX-IS gives:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{IS}_{\max}}{\vert V \vert} \;\leq\; \frac{7}{8} + \delta.
$$

For a YES instance, $\mathrm{IS} = m = \vert V \vert / 3$. The hardness factor for MAX-IS is:

$$
\rho_{\mathrm{IS}} \;=\; \frac{1}{7/8 + \delta} \cdot 3 \;\to\; \frac{24}{7} \approx 3.43 \text{ for constant } \delta.
$$

**Håstad's improvement** (1999) uses a *long-code* based PCP to push this all the way to $n^{1-\epsilon}$:

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{MAX\text{-}IS} \text{ is NP-hard to approximate within } n^{1-\epsilon}, \;\; \forall\, \epsilon > 0.}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation C — From MAX-IS to Min-Vertex Cover (Complement Trick)

Vertex Cover is the *complement* of Independent Set. The exact relationship is:

$$
\boxed{\mathrm{VC}(G) \;=\; \vert V \vert - \mathrm{IS}(G)}
$$

### Gap propagation

Let $\mathrm{IS}_{\max} = \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert$. Then $\mathrm{VC}_{\min} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \vert V \vert$.

For a YES instance (large IS, small VC), $\mathrm{VC}_{\min} \leq c' \cdot \vert V \vert$ with $c'$ small.
For a NO instance (small IS, large VC), $\mathrm{VC}_{\min} \geq s' \cdot \vert V \vert$ with $s'$ large.

Using Håstad's MAX-IS gap with factor $n^{1-\epsilon}$:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{IS}_{\min}}{\vert V \vert} \;\leq\; \frac{1}{n^{1-\epsilon}}, \qquad
\frac{\mathrm{VC}_{\min}}{\vert V \vert} \;\geq\; 1 - \frac{1}{n^{1-\epsilon}}.
$$

The Min-VC hardness factor is:

$$
\rho_{\mathrm{VC}} \;=\; \frac{1 - 1/n}{1 - 1/n^{1-\epsilon}} \;\longrightarrow\; 1 \text{ as } n \to \infty.
$$

A finer analysis (Dinur–Safra 2010) gives a **constant** hardness factor of $1.3606$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Composition of Reductions

| Source | Target | Reduction Type | Parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$ | Hardness Factor Transferred |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gap-3SAT | Gap-MAX-3SAT | Padding | $(1, 1)$ | $7/8 + \epsilon$ |
| Gap-MAX-3SAT | Gap-MAX-IS | L-reduction | $(1, 1)$ | $n^{1-\epsilon}$ |
| Gap-MAX-IS | Gap-MIN-VC | Complement | $(1, 1)$ | $1.3606$ |
| Gap-MIN-VC | Gap-Set-Cover | L-reduction | $(2, 1)$ | $\Omega(\log n)$ |
| Gap-MIN-VC | Gap-Metric-TSP | L-reduction | $(2, 1)$ | $185/184 \approx 1.0045$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's takeaway:** The table above is your *roadmap*. When asked in a KTU exam to "trace the hardness chain from SAT to Metric TSP," reproduce this table with the parameters $\alpha, \beta$ justified by the L-reduction definitions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hardness-Propagation Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the **block-level functional architecture** of how a hardness result propagates from a *base PCP gap* all the way to *practical optimization problems*. This is the *map* you should sketch freehand in any KTU exam answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Base: Gap-3SAT via PCP Theorem"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Pad to 3-CNF"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Gap-MAX-3SAT, factor 7/8 + epsilon"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: L-reduce to Gap-MAX-IS, factor n to the 1 minus epsilon"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Complement to Gap-MIN-VC, factor 1.36"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 5: L-reduce to Gap-Set-Cover, factor log n"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Step 5b: L-reduce to Gap-Metric-TSP, factor 1.0045"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["End: APX-Hardness Statements"]
    nodeG --> nodeH

    classDef baseNode fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stepNode fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeA baseNode
    class nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG stepNode
    class nodeH endNode
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Gap Problem (Promise-Problem View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inst["Input Instance x"] --> yesCheck{"OPT >= c ?"}
    yesCheck -- YES --> yesOut["Instance belongs to YES class"]
    yesCheck -- NO --> noCheck{"OPT < s ?"}
    noCheck -- YES --> noOut["Instance belongs to NO class"]
    noCheck -- NO --> gapZone["Dead Zone: c <= OPT < s is empty by PROMISE"]
    yesOut --> algo["Algorithm A"]
    noOut --> algo
    gapZone -.->|rejected by promise| algo

    classDef yesClass fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef noClass fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef gapClass fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    classDef algoClass fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000

    class yesOut yesClass
    class noOut noClass
    class gapZone gapClass
    class algo algoClass
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed "dead zone"** is the *promise* of the gap problem. Any polynomial-time algorithm that is *forced* to be correct on both YES and NO instances **cannot peek inside the dead zone** — it must commit to a side. This is what makes the hardness factor $c/s$ unbreakable (modulo $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$).

## 4.3 L-Reduction as a Two-Box Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    instA["Instance x of Problem A"] --> fbox["Reduction f: Polynomial-time map"]
    fbox --> instB["Instance f x of Problem B"]
    instB --> solverB["Hypothetical rho-approximation Solver for B"]
    solverB --> solB["Solution y prime to f x"]
    solB --> gbox["Lifting g: Polynomial-time map"]
    gbox --> solA["Solution y to x"]

    fbox -.->|"cost domination: OPT_B <= alpha OPT_A"| note1["Box F"]
    gbox -.->|"solution lift: cost_A <= beta cost_B"| note2["Box G"]

    classDef redBox fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef solBox fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef noteBox fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666,color:#000

    class fbox redBox
    class gbox redBox
    class solverB,solB,solA solBox
    class note1,note2 noteBox
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map: APX, PTAS, FPTAS, APX-Hard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pClass["P: Polynomial-time exact"]
    fptas["FPTAS: Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme"]
    ptas["PTAS: Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme"]
    apx["APX: Constant-factor approximable"]
    apxHard["APX-Hard: No PTAS unless P=NP"]
    npo["NPO: All NP Optimization Problems"]

    pClass --> fptas
    fptas --> ptas
    ptas --> apx
    apx --> npo
    apxHard --> npo
    apx -.->|may be hard| apxHard

    classDef easyClass fill:#d4f4d4,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef midClass fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef hardClass fill:#f4d4d4,stroke:#660000,color:#000
    classDef allClass fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,color:#000

    class pClass,fptas easyClass
    class ptas,apx midClass
    class apxHard hardClass
    class npo allClass
```
